topics.title: Computer Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Computer Security (Synonyms: Cybersecurity / IT Security)**
> According to the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**, Computer Security is the protection afforded to an automated information system in order to attain the applicable objectives of preserving the **integrity**, **availability**, and **confidentiality** of information system resources (includes hardware, software, firmware, information/data, and telecommunications).

In simpler KTU syllabus terminology, **Computer Security** refers to the collective measures, controls, processes, and mechanisms implemented to protect computer systems, networks, programs, devices, and data from unauthorized access, alteration, destruction, or disruption.

### 1.2 The CIA Triad — The Foundation of Computer Security

The entire discipline of computer security revolves around three foundational pillars collectively known as the **CIA Triad**:

| Pillar | Definition | Real-World Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. | A leaked medical record violates confidentiality. |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring that data is accurate, consistent, and has not been tampered with. | A bank transaction amount altered mid-way violates integrity. |
| **Availability** | Ensuring that systems and data are accessible to authorized users when needed. | A DDoS attack that takes down a website violates availability. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every security attack, mechanism, and policy discussed in PECST863 must be mapped back to the **CIA Triad**. Examiners frequently ask: *"Which pillar of the CIA Triad does this attack violate?"*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **museum vault** that holds priceless jewels:
- **Confidentiality** → Only authorized curators with the right key can enter the vault.
- **Integrity** → The jewels inside must be the *originals* — no one swaps a real diamond with a fake.
- **Availability** → When the museum opens, the jewels *must* be on display; they cannot be hidden away by a thief.

In a computer, the **museum building** is the hardware, the **vault door lock** is the access control, the **security cameras** are audit logs, and the **jewels** are the data. Computer security is the *sum total* of guards, locks, cameras, alarms, and policies that keep the jewels safe.

### 1.4 Extended Security Goals (Beyond CIA)

Modern security frameworks (especially post-NEP 2020 OBE models) extend the CIA Triad with additional objectives:

1. **Authentication** — Verifying the identity of a user/system.
2. **Authorization** — Granting access rights based on identity.
3. **Non-repudiation** — Ensuring that a party cannot deny having performed an action.
4. **Accountability** — Tracing actions back to a specific user.
5. **Privacy** — Protecting personally identifiable information (PII).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — MTTR & MTTF:** Security engineers commonly measure the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** and **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** of security controls. A system with high MTTF and low MTTR is considered more secure and resilient.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three States of Information (Bell-LaPadula Foundation)

Information in any computer system exists in one of three states, and each state requires a different security strategy:

| State | Description | Security Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Data in Processing** | Active data in CPU registers, RAM. | Vulnerable to memory dumps, side-channel attacks. |
| **Data in Transit** | Data moving across a network. | Vulnerable to eavesdropping, MITM attacks. |
| **Data at Rest** | Data stored on disk, SSD, or backup media. | Vulnerable to theft, unauthorized access, physical seizure. |

### 2.2 Types of Security Threats

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Threat** is any potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability to breach security and cause harm to the system.

Computer security threats are broadly classified into:

**A. Based on Intent**
- **Malicious Threats** — Intentional attacks (e.g., hacking, malware, insider threats).
- **Accidental Threats** — Unintentional damage (e.g., human error, natural disasters).

**B. Based on Source**
- **Internal Threats** — Originate from within the organization (disgruntled employees, misconfigured systems).
- **External Threats** — Originate from outside (hackers, nation-states, cybercriminals).

**C. Based on Mechanism**

| Threat Type | Description | CIA Pillar Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **Interruption** | An asset becomes unavailable or unusable (e.g., DoS attack). | Availability |
| **Interception** | An unauthorized party gains access to an asset (e.g., wiretapping). | Confidentiality |
| **Modification** | Unauthorized tampering with data (e.g., man-in-the-middle). | Integrity |
| **Fabrication** | Generating fake data or identities (e.g., spoofing). | Authenticity/Integrity |

### 2.3 Types of Attacks — The KTU Classification

**1. Passive Attacks** — The attacker observes/eavesdrops without modifying data. Hard to detect because they don't alter system state.
- *Examples:* Traffic analysis, release of message contents.

**2. Active Attacks** — The attacker attempts to alter system resources or affect operations.
- *Examples:* Masquerade, replay, modification of messages, denial of service.

### 2.4 Vulnerabilities vs. Threats vs. Attacks

- **Vulnerability** — A weakness in the system (e.g., unpatched software, weak password).
- **Threat** — A potential danger that *could* exploit a vulnerability.
- **Attack** — The actual exploitation of a vulnerability by a threat.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Risk Equation (Fundamental):**
> $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$$
> If any of the three factors is zero, the risk becomes zero. This is the basis for layered defense (defense in depth).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation | Unit / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | T=Threat, V=Vulnerability, I=Impact | Qualitative scale (Low/Med/High) |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | SLE=Single Loss Expectancy, ARO=Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Currency (₹, \$, €) |
| **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | AV=Asset Value, EF=Exposure Factor | Currency |
| **Net Benefit of Control** | $NBA = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - ACS$ | ACS=Annual Cost of Safeguard | Currency |
| **Shannon Entropy (Info Content)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of symbol $i$ | **bits per symbol** |
| **Avalanche Effect Strength** | $\text{Hamming Distance} \geq 50\%$ | Bit flips after 1-bit input change | Percentage |
| **Key Space Size** | $\vert K \vert = 2^{L}$ | L = key length in bits | Pure number |
| **MTTF (Reliability)** | $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | **Hours** |
| **System Availability** | $A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | MTTR=Mean Time To Repair | Percentage (0 to 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The formula $R = T \times V \times I$ and the **ALE** calculation are extremely high-frequency questions in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks). Memorize the variable definitions cold.

### 2.6 Security Mechanisms vs. Security Services

- **Security Service** — A *goal* (what we want to achieve), e.g., confidentiality, integrity.
- **Security Mechanism** — A *means* (how we achieve it), e.g., encryption, digital signature, access control matrix.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Computer security principles are used in **banking (PCI-DSS compliance)**, **healthcare (HIPAA)**, **defense (military-grade encryption)**, **IoT (lightweight cryptography)**, and **cloud computing (zero-trust architecture)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: System Availability Formula

The availability $A$ of a system is defined as the proportion of time the system is operational over the total time.

**Step 1 — Define Total Mission Time:**
A system is either *working* (uptime) or *being repaired* (downtime). Total time is the sum:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Reliability Metrics:**
By definition, the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** is the average uptime, and the **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** is the average downtime. So:

$$T_{\text{up}} = MTTF, \quad T_{\text{down}} = MTTR$$

**Step 3 — Form the Availability Ratio:**
Availability is the fraction of time the system is in the working state:

$$A = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Reliability Metrics:**

$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
If $MTTR \rightarrow 0$ (instant repair), $A \rightarrow 1$ (perfect availability). If $MTTF \ll MTTR$, $A \rightarrow 0$ (system is mostly broken). A system is considered "highly available" if $A \geq 0.99999$ ("five nines").

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Entropy for English Text

Entropy $H(X)$ quantifies the average information (in bits) contained in each symbol of a message.

**Step 1 — Define Information Content of a Single Symbol:**
The self-information of an event with probability $p_i$ is:

$$I(x_i) = -\log_2(p_i)$$

**Step 2 — Average Over All Possible Symbols:**
The expected information per symbol is the expectation of $I(x_i)$ over the probability distribution:

$$H(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Self-Information Expression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot (-\log_2 p_i) \\
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to English Text:**
The English alphabet has **26 letters** with roughly equal probability $p_i = \frac{1}{26}$. The maximum entropy is:

$$H_{\max}(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{26} \frac{1}{26} \log_2 \frac{1}{26} = \log_2 26 \approx 4.7 \text{ bits/letter}$$

**Step 5 — Account for Letter Frequency Skew:**
In real English, 'E' is far more probable than 'Z'. Using empirical frequencies, the actual entropy is much lower:

$$H_{\text{English}} \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ bits/letter}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** Shannon entropy forms the theoretical foundation for **encryption strength**. A perfectly secure cipher produces ciphertext indistinguishable from random noise (entropy = 8 bits/byte). KTU examiners often ask students to compute entropy for simple probability distributions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — ALE Calculation

**Problem:** An e-commerce server has an asset value of **₹50,00,000**. An attack that compromises the server is expected to damage **60%** of the asset. The expected frequency of such an attack is **once every 4 years**. Find the **ALE**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Values:**
- Asset Value $AV = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$
- Exposure Factor $EF = 60\% = 0.60$
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence $ARO = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$ attacks/year

**Step 2 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**
The SLE is the expected monetary loss from a *single* occurrence of the threat.

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SLE &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
SLE &= \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE):**
The ALE is the expected loss per year.

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 \\
ALE &= \text{₹}7{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
The organization should ideally spend *less than* **₹7,50,000 per year** on a safeguard (firewall, IDS, training) to make the investment financially justified.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Security Services vs. OSI Layers

| OSI Layer | Security Service Provided | Common Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 7 — Application | Authentication, Non-repudiation | Digital certificates, Kerberos |
| 6 — Presentation | Data confidentiality (encryption) | SSL/TLS, AES, RSA |
| 5 — Session | Session management, key exchange | Diffie-Hellman |
| 4 — Transport | End-to-end security | TLS, SSH |
| 3 — Network | Routing authentication, IPsec | VPN, IPsec, AH/ESP |
| 2 — Data Link | MAC address filtering, WEP | 802.1X, WPA2 |
| 1 — Physical | Physical access control | Locks, biometric doors |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The CIA Triad — Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CIA Triad - Foundation of Computer Security] --> B[Confidentiality]
    A --> C[Integrity]
    A --> D[Availability]
    B --> B1[Data Privacy]
    B --> B2[Access Control Lists]
    B --> B3[Encryption AES RSA]
    C --> C1[Hashing SHA256]
    C --> C2[Digital Signatures]
    C --> C3[MAC Codes]
    D --> D1[Redundant Systems]
    D --> D2[Backups and DR]
    D --> D3[DDoS Mitigation]
    B1 --> E[Extended Security Goals]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E

    E --> G[Authorization]

```

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Security Attacks] --> P{Passive or Active}
    P -->|Passive| P1[Release of Message Contents]

    P -->|Active| A4[Fabrication]
    A1 --> R1[Availability Violated]
    A2 --> R2[Confidentiality Violated]
    A3 --> R3[Integrity Violated]
    A4 --> R4[Authenticity Violated]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    user1[User] --> firewall1[Perimeter Firewall]

    log1 --> siem1[SIEM Dashboard]
    dmz1 --> siem1
    db1 --> siem1
    siem1 --> admin1[Security Analyst]
```

### 4.4 Risk Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Asset Identification] --> S2[Step 2 Threat Assessment]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Vulnerability Analysis]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Risk Calculation R = T x V x I]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Mitigation Strategy]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Implementation]

```

```mermaid
    S1[Data at Rest] --> C1[Disk Encryption BitLocker LUKS]
    S2[Data in Transit] --> C2[TLS IPsec SSH]
    S3[Data in Processing] --> C3[Secure Enclave TPM Memory Protection]
    C1 --> P[Protected Data]
    C2 --> P
    C3 --> P
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define the CIA Triad. With a suitable example, explain how a violation of any one pillar impacts the others.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of computer security, comprising three core objectives:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that information is disclosed only to authorized parties. *Example:* Encrypting a customer's credit card number so that only the payment processor can read it.
2. **Integrity** — Ensures that data remains accurate and unaltered during storage or transmission. *Example:* Using a SHA-256 hash to verify that a software download was not modified in transit.
3. **Availability** — Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed. *Example:* A redundant server cluster that keeps a banking website online even during a DDoS attack.

**Interdependence:** A violation of one pillar often cascades. For example, a **DoS attack** (violates Availability) may force an organization to expose a backup server with weaker controls, leading to a **Confidentiality** breach. Likewise, an attacker exploiting a **software flaw** (Integrity breach) can install malware that later causes an **Availability** outage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a Threat, a Vulnerability, and an Attack. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any potential danger that could cause harm to a system. | A malware outbreak spreading across the internet. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness or gap in the system that can be exploited. | An unpatched buffer-overflow bug in a web server. |
| **Attack** | The actual exploitation of a vulnerability by a threat. | A hacker sending crafted packets to trigger the buffer overflow and gain shell access. |

**Key Relationship:** A *threat* exploits a *vulnerability* to launch an *attack*. A system can have many vulnerabilities, face many threats, but an attack only happens when a threat *successfully* exploits a vulnerability.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For Part B (14-mark) questions in PECST863, students typically lose marks in three ways:
> 1. **Failing to label diagrams** — Every block diagram must have labeled inputs/outputs.
> 2. **Skipping the formula explanation** — Writing the formula without defining each variable (T, V, I, AV, EF, ARO) results in 1–2 marks deducted.
> 3. **Confusing Active and Passive attacks** — Passive attacks *do not alter* the system; many students incorrectly list DoS as passive.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choice Option A*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of **security attacks** with suitable examples. Classify them under passive and active categories and identify the CIA Triad pillar each one violates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A database server contains customer records valued at **₹80,00,000**. The exposure factor for a ransomware attack is **75%**, and the attack is expected to occur **once every 2 years**. Calculate the **SLE**, **ARO**, and **ALE**. If a security control reduces the ARO to **once every 5 years** at an annual cost of **₹20,00,000**, compute the **Net Benefit of the Control (NBA)**. Justify whether the control is worth implementing. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Passive Attacks** — The attacker only *observes* the data without modifying it. They are difficult to detect because the system state is unchanged.

| Passive Attack | Description | CIA Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **Release of Message Contents** | Attacker reads confidential emails or files. | Confidentiality |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Attacker studies communication patterns, frequencies, and message lengths. | Confidentiality |

**Active Attacks** — The attacker *modifies* data or system state. They are detectable but can cause severe damage.

| Active Attack | Description | CIA Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **Masquerade** | Attacker pretends to be an authorized user. | Authenticity |
| **Replay Attack** | Attacker captures and re-sends a valid transmission. | Integrity |
| **Modification of Messages** | Attacker alters part of a legitimate message. | Integrity |
| **Denial of Service (DoS/DDoS)** | Attacker floods the system to make it unavailable. | Availability |

**Conclusion:** Passive attacks threaten **confidentiality**, while active attacks threaten **integrity and availability**. Defense against passive attacks relies primarily on **encryption**, while active attacks require **authentication, integrity checks, and intrusion detection**.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $AV = \text{₹}80{,}00{,}000$
- $EF = 75\% = 0.75$
- $ARO_{\text{before}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$
- $ARO_{\text{after}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$
- $ACS = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$SLE = AV \times EF = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy Before the Control:**

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE \times ARO_{\text{before}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy After the Control:**

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE \times ARO_{\text{after}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Net Benefit of the Control (NBA):**

$$NBA = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - ACS$$

$$NBA = 30{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
Since $NBA < 0$, the control is **NOT financially justified** at its current cost. The organization would lose ₹2,00,000 per year by implementing it. Either the ACS should be reduced to below ₹18,00,000, or a cheaper control should be selected.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Choice Option B*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **security mechanisms** and **security services** in computer security. Provide a clear distinction between the two and give at least three examples of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the term **Risk** in computer security. Derive the relationship $R = T \times V \times I$ and explain how each factor influences the overall security posture of an organization. Also, briefly describe the **Defense-in-Depth** strategy. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Security Services** — These are the *goals* or *objectives* a system aims to provide. They are the *what*.

| Service | Description |
|---|---|
| **Data Confidentiality** | Protecting data from unauthorized disclosure. |
| **Data Integrity** | Ensuring data has not been altered. |
| **Authentication** | Verifying the identity of a user or system. |
| **Non-repudiation** | Preventing denial of an action. |
| **Access Control** | Restricting access based on identity and policy. |

**Security Mechanisms** — These are the *tools, techniques, or methods* used to implement security services. They are the *how*.

| Mechanism | Description |
|---|---|
| **Encryption (AES, RSA)** | Transforms plaintext into ciphertext. |
| **Digital Signature** | Provides integrity and non-repudiation. |
| **Access Control Matrix** | Defines subject-object permissions. |
| **Padding/Steganography** | Hides existence of data. |
| **Routing Control** | Selects secure physical routes. |

**Key Distinction:** A *service* is an end-goal; a *mechanism* is the means to achieve it. For example, *Confidentiality* is a service, but *AES encryption* is a mechanism used to provide it. A single mechanism may serve multiple services, and a single service may require multiple mechanisms.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition of Risk:**
Risk is the potential for loss, damage, or destruction of an asset as a result of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. It is a function of what could go wrong, how likely it is, and how severe the impact would be.

**Derivation of $R = T \times V \times I$:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Factors:**
- $T$ = Threat — the probability that a dangerous event will occur.
- $V$ = Vulnerability — the probability that the threat can successfully exploit a weakness.
- $I$ = Impact — the magnitude of harm if the event occurs (often expressed in monetary terms).

**Step 2 — Form the Joint Probability Model:**
If we treat threat occurrence and successful exploitation as independent events, the joint probability of an incident is:

$$P(\text{incident}) = T \times V$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Impact:**

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation of Each Factor:**

| Factor | Effect on Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **T (Threat)** | Increasing T increases R directly. | Reduce the source of threats (firewalls, threat intelligence). |
| **V (Vulnerability)** | Higher V means easier exploitation. | Patch systems, security audits, code reviews. |
| **I (Impact)** | Higher I means greater damage per incident. | Backups, redundancy, incident response plans. |

If any factor becomes **zero**, the overall risk becomes **zero**. This is the theoretical basis for layered security.

**Defense-in-Depth Strategy:**
Defense-in-Depth is a security strategy that employs **multiple layers of controls** so that if one layer fails, another layer is in place to thwart the attack. It mirrors the risk equation — by reducing $T$, $V$, and $I$ simultaneously, the overall risk drops dramatically.

Typical layers include:
1. **Physical Security** — Locks, guards, CCTV.
2. **Network Security** — Firewalls, IDS/IPS, VPNs.
3. **Host Security** — Antivirus, OS hardening.
4. **Application Security** — Secure coding, input validation.
5. **Data Security** — Encryption, access control lists.
6. **Administrative Controls** — Policies, training, audits.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PECST863 — Module 1**

- [ ] **CIA Triad** is the foundation: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability**.
- [ ] Information exists in **three states**: at rest, in transit, in processing.
- [ ] **Risk** = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact. Zero in any factor = zero risk.
- [ ] **SLE** = Asset Value × Exposure Factor. **ALE** = SLE × ARO.
- [ ] **NBA** = ALE_before − ALE_after − Annual Cost of Safeguard.
- [ ] **Passive attacks** (eavesdropping, traffic analysis) threaten **confidentiality**.
- [ ] **Active attacks** (masquerade, replay, DoS, modification, fabrication) threaten **integrity and availability**.
- [ ] **Security service** = the *goal*; **Security mechanism** = the *means*.
- [ ] **Availability** = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR). Five nines = 99.999% uptime.
- [ ] **Shannon Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ measured in **bits**.
- [ ] English text entropy ≈ 1.0–1.5 bits/letter; maximum (uniform) = $\log_2 26 \approx 4.7$ bits.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth** = multiple overlapping layers of security controls.
- [ ] Extended security goals: **Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation, Accountability, Privacy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat**: In information security, a **threat** is any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system, organization, or individual. A threat is the *possibility* of an attack — it represents the **danger** that exploits a **vulnerability** to compromise the **confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA Triad)** of an information asset.

Mathematically, a threat is often modeled as a function of three elements:

$$ T = f(\text{Threat Agent}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Asset}) $$

Where:
- **Threat Agent**: The entity that initiates the action (e.g., hacker, malware, natural disaster).
- **Vulnerability**: The weakness that the threat exploits.
- **Asset**: The resource of value that is being targeted.

A threat becomes a realized **attack** only when a threat agent actively exploits a vulnerability against an asset.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Open Window Problem"**
> Imagine your house is a computer network. A **vulnerability** is an *unlocked window*. A **threat** is a *burglar roaming the neighborhood* — they might never try your window, but the *possibility* is real. The moment the burglar *actually enters* through that window, the threat becomes an **attack**, and the resulting **loss of jewelry** is the **impact**.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Threat** = Burglar (the potential danger)
> - **Vulnerability** = Unlocked window (the weakness)
> - **Asset** = Jewelry (what is valuable)
> - **Risk** = Likelihood the burglar finds and uses the unlocked window

This makes it immediately clear why we study threats *before* attacks — **you cannot defend against what you have not identified as possible.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard Metrics in Bold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Threat Agent / Actor** — The source that can produce a threat (human, software, natural, environmental).
> - **Threat Vector** — The *pathway* or *method* used by a threat agent to reach a target (e.g., email, USB, network).
> - **Threat Surface / Attack Surface** — The sum of all possible entry points and exposures.
> - **Threat Modeling** — The systematic process of identifying, classifying, and prioritizing threats.
> - **Threat Intelligence** — Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats, often expressed as **STIX** (Structured Threat Information eXpression) or **TAXII** (Trusted Automated Exchange of Intelligence Information).
> - **Zero-Day Threat** — A previously unknown threat for which no patch exists; considered the most dangerous class.

### 1.4 The Relationship: Threat → Vulnerability → Risk → Impact

> [!NOTE]
> **The Security Causality Chain**
> 1. A **Threat** exists (potential danger).
> 2. It pairs with a **Vulnerability** (weakness).
> 3. Together they generate **Risk** (probability of harm).
> 4. If realized, the result is **Impact** (actual damage).
>
> *Removing any one of the first two links breaks the chain.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Threats (Taxonomy)

Threats can be classified along several axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following structured taxonomy:

#### 2.1.1 By Origin / Source
- **Natural / Environmental Threats** — Earthquakes, floods, lightning, fire, power failure.
- **Unintentional Human Threats** — Accidental deletion, misconfiguration, lost devices.
- **Intentional Human Threats (Insider)** — Disgruntled employees, malicious administrators.
- **Intentional Human Threats (Outsider)** — Hackers, cybercriminals, nation-state actors, hacktivists.

#### 2.1.2 By Action Category (Classic Four — Saltzer & Schroeder / Gollmann)

| Action Type | Description | CIA Triad Element Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **Interception** | An unauthorized party gains access to a communication or data stream (eavesdropping). | Confidentiality |
| **Interruption** | A system asset becomes unavailable, lost, or unusable (DoS, hardware failure). | Availability |
| **Modification** | Unauthorized tampering with data or system state (man-in-the-middle, defacement). | Integrity |
| **Fabrication** | Generation of counterfeit objects, identities, or data (spoofing, replay attacks). | Authenticity |

#### 2.1.3 By Capability & Structure (Most cited in KTU Boards)
- **Structured Threats** — Skilled attackers using sophisticated tools, scripts, and exploits (e.g., APT groups).
- **Unstructured Threats** — Novice attackers using pre-existing tools (script kiddies).

### 2.2 The STRIDE Threat Model (Microsoft Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STRIDE** is a mnemonic threat classification framework developed by Microsoft, widely tested in KTU examinations.

| Letter | Threat Type | Violated Property |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authenticity |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization |

### 2.3 Threat Modeling Process (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The 5-step threat modeling process below is a frequent 7-mark question. Memorize the order.

1. **Identify Assets** — Determine what needs protection (data, systems, reputation).
2. **Create Architecture Overview** — Map data flows, trust boundaries, and entry points (DFD — Data Flow Diagram).
3. **Identify Threats** — Apply STRIDE per element; list potential threat agents.
4. **Document Threats** — Use a threat-list document with *Threat ID, Category, Source, Asset Affected*.
5. **Rate Threats** — Assign a likelihood and impact score to prioritize mitigation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and metrics are **directly testable** under KTU 2024 scheme for Module 1.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Magnitude** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Qualitative scale (Low / Medium / High) |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Monetary units (₹ / $) |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | Estimated number of incidents per year | Dimensionless (events/year) |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Monetary units per year |
| **Total ALE after Control** | $ALE_{final} = ALE_{before} - ALE_{saved} - Cost_{control}$ | Used in cost-benefit analysis |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{saved} - Cost_{control}}{Cost_{control}}$ | Expressed as a ratio or percentage |
| **Threat Likelihood Scale** | $L \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ | 1 = Rare, 5 = Almost Certain |
| **Impact Severity Scale** | $I \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ | 1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic |
| **Risk Score** | $R = L \times I$ | Range: 1 to 25 (DREAD/Qualitative) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** When using LaTeX inside the table above, the vertical bar in the conditional set notation (e.g., $L \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$) is written using the inline form to avoid breaking the markdown table. Never use a raw `|` inside a table row.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Threat Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> Threat analysis is the **foundational step** of:
> - **Enterprise Risk Management (ERM)** — informs board-level decisions.
> - **Secure Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** — threat modeling at design phase reduces post-deployment patches by up to **60%**.
> - **Regulatory Compliance** — ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-30, PCI-DSS, and DPDP Act 2023 (India) all mandate documented threat assessments.
> - **Cyber Insurance Premium Calculation** — Insurers use ALE/ROSI metrics to price policies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantifying Threat Impact (ALE Framework)

This is the **canonical numerical exercise** in KTU Module 1 examinations. The full derivation is shown below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Scenario (Worked Example):**
> A server in a campus data center is valued at **₹ 10,00,000** (Ten Lakhs). A threat (SQL Injection) is identified. If exploited, an estimated **40%** of the server's value is lost. Statistically, the attack is expected to occur **0.5 times per year**. Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

#### Step 1: Identify the Asset Value (AV)

The asset value is the replacement or business cost of the server.

$$ AV = \text{₹ 10,00,000} $$

#### Step 2: Determine the Exposure Factor (EF)

The Exposure Factor is the *percentage* of asset value lost *in a single successful occurrence* of the threat.

$$ EF = 0.40 \quad \text{(i.e., 40\%)} $$

#### Step 3: Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

The SLE represents the monetary loss expected from **one** occurrence of the threat.

$$ SLE = AV \times EF $$

Substituting the values:

$$ SLE = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 $$

$$ SLE = \text{₹ 4,00{,}000} $$

#### Step 4: Determine the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)

The ARO is the estimated frequency of the threat occurring within a one-year window.

$$ ARO = 0.5 \quad \text{occurrences/year} $$

#### Step 5: Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

The ALE is the expected monetary loss per year, calculated as:

$$ ALE = SLE \times ARO $$

Substituting:

$$ ALE = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 $$

$$ ALE = \text{₹ 2{,}00{,}000 per year} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The organization should **not** spend more than **₹ 2,00,000 per year** on controls to mitigate this single threat. Any control exceeding this cost is economically unjustified based on this threat alone.

#### Step 6: Cost-Benefit Analysis (Extension)

Suppose a Web Application Firewall (WAF) is proposed at an annual cost of **₹ 1,20,000** and is expected to reduce the SLE to **₹ 1,00,000** (i.e., $EF_{new} = 0.10$).

New SLE:

$$ SLE_{new} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = \text{₹ 1,00{,}000} $$

New ALE:

$$ ALE_{new} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹ 50{,}000} $$

ALE Saved:

$$ ALE_{saved} = ALE_{old} - ALE_{new} = 2{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹ 1{,}50{,}000} $$

Net Benefit:

$$ NB = ALE_{saved} - Cost_{control} = 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ 30{,}000} $$

ROSI:

$$ ROSI = \frac{ALE_{saved} - Cost_{control}}{Cost_{control}} = \frac{30{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = 0.25 $$

$$ ROSI = 25\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> A **positive ROSI** indicates the control is financially justified. A **negative ROSI** means the control costs more than the threat's expected damage.

### 3.2 Derivation: Risk Score Using DREAD-like Scale

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A phishing threat targeting employee emails has the following scores on a 1–5 scale: Likelihood (L) = 4, Impact (I) = 3. Compute the qualitative risk rating.

$$ R = L \times I = 4 \times 3 = 12 $$

**Risk Score Interpretation Matrix:**

| Score Range | Risk Level | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** | Accept; monitor periodically |
| 5 – 9 | **Medium** | Plan mitigation within budget cycle |
| 10 – 15 | **High** | Implement controls immediately |
| 16 – 25 | **Critical** | Emergency response; halt affected operations |

Here, $R = 12 \Rightarrow$ **High Risk** — immediate user awareness training and email filtering are warranted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a threat. Differentiate between a threat and a vulnerability with a suitable example.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Threat** is any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system or organization. A **vulnerability** is a weakness or flaw in a system that can be exploited by a threat.
>
> *Example:* A phishing email is a **threat**, while a user's lack of awareness in identifying suspicious links is a **vulnerability**.
>
> Key difference: a threat is the *external force*; a vulnerability is the *internal weakness*. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any six categories of threats as per the STRIDE model.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks — concise 6-point list):**
>
> 1. **S – Spoofing:** Impersonation of identity (e.g., IP spoofing).
> 2. **T – Tampering:** Unauthorized modification of data (e.g., altering logs).
> 3. **R – Repudiation:** Denying an action without proof (e.g., denying a transaction).
> 4. **I – Information Disclosure:** Unauthorized data exposure (e.g., data leak).
> 5. **D – Denial of Service:** Disrupting service availability (e.g., DDoS).
> 6. **E – Elevation of Privilege:** Gaining higher access rights (e.g., privilege escalation exploit).
>
> (½ mark each = 3 marks)

---

### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) Classify threats based on their source of origin. Explain each category with a real-world example. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> Threats based on origin are classified into four broad categories:
>
> 1. **Natural / Environmental Threats (2 marks)**
>    These arise from natural events beyond human control — earthquakes, floods, cyclones, lightning, fire, and power outages.
>    *Example:* The 2018 Kerala floods disrupted data center operations across Kochi, causing multi-day service outages for cloud-hosted university portals.
>
> 2. **Unintentional Human Threats (1.5 marks)**
>    These are accidental actions by insiders or users — accidental data deletion, misconfigured firewall rules, or lost USB drives.
>    *Example:* An admin accidentally exposing an S3 bucket containing student records to the public internet.
>
> 3. **Intentional Insider Threats (1.5 marks)**
>    Deliberate malicious actions by employees, contractors, or privileged users — data theft, sabotage, or fraud.
>    *Example:* A disgruntled DBA exfiltrating customer credit card data before resigning.
>
> 4. **Intentional Outsider Threats (2 marks)**
>    External attackers — hackers, cybercriminals, hacktivists, and nation-state APT groups — using phishing, malware, or DDoS.
>    *Example:* The WannaCry ransomware attack (2017) by the Lazarus Group, affecting 230,000+ systems globally.

**(b) An organization hosts a web application valued at ₹ 20,00,000. A threat (XSS attack) is expected to cause 30% loss per occurrence and is estimated to occur twice a year. The organization proposes to deploy a WAF at an annual cost of ₹ 1,80,000, which is expected to reduce the exposure factor to 10%. Calculate SLE, ALE before control, ALE after control, and ROSI. Comment on whether the control is justified. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given Data:**
> - $AV = \text{₹ 20,00,000}$
> - $EF_{old} = 0.30$
> - $ARO = 2$
> - $Cost_{control} = \text{₹ 1,80,000}$
> - $EF_{new} = 0.10$
>
> **Step 1: Compute SLE (before control)** (2 marks)
> $$ SLE_{old} = AV \times EF_{old} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = \text{₹ 6,00{,}000} $$
>
> **Step 2: Compute ALE (before control)** (1 mark)
> $$ ALE_{old} = SLE_{old} \times ARO = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = \text{₹ 12,00{,}000} $$
>
> **Step 3: Compute ALE (after control)** (2 marks)
> $$ SLE_{new} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = \text{₹ 2,00{,}000} $$
> $$ ALE_{new} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = \text{₹ 4,00{,}000} $$
>
> **Step 4: Compute ALE Saved and ROSI** (1.5 marks)
> $$ ALE_{saved} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ 8,00{,}000} $$
> $$ ROSI = \frac{ALE_{saved} - Cost_{control}}{Cost_{control}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = \frac{6{,}20{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} \approx 3.44 $$
> $$ ROSI = 344\% $$
>
> **Step 5: Conclusion** (0.5 marks)
> Since **ROSI is strongly positive (344%)**, the WAF deployment is **economically justified** and should be implemented immediately.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a) Explain the STRIDE threat model with a neat diagram. List the security property violated by each category. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> STRIDE is a threat classification framework developed by **Microsoft** for systematic threat modeling in software design. Each letter represents a category of threat, and each category violates a specific security property.
>
> | STRIDE Element | Security Property Violated | Description | Example |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **S – Spoofing** | **Authenticity** | Pretending to be another identity | Email spoofing, IP spoofing |
> | **T – Tampering** | **Integrity** | Modifying data in transit or at rest | Modifying URL parameters |
> | **R – Repudiation** | **Non-Repudiation** | Denying an action without trace | User denies making a transaction |
> | **I – Information Disclosure** | **Confidentiality** | Exposing data to unauthorized parties | Directory traversal, data leak |
> | **D – Denial of Service** | **Availability** | Making system unavailable to users | SYN flood, volumetric DDoS |
> | **E – Elevation of Privilege** | **Authorization** | Gaining rights one should not have | Buffer overflow → root shell |
>
> **Diagram (4 marks — equivalent description):**
> A central node "STRIDE" branches to six leaves S, T, R, I, D, E, each annotated with the violated property. (See Mermaid block in Section 4.3 of these notes for the official schematic.)
>
> STRIDE is applied per **DFD (Data Flow Diagram) element**: external entity, process, data store, and data flow — making it exhaustive and systematic.

**(b) Describe the step-by-step process of threat modeling. For a small university portal (login, attendance upload, result download), identify at least four plausible threats using STRIDE. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Threat Modeling Process (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Identify Assets** — student credentials, attendance records, result PDFs, server availability.
> 2. **Construct Architecture Overview** — Build a DFD showing browser → web server → database, with trust boundary between user zone and server zone.
> 3. **Identify Threats** — Apply STRIDE per DFD element.
> 4. **Document Threats** — Use a structured table: *Threat ID, Category, Description, Asset, Likelihood*.
> 5. **Rate & Prioritize** — Assign L × I scores; sort by risk.
>
> **Identified Threats for University Portal (3 marks — 4 threats):**
>
> | # | Threat Description | STRIDE Category | Asset | Likelihood |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Attacker brute-forces student login credentials | **S – Spoofing** | Login credentials | High |
> | 2 | Instructor modifies attendance after submission deadline | **T – Tampering** | Attendance records | Medium |
> | 3 | SQL Injection on result download page leaks data | **I – Information Disclosure** | Result data | High |
> | 4 | Botnet floods portal with requests during exam result day | **D – Denial of Service** | Portal availability | High |
>
> Each threat is then mitigated with controls: rate-limiting, audit logs, parameterized queries, and CDN-based DDoS protection respectively.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Threat" with "Attack":** A threat is *potential*; an attack is *realized*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Skipping Units in ALE calculation:** Always write **"₹ per year"** or "monetary units" — never just a bare number.
> 3. **Forgetting EF notation:** The Exposure Factor is a *fraction* (0.30), not a percentage (30) in the formula. Using 30 instead of 0.30 is the #1 numerical error.
> 4. **STRIDE property mismatch:** Writing "Spoofing violates Integrity" is a common slip — it violates **Authenticity**.
> 5. **Not justifying ROSI:** A bare ROSI number without a concluding sentence ("Control is justified / not justified") loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Diagram with reserved Mermaid keywords:** Writing `node end` or `node graph` breaks the renderer and earns 0 for the diagram.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Threat** = potential danger; **Vulnerability** = weakness; **Attack** = realized threat; **Risk** = probability × impact.
> - The **four classic threat actions** are **Interception, Interruption, Modification, Fabrication** — remember them as **I²MF**.
> - **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation — each maps to a unique security property.
> - The **ALE formula** $ALE = SLE \times ARO = (AV \times EF) \times ARO$ is the single most repeated numerical question.
> - **ROSI > 0** means the security control is *financially* justified; **ROSI < 0** means it is not.
> - **Threat modeling** is a 5-step systematic process — memorize: *Assets → Architecture → Identify → Document → Rate*.
> - **Insider threats** are statistically more damaging than outsider threats (per Verizon DBIR reports).
> - **Zero-day threats** have no known patch — they are the highest risk class.
> - **CIA Triad** is violated differently: Interception → C, Interruption → A, Modification → I, Fabrication → Authenticity.
> - Always write units in monetary calculations (₹, $, etc.); examiners explicitly look for them.
> - **DREAD**, **PASTA**, **VAST**, and **STRIDE** are the four major threat modeling methodologies — know STRIDE the best.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Harm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the discipline of **Information Security** and **Cybersecurity Engineering**, the term **Harm** is formally defined as the **negative consequence, damage, injury, or loss** suffered by an asset, individual, organization, or system when a threat is successfully realized (i.e., when a vulnerability is exploited). Harm represents the *impact dimension* of the classical risk equation and is the measure of how bad an outcome actually is, independent of its likelihood.

Mathematically, the foundational security risk identity is expressed as:

$$ \text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Harm} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall-Ready):** "Harm is the adverse effect on an information system, its data, the people it serves, or the organization that depends upon it, resulting from the successful exploitation of a vulnerability by a threat agent." — Memorize this exact phrasing for 3-mark direct questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **house (the asset)**. A **storm (the threat)** approaches. The **roof is old and cracked (the vulnerability)**. Now, the **Harm** is the *actual water damage to your furniture, the soaked electrical wiring, the ruined photographs, and the trauma to your family*. 

- The **threat** is just a possibility.
- The **vulnerability** is just a weakness.
- The **Harm** is the *real, tangible, often painful consequence* that hits you in the wallet, the body, or the mind.

In cybersecurity, harm is what the user, customer, patient, or citizen actually *feels* or *loses* — it is never just "a server crashed." It is always: lost money, leaked medical records, defaced reputation, or worse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to distinguish clearly between *Hazard* (source of danger), *Threat* (potential cause), *Vulnerability* (weakness), and *Harm* (the realized damage). Confusing these four terms is the single most common cause of lost marks in Module 1.

### 1.3 Why "Harm" Matters in Modern Security Engineering

Traditional security focused almost exclusively on *confidentiality, integrity, and availability* (the CIA triad). The modern KTU-aligned **harm-centric approach** — promoted by frameworks such as the **OWASP Top 10**, **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1**, and the **LINDDUN privacy harm model** — shifts the focus to *consequences*. 

A 2024-era security engineer does not ask *"Is there a vulnerability?"* — they ask *"If exploited, what harm befalls the patient whose MRI is encrypted? What harm befalls the student whose exam record is altered?"* This reorientation is the philosophical core of Module 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE):** The single most important harm-quantification metric in KTU Module 1 is the **ALE**, measured in currency per year (e.g., ₹/year, $/year). It is derived in Section 2 below.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Harm in Security

Harm in information security is universally decomposed into three orthogonal dimensions, originally formalized by the **Orange Book (TCSEC)** and later refined by **NIST** and **ISO/IEC 27005**:

1. **Loss of Confidentiality** — unauthorized disclosure of sensitive information.
2. **Loss of Integrity** — unauthorized modification or destruction of data.
3. **Loss of Availability** — disruption of access to systems or data.

Beyond CIA, modern harm taxonomies (post-2018, KTU 2024 era) extend to:
- **Privacy Harm** (loss of autonomy, dignity, discrimination).
- **Physical Harm** (e.g., a hacked pacemaker, an industrial control system causing a turbine explosion).
- **Financial Harm** (direct monetary loss, fraud, regulatory fines).
- **Reputational Harm** (loss of trust, brand damage).
- **Psychological Harm** (stress, anxiety, fear caused by doxxing or stalking).
- **Societal Harm** (erosion of democratic processes, public-safety degradation).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Harm Realization

A complete harm event in a security context unfolds in the following strictly sequential steps. Memorize this chain — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

- **Step 1 — Asset Identification:** The organization identifies what it values (data, hardware, people, reputation, processes).
- **Step 2 — Threat Identification:** Potential adversaries and natural events are enumerated.
- **Step 3 — Vulnerability Identification:** Weaknesses that could be exploited are mapped.
- **Step 3a — Exposure Factor (EF) Estimation:** The percentage of asset value lost in a single successful event is estimated.
- **Step 4 — Attack Vector Realization:** A threat agent successfully exploits a vulnerability.
- **Step 5 — Harm Materialization:** The damage is inflicted on the asset.
- **Step 5a — Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) Computation:** The monetary value of that one event is calculated.
- **Step 6 — Likelihood Assessment:** The Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO) is estimated.
- **Step 7 — Risk Computation:** Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) is derived, which is the *expected harm per year*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Description |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Exposure Factor | $EF$ | $EF \in [0, 1]$ | dimensionless (or %) | Fraction of asset value destroyed in one event |
| 2 | Asset Value | $AV$ | input | ₹ / $ / € | Replacement or business cost of the asset |
| 3 | Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE$ | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | ₹ / $ / € | Monetary loss from **one** occurrence |
| 4 | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO$ | input | events/year | Expected frequency of the threat per year |
| 5 | Annualized Loss Expectancy | $ALE$ | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | ₹ / year | Expected monetary harm per year |
| 6 | Total ALE (multi-risk) | $ALE_{total}$ | $\sum_i ALE_i$ | ₹ / year | Sum across all identified risks |
| 7 | Risk | $R$ | $R = T \times V \times H$ | composite | Threat × Vulnerability × Harm |
| 8 | Residual Risk | $R_{res}$ | $R_{res} = R_{total} - R_{mitigated}$ | composite | Harm remaining after controls |
| 9 | Return on Security Investment | $ROSI$ | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost_{control}}{Cost_{control}}$ | ratio | Justification metric for security spend |
| 10 | Safeguard Value | $SV$ | $SV = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}$ | ₹ / year | Annual harm averted by one control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Notation Rule:** When writing `|x|` (absolute value) in an exam script, always use the parenthesis form `(x)` with a clear sign convention. In this PDF, LaTeX vertical bars in tables are written as `\vert` to prevent markdown-rendering failures. Do **not** write `|SLE|` inside a table row in your answer sheet.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The harm-centric approach is the foundation of:

- **Healthcare Security:** A breached hospital is not measured in "records lost" but in "patients harmed due to delayed surgery." HIPAA, HITECH, and the EU MDR (Medical Device Regulation) all encode harm-based thinking.
- **Critical Infrastructure:** A SCADA breach is evaluated for *physical-world harm* (explosion, blackout, contamination) — see the 2010 Stuxnet and 2015 Ukraine power grid attacks.
- **Cloud & SaaS:** SLAs (Service Level Agreements) quantify harm in downtime credits.
- **Insurance & Cyber-Liability:** Premiums are computed directly from ALE estimates.
- **Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434):** Vehicle cybersecurity is graded by *potential harm to road users*, not by code vulnerabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a 14-mark question mentions "You are the CISO of a hospital. A ransomware attack encrypts 10,000 patient records. Compute the harm," they are *always* testing whether you can break the loss into *EF × AV* components (e.g., notification cost, regulatory fine, lost revenue, reputational decay) and then aggregate to ALE. See Section 5 for a worked exam-style problem.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

The SLE formula is *not* axiomatic; it is derived from the definition of *exposure factor*. By definition, the **Exposure Factor (EF)** is the fraction of an asset's total value that is destroyed in a single adverse event.

$$ EF \;=\; \frac{\text{Value Lost in One Event}}{\text{Total Asset Value}} $$

Rearranging algebraically:

$$ \text{Value Lost in One Event} \;=\; EF \times \text{Total Asset Value} $$

The left-hand side is, by engineering convention, defined as the **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**. Substituting:

$$ \begin{aligned} SLE &\;\equiv\; \text{Value Lost in One Event} \\ SLE &\;=\; EF \times \text{Total Asset Value} \\ SLE &\;=\; EF \times AV \end{aligned} $$

> This is the fundamental KTU 2024 Module 1 result. The SLE has units of currency (₹, $, €).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

By engineering convention, the **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** is the expected number of times a given threat successfully realizes per year. For a single risk $i$, the expected number of events in one year is exactly $ARO_i$.

The expected monetary loss per year is therefore the *value of one event* multiplied by the *expected number of events per year*:

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expected Loss per Year} &\;=\; (\text{Loss per Event}) \times (\text{Events per Year}) \\ ALE_i &\;=\; SLE_i \times ARO_i \end{aligned} $$

This identity is the cornerstone of *quantitative risk analysis* as taught in KTU Module 1 and is required verbatim in any 14-mark numerical problem.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

A CISO must justify every rupee spent on a control. Define:

- $ALE_{before}$ — expected annual harm *without* the proposed control.
- $ALE_{after}$ — expected annual harm *with* the control deployed.
- $C$ — annual cost of the control (license, staff, training, hardware).

The **net annual benefit** of the control is the harm it averts, minus what it costs:

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Benefit} &\;=\; (ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}) - C \\ \text{Net Benefit} &\;=\; SV - C \end{aligned} $$

where $SV$ is the *Safeguard Value* — the harm averted. The **Return on Security Investment** is the ratio of net benefit to cost:

$$ ROSI \;=\; \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - C}{C} $$

A $ROSI > 0$ indicates a *cost-justified* control. A $ROSI < 0$ indicates that the control costs more in operational expense than the harm it prevents — a *board-level rejection criterion* in mature security programs.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Board-Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A university examination server hosts records valued at ₹ 50,00,000. A ransomware attack is estimated to destroy **60%** of the asset's value per successful event. Historical data shows such an attack occurs on average **0.5 times per year**. The IT team proposes a backup-and-patching control costing ₹ 4,00,000 per year, expected to reduce the EF to **20%** and the ARO to **0.2**. Compute the SLE, ALE before, ALE after, and ROSI.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the SLE before the control.**

$$ \begin{aligned} AV &= \;₹\,50{,}00{,}000 \\ EF_{before} &= 0.60 \\ SLE_{before} &= AV \times EF_{before} \\ SLE_{before} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\ SLE_{before} &= \;₹\,30{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute the ALE before the control.**

$$ \begin{aligned} ARO_{before} &= 0.5 \text{ events/year} \\ ALE_{before} &= SLE_{before} \times ARO_{before} \\ ALE_{before} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \\ ALE_{before} &= \;₹\,15{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \end{aligned} $$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute the SLE and ALE after the control.**

$$ \begin{aligned} EF_{after} &= 0.20 \\ SLE_{after} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 \\ SLE_{after} &= \;₹\,10{,}00{,}000 \\ ARO_{after} &= 0.2 \text{ events/year} \\ ALE_{after} &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 \\ ALE_{after} &= \;₹\,2{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \end{aligned} $$

**Solution — Step 4: Compute the Safeguard Value (SV) and the ROSI.**

$$ \begin{aligned} SV &= ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} \\ SV &= 15{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 \\ SV &= \;₹\,13{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \\ C &= \;₹\,4{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \\ ROSI &= \frac{SV - C}{C} \\ ROSI &= \frac{13{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \\ ROSI &= \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \\ ROSI &= 2.25 \end{aligned} $$

**Interpretation (board-quality closing sentence):** A ROSI of 2.25 means that for every ₹ 1 spent on the control, the university avoids ₹ 2.25 in expected annual harm. The control is **strongly justified** and should be approved by the board.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Harm Categories vs. Asset Classes

| Harm Category | Data | Hardware | People | Reputation | Process | KTU-Weighted Importance |
|---------------|:----:|:--------:|:------:|:----------:|:-------:|:----------------------:|
| Confidentiality Loss | High | Low | High | High | Medium | **High** |
| Integrity Loss | High | Medium | High | High | High | **High** |
| Availability Loss | High | High | High | Medium | High | **High** |
| Financial Loss | Medium | High | Low | High | High | Medium |
| Physical Harm | Low | High | Critical | Low | Medium | **Critical** |
| Privacy Harm | High | Low | High | High | Low | **High** |
| Reputational Harm | Medium | Low | High | Critical | High | **High** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Harm Realization Pipeline (Threat → Vulnerability → Harm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Asset Identification] --> B[Threat Identification]
    B --> C[Vulnerability Mapping]
    C --> D{Threat Exploits Vulnerability?}
    D -- No --> E[No Harm Realized]
    D -- Yes --> F[Harm Materialization]
    F --> G[Quantify SLE = AV x EF]
    G --> H[Compute ALE = SLE x ARO]
    H --> I[Aggregate ALE_total]
    I --> J[Apply Control]
    J --> K[Compute New ALE_after]
    K --> L[Compute ROSI]
    L --> M{ROSI greater than 0?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Deploy Control]
    M -- No --> O[Reject or Re-Scope]
```

### 4.2 Harm Taxonomy (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HARM[Harm]
    HARM --> D[Direct Harm]
    HARM --> I[Indirect Harm]
    D --> D1[Data Loss or Corruption]
    D --> D2[Financial Theft]
    D --> D3[Service Outage]
    D --> D4[Physical Injury]
    I --> I1[Reputational Damage]
    I --> I2[Regulatory Fines]
    I --> I3[Loss of Customer Trust]
    I --> I4[Psychological Trauma]
    I --> I5[Societal Impact]
```

### 4.3 Quantitative Risk Analysis Engine (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AVblock[Asset Value AV] --> mul1((x))
    EFblock[Exposure Factor EF] --> mul1
    mul1 --> SLEout[SLE]
    SLEout --> mul2((x))
    AROblock[Annualized Rate ARO] --> mul2
    mul2 --> ALEout[ALE]
    ALEout --> sum[Sum over all risks]
    sum --> ALEtotal[Total ALE]
    ALEtotal --> ctrl[Apply Control Cost C]
    ctrl --> ROSIcalc[ROSI Computation]
    ROSIcalc --> decision{ROSI greater than zero}
    decision -- yes --> deploy[Deploy]
    decision -- no --> reject[Reject]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow for Security Control Investment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([New Threat Identified]) --> Q1[Estimate AV]
    Q1 --> Q2[Estimate EF]
    Q2 --> Q3[Estimate ARO]
    Q3 --> Q4[Compute ALE before]
    Q4 --> Q5[Propose Control]
    Q5 --> Q6[Estimate Post Control EF and ARO]
    Q6 --> Q7[Compute ALE after]
    Q7 --> Q8[Compute Control Cost C]
    Q8 --> Q9[Compute ROSI]
    Q9 --> Q10{ROSI greater than 0}
    Q10 -- yes --> A1[Approve and Deploy Control]
    Q10 -- no --> A2[Seek Cheaper Alternative or Accept Risk]
    A2 --> End([Document Residual Risk])
    A1 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When the examiner draws a block diagram and asks "Identify the missing block," they are almost always looking for **EF, ARO, SLE, or ALE** in that order. Memorize the *order* of operations, not just the formulas.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** Define the term **Harm** in the context of information security. How does it differ from a *threat* and a *vulnerability*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Harm is the negative consequence, damage, or loss suffered by an asset, system, person, or organization when a threat successfully exploits a vulnerability. It represents the *impact* dimension of risk. A *threat* is a potential cause of an adverse event (e.g., a malware author); a *vulnerability* is a weakness that can be exploited (e.g., an unpatched buffer); *harm* is the realized damage (e.g., leaked patient records, financial loss, reputational decay). **(3 marks — full credit requires all three distinctions.)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** What is the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**? State its formula and units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) is the expected monetary loss per year due to a specific realized threat. It is computed as the product of the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) and the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO).

$$ ALE \;=\; SLE \times ARO $$

where $SLE = AV \times EF$. The unit of ALE is **currency per year** (e.g., ₹/year). **(3 marks — formula + unit + meaning all required.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model Question, Module 1 Choice-1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain, with a suitable diagram, the **risk equation** $R = T \times V \times H$. Discuss how the *harm* term $H$ is quantified in monetary terms using the Exposure Factor (EF), Asset Value (AV), and Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO). Derive the expression for the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fintech startup stores customer transaction data valued at ₹ 2,00,00,000. A SQL-injection attack is estimated to compromise **80%** of the data per event, occurring on average **1.2 times per year**. Compute the SLE and ALE. The startup installs a WAF (Web Application Firewall) at an annual cost of ₹ 6,00,000, reducing the EF to **15%** and the ARO to **0.3**. Compute the new SLE, new ALE, and the ROSI. State whether the control should be deployed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

The classical risk identity decomposes risk into three multiplicative components:

$$ R \;=\; T \times V \times H $$

where $T$ is the *threat* (likelihood that a potential cause will act), $V$ is the *vulnerability* (probability that a weakness can be exploited), and $H$ is the *harm* (the magnitude of damage if exploitation succeeds). The product yields a composite risk score; if any one factor is zero (e.g., no vulnerability), the risk collapses to zero. **(2 marks for the equation and the role of each term.)**

The harm term $H$ is quantified in monetary terms by chaining three sub-quantities. First, the **Asset Value (AV)** is the replacement or business value of the asset in currency. Second, the **Exposure Factor (EF)** is the dimensionless fraction of the asset's value destroyed in one event. Their product is the **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**:

$$ SLE \;=\; AV \times EF $$

Third, the **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** is the expected number of successful events per year. The expected annual monetary harm, the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**, is therefore:

$$ \begin{aligned} ALE &\;=\; SLE \times ARO \\ ALE &\;=\; (AV \times EF) \times ARO \end{aligned} $$

This identity is the foundation of quantitative risk analysis. **(3 marks for the derivation chain.)**

A block diagram showing the *flow* $AV, EF \to SLE \to ALE$ should be drawn. **(2 marks for the diagram; refer to Section 4.3.)**

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute SLE before:**

$$ \begin{aligned} AV &= \;₹\,2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\ EF_{before} &= 0.80 \\ SLE_{before} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 \\ SLE_{before} &= \;₹\,1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE before:**

$$ \begin{aligned} ARO_{before} &= 1.2 \\ ALE_{before} &= 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 1.2 \\ ALE_{before} &= \;₹\,1{,}92{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 3 — Compute SLE and ALE after the WAF:**

$$ \begin{aligned} EF_{after} &= 0.15 \\ SLE_{after} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 \\ SLE_{after} &= \;₹\,30{,}00{,}000 \\ ARO_{after} &= 0.3 \\ ALE_{after} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3 \\ ALE_{after} &= \;₹\,9{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Compute ROSI:**

$$ \begin{aligned} SV &= 1{,}92{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = \;₹\,1{,}83{,}00{,}000 \\ C &= \;₹\,6{,}00{,}000 \\ ROSI &= \frac{1{,}83{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \\ ROSI &= \frac{1{,}77{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \\ ROSI &= 29.5 \end{aligned} $$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $ROSI = 29.5 \gg 0$, the WAF control is *strongly justified* and **must be deployed**. For every ₹ 1 spent, ₹ 29.5 of expected annual harm is averted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Writing "$ALE = 1.92$" without "₹/year" or "crore/year" loses 0.5 to 1 mark. Always state units.
> 2. **Skipping the conclusion.** Many students compute ROSI and stop. The KTU board *requires* a one-line interpretation: "ROSI > 0, hence deploy / ROSI < 0, hence reject." Omission costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Conflating EF with probability.** EF is *not* a probability. It is a fraction of asset value destroyed. Writing "EF = 80% chance" instead of "EF = 0.80, i.e., 80% of the asset value" is a mark-losing conceptual error.
> 4. **Missing the WAF benefits** — students often forget to *also* lower the ARO when a control is deployed. The ARO reduction is part of the control's benefit.

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model Question, Module 1 Choice-2]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **different categories of harm** in modern information security. Extend the discussion beyond the classical CIA triad to include privacy harm, physical harm, psychological harm, reputational harm, and societal harm. Provide one real-world engineering example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hospital PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) contains 50,000 patient MRI images. Each image is valued at ₹ 500 (regulatory + clinical value). A ransomware attack is estimated to encrypt **all** images per event, occurring **0.4 times per year**. Post-attack, the hospital suffers: (i) ₹ 20,00,000 in regulatory fines, (ii) ₹ 10,00,000 in operational recovery, (iii) a **30%** drop in patient throughput for one year worth ₹ 50,00,000 in lost revenue. Compute the *total harm per event* (the true SLE), the ALE, and recommend whether the hospital should invest ₹ 40,00,000 per year in an immutable backup system that brings the ARO to **0.05** and the data-loss EF to **0.02**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

Classical information security, as codified in the **TCSEC Orange Book (1983)** and **ITSEC (1991)**, recognized three harm categories aligned with the CIA triad: **loss of confidentiality, loss of integrity, and loss of availability.** A fourth, *accountability*, was added later. **(1 mark for CIA + accountability.)**

Modern KTU 2024-era security recognizes that these three are necessary but not sufficient. The following extended categories are mandatory in any complete answer:

- **Privacy Harm (1 mark):** The unauthorized disclosure of personal data leading to identity theft, discrimination, or loss of autonomy. *Example:* The 2017 Equifax breach exposed 147 million Americans' SSNs, causing years of identity-theft-related financial harm.
- **Physical Harm (1 mark):** Direct injury or loss of life caused by a cyber-physical attack. *Example:* The 2015 Ukraine power-grid attack caused a multi-hour blackout affecting 230,000 people; in 2021 a Florida water-treatment plant's sodium hydroxide was briefly remotely increased to dangerous levels.
- **Psychological Harm (1 mark):** Mental distress, anxiety, fear, or trauma inflicted on individuals. *Example:* The 2014 celebrity "Celebgate" iCloud breach caused documented long-term psychological harm to victims.
- **Reputational Harm (1 mark):** Erosion of brand trust and customer loyalty. *Example:* The 2017 Uber breach cover-up destroyed significant customer and investor trust, even more than the breach itself.
- **Societal Harm (1 mark):** Large-scale damage to public welfare, democratic processes, or critical infrastructure. *Example:* State-sponsored interference in electoral systems or the WannaCry ransomware (2017) that paralyzed the UK's National Health Service, delaying surgeries and ambulance dispatches.

*(1 additional mark for the conclusion — modern security = CIA + the five extended categories.)*

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the image-data component of harm per event:**

$$ \begin{aligned} AV_{images} &= 50{,}000 \times 500 = \;₹\,25{,}00{,}000 \\ EF_{images} &= 1.00 \text{ (all images encrypted)} \\ SLE_{images} &= 25{,}00{,}000 \times 1.00 = \;₹\,25{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$

**Step 2 — Add the secondary harm components per event:**

$$ \begin{aligned} SLE_{fines} &= \;₹\,20{,}00{,}000 \\ SLE_{recovery} &= \;₹\,10{,}00{,}000 \\ SLE_{revenue} &= 0.30 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = \;₹\,15{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the total true SLE per event:**

$$ \begin{aligned} SLE_{total} &= 25{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 \\ SLE_{total} &= \;₹\,70{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Compute ALE before the backup system:**

$$ \begin{aligned} ARO_{before} &= 0.4 \\ ALE_{before} &= 70{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 \\ ALE_{before} &= \;₹\,28{,}00{,}000 \;/\;\text{year} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Compute ALE after the backup system:**

$$ \begin{aligned} AV_{images,\,after} &= 25{,}00{,}000 \\ EF_{after} &= 0.02 \\ SLE_{after,\,images} &= 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = \;₹\,50{,}000 \end{aligned} $$

*(Note: Secondary harm components — fines, recovery, and revenue loss — are assumed eliminated by the immutable backup since recovery becomes possible. The board examiner accepts this assumption; state it explicitly.)*

$$ \begin{aligned} SLE_{after,\,total} &\approx \;₹\,50{,}000 \\ ARO_{after} &= 0.05 \\ ALE_{after} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.05 \\ ALE_{after} &= \;₹\,2{,}500 \;/\;\text{year} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 6 — Compute ROSI:**

$$ \begin{aligned} SV &= 28{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}500 = \;₹\,27{,}97{,}500 \\ C &= \;₹\,40{,}00{,}000 \\ ROSI &= \frac{27{,}97{,}500 - 40{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} \\ ROSI &= \frac{-12{,}02{,}500}{40{,}00{,}000} \\ ROSI &= -0.30 \end{aligned} $$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A negative ROSI of $-0.30$ indicates that the immutable backup system, as priced (₹ 40,00,000/year), *costs more than the harm it averts*. The hospital should **reject** this specific quote and seek a cheaper alternative — for example, a cloud-based immutable backup at ₹ 8,00,000/year, which would yield $ROSI = (27{,}97{,}500 - 8{,}00{,}000)/8{,}00{,}000 = 2.50$, a strongly justified investment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss on Question B:**
> 1. **Ignoring secondary harms.** A common student mistake is to compute SLE *only* from the data value. KTU board examiners *deliberately* include fines, recovery, and revenue in the question stem to test whether you can aggregate all harm components. Missing even one component costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Stating the conclusion wrongly.** A negative ROSI does *not* mean "do nothing." It means "find a cheaper control." The KTU board expects a *strategic recommendation*, not a one-word "reject."
> 3. **Mixing per-event and per-year quantities.** Secondary harms such as "revenue loss for one year" must be carefully interpreted — the 30% drop is for *one year* following the event, not for the rest of the asset's life. Be explicit.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Harm = Realized Damage.** It is the consequence, not the cause. The cause is the *threat*; the weakness is the *vulnerability*.
- **Core Identity:** $R = T \times V \times H$. Memorize it in this exact form.
- **SLE Formula:** $SLE = AV \times EF$. $EF$ is a fraction, not a probability.
- **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$. Units: currency per year.
- **ROSI Formula:** $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - C}{C}$. A positive ROSI justifies the control.
- **Three Core Harm Categories (Classical):** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- **Five Extended Harm Categories (Modern KTU 2024):** Privacy, Physical, Psychological, Reputational, Societal.
- **Always State Units** in the answer script: ₹/year for ALE, fraction or % for EF, events/year for ARO.
- **Always End with a Conclusion** in 14-mark problems. A computation without a recommendation is incomplete.
- **The harm-centric shift:** Modern security asks *"What harm could befall the user?"*, not just *"Is the system vulnerable?"* — this is the KTU 2024 philosophical anchor of Module 1.
- **Common confusions to avoid (high-frequency pitfall):**
  - Hazard ≠ Threat ≠ Vulnerability ≠ Harm.
  - EF is *not* probability. Probability is in ARO.
  - Risk is not just harm; harm is one of three multiplicative factors.
  - A negative ROSI is *not* "do nothing" — it is "find a cheaper control."
- **Memorize one real-world example** for each of the five extended harm categories — board examiners reward application-level answers with bonus half-marks.
- **Block-diagram drawing skill:** Practice drawing the chain $AV, EF \to SLE \to ALE$ at least three times before the exam; it is a guaranteed 2-mark sub-part in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vulnerabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Vulnerability (NIST SP 800-30 Terminology):**
> A weakness in an information system, system security procedures, internal controls, or implementation that could be exploited or triggered by a threat source.

In the **STRIDE / CIA Triad** framework adopted by KTU 2024 scheme security modules, a vulnerability is formally defined as a **flaw or gap in the system's design, implementation, configuration, operation, or management** that can be leveraged by an adversary to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA)** of the asset.

Mathematically, vulnerability is the multiplicative factor inside the classic risk model:

$$R = f(T, V, I)$$

where:
- $R$ = Risk
- $T$ = Threat
- $V$ = Vulnerability
- $I$ = Impact (Asset Value)

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> Module 1 of PECST863 introduces the foundational pillars of security analysis — *Threats, Vulnerabilities, Attacks, and Controls*. A vulnerability is the *passive enabling condition* that makes an attack possible; without a vulnerability, a threat cannot materialize into an attack.

### 1.2 Distinguishing Vulnerability from Related Terms

| Term | Definition | Analogy |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Threat** | Any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm | A burglar with a crowbar |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited | An unlocked back window |
| **Attack** | An actual attempt to exploit a vulnerability | The burglar reaching the window |
| **Risk** | Probability × Impact of threat exploiting vulnerability | Likelihood of theft + value of goods |
| **Control** | Safeguard counteracting a threat | Deadbolt lock + alarm system |
