> [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A **threat without a vulnerability** is harmless. A **vulnerability without a threat** is *latent* but not yet an incident. The intersection of both creates an **exploitable exposure**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Fortress and the Cracks"

Imagine a heavily fortified medieval castle. The stone walls are thick, the moat is deep, and the gates are reinforced. However:
- A **hairline crack** in the outer wall (vulnerability) is harmless on its own.
- A **trebuchet loaded with boulders** (threat) sitting outside is also harmless if the wall has no cracks.
- The **moment a siege engineer (attacker) maps the crack** and aims the trebuchet at it, a **breach** occurs.

In cybersecurity, the castle is your **system/network**, the cracks are **vulnerabilities**, the trebuchet is a **threat agent**, and the breach is a **realized attack**. Security engineering is the art of *patching the cracks before the trebuchet arrives*.

### 1.4 Core Terminology (Mandatory KTU Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Frequency KTU Acronyms (Commit to Memory):**
> - **CVE** — Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (the dictionary of known flaws).
> - **CWE** — Common Weakness Enumeration (taxonomy of weakness types).
> - **CVSS** — Common Vulnerability Scoring System (0.0 to 10.0 severity).
> - **NVD** — National Vulnerability Database (US-NIST maintained feed).
> - **ZDI** — Zero Day Initiative (commercial bug-bounty broker).
> - **OWASP** — Open Web Application Security Project (Top-10 baseline).
> - **IOC** — Indicator of Compromise (artifact of a successful exploitation).

### 1.5 The Three Pillars of a Vulnerability (Vulnerability Triad)

Every vulnerability is uniquely characterized by three pillars — a question examiners **love to ask**:

1. **Origin** — Where did it come from? (Design / Implementation / Configuration / Operational)
2. **State** — Is it known or unknown? (Known / Unknown / Zero-Day)
3. **Exploitability** — How easy is it to weaponize? (CVSS Exploitability Sub-Score)

> [!TIP] > **Mnemonic: "OSE"** — *Origin, State, Exploitability*. Memorize this for 3-mark definition questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vulnerabilities (KTU Exam-Favorite)

Vulnerabilities can be taxonomically classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Origin (Where the Flaw Lives)

1. **Design Vulnerabilities** — Flaws in the *architectural blueprint* (e.g., plaintext protocol design like HTTP, missing rate-limiting logic). These are the most expensive to fix because they require re-architecture.
2. **Implementation Vulnerabilities** — Bugs introduced during *coding* (e.g., buffer overflow, SQL injection, race conditions). These are the most numerous (≈ 75% of CVEs).
3. **Configuration Vulnerabilities** — Errors in *deployment and setup* (e.g., default admin credentials, open S3 buckets, debug mode left enabled in production).
4. **Operational Vulnerabilities** — Weaknesses in *processes and people* (e.g., unpatched systems, lack of security training, weak password policy).

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Hook:** KTU 2024 expects students to map every CVE example to one of these four origins.

### B. By State (Disclosure Status)

- **Known Vulnerability** — Publicly disclosed; a patch exists. (e.g., CVE-2017-0144 EternalBlue, patched via MS17-010).
- **Unknown Vulnerability** — Known only to the discoverer (e.g., held in a private bug-bounty program).
- **Zero-Day Vulnerability** — Unknown to the vendor; no patch exists. (e.g., CVE-2021-44228 Log4Shell at T = 0).

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "zero-day" with the *attack*. Zero-day refers to the **vulnerability**, not the exploit. The corresponding exploit is termed a *zero-day exploit*.

### C. By Resource Being Affected

| Layer | Example Vulnerability | CVE |
|-------|----------------------|-----|
| Hardware | Spectre / Meltdown speculative execution | CVE-2017-5753 / CVE-2017-5754 |
| Firmware | UEFI bootkit | CVE-2018-3616 |
| OS Kernel | Windows SMBv1 RCE | CVE-2017-0144 |
| Application | Apache Log4j RCE | CVE-2021-44228 |
| Database | MySQL authentication bypass | CVE-2012-2122 |
| Network | Heartbleed (OpenSSL buffer over-read) | CVE-2014-0160 |
| Web | Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) | CWE-79 |
| Human/Social | Phishing susceptibility | CWE-451 |

### D. By CIA Property Violated

A vulnerability is also classified by the **security property it breaks**:
- **Confidentiality Vulnerability** (e.g., information disclosure)
- **Integrity Vulnerability** (e.g., unauthorized write)
- **Availability Vulnerability** (e.g., DoS / DDoS amplification)
- **Authentication / Authorization Vulnerability** (e.g., privilege escalation, IDOR)
- **Non-Repudiation Vulnerability** (e.g., missing audit logs)

---

## 2.2 The CVSS Framework — The Mathematical Heart of Vulnerability Scoring

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System v3.1 (CVSS v3.1)** is the de-facto industry standard mandated by KTU 2024 for severity assignment. It is composed of three metric groups:

1. **Base Metrics** (intrinsic — does not change over time)
2. **Temporal Metrics** (changes with time as patches appear)
3. **Environmental Metrics** (specific to a given deployment)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Focus Point:** Examiners **expect** students to know the Base Score formula. Memorize it.

### 2.3 CVSS Base Score Formula (Verbatim)

> The CVSS v3.1 Base Score is derived from the **Impact Sub-Score (ISS)** and the **Exploitability Sub-Score**.

**Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right]$$

where $C$, $I$, $A$ are the **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability Impact** coefficients:

- None: $0.00$
- Low: $0.22$
- High: $0.56$

**Step 2 — Impact (full):**

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times ISS$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability Sub-Score:**

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$$

where $AV$ (Attack Vector), $AC$ (Attack Complexity), $PR$ (Privileges Required), $UI$ (User Interaction) take the values:

| Metric | Network | Adjacent | Local | Physical |
|--------|---------|----------|-------|----------|
| **AV** | $0.85$ | $0.62$ | $0.55$ | $0.20$ |

| Metric | Low | High |
|--------|-----|------|
| **AC** | $0.77$ | $0.44$ |
| **PR** | $0.85$ (None) | $0.62$ (Low) / $0.27$ (High) |
| **UI** | $0.85$ (None) | $0.62$ (Required) |

**Step 4 — Final Base Score:**

If $ISS \le 0$ then $\text{BaseScore} = 0$. Otherwise:

$$\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}\left[\min\left(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10\right)\right]$$

### 2.4 CVSS Severity Rating Scale

| Base Score Range | Severity | Patch Priority |
|------------------|----------|----------------|
| 0.0 | None | Skip |
| 0.1 – 3.9 | Low | Routine |
| 4.0 – 6.9 | Medium | Scheduled |
| 7.0 – 8.9 | High | Urgent |
| 9.0 – 10.0 | Critical | Emergency (off-cycle) |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Used In |
|---|----------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Risk is a function of Threat, Vulnerability, Impact | Risk analysis |
| 2 | $ISS = 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$ | Impact Sub-Score (CVSS) | CVSS calculation |
| 3 | $\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times ISS$ | CVSS Impact term | CVSS calculation |
| 4 | $\text{Expl} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ | CVSS Exploitability | CVSS calculation |
| 5 | $\text{Base} = \text{round}[\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Expl},\ 10)]$ | CVSS Base Score | CVSS calculation |
| 6 | $\text{SLE} = \text{AssetValue} \times \text{EF}$ | Single Loss Expectancy | Quantitative risk |
| 7 | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | Quantitative risk |
| 8 | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE} - \text{CS}}{\text{CS}}$ | Return on Security Investment | Security economics |
| 9 | $\text{MTTR} + \text{MTBF} = \text{MTTSF}$ | Mean time system failure | Reliability math |
| 10 | $P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Probability of at least one breach in $n$ trials | Probability theory |

> [!NOTE] > **Pro Tip:** For a 14-mark CVSS problem, examiners allot **7 marks** for stating the formula correctly and **7 marks** for plugging in the values and computing the rounded base score.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CVSS framework is the **standardized currency** of the vulnerability economy:
- **Patch Management Teams** use it to prioritize patch cycles.
- **Bug-Bounty Platforms** (HackerOne, Bugcrowd) use it to compute payouts.
- **Cyber Insurance Underwriters** use it to compute premiums.
- **SIEM / SOAR Platforms** (Splunk, Chronicle) auto-enrich CVEs with CVSS scores.
- **GRC Tools** (ServiceNow, Archer) map CVSS to control frameworks (NIST 800-53, ISO 27001).

In Kerala's IT industry (e.g., TCS Infopark, UST Global Trivandrum, IBS Campus), CVSS-based prioritization is the **first automated step** in any enterprise vulnerability management pipeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked CVSS Base Score — Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160) — Exhaustive Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (Full 14-mark model answer):**
> Compute the CVSS v3.1 Base Score for the *Heartbleed* vulnerability, given the following metric values:

**Given Metrics:**
- Confidentiality Impact $(C) = 0.56$ (High — memory contents are disclosed)
- Integrity Impact $(I) = 0.00$ (None — no writes occur)
- Availability Impact $(A) = 0.00$ (None — no crash triggered)
- Attack Vector $(AV) = 0.85$ (Network)
- Attack Complexity $(AC) = 0.77$ (Low)
- Privileges Required $(PR) = 0.85$ (None)
- User Interaction $(UI) = 0.85$ (None)

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

We begin by substituting $C, I, A$ into the ISS definition:

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right]$$

Plug in the metric values:

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.00) \times (1 - 0.00) \right]$$

Evaluate each inner parenthesis:

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ (0.44) \times (1.00) \times (1.00) \right]$$

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ 0.44 \right]$$

$$ISS = 0.56$$

Since $ISS = 0.56 > 0$, the base score is non-zero, and we proceed to compute the full Impact.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Full Impact Term

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times ISS$$

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.56$$

$$\text{Impact} = 3.5952$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85$$

Compute step-by-step (associative from the left):

$$8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.9870$$

$$6.9870 \times 0.77 = 5.379990 \approx 5.3800$$

$$5.3800 \times 0.85 = 4.5730$$

$$4.5730 \times 0.85 = 3.8871 \approx 3.89$$

---

### Step 4 — Apply the Cap and Round

We now compute:

$$\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}\left[\min\left(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10\right)\right]$$

Sum the two terms:

$$\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability} = 3.5952 + 3.8871 = 7.4823$$

Apply the cap:

$$\min(7.4823,\ 10) = 7.4823$$

Apply the rounding rule (CVSS rounds **up** to one decimal):

$$\text{BaseScore} = 7.5$$

---

### Step 5 — Map to Severity and Interpret

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Base Score | **7.5** |
| Severity | **High** |
| Patch Priority | **Urgent (within 14 days)** |
| Reference | CVE-2014-0160 (matches NVD's official 7.5) |

> [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Insight:** Our hand-calculated 7.5 **exactly matches** the NVD's official CVSS for Heartbleed. This validates the formula's real-world accuracy.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative CVSS for 5 Famous CVEs

| # | CVE | Name | $C$ | $I$ | $A$ | $AV$ | $AC$ | $PR$ | $UI$ | Computed Base | Severity |
|---|-----|------|-----|-----|-----|------|------|------|------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | CVE-2014-0160 | Heartbleed | 0.56 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.85 | 0.77 | 0.85 | 0.85 | **7.5** | High |
| 2 | CVE-2017-0144 | EternalBlue | 0.56 | 0.56 | 0.56 | 0.85 | 0.77 | 0.85 | 0.85 | **9.3** *(approx; scope-changed)* | Critical |
| 3 | CVE-2021-44228 | Log4Shell | 0.56 | 0.56 | 0.56 | 0.85 | 0.77 | 0.85 | 0.85 | **10.0** *(scope-changed)* | Critical |
| 4 | CVE-2017-5753 | Spectre | 0.56 | 0.22 | 0.22 | 0.85 | 0.77 | 0.85 | 0.85 | **8.2** | High |
| 5 | CVE-2014-6271 | Shellshock | 0.56 | 0.56 | 0.56 | 0.85 | 0.77 | 0.85 | 0.85 | **9.8** *(scope-changed)* | Critical |

> [!NOTE] > **Pattern Recognition:** Notice that scope-changed vulnerabilities (where exploitation crosses trust boundaries) get an extra multiplier that easily pushes them into the *Critical 9.0+* tier.

---

## 3.3 Risk Computation — Quantitative Worked Example

> **Problem:** An e-commerce web application in a Kochi-based firm processes ₹50,00,000 daily. Industry breach data shows it is hit **12 times per year** (ARO = 12), and each successful breach exposes **70%** of daily revenue (Exposure Factor $EF = 0.7$).

**Step 1 — Single Loss Expectancy:**

$$SLE = \text{AssetValue} \times EF = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7 = 35{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Annual Loss Expectancy:**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 12 = 4{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}$$

**Step 3 — Countermeasure Cost:** A Web Application Firewall (WAF) costs ₹80,00,000/year to operate.

**Step 4 — Return on Security Investment (ROSI):**

$$ROSI = \frac{ALE - CS}{CS} = \frac{4{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} = \frac{3{,}40{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} = 4.25$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** ROSI = 4.25 = **425 % return** ⇒ deploying the WAF is *economically rational* and reduces ALE by ~80 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Vulnerability Triad (Origin × State × CIA)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vulnerability] --> B[Origin Dimension]
    A --> C[State Dimension]
    A --> D[CIA Dimension]
    A --> E[Exploitability Dimension]

    B --> B1[Design Flaw]
    B --> B2[Implementation Bug]
    B --> B3[Configuration Error]
    B --> B4[Operational Weakness]

    C --> C1[Known CVE]
    C --> C2[Unknown Private]
    C --> C3[Zero-Day]

    D --> D1[Confidentiality Break]
    D --> D2[Integrity Break]
    D --> D3[Availability Break]

    E --> E1[Network Reachable]
    E --> E2[Local Reachable]
    E --> E3[Physical Required]
```

> **Figure 4.1** — The four orthogonal axes of vulnerability classification, each producing a unique "fingerprint" for any CVE.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Vulnerability Lifecycle (Discovery → Closure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Discovery] --> P2[Stage 2: Private Disclosure]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Vendor Confirmation]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Patch Development]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Patch Release]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Public Disclosure]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7: Mass Exploitation Window]
    P7 --> P8[Stage 8: Mitigation in Field]
    P8 --> P9[Stage 9: Closure / Post-mortem]

    P1 -."bug bounty researcher".-> P1
    P6 -."CVE assignment NVD".-> P6
    P7 -."honeypot telemetry".-> P7
```

> **Figure 4.2** — Generic vulnerability lifecycle, useful for answering "explain the stages of vulnerability handling" type questions (worth ~7 marks).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Risk Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Asset Inventory]
        A2[Threat Catalog]
        A3[Vulnerability Database CVE/NVD]
    end

    subgraph Engine
        B1[Asset Valuation Module]
        B2[Threat Probability Estimator]
        B3[CVSS Score Engine]
        B4[Risk Calculator]
    end

    subgraph Output
        C1[Prioritized Patch List]
        C2[Compensating Controls]
        C3[Risk Register GRC]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3

    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4

    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
```

> **Figure 4.3** — A Vulnerability Management Platform's high-level architecture, showing the data flow from inputs (assets, threats, CVEs) through the scoring engine to actionable outputs.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — CVE Naming Convention and Parsing

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CVE-YYYY-NNNNN] --> B[CVE Prefix]
    A --> C[YYYY Year Assigned]
    A --> D[NNNNN Sequence Number]

    B --> B1[Common Vulnerabilities Exposures Registry]
    C --> C1[Mitre Corporation Maintainer]
    D --> D1[Sequential Per Year]

    E[Example: CVE-2021-44228] --> F[Log4Shell]
    E --> G[Year 2021]
    E --> H[Sequence 44228]
```

> **Figure 4.4** — Anatomy of a CVE identifier, a common 3-mark question in KTU theory papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**Define the term *Vulnerability* in information security. How does it differ from a *Threat* and a *Risk*? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **Vulnerability** is a weakness or flaw in an information system's design, implementation, configuration, or operation that can be exploited by a threat source to compromise security.

- A **Threat** is the *external force* (e.g., an attacker, malware, natural disaster) that *could* cause harm.
- A **Risk** is the *quantified likelihood* of a threat successfully exploiting a vulnerability, multiplied by the resulting impact.

**Key Distinction:** A vulnerability is a *property of the system* (passive), while a threat is a *property of the environment* (active). Risk emerges only when both intersect. (1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for differentiation, 0.5 mark for example.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**What is CVE? Explain CVSS and its severity rating scale. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** A globally unique identifier assigned to every publicly known cybersecurity vulnerability, maintained by MITRE Corporation. Format: `CVE-YYYY-NNNNN` (e.g., `CVE-2021-44228` for Log4Shell).
- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** An open framework for communicating the *characteristics and severity* of software vulnerabilities. The current version is **CVSS v3.1**, which produces a Base Score from **0.0 to 10.0**.

**Severity Bands:**
- 0.0 — None
- 0.1 – 3.9 — Low
- 4.0 – 6.9 — Medium
- 7.0 – 8.9 — High
- 9.0 – 10.0 — Critical

(1 mark for CVE definition, 1 mark for CVSS definition, 1 mark for severity scale.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the various **classes of vulnerabilities** in information systems with suitable examples. *— 7 Marks*

**(b)** A web application has the following CVSS v3.1 metrics:
- Confidentiality Impact: High
- Integrity Impact: High
- Availability Impact: High
- Attack Vector: Network
- Attack Complexity: Low
- Privileges Required: None
- User Interaction: None

Compute the **CVSS Base Score** and state the **severity rating**. *— 7 Marks*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Classification of Vulnerabilities (7 Marks)

Vulnerabilities are classified along **four orthogonal dimensions** in modern security practice (per OWASP, NIST SP 800-30, and KTU 2024 syllabus):

1. **Design Vulnerabilities** *(2 marks)* — Architectural flaws in the system's blueprint.
   - *Example:* Using MD5 for password hashing (cryptographic design weakness).
   - *Example:* HTTP without TLS (cleartext protocol design flaw).
   - *Fix cost:* Very high (re-architecture required).

2. **Implementation Vulnerabilities** *(2 marks)* — Bugs in source code.
   - *Example:* Buffer overflow (CWE-120), SQL Injection (CWE-89), Cross-Site Scripting (CWE-79).
   - *Example:* Use-after-free, integer overflow, race condition.
   - *Fix cost:* Moderate (code patches).

3. **Configuration Vulnerabilities** *(1.5 marks)* — Deployment misconfigurations.
   - *Example:* Default `admin/admin` credentials left unchanged.
   - *Example:* Open S3 buckets, exposed `.env` files, debug mode in production.
   - *Fix cost:* Low (settings change).

4. **Operational Vulnerabilities** *(1.5 marks)* — Process and people weaknesses.
   - *Example:* Unpatched OS for 6 months, no security awareness training.
   - *Example:* Single-factor authentication on critical systems.
   - *Fix cost:* Medium (policy + training).

> [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** A robust vulnerability management program must address all four classes — patching alone (operational) is insufficient without secure design and hardening.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — CVSS Base Score Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the coefficient values:**
- $C = 0.56$ (High)
- $I = 0.56$ (High)
- $A = 0.56$ (High)
- $AV = 0.85$ (Network)
- $AC = 0.77$ (Low)
- $PR = 0.85$ (None)
- $UI = 0.85$ (None)

**Step 2 — Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \right]$$

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ 0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44 \right]$$

$$ISS = 1 - \left[ 0.085184 \right] = 0.914816$$

**Step 3 — Compute Impact term:**

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times ISS = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.8731$$

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability term:**

$$\text{Expl} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85$$

- $8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.9870$
- $6.9870 \times 0.77 = 5.3800$
- $5.3800 \times 0.85 = 4.5730$
- $4.5730 \times 0.85 = 3.8871$

**Step 5 — Apply cap and round:**

$$\text{Base} = \text{round}\left[\min(5.8731 + 3.8871,\ 10)\right]$$

$$= \text{round}\left[\min(9.7602,\ 10)\right]$$

$$= \text{round}(9.7602) = 9.8$$

**Step 6 — Severity Mapping:**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Base Score | **9.8** |
| Severity | **Critical** |
| Action | **Emergency patch within 72 hours** |

> **Final Answer:** Base Score = **9.8 (Critical)**

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **vulnerability lifecycle** in detail, mentioning the role of disclosure policy (coordinated vs. full). *— 7 Marks*

**(b)** Explain the **types of vulnerability scans** and differentiate between **vulnerability scanning and penetration testing**. *— 7 Marks*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Vulnerability Lifecycle (7 Marks)

The vulnerability lifecycle describes the journey of a vulnerability from discovery to closure:

1. **Discovery / Research Phase** *(1.5 marks)* — A security researcher, red-team, or end user identifies a flaw. Discovery may be accidental (e.g., a system crash) or methodical (fuzzing, code review, static analysis).

2. **Private / Responsible Disclosure** *(1.5 marks)* — The discoverer notifies the vendor privately under a *responsible disclosure* timeline (typically 90 days — Google's Project Zero policy). During this phase, the vulnerability is *known* but not yet public.

3. **Vendor Confirmation & Patch Development** *(1 mark)* — The vendor reproduces the issue, assigns an internal ticket, and develops a fix. A CVE ID is reserved from MITRE.

4. **Patch Release & Public Disclosure** *(1.5 marks)* — The vendor releases the security bulletin (e.g., Microsoft's Patch Tuesday, Oracle's CPU) and the CVE is published to the NVD.

5. **Exploitation Window / Mass Weaponization** *(1 mark)* — Between disclosure and full patching in the wild, attackers reverse-engineer the patch to create *n-day exploits*. *Zero-day exploits* (used before patch) represent maximum risk.

6. **Mitigation & Closure** *(0.5 mark)* — Organizations apply patches, compensating controls (WAF rules, virtual patches), and conduct post-mortems.

**Disclosure Models:**
- **Coordinated Disclosure (CD):** Researcher + vendor collaborate on timing to minimize user risk. (Industry standard.)
- **Full Disclosure:** Vulnerabilities are posted publicly without vendor coordination, forcing rapid patching but risking widespread exploitation (e.g., HD Moore's Metasploit style).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Vulnerability Scans vs. Penetration Testing (7 Marks)

**Types of Vulnerability Scans** *(4 marks)*:

| Scan Type | Authenticated? | Scope | Speed | Noise |
|-----------|---------------|-------|-------|-------|
| **External / Unauthenticated** | No (black-box) | Internet-facing services | Fast | Low |
| **Internal / Authenticated** | Yes (with credentials) | Internal hosts, configs | Slower | Higher |
| **Active Scanning** | Probes send packets | Open ports, services | Slower | Detectable by IDS |
| **Passive Scanning** | Sniffs traffic only | Already-deployed services | Slower | Silent / undetectable |

Tools: **Nessus**, **Qualys VMDR**, **OpenVAS**, **Rapid7 InsightVM**.

**Vulnerability Scanning vs. Penetration Testing** *(3 marks)*:

| Property | Vulnerability Scan | Penetration Test |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Goal** | Identify and list known vulnerabilities | Actively exploit and demonstrate impact |
| **Depth** | Breadth (scan many systems) | Depth (chain exploits for maximum impact) |
| **Automation** | Fully automated | Manual + automated hybrid |
| **Frequency** | Weekly / monthly | Annually or after major changes |
| **Output** | CVSS-scored CVE list | Executive report + business risk narrative |
| **Skill** | Tool operator | Red-team operator (CRTP/OSCP-level) |
| **False Positives** | Higher (no exploitation) | Lower (proven exploitation) |

> **Analogy:** A vulnerability scan is *a doctor's stethoscope check-up*; a penetration test is *a full cardiac stress test with angiography*. Both are necessary — neither replaces the other.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing vulnerability with attack:** Vulnerability is the *flaw*; attack is the *action*. Don't interchange them in definitions — 0.5 mark lost.
> 2. **Forgetting the `min(…, 10)` cap:** A student who computes Base Score = 12.3 in a CVSS problem loses 1 mark. Always cap at 10.
> 3. **Wrong rounding direction:** CVSS Base Score rounds **up** to one decimal. 9.03 → 9.1, not 9.0.
> 4. **Skipping the "ISS ≤ 0 ⇒ BaseScore = 0" rule:** If a student computes ISS = 0 (all three impacts = None) and then returns a non-zero base score, full step is invalidated.
> 5. **Mixing CVSS v2 and v3.1 coefficients:** In v2, $C, I, A$ used 0, 0.275, 0.660; in v3.1 they are 0, 0.22, 0.56. Use **v3.1** — it is the KTU 2024 standard.
> 6. **Not mentioning scope-changed multiplier:** Many "Critical" CVEs (Log4Shell 10.0) achieve their score partly due to a scope change. Examiners expect this nuance for a 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Vulnerability = Weakness**, **Threat = External Force**, **Risk = Probability × Impact**.
- Vulnerability **does not equal attack** — vulnerability is the *flaw*, attack is the *exploitation*.
- CVE format is `CVE-YYYY-NNNNN`; maintained by **MITRE**; published in **NVD**.
- CVSS v3.1 Base Score ranges from **0.0 to 10.0**; severity bands are **None / Low / Medium / High / Critical**.
- CVSS Base Score formula:
  $$ISS = 1 - \left[(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)\right]$$
  $$\text{Base} = \text{round}\left[\min(6.42 \cdot ISS + 8.22 \cdot AV \cdot AC \cdot PR \cdot UI,\ 10)\right]$$
- High coefficient for $C/I/A$ = 0.56, Low = 0.22, None = 0.00.
- High coefficient for $AV$ = 0.85, $AC$ = 0.77, $PR$(None) = 0.85, $UI$(None) = 0.85.
- Vulnerability **origin** = Design / Implementation / Configuration / Operational (DICO mnemonic).
- Vulnerability **state** = Known / Unknown / Zero-Day.
- Zero-Day = vulnerability unknown to vendor; no patch exists at the time of discovery.
- **Coordinated Disclosure** is the industry standard; full disclosure is risky.
- **ALE = SLE × ARO**; **SLE = AssetValue × EF**; **ROSI = (ALE − CS) / CS**.
- **Vulnerability Scan** = automated, broad, identifies CVEs; **Penetration Test** = manual, deep, exploits CVEs.
- Famous CVEs to remember:
  - **CVE-2014-0160** Heartbleed → CVSS 7.5 (Confidentiality break, Network).
  - **CVE-2017-0144** EternalBlue → CVSS 8.1 / 9.3 (RCE, Network).
  - **CVE-2021-44228** Log4Shell → CVSS 10.0 (Critical, Scope-Changed, Network).
  - **CVE-2014-6271** Shellshock → CVSS 9.8 (Bash RCE).
  - **CVE-2017-5753** Spectre → CVSS 8.2 (CPU speculative execution).
- Tools to mention: **Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, Nmap, Burp Suite, Metasploit, Wireshark**.
- Frameworks: **STRIDE, DREAD, PASTA, OWASP Top 10, NIST SP 800-30, ISO 27005**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always cite CVE IDs and CVSS scores in real-world questions — it earns *methodology marks* even when the answer is partially incorrect.

---

**End of Module 1 — Vulnerabilities Notes** | *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Authentication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Authentication

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for **PECST863 — Topics in Security**, **Authentication** is defined as the cryptographic and procedural mechanism by which a system, network, or application *verifies the claimed identity* of a communicating principal (a user, a device, a process, or a service) prior to granting access to protected resources.

Formally, an authentication function can be expressed as a binary decision process:

$$A: I \times C \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$$

Where:
- $I$ = the claimed identity of the principal
- $C$ = the presented credential (proof of identity)
- The output is **1 (ACCEPT)** if credentials match the trusted reference, and **0 (REJECT)** otherwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Verbatim Definition:**
> *"Authentication is the process of ensuring that the communicating entity is the one that it claims to be. It is the binding of an identity to a specific principal."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you arrive at **Cochin International Airport** for an international flight:

1. You approach the **check-in counter** and present your **passport + ticket**.
2. The airline staff verifies your passport photo, name, and visa against the immigration database.
3. Once your identity is **proven**, you are issued a **boarding pass** that authorizes you to enter the gate and board.

In this analogy:
- **Authentication** = the passport/ID check at the counter (proving *who you are*).
- **Authorization** = the boarding pass that determines *what you are allowed to do* (enter gate, board flight).
- **Credentials** = the passport + ticket.
- **Trusted Third Party** = the immigration database / government.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick (often used in KTU viva):**
> **A**uthentication = **A**re you who you say you are?
> **A**uthorization = **A**re you allowed to do it?

## 1.3 The Authentication Triad

The three pillars of security that authentication supports are:

| Pillar | Purpose | KTU Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Preventing unauthorized disclosure | C |
| **Integrity** | Preventing unauthorized modification | I |
| **Authentication** | Verifying identity | A |

> [!TIP]
> Authentication is one of the foundational pillars of the **CIA Triad extended to CIANA+ (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Availability)**. In KTU 2024 schemes, *Non-repudiation* is often a derivative of strong authentication.

## 1.4 Authentication vs. Identification

| Aspect | Identification | Authentication |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Claiming an identity | Proving the claimed identity |
| **Action** | "I am User-X" | "Here is the proof" |
| **Example** | Typing a username | Entering a password |
| **Analogy** | Saying your name | Showing your ID card |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five (Six) Authentication Factors

The KTU 2024 PECST863 syllabus categorizes authentication credentials into the following factor classes. A robust system uses **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** — combining two or more independent factors.

### 2.1.1 Knowledge Factor (Type 1) — "Something You Know"
Examples: Passwords, PINs, passphrases, security questions.

- **Strengths:** Simple, low cost, easy to deploy.
- **Weaknesses:** Susceptible to shoulder-surfing, dictionary attacks, keyloggers, social engineering.

### 2.1.2 Possession Factor (Type 2) — "Something You Have"
Examples: Smart cards, hardware tokens (RSA SecurID), mobile phones (OTP), USB security keys (YubiKey).

- **Strengths:** Hard to duplicate physically, requires physical theft.
- **Weaknesses:** Can be lost, stolen, or duplicated.

### 2.1.3 Inherence Factor (Type 3) — "Something You Are"
Examples: Fingerprint, iris scan, facial recognition, voice print, palm geometry.

- **Strengths:** Cannot be lost or forgotten, very difficult to forge.
- **Weaknesses:** Privacy concerns, false accept/reject rates, expensive sensors.

### 2.1.4 Location Factor (Type 4) — "Somewhere You Are"
Examples: IP address geolocation, GPS coordinates, network MAC address.

### 2.1.5 Time Factor (Type 5) — "When You Act"
Examples: Time-windowed access (e.g., only during office hours).

### 2.1.6 Behavior Factor (Type 6) — "How You Behave"
Examples: Keystroke dynamics, mouse movement patterns, gait analysis, voice cadence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** A genuine **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** system must combine factors from *at least two different categories* above. Two passwords (both Type 1) do **not** constitute MFA — this is called "two-step verification" or 2SV, not true MFA.

## 2.2 Password-Based Authentication — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Password Entropy

The strength of a password is measured in **bits of entropy**. Higher entropy = exponentially harder to brute-force.

$$H = L \times \log_2(N)$$

Where:
- $H$ = entropy in **bits**
- $L$ = length of the password
- $N$ = size of the character set (e.g., 26 for lowercase, 62 for alphanumeric, 94 for printable ASCII)

### 2.2.2 Search Space (Theoretical Password Space)

The total number of possible passwords:

$$S = N^L$$

### 2.2.3 Time to Brute-Force

$$T_{crack} = \frac{S}{2 \times R}$$

Where $R$ is the attacker's hash rate (guesses per second). The factor of 2 assumes the password is found on average at the midpoint of the search space.

### 2.2.4 Password Hashing (One-Way Function)

A password $P$ is never stored in plaintext. It is transformed via a cryptographic hash function:

$$h = \text{Hash}(P + \text{salt})$$

Where:
- $h$ = stored hash value (fixed length, e.g., **256 bits** for SHA-256)
- $P$ = user password
- $\text{salt}$ = random nonce prepended to defeat rainbow table attacks

> [!WARNING]
> KTU 2024 examiners specifically check whether students know that **MD5 and SHA-1 are broken** for password hashing. Use **bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2** instead. KTU 2024 syllabus mandates awareness of modern adaptive hashing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Password Entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | bits |
| 2 | Password Search Space | $S = N^L$ | dimensionless count |
| 3 | Expected Brute-Force Time | $T_{crack} = S / (2R)$ | seconds |
| 4 | HMAC Construction | $HMAC(K, M) = H((K_0 \oplus opad) \parallel H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | bits |
| 5 | Hash Length (SHA-256) | $L_h = 256$ | bits |
| 6 | Biometric FAR | $FAR = N_{FA} / N_{total}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ |
| 7 | Biometric FRR | $FRR = N_{FR} / N_{total}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ |
| 8 | Cross-Over Error Rate | $CER = EER$ where $FAR = FRR$ | percentage |
| 9 | OTP Time Validity | $T_{valid} = 30$ s (TOTP) | seconds |
| 10 | Kerberos Ticket Lifetime | $T_{life} \in [1, 1440]$ | minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> In formulas above, $\parallel$ denotes bitwise **concatenation**, $\oplus$ denotes **XOR**, and $H(\cdot)$ denotes a cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-256).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Authentication is the **gatekeeper** of:
- **Banking systems** (UPI, NEFT, ATM PIN verification)
- **Cloud platforms** (AWS IAM, Azure AD, GCP Identity)
- **IoT device pairing** (Bluetooth pairing, Zigbee trust center)
- **Military communications** (TACLANE encryptors with hardware tokens)
- **Healthcare HIPAA-compliant systems** (biometric patient ID)
- **E-Governance** (Aadhaar OTP, e-Sign, DigiLocker)

In the **KTU Kerala context**, authentication is the cornerstone of:
- **Kerala State Wide Area Network (KSWAN)**
- **e-Health Kerala** (patient record access)
- **Digital University Kerala** (online exam proctoring)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Password Entropy Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem (KTU 2019 Model):** A password uses lowercase English letters (26 characters) and is exactly 8 characters long. Compute its entropy and the expected brute-force time at a rate of $10^{10}$ guesses/sec.

### Step 1: Identify parameters
- Character set: lowercase English = 26 characters, so $N = 26$
- Password length: $L = 8$

### Step 2: Apply the entropy formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= L \cdot \log_2(N) \\
H &= 8 \cdot \log_2(26) \\
H &= 8 \cdot 4.7004 \\
H &= 37.6035 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The password has approximately **37.6 bits** of entropy. This is considered *weak* by NIST SP 800-63B standards (which require $\geq 30$ bits of entropy only for memorization feasibility, but $\geq 50$ bits is recommended).

### Step 3: Compute the search space

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= N^L \\
S &= 26^8 \\
S &= 208{,}827{,}064{,}576 \\
S &\approx 2.088 \times 10^{11} \text{ possible passwords}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute expected brute-force time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{crack} &= \frac{S}{2 \times R} \\
T_{crack} &= \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
T_{crack} &= \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2.0 \times 10^{10}} \\
T_{crack} &= 10.44 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** An 8-character lowercase-only password falls in **~10.44 seconds** at a modern GPU brute-force rate. This is why KTU 2024 syllabus mandates long passphrases + multi-factor authentication.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: HMAC-Based Challenge-Response Authentication

**Problem:** Explain how HMAC enables secure challenge-response authentication between Alice ($A$) and Bob ($B$) who share a secret key $K$.

### Step 1: Setup
- Alice and Bob pre-share a secret symmetric key $K$.
- An eavesdropper **Eve** can observe the channel but does not know $K$.

### Step 2: Bob (verifier) generates a random challenge

Bob generates a cryptographically secure random nonce $C_B$ of, say, 128 bits:

$$C_B \xleftarrow{R} \{0,1\}^{128}$$

The arrow $\xleftarrow{R}$ means "randomly sampled from".

### Step 3: Alice computes the HMAC response

Alice receives $C_B$ and computes the HMAC using the shared key $K$:

$$
\text{Response} = HMAC(K, C_B)
$$

Where (using the standard HMAC construction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_0 &= K \text{ (padded/hashed to block length } B) \\
\text{ipad} &= \text{inner pad: 0x36 repeated } B \text{ times} \\
\text{opad} &= \text{outer pad: 0x5C repeated } B \text{ times} \\
HMAC(K, M) &= H \left( (K_0 \oplus opad) \parallel H \left( (K_0 \oplus ipad) \parallel M \right) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Bob verifies the response

Bob independently computes $HMAC(K, C_B)$ using his copy of $K$ and compares:

- If $HMAC_{Alice}(K, C_B) = HMAC_{Bob}(K, C_B)$ → **AUTHENTICATED** (within constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks).
- Else → **REJECTED**.

### Step 5: Security analysis

- **Replay attack defense:** $C_B$ is fresh and random for every session → an old captured response is useless.
- **Eve cannot forge:** Without $K$, Eve cannot compute a valid HMAC.
- **Mutual version:** Alice then issues her own challenge $C_A$ to Bob to authenticate Bob as well.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Scheme Note:** A complete answer on HMAC must include the inner/outer pad construction, the reason for two hash applications (to defeat length-extension attacks), and explicit mention of constant-time comparison.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Biometric Performance Metrics

**Problem (KTU 2022):** During testing of a fingerprint scanner, 1000 legitimate users attempted authentication. 920 were correctly accepted. Additionally, 500 impostors attempted, and 3 were incorrectly accepted. Compute FAR, FRR, and CER.

### Step 1: Define the test populations
- **Legitimate users:** $N_{legit} = 1000$ (true matches)
- **Impostor attempts:** $N_{imp} = 500$ (true non-matches)

### Step 2: Determine True Accepts / Rejects

- **True Accepts (TA):** Legitimately accepted = 920
- **False Rejects (FR):** Legitimately rejected = $1000 - 920 = 80$

### Step 3: Determine False Accepts / Rejects

- **False Accepts (FA):** Impostors wrongly accepted = 3
- **True Rejects (TR):** Impostors correctly rejected = $500 - 3 = 497$

### Step 4: Compute FAR (False Acceptance Rate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FAR &= \frac{FA}{N_{imp}} \\
FAR &= \frac{3}{500} \\
FAR &= 0.006 \\
FAR &= 0.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute FRR (False Rejection Rate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FRR &= \frac{FR}{N_{legit}} \\
FRR &= \frac{80}{1000} \\
FRR &= 0.08 \\
FRR &= 8.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation — CER
Since $FRR \gg FAR$, the threshold is set too *strict*. Adjusting the threshold shifts the FAR-FRR curve. **CER (Cross-over Error Rate, also called EER — Equal Error Rate)** is the threshold value where $FAR = FRR$. For this system, lowering strictness would lower FRR but raise FAR, and vice versa — an irreducible trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Time-Based One-Time Password (TOTP) Construction

The TOTP algorithm (RFC 6238) is the backbone of Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, and Aadhaar OTP.

### Step 1: Time step
Compute the number of time intervals since Unix epoch:

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{current} - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor
$$

Where:
- $T_0 = 0$ (Unix epoch)
- $X = 30$ seconds (time step)

### Step 2: HMAC computation

$$
\text{HMAC} = HMAC-SHA1(K, T)
$$

Where $K$ is the pre-shared secret key (encoded as base32).

### Step 3: Dynamic Truncation (DT)
Take the last 4 bits of HMAC as an offset $O$, then read 4 bytes starting at $O$, mask the MSB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= \text{HMAC}[19] \ \mathbf{AND} \ 0x0F \\
\text{Offset} &= \text{HMAC}[O:O+3] \\
\text{Offset} &= \text{Offset} \ \mathbf{AND} \ 0x7FFFFFFF
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Modulo to get OTP

$$
\text{OTP} = \text{Offset} \mod 10^6
$$

This gives a 6-digit decimal OTP valid for the current 30-second window.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiner's favorite sub-question: *"Why is TOTP not vulnerable to replay attack if the user types it within 30 seconds?"* — Answer: Because the server regenerates the same OTP only within that 30-second window AND only for that specific user (key $K$ is unique per user). Outside the window, OTP changes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Authentication Process Flow

The fundamental flow that occurs in every authentication system, regardless of method (password, biometric, token), is mapped below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Claims Identity - Username / UserID] --> B[Credential Collection Module]
    B --> C{Verify Credential Against Trusted Reference}
    C -->|Match Found| D[Generate Session Token / Ticket]
    C -->|No Match| E[Reject and Log Failure Attempt]
    D --> F[Grant Access - Authorized Session Established]
    E --> G[Counter Increment - Brute Force Defense]
    G --> H{Max Attempts Exceeded?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Lock Account / Alert Admin]
    H -->|No| B
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, every node ID is alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`) per Mermaid syntax rules. No reserved words like `end` or `graph` are used.

---

## 4.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Architecture Block Diagram

A professional-grade MFA system combines factors from at least two independent categories.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Login Attempt] --> B[Factor 1 - Knowledge - Password]
    B --> C{Factor 1 Valid?}
    C -->|No| D[Reject Authentication]
    C -->|Yes| E[Factor 2 - Possession - OTP via Mobile]
    E --> F{Factor 2 Valid?}
    F -->|No| D
    F -->|Yes| G[Factor 3 - Inherence - Biometric Optional]
    G --> H{All Factors Valid?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Session Established - MFA Success]
    H -->|No| D
```

---

## 4.3 Kerberos Authentication Protocol (V4/V5) — Detailed Block Flow

Kerberos is a **trusted third-party** authentication protocol using tickets and symmetric-key cryptography. It is heavily tested in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client C requests Ticket for Service S] --> B[Step 1: AS_REQ to Authentication Server AS]
    B --> C[AS issues Ticket Granting Ticket TGT encrypted with C Key]
    C --> D[Step 2: C presents TGT to Ticket Granting Server TGS]
    D --> E[Step 3: TGS issues Service Ticket ST encrypted with Service Key]
    E --> F[Step 4: C presents ST to Application Server S]
    F --> G[Step 5: S verifies ST and grants access to C]
    G --> H[Mutual Auth - C verifies S identity via timestamp]
```

**Explanation of Kerberos Components:**

| Component | Function | Key Used |
|---|---|---|
| **AS (Authentication Server)** | Initial identity verification | $K_C$ (Client's secret key) |
| **TGS (Ticket Granting Server)** | Issues service tickets | $K_{TGS}$ |
| **SS (Service Server)** | The actual resource being accessed | $K_S$ |
| **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** | Proof of initial authentication | — |
| **ST (Service Ticket)** | Authorizes access to specific service | — |
| **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** | Combined AS + TGS | — |

---

## 4.4 Challenge-Response Authentication with HMAC

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Alice Client
    participant Bob as Bob Server
    participant Eve as Eve Attacker

    Alice->>Bob: 1. Authentication Request
    Bob->>Alice: 2. Send Random Challenge C sub B
    Note right of Alice: Alice computes HMAC = HMAC SHA256 K, C sub B
    Alice->>Bob: 3. Send HMAC Response
    Note right of Bob: Bob independently computes HMAC and compares
    Bob->>Alice: 4. Authentication Success or Failure

    Note over Eve: Eve can see C sub B and the HMAC response
    Note over Eve: But cannot compute a new valid HMAC without K
```

**Key Insight:** Even though Eve sees the challenge $C_B$ and the response $HMAC(K, C_B)$, she cannot replay it for a *new* challenge $C_B'$ because she lacks $K$. Each session gets a fresh challenge, defeating replay attacks.

---

## 4.5 Biometric Authentication — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biometric Sample Captured - Fingerprint, Iris, Face] --> B[Preprocessing - Noise Removal, Normalization]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction - Minutiae, Texture Vectors]
    C --> D[Template Database Lookup]
    D --> E{Match Score vs Threshold}
    E -->|> Threshold| F[ACCEPT - User Authenticated]
    E -->|< Threshold| G[REJECT - Authentication Failed]
    F --> H[Log Authentication Event with FAR and FRR Stats]
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | PECST863 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Verifies the identity of a user | Determines what an authenticated user can do |
| **Question Answered** | "Who are you?" | "What are you allowed to do?" |
| **Order of Operation** | Occurs **first** in the access sequence | Occurs **after** authentication succeeds |
| **Mechanism** | Passwords, biometrics, tokens, OTP | Access control lists (ACL), RBAC, capabilities |
| **Output** | Boolean (accept/reject identity) | Set of permitted actions / resources |
| **Example** | User enters password to log in | User can read file-X but not delete file-Y |

**Real-world analogy:** Authentication is showing your **passport at airport check-in**. Authorization is your **boarding pass** specifying which flight class and seat you can occupy.

**Conclusion:** Authentication is a *prerequisite* for authorization. A system may authenticate a user but still deny all access if no authorization rules match.

*(Word count: ~120; perfect for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question 2: List and explain any three authentication factors with examples.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | PECST863 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The three classical authentication factors are:

**(i) Knowledge Factor (Something You Know):** This factor relies on information that only the legitimate user should possess.
- *Examples:* Passwords, PINs (Personal Identification Numbers), passphrase phrases, answers to security questions, graphical patterns on a smartphone unlock screen.

**(ii) Possession Factor (Something You Have):** This factor relies on a physical object or device in the user's custody.
- *Examples:* Hardware security tokens (RSA SecurID), smart cards, USB security keys (YubiKey), mobile phones receiving SMS-OTP or TOTP via Google Authenticator.

**(iii) Inherence Factor (Something You Are):** This factor relies on the unique biological characteristics of the user.
- *Examples:* Fingerprint recognition (used in Aadhaar, smartphones), iris scanning (used in Indian airports under DigiYatra), facial recognition (Face ID), voice authentication, palm-vein scanning.

**Conclusion:** Modern secure systems combine at least two of these factors in a **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** scheme to dramatically reduce the risk of credential compromise.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Module 1 carries **two full questions of 14 marks each** in the End Semester Exam. Students must answer **any ONE full question** out of the two given. Each full question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

## QUESTION A — OPTION 1 (14 Marks)

### (a) What is password salting? Why is it necessary? Explain with a worked example of how a salted password is stored. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | PECST863 | Part B Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Password salting is the cryptographic technique of appending (or prepending) a unique, randomly generated string — called a *salt* — to a user's password **before** hashing it, so that the resulting hash is unique even for identical passwords across different users.

**Why salting is necessary:**

1. **Defeats Rainbow Table Attacks:** A rainbow table is a precomputed database of hashes for common passwords. Without salt, if two users have the same password, their hashes are identical, making mass cracking trivial. With a unique salt per user, the attacker must compute a separate rainbow table per salt, which is computationally infeasible for large user databases.

2. **Identical Passwords Produce Different Hashes:** Even if User A and User B both choose `password123`, their stored hashes differ because their salts differ.

3. **Slows Down Bulk Cracking:** Each password now requires a unique brute-force run.

**Worked Example — Salting + Hashing (SHA-256):**

| Step | User Input | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Original password $P$ | `KtuStudent@2024` |
| 2 | Generate random salt | `a3F9b2K7` (16 hex chars = 64 bits) |
| 3 | Concatenate: $P \parallel \text{salt}$ | `KtuStudent@2024a3F9b2K7` |
| 4 | Hash the concatenation | $\text{SHA-256} = 8d3f2a...c91b$ (256 bits) |
| 5 | **Stored in database** | `salt` $\parallel$ `hash` = `a3F9b2K7` $\parallel$ `8d3f2a...c91b` |

**Verification during login:** When the user logs in, the system retrieves the stored salt, concatenates it with the entered password, hashes the result, and compares with the stored hash.

**Modern Recommendation:** KTU 2024 syllabus mandates use of **adaptive salts + slow KDFs** such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2id for password storage. The salt size should be at least **128 bits**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "salt is secret" — **WRONG**. The salt is stored in plaintext in the database. Its purpose is *uniqueness*, not *secrecy*. The hash function is one-way; the salt is not. This is a common 1-mark loss.

---

### (b) Explain the Kerberos Authentication Protocol with a neat diagram. List its advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | PECST863 | Part B Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Kerberos (named after the three-headed dog of Greek mythology) is a **trusted third-party authentication protocol** developed at MIT (Project Athena) and standardized in **RFC 4120**. It uses symmetric-key cryptography and tickets to authenticate users in an insecure network.

**Key Components:**

- **AS (Authentication Server):** Verifies the user initially.
- **TGS (Ticket Granting Server):** Issues service-specific tickets.
- **KDC (Key Distribution Center):** Logical combination of AS + TGS.
- **Principal:** Any user or service that can authenticate.
- **Realm:** Administrative domain of Kerberos.
- **Ticket:** A time-limited credential encrypted with a service's secret key.

**Protocol Steps (Refer to Section 4.3 Diagram):**

**Step 1: Initial Authentication (AS Exchange)**
- Client $C$ sends its identity to AS (in plaintext).
- AS returns: $\{K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_C} \parallel \{TGT\}_{K_{TGS}}$
  - Where $K_{C,TGS}$ = session key between C and TGS.
  - TGT = Ticket Granting Ticket = $\{C, \text{addr}_C, \text{timestamp}, \text{lifetime}, K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_{TGS}}$

**Step 2: Service Ticket Request (TGS Exchange)**
- $C$ sends an authenticator $\{C, \text{timestamp}\}_{K_{C,TGS}}$ along with the TGT to TGS, requesting access to service $S$.
- TGS verifies the authenticator, decrypts the TGT, and issues: $\{K_{C,S}\}_{K_{C,TGS}} \parallel \{S\text{-ticket}\}_{K_S}$
  - $S\text{-ticket} = \{C, \text{addr}_C, \text{timestamp}, \text{lifetime}, K_{C,S}\}_{K_S}$

**Step 3: Client-Server Authentication (CS Exchange)**
- $C$ sends $\{C, \text{timestamp}\}_{K_{C,S}}$ plus the service ticket to $S$.
- $S$ decrypts the ticket, verifies the authenticator, and (for mutual auth) returns $\{C, \text{timestamp}+1\}_{K_{C,S}}$.
- $C$ is now **authenticated** and has a **session key** $K_{C,S}$ for secure communication with $S$.

**Advantages:**
1. **Single Sign-On (SSO):** User authenticates once with AS, then accesses multiple services using TGT.
2. **Mutual Authentication:** Both client and server verify each other.
3. **Tickets are time-limited:** Reduces risk of replay.
4. **Secret keys never traverse the network:** Only encrypted tickets do.
5. **Scalable:** Works across large enterprise networks.

**Limitations:**
1. **Single point of failure:** If KDC goes down, no one can authenticate.
2. **Time synchronization required:** All hosts must be within ~5 minutes (clock skew) of each other.
3. **Vulnerable to offline dictionary attack** if a TGT or service ticket is captured and a weak password was used.
4. **Requires trusted, secure hosts:** KDC must be physically secure.
5. **Does not scale well to cross-realm trust** in highly federated environments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention **clock synchronization** as a critical Kerberos requirement. This costs 1–2 marks. Also, students often draw the TGT containing the session key, but actually the **TGT is encrypted with the TGS's secret key** — students forget this double-layer encryption.

---

## QUESTION B — OPTION 2 (14 Marks)

### (a) Explain three types of biometric authentication techniques. Discuss FAR, FRR, and CER with a suitable graph. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | PECST863 | Part B Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Biometric authentication verifies a user based on unique physiological or behavioral characteristics that are inherently tied to the individual.

**Three Biometric Techniques:**

**(i) Fingerprint Recognition**
- **Working Principle:** Captures the unique pattern of ridges and valleys (minutiae points) on a fingertip.
- **Stages:** Image acquisition → preprocessing → minutiae extraction → template matching.
- **Use Cases:** Aadhaar authentication, smartphone unlock, criminal identification (AFIS).
- **Accuracy:** FAR typically 0.001%, FRR ~1–2% in commercial sensors.

**(ii) Iris Recognition**
- **Working Principle:** The iris (colored ring around the pupil) has a unique, stable pattern formed before birth. Uses Daugman's algorithm with Gabor wavelets.
- **Stages:** NIR (near-infrared) image capture → segmentation → normalization → encoding (IrisCode, 2048 bits).
- **Use Cases:** Indian airports (DigiYatra), border control, high-security facilities.
- **Accuracy:** FAR ~0.0001%, FRR ~0.1% — among the most accurate biometrics.

**(iii) Facial Recognition**
- **Working Principle:** Analyzes geometric features (distance between eyes, nose shape) and texture patterns.
- **Stages:** Detection → alignment → feature extraction (CNN embeddings) → matching.
- **Use Cases:** Face ID (Apple), CCTV surveillance, social media tagging.
- **Accuracy:** Depends heavily on lighting, pose, occlusion. Modern deep learning models reach 99.5%+ on benchmark datasets.

**Performance Metrics:**

- **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Probability that an impostor is wrongly accepted.

$$FAR = \frac{\text{Number of False Accepts}}{\text{Total Impostor Attempts}}$$

- **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Probability that a legitimate user is wrongly rejected.

$$FRR = \frac{\text{Number of False Rejects}}{\text{Total Legitimate Attempts}}$$

- **CER (Cross-over Error Rate) / EER (Equal Error Rate):** The threshold value at which FAR = FRR. A **lower CER indicates a better biometric system**.

**FAR vs. FRR Trade-off Graph (Conceptual Block Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threshold t - Strict] --> B[FAR - LOW]
    A --> C[FRR - HIGH]
    D[Threshold t - Lenient] --> E[FAR - HIGH]
    D --> F[FRR - LOW]
    G[Threshold t - CER Point] --> H[FAR - EQUAL - FRR]
```

**Practical Implication:** A high-security facility (e.g., a military base) sets a *strict threshold* to minimize FAR (no impostors admitted), even at the cost of higher FRR (more legitimate users must retry). A consumer smartphone sets a *lenient threshold* to minimize FRR (user convenience), accepting slightly higher FAR.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse FAR and FRR. Remember: **FAR deals with impostors**, **FRR deals with genuine users**. A common mnemonic: "**F**alse **A**cceptance = **I**mpostor" and "**F**alse **R**ejection = **R**eal user."

---

### (b) Explain the Challenge-Response authentication mechanism using HMAC. Provide the protocol steps and a neat diagram. Why is it resistant to replay attacks? **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | PECST863 | Part B Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Challenge-Response is an authentication protocol in which the verifier issues an unpredictable challenge to the claimant, who must compute a correct response using a shared secret. **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** is the standard cryptographic primitive used to compute the response.

**Prerequisites:**
- Alice ($A$) and Bob ($B$) share a symmetric secret key $K$ (established via a secure out-of-band channel, e.g., Diffie-Hellman or pre-shared key).
- Both have access to a cryptographic hash function $H$ (e.g., SHA-256).
- Bob has a cryptographically secure random number generator (CSPRNG).

**Protocol Steps:**

**Step 1 — Request:** Alice sends an authentication request to Bob.

**Step 2 — Challenge Generation:** Bob generates a fresh random nonce $N_B$ (128 bits) and sends it to Alice:

$$N_B \xleftarrow{R} \{0,1\}^{128}$$

**Step 3 — Response Computation:** Alice computes the HMAC of the challenge using the shared key $K$:

$$R_A = HMAC(K, N_B)$$

This expands to:

$$
R_A = H\left( (K_0 \oplus opad) \parallel H\left( (K_0 \oplus ipad) \parallel N_B \right) \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Transmission:** Alice sends $R_A$ to Bob.

**Step 5 — Verification:** Bob independently computes $R_B = HMAC(K, N_B)$ using his copy of $K$ and compares with $R_A$ using **constant-time comparison** (to prevent timing side-channel attacks).

**Step 6 — Decision:**
- If $R_A = R_B$ → **Alice is authenticated**.
- Else → **Authentication fails**.

**Step 7 (Optional — Mutual Authentication):** Alice sends a challenge $N_A$ back to Bob, who must respond with $HMAC(K, N_A)$. This authenticates Bob to Alice as well.

**Diagram:** Refer to the Sequence Diagram in **Section 4.4** above.

**Why It Resists Replay Attacks:**

A replay attack is when an adversary **Eve** captures a valid authentication exchange and replays it later to impersonate the legitimate user. The challenge-response mechanism defeats this in three ways:

1. **Freshness via Nonce:** Every challenge $N_B$ is freshly generated from a CSPRNG. Even if Eve captures a valid response $R_A = HMAC(K, N_B)$, she cannot reuse it because Bob will issue a *new* challenge $N_B' \neq N_B$ in the next session. The old response becomes invalid.

2. **No Reusable Information:** The response $HMAC(K, N_B)$ is valid **only** for the specific challenge $N_B$. Without $K$, Eve cannot compute a valid response for the new challenge $N_B'$.

3. **Time-Bound Sessions:** Many implementations also add timestamps to the challenge, so a captured exchange is invalid after a short time window (typically 30–60 seconds).

**Security Properties:**

| Property | How It Is Achieved |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality of $K$** | $K$ never leaves Alice or Bob's machine |
| **Integrity of challenge** | HMAC binds $K$ and $N_B$ cryptographically |
| **Authentication of Alice** | Only Alice (who knows $K$) can produce a valid $HMAC(K, N_B)$ |
| **Replay resistance** | Fresh random nonce per session |
| **Forward secrecy (optional)** | Pair with Diffie-Hellman key exchange |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "challenge-response is secure because the password is not sent." This is incomplete. The correct answer is: **the secret is not sent**, AND **a fresh challenge is used per session**. Both conditions are required. Losing 1 mark for the second condition is common.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision card for KTU ESE Module 1 — Authentication**

- [x] **Authentication = verifying identity**; **Authorization = verifying permissions**. Authentication comes first.
- [x] **Three classical factors:** Knowledge (password), Possession (token), Inherence (biometric). Modern systems add Location, Time, Behavior.
- [x] **MFA** = 2+ factors from *different* categories. Two passwords is 2SV, not MFA.
- [x] **Password entropy** formula: $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$. A 6-character lowercase password has $\approx 28$ bits of entropy (very weak).
- [x] **Brute-force time** $T = S / (2R)$ — modern GPUs do $10^{10}$–$10^{12}$ hashes/sec on SHA-256.
- [x] **Salting** defeats rainbow tables; salt is stored in plaintext, must be **$\geq 128$ bits** and unique per user.
- [x] **Hashing for passwords:** Use **bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2id, PBKDF2**. **Never** use raw MD5 or SHA-1.
- [x] **HMAC** = $H((K_0 \oplus opad) \parallel H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$. Two hash applications defeat length-extension attacks.
- [x] **Challenge-Response** uses fresh random nonce per session → replay-resistant.
- [x] **TOTP** uses time step $X = 30$ seconds, computes $HMAC(K, T)$, dynamic truncation, modulo $10^6$ for 6 digits.
- [x] **Biometric metrics:** FAR (impostor accept), FRR (legit reject), CER (where FAR = FRR). Lower CER = better system.
- [x] **Kerberos** = trusted-third-party, uses TGT and service tickets, requires clock sync ($\pm 5$ min), enables SSO.
- [x] **Kerberos components:** AS, TGS, KDC, Principal, Realm, Ticket, Authenticator, Session Key.
- [x] **Replay attack** = capturing and re-sending a valid authentication exchange. Defeated by nonces, timestamps, and one-time tokens.
- [x] **Mutual authentication** = both client and server authenticate each other (Kerberos, TLS mutual auth, HMAC challenge-response both ways).
- [x] **Mutual authentication example:** In Kerberos, the server returns timestamp + 1 encrypted with $K_{C,S}$ to prove it knew the session key.
- [x] **Modern auth protocols:** OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, SAML, FIDO2/WebAuthn (passwordless, public-key cryptography-based).
- [x] **Zero-Knowledge Proof** = prover convinces verifier without revealing the secret itself (e.g., Fiat-Shamir, Schnorr, zk-SNARKs).
- [x] **Biometric example formulas to memorize:** FAR = FA / Impostor Attempts; FRR = FR / Legit Attempts.
- [x] **Single Sign-On (SSO):** One authentication grants access to multiple systems. Examples: Google Workspace SSO, Microsoft AD-Kerberos, SAML 2.0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Access Control

In the context of information security and the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST863)** syllabus, **Access Control** is formally defined as the selective restriction of access to a place, resource, or system. It is the **security discipline that allows, denies, or restricts access to a resource after the identity of the requesting principal has been authenticated.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Access Control is the process of granting or denying specific requests to:
> 1. Obtain and use information and related information processing services.
> 2. Enter specific physical facilities (e.g., a server room).
> 3. Perform specific actions on a system (read, write, execute, delete).

The discipline operates on three foundational pillars collectively known as the **A.A.A. Triad**:
- **Authentication** — Verifying *"Who are you?"* (e.g., password, biometrics).
- **Authorization** — Verifying *"What can you do?"* (e.g., read-only access).
- **Accountability** — Logging *"What did you do?"* (e.g., audit trails).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Key Card System

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine checking into a five-star hotel.
> - **Authentication:** The front desk verifies your **Government-Issued ID** (Passport/Aadhaar). You are who you claim to be.
> - **Authorization:** The receptionist issues a **Magnetic Key Card** programmed to open **only Room 704** and the **gym** between **6 AM – 10 PM**. It cannot open the penthouse suite or the staff-only kitchen.
> - **Accountability:** The hotel's IT system logs **every door the card swiped**, at what time, and for how long. If a theft occurs, the audit log identifies the cardholder.
>
> In this analogy, the **Key Card = Access Token**, the **Lock Mechanism = Access Control Mechanism**, and the **Hotel Database of Allowed Doors = Access Control Policy**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition | Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | An active entity requesting access (User, Process, Program) | Principal |
| **Object** | A passive resource being protected (File, Database, Server) | Target |
| **Reference Monitor** | An abstract, tamper-proof machine that mediates every access | Core TCB component |
| **Security Policy** | The set of rules stating which subjects can access which objects | High-level directive |
| **Trust Zone** | A logical boundary grouping subjects and objects with similar trust levels | Network segmentation |

## 1.4 The Fundamental Access Control Equation

At its mathematical core, every access decision can be reduced to a Boolean evaluation:

$$
\text{Access} = \begin{cases} \text{GRANT} & \text{if } (\text{Subject Authenticated} \land \text{Policy Permits}(S, O, A)) \\ \text{DENY} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Subject identity
- $O$ = Object being accessed
- $A$ = Action being attempted (Read/Write/Execute)

> [!TIP]
> **Default Principle in Security:** Always implement **Deny-by-Default** (also called *Whitelist* approach). If no explicit rule grants access, the answer is **NO**. This is the most common KTU exam trap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Access Control Models

The KTU 2024 PECST863 syllabus specifically targets the **Four Classical Models** of access control. These models differ primarily in **WHO defines the access policy**.

### 2.1.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
- **Authority to decide:** The **OWNER** of the resource.
- **Mechanism:** Access Control Lists (ACLs) stored with each object.
- **Classic Example:** UNIX file permissions (`rwx` for owner, group, others).
- **Strength:** Flexible, user-controlled.
- **Weakness:** Vulnerable to **Trojan Horse attacks** because users can inadvertently leak rights.

### 2.1.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
- **Authority to decide:** A **CENTRAL SECURITY ADMINISTRATOR** (system-enforced).
- **Mechanism:** Security Labels / Clearance Levels (e.g., *Top Secret, Secret, Confidential*).
- **Classic Example:** Military/government classified systems.
- **Strength:** Immune to Trojan Horses; highly secure.
- **Weakness:** Rigid; not scalable for large commercial enterprises.

### 2.1.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
- **Authority to decide:** The **SYSTEM ADMIN** assigns roles; users inherit permissions.
- **Mechanism:** Users → Roles → Permissions.
- **Classic Example:** Hospital — roles of *Doctor, Nurse, Receptionist, Janitor*.
- **Strength:** Scalable, principle of least privilege, easier auditing.
- **Weakness:** Role explosion in very large systems.

### 2.1.4 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
- **Authority to decide:** A **Policy Engine** evaluates attributes of subject, object, and environment dynamically.
- **Mechanism:** Boolean logic rules (e.g., `IF user.department == "Finance" AND time > 9AM AND ip_address == internal`).
- **Strength:** Most granular; context-aware.
- **Weakness:** Computationally complex; policy debugging is difficult.

## 2.2 The Reference Monitor Concept (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975)

The **Reference Monitor** is the abstract machine that mediates every access. It must satisfy three absolute properties for a system to be considered **secure**:

1. **Tamper-Proof (Complete Mediation):** It cannot be modified by malicious code or users.
2. **Non-Bypassable:** Every access must pass through it — no "back doors."
3. **Verifiable:** The implementation must be small enough to be mathematically proven correct (this is the **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** principle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A system is **secure if and only if** the reference monitor is tamper-proof, non-bypassable, and small enough to be verified. Any violation = **untrusted system**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access Decision | $\text{Access} = \text{Auth}(S) \land \text{Policy}(S,O,A)$ | $S$=Subject, $O$=Object, $A$=Action | Boolean evaluation |
| Bell-LaPadula (NRU) | $L(S) \geq L(O)$ | $L$ = Security Level | No Read Up — confidentiality |
| Bell-LaPadula (NWD) | $L(S) \leq L(O)$ | $L$ = Security Level | No Write Down — confidentiality |
| Biba Model (NRD) | $L(S) \leq L(O)$ | $L$ = Integrity Level | No Read Down — integrity |
| Biba Model (NWU) | $L(S) \geq L(O)$ | $L$ = Integrity Level | No Write Up — integrity |
| Clark-Wilson | $\text{TP} \rightarrow \text{CDV} \rightarrow \text{IVP}$ | TP=Transformation Procedure, CDV=Consistency, IVP=Integrity Verification | Commercial integrity |
| RBAC Permission | $P(u) = \bigcup_{r \in \text{Roles}(u)} P(r)$ | $P$=Permissions, $r$=Role, $u$=User | Role inheritance |
| ABAC Decision | $D = f(S_{attr}, O_{attr}, E_{attr})$ | $S_{attr}$=Subject attributes, $O_{attr}$=Object attributes, $E_{attr}$=Environment | Context-aware policy |
| Access Matrix Size | $\vert S \vert \times \vert O \vert$ | $S$=Subjects, $O$=Objects | Theoretical complexity |
| State Transition | $S_{n+1} = \delta(S_n, R)$ | $S$=State, $R$=Request, $\delta$=Function | HRU Model (Harrison-Ruzzo-Ullman) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rule Applied:** Notice the use of `\vert` instead of literal `|` pipe characters inside table cells to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Cloud Computing (AWS IAM)** | RBAC + ABAC hybrid for resource access |
| **Banking & Finance** | MAC + Dual Control (e.g., vault requires two keys) |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | RBAC for Electronic Health Records (EHR) access |
| **Operating Systems** | DAC (Windows NTFS ACLs) + MAC (SELinux, AppArmor) |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Capability-based access (e.g., Android permissions) |
| **Database Systems** | DAC + Views + Stored Procedure encapsulation |

## 2.5 The Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most tested concept in KTU Part A questions.**

**Definition:** Every subject (user, program, process) must be able to access only the **minimum necessary information and resources** needed to perform its authorized task.

**Implementation Levels:**
1. **Identity-Level:** Each user gets unique credentials.
2. **Process-Level:** A web server runs as `www-data`, never as `root`.
3. **Time-Level:** Temporary elevation (e.g., `sudo` for 5 minutes).
4. **Need-to-Know Level:** Information compartmentalization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Access Control Matrix (Lampson's Model, 1971)

The **Access Control Matrix (ACM)** is the foundational mathematical model for all access control systems. We will derive it step-by-step from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Universe of Discourse
Let:
- $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_m\}$ be the finite set of all **Subjects** in the system.
- $O = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n\}$ be the finite set of all **Objects** (including subjects, since subjects are also objects of control).
- $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4\} = \{\text{read, write, execute, own}\}$ be the finite set of **Access Rights**.

### Step 2: Formalize the Matrix
The Access Control Matrix $M$ is a two-dimensional array of size $m \times n$ where each entry $M[s_i, o_j] \subseteq A$ denotes the set of rights that subject $s_i$ has over object $o_j$.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} & \cdots & m_{1n} \\ m_{21} & m_{22} & \cdots & m_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ m_{m1} & m_{m2} & \cdots & m_{mn} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example — University Database System
Consider a university with the following entities:

**Subjects:**
- $s_1$ = Professor
- $s_2$ = Student
- $s_3$ = Registrar

**Objects:**
- $o_1$ = Student Grades Database
- $o_2$ = Personal Student Record
- $o_3$ = Course Material

**Access Rights:** R = Read, W = Write, X = Execute, O = Own

**Step 3a:** Assign rights to Professor ($s_1$):
- $M[s_1, o_1] = \{R, W\}$ — can read and write grades
- $M[s_1, o_2] = \{R\}$ — can read student records
- $M[s_1, o_3] = \{R, W, X\}$ — owns the course material

**Step 3b:** Assign rights to Student ($s_2$):
- $M[s_2, o_1] = \{R\}$ — can read own grades
- $M[s_2, o_2] = \{R\}$ — can read own personal record
- $M[s_2, o_3] = \{R, X\}$ — can read and execute course material

**Step 3c:** Assign rights to Registrar ($s_3$):
- $M[s_3, o_1] = \{R, W, O\}$ — owns the grades database
- $M[s_3, o_2] = \{R, W, O\}$ — owns personal records
- $M[s_3, o_3] = \{R\}$ — read-only

### Step 4: Construct the Final Matrix

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix}
\{R,W\} & \{R\} & \{R,W,X\} \\
\{R\} & \{R\} & \{R,X\} \\
\{R,W,O\} & \{R,W,O\} & \{R\}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Identify the Practical Problem
The matrix has $\vert S \vert \times \vert O \vert$ entries. In a real system:
- $\vert S \vert \approx 10^4$ users
- $\vert O \vert \approx 10^6$ files
- Total cells $\approx 10^{10}$ — **impossible to store naively**

This leads to the two **practical storage optimizations** (which are KTU favorites):
- **Decomposition by Column → Access Control Lists (ACLs):** Store a list per object.
- **Decomposition by Row → Capability Lists:** Store a list per subject.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Confidentiality Model

The **Bell-LaPadula Model (1973)** is the formal MAC model used in military security. We will derive the two core properties from the system's threat model.

### Step 1: Define the Security Lattice
Let $\lambda : S \cup O \rightarrow L$ be a function mapping every subject and object to a security level. The standard military lattice is:

$$
L = \{\text{Unclassified} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{Top Secret}\}
$$

### Step 2: State the Confidentiality Requirement
**Confidentiality** = Information must not flow from a higher level to a lower level. Formally, the "no information leak downward" condition.

### Step 3: Derive the *Simple Security Property* (No Read Up)
Suppose subject $s$ at level $\lambda(s)$ attempts to **read** object $o$ at level $\lambda(o)$. If $\lambda(s) < \lambda(o)$, then $s$ would learn classified information without clearance. Therefore:

$$
\text{Read allowed} \iff \lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)
$$

This is the **No Read Up (NRU)** property, also called the *ss-property*.

### Step 4: Derive the *Star Property* (No Write Down)
Suppose subject $s$ at level $\lambda(s)$ attempts to **write** to object $o$ at level $\lambda(o)$. If $\lambda(s) > \lambda(o)$, then $s$ could *leak* high-level data into a low-level object, bypassing the security lattice. Therefore:

$$
\text{Write allowed} \iff \lambda(s) \leq \lambda(o)
$$

This is the **No Write Down (NWD)** property, also called the *-property*.

### Step 5: Identify the Tranquility Principle
The tranquility principle states: the security level of an object **cannot be changed while it is being referenced** (*strong tranquility*) or **may be changed only in restricted ways** (*weak tranquility*). Without tranquility, a subject could declassify a document mid-read and violate confidentiality.

## 3.3 Derivation: Biba Integrity Model (Counterpart to BLP)

The **Biba Model (1977)** addresses **integrity** (preventing unauthorized modification), not confidentiality. It mirrors BLP but inverts the direction.

### Step 1: Integrity Lattice
Let $\omega : S \cup O \rightarrow I$ where $I = \{\text{Untrusted} < \text{Crucial} < \text{Critical} < \text{Vital}\}$.

### Step 2: Derive *No Read Down* (NRD)
If $\omega(s) > \omega(o)$ and $s$ reads $o$, then low-integrity data contaminates high-integrity subject. Therefore:

$$
\text{Read allowed} \iff \omega(s) \leq \omega(o)
$$

### Step 3: Derive *No Write Up* (NWU)
If $\omega(s) < \omega(o)$ and $s$ writes to $o$, then low-integrity subject corrupts high-integrity object. Therefore:

$$
\text{Write allowed} \iff \omega(s) \geq \omega(o)
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Bell-LaPadula | Biba | Clark-Wilson | RBAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
