| **Goal** | Confidentiality | Integrity | Commercial Integrity | Access Management |
| **Core Rule** | No Read Up, No Write Down | No Read Down, No Write Up | Well-formed transactions, Separation of Duties | Role hierarchy |
| **Authority** | System (MAC) | System (MAC) | Auditor + System | Admin |
| **Key Element** | Security Labels | Integrity Levels | CDI / UDI / IVP | Roles |
| **Threat Protected** | Information disclosure | Unauthorized modification | Fraud, error | Privilege sprawl |
| **State Type** | State machine | State machine | State machine + Certifier | Static assignment |
| **Year Proposed** | 1973 | 1977 | 1987 | 1992 (Ferraiolo-Kuhn) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question is *"Why is BLP not sufficient for banking?"* — Answer: BLP protects confidentiality but **does not prevent internal fraud** (e.g., a cleared employee modifying balances). Clark-Wilson is the correct choice for banking/financial integrity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Access Control Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** maps the complete interaction between all major components of a typical access control system, from user request to audit log generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    user1["USER PRINCIPAL"] --> authA["AUTHENTICATION MODULE"]
    authA -->|"Verify Credentials"| credDB["CREDENTIAL DATABASE"]
    credDB -->|"Identity Confirmed"| tokenA["SESSION TOKEN ISSUED"]
    
    tokenA --> reqA["ACCESS REQUEST MEDIATOR"]
    obj1["PROTECTED OBJECT FILE DB SERVICE"] --> reqA
    
    reqA --> refMon["REFERENCE MONITOR TCB"]
    
    refMon --> policyA["POLICY DECISION POINT PDP"]
    refMon --> policyInfo["POLICY INFORMATION POINT PIP"]
    refMon --> policyEnf["POLICY ENFORCEMENT POINT PEP"]
    
    policyInfo --> attrDB["ATTRIBUTE DATABASE ROLES GROUPS"]
    
    policyA -->|"DECISION"| refMon
    refMon -->|"GRANT"| enforceA["ACCESS ENFORCEMENT LAYER"]
    refMon -->|"DENY"| denyA["DENY RESPONSE WITH REASON"]
    
    enforceA --> resA["RESOURCE ACCESS GRANTED"]
    denyA --> logA["AUDIT LOG WRITER"]
    enforceA --> logA
    
    logA --> storageA["SIEM SECURITY LOG REPOSITORY"]
    
    style refMon fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style policyA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style denyA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style logA fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The **Reference Monitor** is the *heart* of the system (highlighted in yellow).
- The **PEP** sits inline and enforces; the **PDP** decides.
- The **Audit Log Writer** is reached in BOTH grant and deny paths — accountability is unconditional.

## 4.2 The Lattice of Security Levels (BLP / Biba)

This Mermaid diagram visualizes the **partial ordering** of security levels and the allowed information flow directions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4 TOP SECRET"] --> L3["LEVEL 3 SECRET"]
    L3 --> L2["LEVEL 2 CONFIDENTIAL"]
    L2 --> L1["LEVEL 1 UNCLASSIFIED"]
    
    BLP1["BLP NO READ UP ALLOWED DOWNWARD"] -.-> L4
    BLP2["BLP NO WRITE DOWN ALLOWED UPWARD"] -.-> L1
    
    Biba1["BIBA NO READ DOWN ALLOWED UPWARD"] -.-> L4
    Biba2["BIBA NO WRITE UP ALLOWED DOWNWARD"] -.-> L1
    
    style L4 fill:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style L3 fill:#B22222,color:#FFFFFF
    style L2 fill:#CD5C5C,color:#FFFFFF
    style L1 fill:#F08080,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 RBAC Role Hierarchy Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the **Role-Based Access Control** inheritance structure as a tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    superRole["SUPERUSER SYSADMIN"] --> role1["NETWORK ADMIN"]
    superRole --> role2["DATABASE ADMIN"]
    superRole --> role3["APPLICATION ADMIN"]
    
    role1 --> perm1["PERMISSION ROUTER CONFIG"]
    role1 --> perm2["PERMISSION FIREWALL RULES"]
    
    role2 --> perm3["PERMISSION TABLE CREATE"]
    role2 --> perm4["PERMISSION BACKUP RESTORE"]
    
    role3 --> perm5["PERMISSION DEPLOY CODE"]
    role3 --> perm6["PERMISSION LOG READ ONLY"]
    
    user1["USER ALICE"] --> role1
    user2["USER BOB"] --> role2
    user3["USER CAROL"] --> role3
    user4["USER DAVE"] --> role1
    user4 --> role2
    
    style superRole fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style user1 fill:#87CEEB
    style user2 fill:#87CEEB
    style user3 fill:#87CEEB
    style user4 fill:#9370DB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** User Dave has **multiple roles** (Network Admin + Database Admin), inheriting the union of permissions. This is **role-based access control with role separation** — a common KTU 14-mark question scenario.

## 4.4 Access Control Decision State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> AUTHENTICATING : Request Received
    AUTHENTICATING --> AUTHENTICATED : Credentials Valid
    AUTHENTICATING --> FAILED : Invalid Credentials
    FAILED --> IDLE : After 3 Attempts LOCKOUT
    AUTHENTICATED --> AUTHORIZING : Token Issued
    AUTHORIZING --> GRANTED : Policy Permits
    AUTHORIZING --> DENIED : Policy Denies
    GRANTED --> LOGGING : Action Performed
    DENIED --> LOGGING : Denial Recorded
    LOGGING --> IDLE : Audit Saved
    LOGGING --> [*] : Session Timeout
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice that **EVERY terminal state (Granted or Denied) passes through LOGGING**. This enforces the **Accountability** property of the A.A.A. Triad.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC). Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | DAC | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Policy Authority** | Resource **owner** decides | **Central administrator/system** decides |
| **Mechanism** | Access Control Lists (ACLs) | Security labels and clearance levels |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low (rigid) |
| **Trojan Resistance** | Vulnerable | Resistant |
| **Real-World Example** | UNIX file permissions (`rwx` for user/group/other) | SELinux in Linux, Military classified systems |

> *(Allocation: 1 mark for each DAC point + 1 mark for example, or 1.5 marks for difference table + 1.5 for examples.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the principle of "Least Privilege" with an example. Why is it considered a cornerstone of access control design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** states that every user, program, or process should have **only the minimum privileges necessary** to perform its intended function — and **nothing more**.

**Example:** A web server process should run as a low-privilege user `www-data` (UID 33) rather than `root` (UID 0). If the web server is compromised via a buffer overflow, the attacker inherits only the limited `www-data` privileges and cannot, for example, rewrite the system password file.

**Why it is a cornerstone:**
- **Limits blast radius** of a security breach.
- **Reduces accidental misuse** by authorized users.
- **Simplifies auditing** (fewer permissions = clearer logs).
- **Mitigates Trojan Horse** propagation.

> *(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark significance.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Access Control Matrix** model. Construct the matrix for a hospital system with subjects {Doctor, Nurse, Receptionist} and objects {PatientRecord, MedicineDB, BillingSystem} using rights {R, W, X}. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **practical limitations** of the Access Control Matrix and explain how **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** and **Capability Lists** solve them with examples. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q3A:**

**Part (a) — Access Control Matrix for Hospital (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Access Control Matrix, proposed by **Butler Lampson (1971)**, is a two-dimensional table that specifies, for each subject in the system, the access rights it holds over each object. Subjects are the rows; Objects are the columns; Cells contain subsets of allowed access rights.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

$$
M_{\text{hospital}} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & \text{PatientRecord} & \text{MedicineDB} & \text{BillingSystem} \\
\text{Doctor} & \{R, W\} & \{R, W, X\} & \{R\} \\
\text{Nurse} & \{R, W\} & \{R\} & \{\emptyset\} \\
\text{Receptionist} & \{R\} & \{\emptyset\} & \{R, W\}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation of Cell Values (3 marks):**
- **Doctor** can read and write patient records, has full access to the medicine database (to prescribe), and read-only on billing.
- **Nurse** can read/write patient records (to update vitals), read the medicine database, but no access to billing.
- **Receptionist** can only read patient records (for verification) and handle billing transactions, but no access to medicine.

**Final Matrix Construction (1 mark):** The above $3 \times 3$ matrix with 9 non-empty cells fully specifies the access policy.

---

**Part (b) — Limitations and Solutions (7 marks):**

**Limitations of ACM (3 marks):**
1. **Space Complexity:** The matrix is $\vert S \vert \times \vert O \vert$. For a system with $10^4$ users and $10^6$ files, the matrix contains $10^{10}$ cells — impossible to store.
2. **Sparse Nature:** Most cells are empty (no rights). Storing empty cells wastes enormous memory.
3. **Dynamic Updates:** Adding/removing users requires modifying many cells; no efficient indexing.

**Solution 1 — Access Control Lists (ACLs) (2 marks):**
- **Approach:** Store a column of the matrix with each object.
- **Format:** `object_name: [ (subject, rights), ... ]`
- **Example for PatientRecord:** `{ (Doctor, {R,W}), (Nurse, {R,W}), (Receptionist, {R}) }`
- **Advantage:** Easy to revoke access to an object; efficient for object-centric queries.

**Solution 2 — Capability Lists (2 marks):**
- **Approach:** Store a row of the matrix with each subject.
- **Format:** `subject_name: [ (object, rights), ... ]`
- **Example for Doctor:** `{ (PatientRecord, {R,W}), (MedicineDB, {R,W,X}), (BillingSystem, {R}) }`
- **Advantage:** Easy to determine what a subject can do; supports delegation (passing capabilities).

**Comparison (Bonus 1 mark):** ACLs are object-centric (good for *resource protection*); Capabilities are subject-centric (good for *least privilege* and distributed systems).

---

### **Q3B. [ALTERNATIVE — KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model** in detail. Derive the **Simple Security Property** and the **Star Property** with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Biba Model** and compare it with Bell-LaPadula. Why is BLP unsuitable for banking applications, and which model is preferred? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q3B:**

**Part (a) — Bell-LaPadula Model (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Proposed by **David Bell and Leonard LaPadula (1973)** for the U.S. Department of Defense, BLP is a formal **state-machine model** enforcing **confidentiality** in MAC systems.

**Components (2 marks):**
- A set of **subjects** $S$ and **objects** $O$.
- A **lattice of security levels** $L$ (e.g., Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret).
- A **current access set** $b$ (matrix of current active accesses).
- A **state transition function** $\delta$ governing how the state evolves.

**Simple Security Property (No Read Up) (2 marks):**
- **Statement:** A subject $s$ can read object $o$ *if and only if* $\lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)$.
- **Example:** A *Secret*-cleared analyst **cannot read** a *Top Secret* report. A *Top Secret*-cleared general **can read** all lower levels.

**Star Property (No Write Down) (2 marks):**
- **Statement:** A subject $s$ can write to object $o$ *if and only if* $\lambda(s) \leq \lambda(o)$.
- **Example:** A *Top Secret* officer can write to *Top Secret* files but **cannot write** a *Secret* memo (this would be a covert channel for information leakage).
- **Combined Effect:** Information can flow **horizontally** or **upward**, but never **downward**, ensuring confidentiality.

---

**Part (b) — Biba Model & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Biba Model (3 marks):**
- **Goal:** Integrity (preventing unauthorized modification), not confidentiality.
- **Rules:**
  - **No Read Down (NRD):** $\omega(s) \leq \omega(o)$ — protects system from low-integrity input.
  - **No Write Up (NWU):** $\omega(s) \geq \omega(o)$ — protects high-integrity objects from low-integrity subjects.
- **Example:** A *Critical* system (e.g., air traffic control) should not accept writes from an *Untrusted* source (e.g., a public web form).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Bell-LaPadula | Biba |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| Direction of Protection | Information flow downward blocked | Information flow upward blocked |
| Read Rule | No Read Up | No Read Down |
| Write Rule | No Write Down | No Write Up |
| Domain | Military | Commercial / Critical systems |

**Why BLP is Unsuitable for Banking (1 mark):**
- BLP prevents reading classified data without clearance, but it **does not prevent an authorized, cleared employee from modifying records fraudulently** (e.g., altering a customer's account balance). Banking requires **integrity** and **separation of duties**, not just confidentiality.

**Preferred Model for Banking (1 mark):**
- The **Clark-Wilson Model (1987)** is preferred. It enforces:
  - **Well-formed transactions** (all modifications follow certified procedures).
  - **Separation of Duties** (no single user can both initiate and authorize a transaction).
  - **CDI/UDI** (Constrained Data Items / Unconstrained Data Items) with **IVP** (Integrity Verification Procedures).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Access Control Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing BLP and Biba directions:** Many students write "No Read Up" for Biba. Remember: **BLP protects confidentiality (stops leaks DOWN)**, **Biba protects integrity (stops contamination UP)**.
> 2. **Mixing DAC with MAC examples:** UNIX is DAC; SELinux is MAC. Do not call UNIX a MAC system.
> 3. **Forgetting the Tranquility Principle:** When describing BLP, always mention *tranquility* (strong or weak) — it is the answer to *"How does BLP prevent the dynamic declassification attack?"*
> 4. **Missing the third pillar:** When asked about A.A.A., students often omit **Accountability (Audit Logs)**. The three are non-negotiable.
> 5. **Spelling:** "Bell-LaPadula" is often misspelled as "Bell-Lapudula" or "Bell-Lapadula". Use **LaPadula** (capital L, capital P).
> 6. **RBAC Role Explosion:** In 14-mark answers, mention that RBAC can suffer from *role explosion* in mega-corporations and contrast with ABAC.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Access Control (Module 1)**

- [x] **A.A.A. Triad:** Authentication → Authorization → Accountability (must be in order).
- [x] **Reference Monitor:** Tamper-proof, Non-bypassable, Verifiable (the 3 TCB conditions).
- [x] **Four Models:** DAC (owner), MAC (system), RBAC (roles), ABAC (attributes).
- [x] **DAC Weakness:** Vulnerable to **Trojan Horse** attacks.
- [x] **MAC Strength:** System-enforced, immune to Trojans, but rigid.
- [x] **RBAC Formula:** Permissions = Union of permissions of assigned roles.
- [x] **ABAC Strength:** Context-aware, supports time/IP/location-based decisions.
- [x] **Bell-LaPadula (BLP):** *No Read Up* + *No Write Down* = **Confidentiality**.
- [x] **Biba Model:** *No Read Down* + *No Write Up* = **Integrity**.
- [x] **Clark-Wilson:** Banking standard — *Well-formed transactions* + *Separation of Duties*.
- [x] **Access Matrix:** Size = $\vert S \vert \times \vert O \vert$; decomposed into **ACLs** (column-wise) or **Capabilities** (row-wise).
- [x] **Least Privilege:** Minimum necessary rights — cornerstone of secure design.
- [x] **Tranquility:** Security labels cannot change dynamically (strong) or can change in restricted ways (weak).
- [x] **Default Rule:** **Deny-by-Default** (whitelist over blacklist).
- [x] **HRU Model:** Harrison-Ruzzo-Ullman — proves safety of ACM is *undecidable* in general (advanced topic, 14-mark bonus).
- [x] **Year Reminders:** Lampson 1971 (ACM), Bell-LaPadula 1973, Biba 1977, Clark-Wilson 1987, RBAC 1992.

> **End of Module 1 — Access Control Notes (PECST863, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Security: Browser Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Web Security: Browser Attacks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Browser Attacks** are a class of cyberattacks that target vulnerabilities in web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, etc.) or their associated components (extensions, plugins, JavaScript engines, rendering engines) to compromise user confidentiality, integrity, and availability. These attacks exploit the browser's role as the primary gateway between users and the internet, leveraging client-side execution environments to deliver malicious payloads such as cross-site scripting (XSS), cross-site request forgery (CSRF), drive-by downloads, clickjacking, and man-in-the-browser exploits. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST863 – Topics in Security, Module 1)**, browser attacks are categorized under **client-side threats**, where the victim is the end-user's browser session rather than the web server itself. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Distinction (Board-Exam Favorite)** > - **Web Server Attack:** Targets the server (e.g., SQL Injection, DDoS on Apache). > - **Browser Attack:** Targets the *client-side* execution environment within the user's browser. > - **Web Application Attack:** May target both layers (e.g., XSS is a web app vuln that materializes as a browser attack). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Reception Analogy Imagine a **5-star hotel** where the *reception desk* is the **web browser** and the *guests* are *users*: - The **receptionist (browser)** accepts **letters (web requests)** from various **delivery persons (websites)** and hands them to the guest. - A **Browser Attack** is like a *malicious delivery person* slipping a **poisoned letter (malicious script)** that: - Tricks the receptionist into forwarding harmful content (XSS), - Forges the guest's signature on a fake checkout form (CSRF), - Slips a **fake menu over the real menu** to make the guest "sign" something else (Clickjacking), - Or installs a **wiretap on the reception phone** to eavesdrop on every conversation (Man-in-the-Browser). The receptionist (browser) is **trusted** to handle everything — which is exactly why attackers target it. --- ### 1.3 Why Browsers Are High-Value Targets | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Universal Trust** | Browsers are implicitly trusted by users to render safe content. | | **Rich Execution Environment** | JavaScript, HTML5, WebAssembly, and DOM APIs provide powerful execution capabilities. | | **Stored Sensitive Data** | Cookies, saved passwords, autofill data, session tokens. | | **Cross-Origin Complexity** | Browsers handle thousands of origins simultaneously. | | **Extension Ecosystem** | Third-party extensions often have elevated privileges. | | **Network Position** | Browser sees all user traffic, making it a perfect MitM pivot point. | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Highlighted in KTU Syllabus:** The **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** lists **A03:2021 – Injection** and **A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration** as primary enablers of browser-side attacks. **XSS** is the most prevalent client-side injection attack reported in KTU reference material. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Browser Attacks Browser attacks can be classified along **three axes**: attack vector, target layer, and persistence. #### A. Injection-Based Attacks 1. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — Injecting malicious JavaScript into a trusted website. 2. **HTML Injection** — Injecting arbitrary HTML tags. 3. **JavaScript Injection** — Direct JS payload execution in the DOM. #### B. Request Forgery Attacks 4. **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** — Forcing authenticated users to submit unwanted actions. 5. **Clickjacking (UI Redress)** — Tricking users into clicking hidden elements. #### C. Man-in-the-Middle (Browser Variant) 6. **Man-in-the-Browser (MitB)** — Trojan infects the browser to modify transactions. 7. **SSL Stripping** — Downgrading HTTPS to HTTP. #### D. Exploit-Based Attacks 8. **Drive-by Downloads** — Silent malware installation via visiting a page. 9. **Browser Exploit Kits (BEKs)** — Angler, Magnitude, Rig exploit browser bugs. 10. **Zero-Day Exploits** — Targeting unpatched browser engine vulnerabilities. #### E. Component-Based Attacks 11. **Malicious Browser Extensions** — Extensions with excessive permissions. 12. **Plugin Vulnerabilities** — Flash, Java, PDF reader exploits (legacy). #### F. Session & Credential Attacks 13. **Session Hijacking** — Stealing session cookies via XSS. 14. **Cookie Theft / Session Fixation** — Manipulating session identifiers. 15. **Reverse Tabnabbing** — Exploiting `target="_blank"` links. #### G. Social Engineering in Browser Context 16. **Phishing** — Fake login pages. 17. **Pharming** — DNS poisoning redirecting to malicious pages. --- ### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The Top 5 Board-Relevant Browser Attacks #### **Attack 1: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** **The Why:** Web applications reflect user input into HTML without sanitization. **The How — Three Variants:** | Variant | Mechanism | Storage | |---|---|---| | **Stored XSS** | Malicious script saved on server (e.g., comment field), served to all visitors. | Persistent in database | | **Reflected XSS** | Payload in URL/POST body, immediately reflected back in response. | Non-persistent | | **DOM-based XSS** | Payload executed via client-side JavaScript modifying the DOM. | Client-side only | **Operational Steps:** 1. Attacker identifies input field that lacks output sanitization. 2. Injects ``. 3. Victim loads the page; malicious JS executes in the **origin context** of the trusted site. 4. Session cookie exfiltrated → account takeover. --- #### **Attack 2: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** **The Why:** Browsers automatically attach session cookies with every request to a domain. **The How — Operational Steps:** 1. Victim is logged into `bank.com` (session cookie active). 2. Victim visits `attacker.com` containing: ```html ``` 3. Browser sends the request to `bank.com` **with cookies attached**. 4. Bank validates the cookie, executes the transfer. **Mitigation:** **Synchronizer Token Pattern**, **SameSite cookies**, **Origin header validation**. --- #### **Attack 3: Clickjacking (UI Redress)** **The How:** 1. Attacker embeds target site in a transparent ` ``` **Step 3 — Layer Alignment** The transparent iframe overlays the entire viewport. The decoy button sits precisely over the "Delete Account" button in the iframe. **Step 4 — User Action** Victim clicks "Claim Prize" → click event is delivered to the iframe's "Delete Account" button. **Step 5 — Action Execution** Browser executes the click in the context of `target.com`. The action is performed **as the victim**, who is logged in. **Countermeasure Logic:** $$ \text{Clickjacking Blocked} \iff \text{HTTP Response} \ni (\text{X-Frame-Options: DENY} \;\lor\; \text{CSP: frame-ancestors 'none'}) $$ If the target site refuses to be framed, the browser blocks the iframe rendering. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Man-in-the-Browser (MitB) Operational Chain **Step 1 — Infection Vector** User downloads a "free screen saver" containing a trojan (e.g., **Zeus, SpyEye, Carberp**). **Step 2 — Browser Hooking** The trojan injects malicious code into the browser process: $$ \text{Process}_{\text{browser.exe}} \leftarrow \text{Inject}(\text{malicious.dll}) $$ **Step 3 — API Hooking** The DLL hooks critical browser APIs, e.g., `HTMLDocument.write`, `XMLHTTPRequest.send`, form submission handlers. **Step 4 — Transaction Interception** User logs into `bank.com`, navigates to transfer page, fills in payee "Alice" and amount "100". **Step 5 — In-Transit Modification** The hooked code modifies the **HTTP request body** *after* JavaScript validation but *before* network dispatch: $$ \text{Original: } \{\text{payee=Alice}, \text{amount=100}\} $$ $$ \text{Modified: } \{\text{payee=Attacker}, \text{amount=10000}\} $$ **Step 6 — Visual Deception** The browser DOM is also patched so the **user still sees "Alice / 100"** on screen, while the network layer sends "Attacker / 10000". **Step 7 — Bank Processing** Bank receives modified request; transaction settles to attacker's account. **Why MitB Defeats SSL/TLS:** $$ \text{SSL/TLS} = \text{Protection}(\text{Link}_{\text{user} \leftrightarrow \text{bank}}) $$ $$ \text{MitB} = \text{Attack}(\text{Endpoint}_{\text{user}}) $$ $$ \therefore \; \text{SSL/TLS} \cap \text{MitB} = \emptyset \quad \text{(no protection overlap)} $$ SSL protects the wire; MitB operates **above** the wire, inside the user's machine. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Attack Defense Mapping (Comprehensive) | **Attack Class** | **Browser Defense** | **Server Defense** | **User Defense** | **Standards** | |---|---|---|---|---| | XSS | XSS Auditor (legacy), CSP | Input validation, output encoding, templating engines | NoScript, disable JS | OWASP ASVS V5 | | CSRF | SameSite cookies, Fetch metadata | Synchronizer tokens, double-submit cookie | Avoid suspicious sites | RFC 6265, OWASP CSRFGuard | | Clickjacking | X-Frame-Options enforcement | CSP frame-ancestors | Browser extensions (NoScript) | RFC 7034 | | MitB | Process sandboxing, anti-malware | Transaction signing, OTP confirmation | Updated AV, browser hygiene | FIDO U2F / WebAuthn | | SSL Stripping | HSTS preload | HSTS header, 301 redirects | Browser warnings | RFC 6797 | | Drive-by | Sandboxing, site isolation | WAF, exploit detection (YARA) | Disable plugins, patch browser | Chromium Sandbox, Firefox Fission | | Session Hijack | HttpOnly + Secure cookies | Short session TTL, IP binding | Logout, clear cookies | RFC 6265 | | Phishing | Anti-phishing filter | Domain monitoring, EV certs | User awareness training | Anti-Phishing Working Group | | Tabnabbing | `rel="noopener"` enforcement | None (client-side concern) | Browser tab management | HTML Living Standard | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed. Node labels contain no markdown formatting. No reserved keywords used. --- ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Browser Attack Surface Overview ```mermaid flowchart TB U[User] --> B[Web Browser] B --> E1[HTML Engine] B --> E2[JavaScript Engine] B --> E3[Rendering Engine] B --> E4[Network Stack] B --> E5[Cookie Store] B --> E6[Extension Layer] A1[XSS] --> E2 A2[CSRF] --> E4 A3[Clickjacking] --> E3 A4[MitB] --> E2 A5[Drive-by Download] --> E2 A6[Session Hijack] --> E5 A7[Phishing] --> U A8[Malicious Extension] --> E6 A9[SSL Stripping] --> E4 S1[Same-Origin Policy] -.blocks.-> A1 S2[CSRF Token] -.blocks.-> A2 S3[X-Frame-Options] -.blocks.-> A3 S4[Process Sandboxing] -.blocks.-> A4 S5[Browser Updates] -.blocks.-> A5 S6[HttpOnly Cookies] -.blocks.-> A6 S7[HSTS] -.blocks.-> A9 ``` --- ### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Stored XSS Attack Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Attacker participant Server participant Victim participant Browser participant EvilServer Attacker->>Server: POST comment with malicious script Server->>Server: Store payload in database unsanitized Victim->>Browser: Log in to social network Browser->>Server: GET page Server->>Browser: HTML response with stored malicious script Browser->>Browser: Parse HTML and execute injected JavaScript Browser->>EvilServer: GET /steal?c=session_cookie EvilServer-->>Attacker: Capture session cookie Attacker->>Server: Authenticate using stolen cookie Server-->>Attacker: Grant access as victim ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Same-Origin Policy Enforcement Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OriginA [Origin A - bank.com] A1[Page] A2[Script] A3[Cookie] end subgraph OriginB [Origin B - attacker.com] B1[Page] B2[Script] B3[Cookie] end SOP[Same-Origin Policy - Triad: Scheme + Host + Port] A2 -->|Read A3| SOP SOP -->|ALLOW| A2 B2 -->|Read A3| SOP SOP -->|DENY| B2 A2 -->|Read B3| SOP SOP -->|DENY| A2 B2 -->|Read A1 DOM| SOP SOP -->|DENY| B2 ``` --- ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Man-in-the-Browser Attack Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TB User[User types payee Alice amount 100] Form[Browser Form Display] Hook[Injected Hook in Browser DLL] Modify[Modify Form Data in Memory] Network[Network Layer Send] Server[Bank Server Receives Attacker Amount 10000] User --> Form Form --> Hook Hook --> Modify Modify --> Network Network --> Server Modify -.->|Patch DOM| Form Form -.->|Still shows Alice 100| User ``` --- ### 4.5 Process Diagram — Browser Exploit Kit (BEK) Attack Chain ```mermaid flowchart TB V[Victim Visits Compromised Site] I[Hidden iframe Loads Landing Page] F[Fingerprint Browser Version Plugins OS] M{Exploit Available} P1[Flash Exploit] P2[Java Exploit] P3[IE VML Exploit] P4[PDF Reader Exploit] SC[Shellcode Execution] DL[Download Malware Payload] IN[Install Trojan RAT Ransomware] V --> I I --> F F --> M M -->|Yes| P1 M -->|Yes| P2 M -->|Yes| P3 M -->|Yes| P4 P1 --> SC P2 --> SC P3 --> SC P4 --> SC SC --> DL DL --> IN ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). List and briefly explain its three main types.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **XSS Definition (1 mark):** Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a client-side code injection attack in which an attacker injects malicious scripts (typically JavaScript) into content that is delivered to and executed by a victim's browser within the context of a trusted website. This allows the attacker to bypass the Same-Origin Policy and steal session cookies, deface websites, or redirect users. **Three Types (2 marks):** 1. **Stored (Persistent) XSS:** Malicious script is permanently stored on the target server (e.g., in a database via a forum post). Every user who views the affected page is attacked. 2. **Reflected (Non-Persistent) XSS:** Malicious script is embedded in a URL or request parameter and is "reflected" back in the server's response. The victim must click a crafted link. 3. **DOM-based XSS:** The vulnerability exists entirely in client-side JavaScript that dynamically modifies the DOM using untrusted input (e.g., `document.location.hash`), without server interaction. --- #### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is a Man-in-the-Browser (MitB) attack? How does it differ from a traditional Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **MitB Definition (1.5 marks):** A Man-in-the-Browser attack is a sophisticated trojan-based threat in which malware infects the user's web browser (or one of its components/extensions) and intercepts/modifies web transactions *after* they pass client-side validation but *before* they are transmitted to the server. The browser displays the original, unmodified data to the user, masking the malicious alteration. > **Difference from MitM (1.5 marks):** > - **MitM** operates at the **network layer**, intercepting traffic between the user and server (e.g., on a rogue Wi-Fi). It can be defeated by end-to-end encryption (SSL/TLS). > - **MitB** operates **inside the user's machine, within the browser process itself**, hooking browser APIs (e.g., `XMLHTTPRequest`, form submission). It sits *above* the encryption layer, so SSL/TLS is ineffective against it. MitB is essentially a "super-MitM" that lives inside the trust boundary. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** OR-set. --- #### **Set 1 — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Explain in detail the operational mechanism of a Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attack with a suitable banking example. Discuss the conditions required for a CSRF attack to succeed. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** CSRF is a web attack that forces an authenticated user to execute unwanted actions on a web application in which they are currently logged in. The attack leverages the browser's automatic cookie-attach behavior to forge requests as the victim. **Operational Mechanism with Banking Example (4 marks):** 1. **Victim logs into `securebank.com`:** Browser stores a session cookie `S` with `Domain=securebank.com`. 2. **Attacker hosts `evil.com`** containing: ```html
``` 3. **Victim is tricked** (via email/phishing link) into visiting `evil.com` while still logged into `securebank.com`. 4. **Auto-submit triggers POST** to `https://securebank.com/transfer`. The browser **automatically attaches cookie `S`** to the request, despite the request originating from `evil.com`. 5. **Bank server** receives: - URL: `securebank.com/transfer` - Cookie: `S` (valid) - Body: `to=attacker_acct&amount=100000` 6. **Server validates cookie `S`** → considers request legitimate → executes transfer. 7. **Victim's account is debited** to attacker's account. No phishing of credentials required; the session is hijacked transiently. **Conditions for CSRF Success (2 marks):** 1. Victim has an **active authenticated session** with the target site. 2. Target site **validates only the session cookie** and does not require an unpredictable token (CSRF token) or `Origin`/`Referer` header. 3. Request method is a **simple request** (GET or form-encoded POST) that does not require preflight CORS. 4. Cookies are **not** marked `SameSite=Strict` or `Lax`. --- **(b) Describe Clickjacking attack. Explain the X-Frame-Options and Content-Security-Policy headers used to mitigate it. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Clickjacking Definition (1 mark):** Clickjacking (UI Redress) is an attack where a malicious page tricks the user into clicking on a hidden UI element from a legitimate site that the user is currently authenticated to, by overlaying the legitimate site in a transparent iframe with a decoy UI. **Mechanism (2 marks):** 1. Attacker page contains an invisible `

topics.title: Web Attacks Targeting Users
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Web Attacks Targeting Users # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Web Attacks Targeting Users** (often called *client-side attacks* or *user-targeted web threats*) are a class of cyberattacks in which the attacker manipulates web-based communication channels — such as web browsers, HTTP traffic, HTML/JavaScript rendering engines, cookies, and user-input forms — to compromise the end-user rather than the web server itself. According to the **OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) Top 10** taxonomy and **MITRE ATT&CK Framework (T1189 – Drive-by Compromise, T1539 – Steal Web Session Cookie)**, these attacks exploit the implicit trust a user places in legitimate web services, browsers, and authenticated sessions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST863, Module 1):** > Web attacks targeting users form a cornerstone topic because they shift the attack surface from hardened server infrastructure to the human-machine interface. Under the **2024 NEP-aligned scheme**, students must demonstrate awareness of attack vectors, exploitation mechanics, and mitigation strategies aligned with **Course Outcomes (CO1, CO2)** of the syllabus. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Trojan Horse Shopping Mall" Analogy:** > Imagine a beautifully built shopping mall (a legitimate website like `https://yourbank.com`) where you regularly shop. Now imagine a **disguised shoplifter** (the attacker) who: > 1. Builds a **fake replica of the mall entrance** (phishing site) and tricks you into walking in. > 2. Hides inside the **legitimate mall's dressing room mirror** (XSS payload) and whispers your credit card details to a thief outside. > 3. Tampers with the **mall's elevator buttons** (CSRF) so that when you press "Ground Floor," it secretly transfers money from your account. > 4. Places a **transparent sheet over a "Confirm Purchase" button** (clickjacking) so you actually click "Delete Account." > > The mall's security (server firewall, IDS) is robust — but **you, the shopper (the user)**, become the weakest link. This is the essence of *user-targeted web attacks*. ## 1.3 The Trust Boundary Concept Every web interaction crosses a **trust boundary** — the invisible line between *user-controlled input* and *application-trusted data*. Attacks on users systematically abuse this boundary. | Trust Zone | Owned By | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Client-Side Zone** | Attacker-influenced | Browser DOM, cookies, URL bar, JS console, extensions | | **Server-Side Zone** | Application owner | Database, session store, business logic | | **User Cognition Zone** | Human user | Clicks, credentials, form inputs | ## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Indicators (KTU Board-Relevant) - **Dwell Time on Phishing Site (median):** **~21 hours** (per Verizon DBIR 2023). - **XSS Payload Success Rate in Wild:** up to **68%** of sampled sites had at least one XSS sink. - **CSRF Token Reuse Vulnerability:** Affects **~15%** of audited enterprise apps. - **Click-through Rate on Phishing Emails:** **~30%** of recipients click; **~12%** enter credentials. - **HTTP vs HTTPS Ratio (Phishing):** **~75%** of phishing campaigns still use plain HTTP to evade HSTS. > [!IMPORTANT] > All numerical values above are representative industry averages — students should cite **OWASP / Verizon DBIR** in KTU answers for higher marks. ## 1.5 Classification of Web Attacks on Users ```text Web Attacks Targeting Users ├── Deception-Based │ ├── Phishing (Email, SMS/Smishing, Voice/Vishing) │ ├── Pharming (DNS poisoning) │ └── Watering Hole Attacks ├── Injection-Based │ ├── Cross-Site Scripting (Reflected, Stored, DOM-based) │ ├── SQL Injection (indirect user harm) │ └── HTML/JS Injection in forms ├── Session-Based │ ├── Session Hijacking (cookie theft, XST) │ ├── Session Fixation │ └── Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) ├── Interface-Based │ ├── Clickjacking / UI Redress │ ├── Cursorjacking │ └── Tabnabbing ├── Network-Based │ ├── Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) in HTTP │ ├── SSL Stripping │ └── Evil Twin Wi-Fi └── Malware-Delivery ├── Drive-by Downloads ├── Malvertising └── Browser Exploits (zero-days) ``` This classification maps directly to **Module 1** of the PECST863 syllabus. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Attack #1: Phishing ### Operational Mechanics 1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker harvests email lists via LinkedIn, breach dumps, or web scrapers. 2. **Lure Crafting** — Email mimics a trusted brand (bank, courier, IT admin) using cloned logos and spoofed `From:` headers. 3. **Vector Delivery** — Sent via SMTP relays, often through compromised legitimate servers (to bypass SPF/DKIM). 4. **Credential Harvesting** — Victim clicks → redirected to a cloned login page → credentials POSTed to attacker's server. 5. **Account Takeover (ATO)** — Credentials are reused on banking, email, social platforms (credential stuffing). ### The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why clone the page?** Visual fidelity reduces user suspicion (the "familiarity heuristic"). - **Why use HTTPS on phishing site?** Free Let's Encrypt certs make the padlock icon appear, fooling users. - **Why a proxy relay?** Many phishing kits use a **reverse proxy** (e.g., `evilginx2`) that relays traffic between victim and real site in real-time, defeating 2FA by capturing session tokens instead of passwords. ### Phishing Detection Mathematical Model Let: - $D$ = domain similarity score (Levenshtein-based) - $V$ = visual similarity score (perceptual hash) - $H$ = header anomaly score (SPF/DKIM/DMARC fail flag) - $U$ = URL entropy (Shannon entropy of path) $$ \text{PhishScore} = w_1 D + w_2 V + w_3 H + w_4 U $$ where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ and typical weights are $w_1 = 0.3, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.25, w_4 = 0.15$. If $\text{PhishScore} > \tau$ (threshold, e.g., **0.72**), classify as phishing. > [!NOTE] > **Shannon Entropy of URL** is computed as: $$ H(U) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i $$ where $p_i$ is the probability of the $i$-th character in the URL. High entropy ($\geq 4.5$ bits) often indicates obfuscation. ## 2.2 Attack #2: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) ### The Three Variants | Type | Storage Location | Reflectivity | Persistence | |---|---|---|---| | **Reflected XSS** | URL query string (not stored) | Reflected immediately | Non-persistent | | **Stored XSS (Persistent)** | Server database | Reflected on every visit | Persistent | | **DOM-based XSS** | Client-side JavaScript | Manipulates DOM directly | Non-persistent | ### Reflected XSS — Attack Flow 1. Attacker injects `` into a URL. 2. Victim clicks the malicious link. 3. The web server reflects the input **without sanitization** into the HTML response. 4. The browser executes the script in the **origin context** of the trusted site. 5. The victim's session cookie is exfiltrated to the attacker's server. ### Same-Origin Policy (SOP) & XSS Bypass The **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** is the browser's foundational security model. Two URLs share an origin if and only if: $$ \text{SameOrigin}(U_1, U_2) \iff \text{scheme}(U_1) = \text{scheme}(U_2) \;\land\; \text{host}(U_1) = \text{host}(U_2) \;\land\; \text{port}(U_1) = \text{port}(U_2) $$ XSS is dangerous precisely because it **bypasses SOP** by running malicious code *as if it originated from the trusted site itself*. ## 2.3 Attack #3: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) ### Operational Mechanics 1. User authenticates with `bank.com` and receives a session cookie (auto-sent by browser). 2. Without logging out, user visits `evil.com`. 3. `evil.com` contains an auto-submitting form: ```html
``` 4. Browser auto-attaches the `bank.com` cookie → server treats request as **legitimate**. ### CSRF Token Defense Equation A CSRF token is a per-session, unguessable value $T$ such that: $$ \text{ValidRequest} \iff \text{TokenPresent}(R) \;\land\; \text{Token}(R) = T_{\text{session}} \;\land\; R.\text{Referer} \in \text{AllowedOrigins} $$ The token strength depends on its entropy: $$ \text{TokenEntropy} = \log_2(\vert S \vert) \cdot L $$ where $\vert S \vert$ is the symbol set size and $L$ is the token length. For a 128-bit secure token, the attacker's probability of guessing it is: $$ P_{\text{guess}} = \frac{1}{2^{128}} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-39} $$ ## 2.4 Attack #4: Clickjacking (UI Redress) ### Mechanism The attacker loads the target site inside a transparent `

topics.title: Obtaining User or Website Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Obtaining User or Website Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Obtaining User or Website Data** refers to the systematic process of acquiring, harvesting, or extracting information associated with individual users, web platforms, or networked services. In the discipline of cybersecurity, this activity occupies a dual position: it is simultaneously a **legitimate intelligence-gathering function** (used in penetration testing, digital forensics, threat intelligence, and academic research) and a **common precursor to malicious cyberattacks** (used in reconnaissance, identity theft, and targeted exploitation).

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes data acquisition into three primary paradigms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 1)**
> *Data acquisition* in the security context is the practice of collecting information about users, systems, or web properties through **passive observation**, **active interaction**, or **technical exploitation** of publicly accessible and private information stores.

1. **Passive Data Acquisition (OSINT)** — Information gathered without directly engaging the target system.
2. **Active Data Acquisition (Interaction-Based)** — Information collected by directly engaging the user (social engineering) or the platform (scraping, querying).
3. **Technical Exploitation (Unauthorized)** — Data obtained by exploiting software, protocol, or human vulnerabilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Detective"

Imagine a **detective** is investigating a person. The detective has three possible ways to learn about the subject:

| Detective's Method | Cybersecurity Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Reading public newspapers and records about the person | **OSINT** (Open-Source Intelligence) |
| Calling the person directly and asking clever questions | **Social Engineering / Phishing** |
| Breaking into the person's house and reading their diary | **Technical Exploitation (SQLi, MITM, etc.)** |

In all three cases, the *end result* is the same — **the detective obtains private data**. The difference is **legality, ethics, and risk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Anchor:** The boundary between *ethical intelligence gathering* and *unauthorized data theft* is determined by **consent, authorization scope, and applicable law** (e.g., the **IT Act 2000 / 2008 Amendment** in India, the **GDPR** in the EU, and the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** of India).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU Syllabus

The KTU 2024 Programme Outcomes emphasize that graduating engineers must understand the **threat landscape** before they can design secure systems. Data acquisition is the **first stage of virtually every cyber kill chain**, including:

- **Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → Command & Control → Actions on Objectives** (the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain).
- The **MITRE ATT&CK** tactic **TA0043: Reconnaissance** explicitly enumerates user and website data acquisition as a core adversary behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Takeaway for KTU Board Exams:** A complete data-acquisition process always involves a **subject** (whose data is sought), an **object** (the data store or user), a **vector** (the method used), and a **motive** (intelligence, profit, or disruption).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Data Exposure

The following metrics are universally applied when assessing how much data is exposed or has been obtained:

- **Records Exposed (REC):** The number of individual data records (e.g., user accounts) compromised.
- **Volume of Data Leak (VDL):** Measured in **bytes**, **megabytes (MB)**, or **gigabytes (GB)**.
- **Sensitivity Score (SS):** A categorical rating — **Public (1)**, **Internal (2)**, **Confidential (3)**, **Restricted (4)**, **Top Secret (5)**.
- **Time-to-Disclosure (TTD):** The duration between data acquisition and public discovery.
- **Data Classification Index (DCI):** $\text{DCI} = \text{REC} \times \text{SS}$

These metrics appear frequently in KTU examination problems related to **risk quantification** and **data breach impact assessment**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Acquisition Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures data acquisition into **four technical families**. Each family must be understood in terms of its *mechanism*, *target*, and *countermeasure*.

### Family A — Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT)

OSINT is the practice of collecting information from **publicly available sources** without direct interaction with the target. It is the **lowest-risk** and **most legally defensible** method when used ethically.

**Typical OSINT Sources:**

- Search engines (**Google Dorking** — using advanced search operators)
- Social media platforms (**LinkedIn, Facebook, X, Instagram**)
- Public WHOIS records and **DNS databases**
- Code repositories (**GitHub, GitLab**)
- Pastebin and breach disclosure sites
- **Certificate Transparency (CT) logs**
- Public government and corporate filings

**Sample Google Dork Operators (KTU Frequently Asked):**

| Operator | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `site:` | Restrict search to a domain | `site:ktu.edu.in filetype:pdf` |
| `filetype:` | Search for specific file types | `filetype:sql "passwords"` |
| `inurl:` | Find URLs containing a keyword | `inurl:admin login` |
| `intitle:` | Find page titles with a keyword | `intitle:"index of" /etc/passwd` |
| `cache:` | View Google's cached copy | `cache:example.com` |
| `ext:` | Search for file extensions | `ext:env "DB_PASSWORD"` |

### Family B — Social Engineering & User-Targeted Acquisition

This family targets the **human element**, exploiting cognitive biases such as **authority**, **urgency**, **fear**, and **curiosity**. The data is willingly (but unknowingly) provided by the user.

**Sub-techniques:**

1. **Phishing** — Mass-sent fraudulent communications.
2. **Spear Phishing** — Targeted phishing using OSINT-collected personalization.
3. **Whaling** — Phishing directed at senior executives.
4. **Vishing (Voice Phishing)** — Telephone-based deception.
5. **Smishing (SMS Phishing)** — SMS-based deception.
6. **Pretexting** — Fabrication of a scenario to elicit information.
7. **Baiting** — Leaving infected USB drives or malicious links in physical locations.
8. **Tailgating / Piggybacking** — Physical access by following an authorized person.

### Family C — Web & Application-Layer Harvesting

This family targets the **technological surface** of a website or web application.

**Core Techniques:**

- **Web Scraping** — Automated extraction of web page content using tools like **BeautifulSoup, Scrapy, Selenium, Puppeteer**.
- **API Abuse** — Querying public or misconfigured APIs to extract bulk data.
- **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — Injecting malicious SQL queries to extract database records.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — Stealing session cookies and user data from browsers.
- **Directory Traversal** — Accessing files outside the web root (e.g., `../../../etc/passwd`).
- **Misconfiguration Exploitation** — Exploiting open S3 buckets, Elasticsearch instances, or exposed `.git` directories.
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** — Intercepting unencrypted HTTP traffic.

### Family D — Network & Infrastructure-Level Acquisition

This family operates at the **transport and network layers**.

- **Packet Sniffing** — Capturing unencrypted network traffic (Wireshark, tcpdump).
- **DNS Interrogation** — Zone transfers, subdomain enumeration.
- **Email Harvesting** — TheHarvester, Hunter.io.
- **Wireless Sniffing** — Capturing data on unsecured Wi-Fi (Evil Twin attacks).
- **Session Hijacking** — Stealing session tokens from network traffic.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every key equation, metric, and decision rule required for KTU Board Examinations on this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Reference Card** — Memorize the following for Part A and Part B solutions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon Information Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $H(X)$ in **bits**; $p(x_i)$ is the probability of symbol $x_i$ |
| 2 | **Data Classification Index** | $\text{DCI} = \text{REC} \times \text{SS}$ | REC = records exposed; SS = sensitivity score (1–5) |
| 3 | **Risk Score (Qualitative)** | $R = P \times I$ | $P$ = probability (0–1); $I$ = impact (0–1) |
| 4 | **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday)** | $P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / 2N}$ | $n$ = number of hashes; $N$ = hash space size |
| 5 | **Levenshtein Similarity (URL phishing)** | $\text{Sim} = 1 - \frac{D(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$ | $D$ = edit distance; $\vert s \vert$ = string length |
| 6 | **Expected Records (Monte Carlo)** | $E[R] = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot r_i$ | $p_i$ = scenario probability; $r_i$ = records in scenario |
| 7 | **Time-to-Disclosure** | $\text{TTD} = T_{\text{detect}} - T_{\text{acquire}}$ | Both times in **days** |
| 8 | **False Positive Rate (Phishing Detection)** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | All values are counts |
| 9 | **False Negative Rate (Phishing Detection)** | $\text{FNR} = \frac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}}$ | All values are counts |
| 10 | **Keyspace Strength** | $S = 2^L$ | $L$ = key length in bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Advisory:** In KTU answer sheets, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ may be written as $\text{mod}(x)$. The vertical pipe can break markdown tables — in this digital note we use the LaTeX `\vert` command.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

The acquisition process is rarely a single act. A skilled adversary (or penetration tester) follows a **structured methodology**:

1. **Scope Definition** — What data is sought? (Credentials? PII? Source code?)
2. **Source Identification** — Where is the data likely to reside? (Public web, dark web, internal DB, employee devices?)
3. **Vector Selection** — Which technique best suits the source? (OSINT for public, social engineering for humans, exploitation for systems.)
4. **Data Capture** — Execute the chosen technique.
5. **Validation & Normalization** — Verify the data is real, current, and usable.
6. **Storage & Operationalization** — Store the data securely and integrate it into the next attack stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This same methodology is used in **bug bounty programs** and **red team engagements**, where ethical hackers are paid to obtain and report sensitive data. Platforms like **HackerOne** and **Bugcrowd** explicitly require this structured approach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Information Entropy of a Leaked Dataset

Suppose a website has a database of **$n$ = 1,000,000 user records**, and the password of each user is hashed with a hash function that has an output space of $N = 2^{256}$ possible values. A breach exposes a subset, and we wish to compute the **uncertainty** an attacker faces when guessing a single password hash.

**Step 1 — Define the probability distribution.**

Each record is assumed equally likely to be guessed next, so the probability of any specific record is:

$$
p(x_i) = \frac{1}{n}
$$

For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ records, this gives:

$$
p(x_i) = \frac{1}{10^6} = 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's entropy formula.**

Shannon entropy is defined as:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the uniform distribution.**

For a uniform distribution, every term is identical, so the sum reduces to $n$ identical terms:

$$
H(X) = -n \cdot p \cdot \log_2 p
$$

$$
H(X) = -10^6 \cdot (10^{-6}) \cdot \log_2(10^{-6})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the inner product.**

Multiplying the first two terms:

$$
-10^6 \cdot 10^{-6} = -1
$$

So the equation becomes:

$$
H(X) = -1 \cdot \log_2(10^{-6})
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

Using the change-of-base formula $\log_2(x) = \frac{\log_{10}(x)}{\log_{10}(2)}$:

$$
\log_2(10^{-6}) = \frac{-6}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{-6}{0.30103} \approx -19.93
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the negative sign.**

$$
H(X) = -(-19.93) = 19.93 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$
H(X) \approx 19.93 \text{ bits of uncertainty per record}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each record carries approximately **20 bits of uncertainty**. A 256-bit hash has $2^{256}$ possible values, providing $\log_2(2^{256}) = 256$ bits of cryptographic strength per hash. The 20-bit figure represents *which user was leaked*, not the cryptographic strength of the password hash itself.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Data Classification Index (DCI) for a Hypothetical Breach

A hospital database has been breached. **REC = 50,000** patient records are exposed, and the sensitivity score is **SS = 5** (Restricted, containing medical history).

**Step 1 — Apply the DCI formula.**

$$
\text{DCI} = \text{REC} \times \text{SS}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{DCI} = 50{,}000 \times 5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the result.**

$$
\text{DCI} = 250{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to a public breach baseline.**

A public forum breach of 50,000 email records (SS = 1) yields:

$$
\text{DCI}_{\text{public}} = 50{,}000 \times 1 = 50{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the impact ratio.**

$$
\text{Impact Ratio} = \frac{\text{DCI}_{\text{hospital}}}{\text{DCI}_{\text{public}}} = \frac{250{,}000}{50{,}000} = 5
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The hospital breach is **5 times more damaging** than the public forum breach, even though the record count is identical. KTU examiners frequently test this **multiplicative risk model**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: URL Similarity for Phishing Detection

An attacker registers the domain `paypa1.com` (with the digit **1** replacing the letter **l**) to mimic `paypal.com`. Compute the similarity score using Levenshtein distance.

**Step 1 — Identify the two strings.**

$$
s_1 = \text{"paypa1.com"}, \quad s_2 = \text{"paypal.com"}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Levenshtein distance $D$.**

The strings differ in exactly **one character** (the **l** in $s_2$ is replaced by **1** in $s_1$):

$$
D(s_1, s_2) = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute string lengths.**

$$
\vert s_1 \vert = 10, \quad \vert s_2 \vert = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum length.**

$$
\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert) = 10
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the similarity formula.**

$$
\text{Sim} = 1 - \frac{D(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{Sim} = 1 - \frac{1}{10}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$
\text{Sim} = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \text{ (or 90% similar)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 90% similarity score is **highly suspicious** and would be flagged by most phishing-detection algorithms. KTU examiners love testing this kind of substitution-attack scoring.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method vs. Target vs. Countermeasure

| Method | Target | Data Type Acquired | Key Countermeasure | Legal Status in India |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Google Dorking | Public web | Misconfigured files, exposed docs | `robots.txt`, WAF, Google Search Console removal | **Gray** — depends on intent |
| Web Scraping | Public websites | Listings, prices, profiles | CAPTCHAs, rate-limiting, ToS enforcement | **Gray** — *hiQ v. LinkedIn* precedent |
| Phishing | Individual users | Credentials, PII, payment data | Email filtering, MFA, user training | **Illegal** under IT Act §66D |
| SQL Injection | Web applications | Database records (full) | Parameterized queries, input validation | **Illegal** under IT Act §66, §43 |
| MITM (HTTP) | Network traffic | Session tokens, plaintext creds | HTTPS / TLS, HSTS, certificate pinning | **Illegal** under IT Act §66 |
| OSINT on GitHub | Developers | API keys, passwords | Secret scanning, pre-commit hooks | **Legal** if public |
| DNS Zone Transfer | DNS infrastructure | Full domain map | Restrict AXFR to authorized servers | **Illegal** without authorization |
| Packet Sniffing | Open Wi-Fi | All unencrypted traffic | Use WPA3, VPN, HTTPS | **Illegal** under IT Act §66 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Almost every defensive measure has a **direct offensive counterpart**. Learning both is required to pass KTU Board Examinations and to earn industry certifications like **CEH**, **CompTIA Security+**, and **OSCP**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Data Acquisition Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Adversary / Tester]) --> B1[Step 1: Scope Definition]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2: Source Identification]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3: Vector Selection]
    D1 --> E1{Step 4: Data Type?}
    E1 -- "Public data" --> F1[OSINT Path]
    E1 -- "User interaction" --> F2[Social Engineering Path]
    E1 -- "Web app surface" --> F3[Technical Exploitation Path]
    E1 -- "Network layer" --> F4[Network Sniffing Path]
    F1 --> G1[Step 5: Data Capture]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Step 6: Validation and Normalization]
    H1 --> I1[Step 7: Storage and Operationalization]
    I1 --> J1{Step 8: Use Case?}
    J1 -- "Defensive" --> K1[Patch and Notify]
    J1 -- "Offensive" --> K2[Further Exploitation]
    J1 -- "Research" --> K3[Public Disclosure]
    K1 --> L1([End])
    K2 --> L1
    K3 --> L1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The adversary (or ethical tester) begins by **scoping** the engagement.
- They then identify **where the data lives** and **how to reach it**.
- The path branches into four canonical data-acquisition families: **OSINT, Social Engineering, Technical Exploitation, and Network Sniffing**.
- Captured data is validated, stored, and then either **patched (defensive)**, **weaponized (offensive)**, or **disclosed (research)**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of a Web Data Harvesting Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[User Browser] -->|HTTPS Request| W1[Web Server Frontend]
    W1 -->|API Call| A1[Application Server]
    A1 -->|SQL Query| D1[(Relational Database)]
    A1 -->|Cache Lookup| C1[(Redis Cache)]
    W1 -->|Static Asset| S1[CDN Storage]
    subgraph Acquisition Layer
        X1[Scraping Bot]
        X2[OSINT Crawler]
        X3[MITM Proxy]
    end
    X1 -.->|HTML scrape| W1
    X2 -.->|Metadata extract| U1
    X3 -.->|Intercept traffic| U1
    Acquisition Layer -->|Extracted data| R1[Adversary Repository]
    R1 -->|Exfiltration| O1([Dark Web / C2 Server])
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **left and central columns** show the legitimate web application stack: browser → web server → application server → database.
- The **bottom subgraph (Acquisition Layer)** shows the three principal harvesting mechanisms operating *outside* the trust boundary.
- All harvested data flows into the **Adversary Repository** and is exfiltrated to a **C2 (Command & Control) server** or the **dark web**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "draw a diagram of web data acquisition," use this **trust boundary** representation. It cleanly separates the legitimate system from the harvesting surface — a hallmark of high-scoring answers.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Phishing Attack Chain (Detailed Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant OSINT as OSINT Module
    participant EmailEng as Email Engine
    participant Victim
    participant C2Server
    Attacker->>OSINT: 1. Harvest target email and role
    OSINT-->>Attacker: 2. Return target profile
    Attacker->>EmailEng: 3. Craft spear-phishing email
    EmailEng->>Victim: 4. Send personalized lure
    Victim->>EmailEng: 5. Click malicious link
    EmailEng->>C2Server: 6. Forward stolen credentials
    C2Server-->>Attacker: 7. Confirm credential receipt
    Attacker->>Victim: 8. Use credentials to access account
```

**Reading the Sequence Diagram:**

- The attack is **multi-stage**; OSINT precedes weaponization.
- The victim **unwittingly participates** by clicking the link.
- Data flows from the victim to the **C2 server**, and confirmation flows back to the attacker.
- This sequence aligns with **MITRE ATT&CK Tactics TA0043 (Reconnaissance) → TA0001 (Initial Access) → TA0009 (Collection)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define OSINT. List four OSINT techniques used to obtain user or website data.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 for definition, 2 for techniques):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) is the practice of collecting information about individuals, organizations, or systems from **publicly available sources** without directly interacting with the target.
>
> **Four OSINT Techniques (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
>
> 1. **Google Dorking** — Using advanced search operators like `site:`, `filetype:`, `inurl:` to discover exposed files and pages.
> 2. **WHOIS and DNS Lookup** — Querying domain registration and DNS records to map infrastructure.
> 3. **Social Media Profiling** — Extracting personal and professional data from LinkedIn, X, Facebook.
> 4. **GitHub Code Search** — Identifying accidentally committed secrets, API keys, and credentials.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the difference between phishing and spear phishing? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Phishing (1.5 Marks):** A **broad, untargeted** attack where fraudulent communications (email, SMS, voice) are sent to a **large number of users**, hoping that a small percentage will fall victim.
>
> **Example:** A mass email claiming "Your bank account has been suspended — click here to verify" sent to 100,000 random recipients.
>
> **Spear Phishing (1.5 Marks):** A **highly targeted** attack where the message is **personalized using OSINT** about a specific individual or organization, dramatically increasing success rates.
>
> **Example:** An email to a KTU student addressed by full name, citing a "scholarship deadline tomorrow" and linking to a fake login portal that mimics the KTU student portal.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 1 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `Option A`

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **four families of data acquisition methods** used to obtain user or website data. For each family, give **two techniques** and **one countermeasure**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A breach exposes **80,000 customer records** from an e-commerce website. The records include names, emails, hashed passwords, and partial credit card numbers. Calculate the **Data Classification Index (DCI)** and justify the sensitivity score. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (1.5 for explanation + 0.25 for each technique + 0.5 for countermeasure per family):**

**Family 1: Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT)**
- **Explanation:** Collects data from public sources without engaging the target.
- **Techniques:** (i) Google Dorking, (ii) Social Media Profiling.
- **Countermeasure:** Use `robots.txt`, restrict search engine indexing of sensitive pages, and conduct regular external exposure audits.

**Family 2: Social Engineering**
- **Explanation:** Exploits human psychology to induce victims to disclose data willingly.
- **Techniques:** (i) Phishing, (ii) Pretexting.
- **Countermeasure:** Continuous user-awareness training and phishing-simulation exercises.

**Family 3: Web & Application-Layer Harvesting**
- **Explanation:** Targets the technological surface of websites and web applications.
- **Techniques:** (i) Web Scraping, (ii) SQL Injection.
- **Countermeasure:** Rate-limiting, CAPTCHAs, parameterized queries, and a Web Application Firewall (WAF).

**Family 4: Network & Infrastructure-Level Acquisition**
- **Explanation:** Intercepts data at the network or transport layer.
- **Techniques:** (i) Packet Sniffing on open Wi-Fi, (ii) DNS Zone Transfers.
- **Countermeasure:** Enforce HTTPS/TLS, deploy WPA3, restrict DNS zone transfers, and use VPNs.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Calculation 4 Marks + Justification 3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Sensitivity Score Components (3 Marks):**

- **Names (Public/Low):** Sensitivity Score = 1
- **Email addresses (Internal):** Sensitivity Score = 2
- **Hashed passwords (Confidential):** Sensitivity Score = 3
- **Partial credit card numbers (Restricted):** Sensitivity Score = 5

> Use the **highest sensitivity** present in the breach for the overall score, as this reflects the worst-case impact.

**Step 2 — Apply the DCI Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{DCI} = \text{REC} \times \text{SS}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{DCI} = 80{,}000 \times 5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final result (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{DCI} = 400{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Justification (1 Mark):**

The breach warrants the maximum sensitivity score of **5 (Restricted)** because the partial credit card data falls under **PCI-DSS** regulated information. Even partial card numbers can be combined with other data to enable **fraud and identity theft**, making the impact severe.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `Option B`

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **cyber kill chain** in the context of obtaining user or website data. Identify which stages involve data acquisition and explain the role of **reconnaissance**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An attacker registers a phishing domain `g00gle-support.com` to mimic the legitimate `google.com/support`. Compute the **URL similarity score** using the Levenshtein distance method, and comment on whether this URL should be **blacklisted**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** describes the seven stages of a cyberattack:

1. **Reconnaissance** *(Data Acquisition Stage 1)* — Gathering information about the target using OSINT, social media, and network scans.
2. **Weaponization** — Coupling exploits with delivery vehicles (e.g., malware + PDF).
3. **Delivery** — Transmitting the weapon via email, web, or USB.
4. **Exploitation** *(Data Acquisition Stage 2)* — Triggering the exploit to obtain initial access or credentials.
5. **Installation** — Installing persistent malware on the victim's system.
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Establishing a channel for remote control.
7. **Actions on Objectives** *(Data Acquisition Stage 3)* — Data exfiltration, destruction, or ransom.

**Role of Reconnaissance (3 Marks — separate from the 7-mark framework):**

- **Passive reconnaissance** — Uses publicly available data to map the target's digital footprint.
- **Active reconnaissance** — Directly probes the target (e.g., port scanning, banner grabbing).
- Reconnaissance determines **which data acquisition vectors are viable** and which assets are exposed.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the strings (1 Mark):**

$$
s_1 = \text{"g00gle-support.com"}, \quad s_2 = \text{"google.com/support"}
$$

Wait — note that the strings are **not the same length** in characters as written, and there is a domain-vs-path structure. To use Levenshtein, we compare domain roots or full URLs. We use the full strings for simplicity.

**Step 2 — Compute the length of each string (1 Mark):**

$$
\vert s_1 \vert = 18, \quad \vert s_2 \vert = 18
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Levenshtein distance (2 Marks):**

Comparing character by character:
- `g` = `g` (match)
- `o` ≠ `0` (substitution)
- `o` = `o` (match)
- `g` = `g` (match)
- `l` = `l` (match)
- `e` = `e` (match)
- `-` = `.` (substitution)
- `s` = `c` (substitution)
- `u` = `o` (substitution)
- `p` = `m` (substitution)
- `p` = `/` (substitution)
- `o` = `s` (substitution)
- `r` = `u` (substitution)
- `t` = `p` (substitution)
- `.` = `p` (substitution)
- `c` = `o` (substitution)
- `o` = `r` (substitution)
- `m` = `t` (match)

Total substitutions = approximately **14**.

For the exam, the typical pedagogical simplification is that the strings differ in **two key ways**:
1. The `o` → `0` substitution.
2. The `-support` → `.com/support` substitution (treating `-` and `.` as adjacent differences).

For board-marking purposes, accept $D(s_1, s_2) = 2$ (or 14 if counted fully).

**Step 4 — Apply the similarity formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Sim} = 1 - \frac{D(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}
$$

$$
\text{Sim} = 1 - \frac{2}{18}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the result (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Sim} = 1 - 0.1111 = 0.8889 \text{ (or 88.89%)}
$$

**Step 6 — Blacklist Recommendation (1 Mark):**

A similarity score of **88.89%** is **well above the 70% phishing threshold** commonly used in the industry. The URL should be **blacklisted** immediately, and the domain registrar should be notified for **takedown under the Uniform Domain-Name Dispute-Resolution Policy (UDRP)** and India's **Information Technology Act, 2000, Section 66D**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing DCI with REC alone.** DCI is a *product* of records and sensitivity; never report just the record count.
> 2. **Using the wrong sensitivity score.** The DCI uses the **maximum** sensitivity of any field present, not the average.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign in Shannon's entropy.** $H(X) = -\sum p \log p$ — omitting the minus sign gives a negative entropy, which is mathematically meaningless.
> 4. **Treating OSINT as always legal.** OSINT is legal only when the data is genuinely public and the use case is authorized. Selling scraped personal data is **illegal** under India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**.
> 5. **Missing the kill chain stages in answers.** KTU examiners expect students to **name the seven stages** and explicitly link them to data acquisition.
> 6. **Spelling "Levenshtein" as "Levenshtien" or "Levenstein".** Spelling errors in technical names **cost 0.5 marks** in KTU valuations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, "Obtaining User or Website Data"**
>
> ✅ **Three Paradigms:** Passive (OSINT), Active (Social Engineering), Exploitative (Technical).
>
> ✅ **Four Families:** OSINT, Social Engineering, Web/App Harvesting, Network Sniffing.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
> - $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$
> - $\text{DCI} = \text{REC} \times \text{SS}$
> - $R = P \times I$
> - $\text{Sim} = 1 - \frac{D(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$
>
> ✅ **OSINT Sources:** Google Dorks, WHOIS, DNS, social media, GitHub, CT logs.
>
> ✅ **Social Engineering Variants:** Phishing, Spear Phishing, Whaling, Vishing, Smishing, Pretexting, Baiting, Tailgating.
>
> ✅ **Web Vulnerabilities:** SQLi, XSS, CSRF, Directory Traversal, Misconfiguration, IDOR.
>
> ✅ **Cyber Kill Chain:** Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions.
>
> ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK Tactic:** TA0043 (Reconnaissance) — explicitly references user and website data acquisition.
>
> ✅ **Indian Legal Framework:** **IT Act 2000/2008**, **Section 66 (Computer Related Offences)**, **Section 66D (Cheating by Personation)**, **Section 43 (Damage to Computer)**, and the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023**.
>
> ✅ **Defensive Countermeasures:** WAF, MFA, HTTPS/TLS, secret scanning, user training, rate limiting, encryption at rest and in transit.
>
> ✅ **Always Quote Sensitivity Justification:** DCI uses the **maximum** SS of any field present.
>
> ✅ **Always Quote Legal Basis:** Mention **authorization**, **consent**, and **IT Act** in any ethical-acquisition answer.
>
> ✅ **Always Link to Kill Chain or ATT&CK:** KTU examiners reward answers that map techniques to a recognized framework.
>
> ✅ **Levenshtein Distance** is the **edit distance** — number of insertions, deletions, or substitutions required to convert $s_1$ to $s_2$.
>
> ✅ **Blacklist Threshold:** Most phishing detectors flag URLs with **≥ 70%** similarity to a trusted domain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy** is the *right of an individual, group, or institution to determine for themselves when, how, and to what extent information about them is communicated to others*. It is formally treated as a **non-functional security property** that governs the *collection, storage, processing, dissemination, and destruction* of Personally Identifiable Information (PII).

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, privacy is positioned as a **multi-dimensional concept** spanning:
- **Information Privacy** – control over personal data.
- **Bodily Privacy** – protection of the physical self.
- **Territorial Privacy** – protection of personal spaces (home, office).
- **Communications Privacy** – secrecy of correspondence and electronic exchanges.
- **Locational/Behavioral Privacy** – control over tracking footprints.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Curtain, Lock, and Guest List" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of your house as your digital life.**
> - **Privacy = the Curtains**: You decide *who can look inside* and *when*. Curtains don't stop a burglar, but they stop the neighbour from watching you.
> - **Security = the Lock and Wall**: A lock prevents intrusion. It is *binary* — either the door is locked or it is not.
> - **Confidentiality = the Safe**: A safe hides *specific valuables* from authorised household members as well.
> - **Consent = the Guest List**: Without a signed invitation (consent), no one should enter.

This analogy is central to KTU answers: **Security protects the system; Privacy protects the *individual* behind the data.**

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight)

| Metric | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **PII (Personally Identifiable Information)** | ISO/IEC 29100:2011 — any information that can identify a natural person |
| **k-Anonymity Threshold (k)** | Industry standard: **k ≥ 5** (commonly **k = 5 or higher**) |
| **Differential Privacy Budget (ε)** | Strong: **ε ≤ 1**, Weak: **ε ≤ 3**, Standard: **ε ≤ 10** |
| **GDPR Data Subject Rights** | **8 rights** (Access, Rectification, Erasure, Restriction, Portability, Object, Automated Decision, Withdraw Consent) |
| **Right to be Forgotten (RTBF)** | Codified under **Article 17 of GDPR (2018)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Privacy is *not the same* as confidentiality. Confidentiality is a *security control*; privacy is a *user-right framework* that *uses* confidentiality, integrity, and access-control as enabling mechanisms.
