2. **Mitigation Planning:** Engineers can choose technical controls — anonymization, pseudonymization, encryption, access restriction.
3. **Compliance Evidence:** Provides auditable documentation for regulators and DPOs.
4. **Stakeholder Alignment:** Forces product, legal, and engineering teams to align on data flows.
5. **Continuous Improvement:** Periodic reviews embed privacy into the DevSecOps lifecycle.

**Four Mandatory DPIA Scenarios (GDPR Art. 35(3)):**

1. **Systematic and extensive profiling with significant effects:** E.g., credit scoring algorithms, automated hiring tools.
2. **Large-scale processing of special categories of data:** Health, biometric, genetic, racial/ethnic data.
3. **Systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas on a large scale:** CCTV networks, public Wi-Fi analytics.
4. **Processing of children's personal data:** Any service offered to minors (e.g., EdTech platforms).
5. *(Additional valid scenarios)* Use of **innovative technologies** like AI, IoT, or facial recognition.

*(Marks: Role 4 marks (4 points) + Scenarios 3 marks (4 scenarios))*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing FIPPs with PbD:** FIPPs are *principles for data handlers*; PbD is an *engineering philosophy*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you mix them.
> 2. **Omitting jurisdiction:** When asked about privacy laws, name the *specific act* — GDPR, DPDP Act 2023, IT Act 2000 — not just "privacy laws."
> 3. **Ignoring engineering mapping:** PECST863 is a security *engineering* course. Don't give pure legal answers — connect every principle to a *technical control*.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why":** Marks are reserved for reasoning, not just listing. Always add a 1-line "why it matters."
> 5. **Forgetting Ann Cavoukian:** The PbD founder's name is frequently tested — remember it.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (1-liner):** Privacy is the right to control one's personal data; a Privacy Policy is the document codifying how an organization handles it.
- **FIPPs (5 principles):** Notice, Choice, Access, Integrity, Enforcement — the **OECD 1980** foundation.
- **GDPR 7 Principles (Art. 5):** Lawfulness, Purpose Limitation, Data Minimization, Accuracy, Storage Limitation, Integrity/Confidentiality, Accountability.
- **PbD (7 Principles, Ann Cavoukian):** Proactive, Default Privacy, Embedded, Full Functionality, End-to-End Security, Visibility, User Respect.
- **Key Acts to remember:** GDPR (EU, 2018), CCPA (California, 2020), DPDP Act (India, 2023), IT Act 2000, HIPAA (US Healthcare), COPPA (US Children).
- **Key Metrics:** $P_c$ (Compliance Index), $R_d$ (Privacy Risk Score), $k$-anonymity (re-identification resistance).
- **Key Roles:** DPO (Data Protection Officer), DPIA (Impact Assessment), SAR (Subject Access Request).
- **Engineering mappings:** Notice → CMP banner; Consent → Opt-in API; Access → SAR portal; Integrity → HMAC; Security → AES-256 / TLS; Enforcement → audit logs.
- **Remember the names:** **Ann Cavoukian** (PbD), **OECD** (FIPPs origin, 1980), **Puttuswamy Judgment (2017)** (Right to Privacy as fundamental right in India).
- **GDPR Penalty:** Up to **€20 million** or **4% of global annual turnover** (whichever is higher).
- **Default $k$-anonymity standard:** $k \geq 5$; for medical data, $k \geq 20$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Email Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Email Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Email Security?

**Email Security** refers to the collective measures, protocols, cryptographic techniques, and procedural controls used to protect email communications and accounts from unauthorized access, loss, or compromise. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, it encompasses the end-to-end protection of the email message envelope (header) and content (body) against threats like interception, tampering, forgery, and phishing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST863):** Email Security deals with the techniques used to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** of electronic mail using cryptographic primitives such as symmetric encryption, public-key encryption, hash functions, and digital signatures.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Email Security Services

Any secure email system must guarantee the following four services:

| Service | Meaning | Cryptographic Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Only the intended recipient can read the message | Symmetric Encryption (e.g., AES) + Public-Key Encryption (to exchange session key) |
| **Integrity** | Message has not been altered in transit | Hash Function (e.g., SHA-256) |
| **Authentication** | Sender is genuinely who they claim to be | Digital Signature (e.g., RSA, ECDSA) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Sender cannot later deny having sent the message | Digital Signature + Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sealed Tamper-Proof Notary Letter"

Imagine you want to send a private letter to a friend living across the country. You are worried that:
- The postman may **open and read** it (loss of confidentiality),
- Someone may **tear and re-glue** the page (loss of integrity),
- An imposter may **sign your friend's name** and trick you (loss of authentication).

Here is what you do:
1. You write the letter on paper.
2. You put it in a **locked steel box** whose key only your friend possesses (this is **public-key encryption**).
3. You then **sign your signature with red wax seal** that leaves a unique imprint — anyone trying to alter the letter will break the seal (this is the **digital signature / hash + sign**).
4. You send it through the postman.

The postman cannot read it, cannot modify it without breaking the seal, and your friend knows it is genuinely from you. This is exactly how **PGP and S/MIME** work in the digital world.

## 1.4 Major Email Security Threats

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks 3-mark questions on this classification. Memorize the four major threats.

1. **Phishing** — Fraudulent emails that masquerade as legitimate ones to steal credentials. Spear phishing targets a specific person; whaling targets executives.
2. **Spam (Unsolicited Bulk Email / UBE)** — Unsolicited junk mail sent in bulk; consumes bandwidth and storage.
3. **Email Spoofing** — Forging the "From" address so the message appears to originate from a trusted source.
4. **Malware Attachment** — Emails carrying viruses, worms, ransomware, or trojans as attachments.
5. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack** — An adversary intercepts SMTP traffic between mail servers.
6. **Eavesdropping** — Passive interception of unencrypted email on the wire.

## 1.5 Real-World Email Security Protocols Covered in KTU Module 1

| Protocol | Full Form | Domain | Standardization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PGP** | Pretty Good Privacy | Personal / Enterprise email | OpenPGP (RFC 4880) |
| **S/MIME** | Secure / Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions | Corporate / Enterprise email | RFC 5751 (v3.2) |
| **DKIM** | DomainKeys Identified Mail | Server-to-server email validation | RFC 6376 |
| **SPF** | Sender Policy Framework | Email anti-spoofing | RFC 7208 |
| **DMARC** | Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance | Email policy enforcement | RFC 7489 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** PGP uses a **decentralized web-of-trust** model, whereas S/MIME relies on a **centralized X.509 PKI hierarchy** with Certificate Authorities (CAs). This single difference is the most frequently asked concept in KTU University Exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pretty Good Privacy (PGP) — The OpenPGP Standard

PGP, created by **Phil Zimmermann in 1991**, is the de-facto standard for securing personal email. It is a hybrid cryptosystem — it cleverly combines the **speed of symmetric encryption** with the **convenience of public-key cryptography**.

### 2.1.1 The Five PGP Services

PGP provides **five distinct services** that operate in a defined order:

1. **Authentication** — Digital signature using sender's private key over a hash of the message.
2. **Confidentiality** — Encrypting the message with a random one-time **session key** (symmetric), then encrypting the session key with the recipient's public key.
3. **Compression** — ZIP-style compression applied to the message + signature to save bandwidth (performed **after signing but before encryption** to avoid cryptographic attacks on compressed data).
4. **Email Compatibility** — Radix-64 (Base64) encoding of the binary ciphertext so it survives 7-bit SMTP text channels.
5. **Segmentation & Reassembly** — Breaking very long messages into smaller SMTP-compliant chunks (≤ 50 KB each) and reassembling them at the receiver.

### 2.1.2 PGP Operational Sequence — Transmission Side

The PGP transmitter executes the following pipeline:

**Step 1 — Hash:** Compute `m_hash = SHA-256(message)`.
**Step 2 — Sign:** Compute digital signature `σ = RSA-Sign(sender_private_key, m_hash)`.
**Step 3 — Concatenate:** Form `M' = (message ‖ σ)`.
**Step 4 — Compress:** Form `M'' = ZIP(M')`.
**Step 5 — Generate session key:** Generate a random 128-bit AES session key `Ks`.
**Step 6 — Encrypt message:** `C1 = AES-128(M'', Ks)`.
**Step 7 — Encrypt session key:** `C2 = RSA-Encrypt(recipient_public_key, Ks)`.
**Step 8 — Base64 encode:** Output `Output = Radix64(C1 ‖ C2)`.

> [!NOTE]
> The order **Sign → Compress → Encrypt** is critical. Signing first means the signature validates the **original uncompressed** message, preventing compression-based oracle attacks (like CRIME/BREACH).

## 2.2 S/MIME (Secure / Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)

S/MIME is the **corporate / enterprise-grade** counterpart to PGP. It is built into most modern mail clients (Outlook, Apple Mail, Thunderbird) and uses **X.509 certificates issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA)**.

### 2.2.1 S/MIME Functions

- **Enveloped Data** — Encrypted content + encrypted session key (provides confidentiality).
- **Signed Data** — Digital signature over content (provides integrity + authentication + non-repudiation).
- **Clear-Signed Data** — Signature appended but content remains human-readable.
- **Signed + Enveloped** — Both protections applied.

### 2.2.2 MIME Entity & S/MIME Content Types

S/MIME uses Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions (MIME) to support attachments, character sets, and multimedia content. Key headers:

- `Content-Type: application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=enveloped-data`
- `Content-Type: multipart/signed; protocol="application/pkcs7-signature"`

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table for any 7-mark derivations in the University Exam. Memorize the symbols.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| `M` | Original plaintext message | — |
| `H(M)` | Hash digest of message | SHA-256 → **256 bits** |
| `σ` | Digital signature | `Sign(Pr_S, H(M))` |
| `Ks` | Random symmetric session key | AES-128 / AES-256 |
| `C1` | Encrypted message body | `E(M, Ks)` |
| `C2` | Encrypted session key | `E(Ks, Pu_R)` |
| `Pu_X` | Public key of party X | — |
| `Pr_X` | Private key of party X | — |
| `‖` | Concatenation operator | — |
| `Radix64(X)` | Base64 encoding of binary X | Output length = `⌈(n × 4) / 3⌉` |
| `n` | Input binary byte length | — |
| `ECC` | Elliptic Curve Cryptography | Used in S/MIME v3.2+ |

## 2.4 Radix-64 Encoding — Engineering Utility

Radix-64 maps every **6 bits** of input to one of 64 printable ASCII characters. Its real-world engineering utility is that SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) was originally a **7-bit ASCII text protocol**, so any binary 8-bit ciphertext would be corrupted in transit. Radix-64 expands the message by **33%** (`4/3` ratio) but guarantees transport survival.

## 2.5 Web of Trust vs. PKI Hierarchy

| Property | PGP (Web of Trust) | S/MIME (X.509 PKI) |
|---|---|---|
| Trust anchor | Signed keys of other users (decentralized) | Trusted Root CA (centralized) |
| Certificate format | PGP-specific packet | X.509 v3 |
| Revocation | Manually maintained revocation rings | OCSP / CRL |
| Adoption | Individuals, journalists, security pros | Enterprises, governments |
| Trust bootstrapping | "I trust Alice, and Alice signs Bob's key" | "I trust VeriSign/DigiCert" |

## 2.6 PGP Message Format (Packet Structure)

A PGP message is a stream of **packets** (RFC 4880). Each packet has a tag byte indicating type:

- `Tag 1` — Public-Key Encrypted Session Key Packet
- `Tag 2` — Signature Packet
- `Tag 9` — Symmetrically Encrypted Data Packet
- `Tag 11` — Literal Data Packet
- `Tag 12` — Trust Packet
- `Tag 13` — User ID Packet
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — PGP Transmission Pipeline

Let **Alice (A)** send a confidential, signed message to **Bob (B)**. Notation:
- $Pr_A, Pu_A$ = Alice's private/public key pair
- $Pr_B, Pu_B$ = Bob's private/public key pair
- $M$ = plaintext message
- $H(\cdot)$ = SHA-256 hash function
- $E_S(\cdot, K)$ = symmetric encryption with key $K$
- $E_A(\cdot, K)$ = asymmetric encryption with public key $K$
- $\text{Sign}(\cdot, K)$ = signature generation with private key $K$
- $\text{R64}(\cdot)$ = Radix-64 encoding

**Step 1 — Hash the plaintext.** Alice computes the message digest:
$$H_A = H(M) \in \{0,1\}^{256}$$

This digest uniquely fingerprints the message. Any 1-bit change in $M$ will completely change $H_A$ (avalanche property of SHA-256).

**Step 2 — Sign the digest with Alice's private key.**
$$\sigma = \text{Sign}_{Pr_A}(H_A) \in \{0,1\}^{2048}$$

The signature length depends on the RSA modulus (typically 2048 bits). The signature is bound to Alice because only she possesses $Pr_A$.

**Step 3 — Concatenate message and signature.**
$$M' = M \parallel \sigma$$

This is a one-way concatenation — the signature is appended to the original plaintext (not the hash), so Bob can read $M$ after decryption.

**Step 4 — Compress with ZIP.**
$$M'' = \text{ZIP}(M')$$
$$|M''| < |M'| \text{ (in general)}$$

Compression is applied **after** signing, deliberately. If compression were applied first, an attacker could use compression-oracle attacks (CRIME-style) to recover plaintext from the ciphertext.

**Step 5 — Generate a fresh symmetric session key.**
$$K_s \xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{128}$$

The "$$\xleftarrow{\$}$$" denotes uniform random sampling. $K_s$ is a one-time key (never reused) to ensure semantic security.

**Step 6 — Symmetrically encrypt the compressed data.**
$$C_1 = E_S(M'', K_s) = \text{AES-128-Encrypt}(M'', K_s)$$

AES is ~1000× faster than RSA, so bulk data is always encrypted symmetrically.

**Step 7 — Asymmetrically encrypt the session key with Bob's public key.**
$$C_2 = E_A(K_s, Pu_B) = \text{RSA-Encrypt}(K_s, Pu_B)$$

Only Bob can recover $K_s$ because only Bob holds $Pr_B$.

**Step 8 — Concatenate ciphertexts and Radix-64 encode.**
$$C = C_1 \parallel C_2$$
$$\text{Output} = \text{R64}(C)$$

The output is now safe to traverse any 7-bit SMTP gateway.

**Step 9 — SMTP envelope transfer.** Alice's MUA (Mail User Agent) wraps the output in standard RFC 5322 MIME headers and forwards to her MSA (Mail Submission Agent), which relays through MTAs to Bob's MDA (Mail Delivery Agent).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — PGP Reception Pipeline (Bob's Side)

Bob receives $\text{Output}$ and reverses every step.

**Step 1 — Radix-64 decode:**
$$C = \text{R64}^{-1}(\text{Output})$$

**Step 2 — Split into two parts:**
$$C_1, C_2 \leftarrow \text{Split}(C)$$

**Step 3 — Decrypt session key with Bob's private key:**
$$K_s = D_A(C_2, Pr_B) = \text{RSA-Decrypt}(C_2, Pr_B)$$

**Step 4 — Decrypt the message body:**
$$M'' = D_S(C_1, K_s) = \text{AES-128-Decrypt}(C_1, K_s)$$

**Step 5 — Decompress:**
$$M' = \text{Unzip}(M'')$$

**Step 6 — Split plaintext and signature:**
$$M, \sigma \leftarrow \text{Split}(M')$$

**Step 7 — Hash the recovered plaintext:**
$$H'_A = H(M)$$

**Step 8 — Verify the signature using Alice's public key:**
$$\text{Verify}(\sigma, Pu_A) = H_A$$

The verification algorithm returns the originally-hashed value. If $H_A \stackrel{?}{=} H'_A$, the signature is **valid**, proving:
- **Authentication** (only Alice could have signed with $Pr_A$),
- **Integrity** ($M$ has not been altered),
- **Non-repudiation** (Alice cannot deny signing).

## 3.3 S/MIME Step-by-Step — Enveloped-Data Generation

**Step 1 — Generate random content-encryption key (CEK):**
$$K_{CEK} \xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{128} \quad \text{(or 192/256)}$$

**Step 2 — Encrypt content:**
$$C_{content} = E_S(\text{content}, K_{CEK})$$

**Step 3 — For each recipient $R_i$, encrypt the CEK:**
$$C_{CEK}^{(i)} = E_A(K_{CEK}, Pu_{R_i})$$

**Step 4 — Build ASN.1 DER structure** containing algorithm OIDs, $C_{content}$, and the set $\{C_{CEK}^{(i)}\}$ — encoded as a PKCS#7 / CMS object.

**Step 5 — Base64-encode the DER blob** and embed it in the MIME body.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PGP vs. S/MIME vs. DKIM/SPF/DMARC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **14-mark favourite** in KTU University Exams.

| Criterion | PGP | S/MIME | DKIM / SPF / DMARC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer of operation** | End-to-end (MUA ↔ MUA) | End-to-end (MUA ↔ MUA) | Hop-by-hop (MTA ↔ MTA) |
| **Trust model** | Web of trust (decentralized) | X.509 PKI (centralized CA) | DNS-based (organizational policy) |
| **Provides confidentiality?** | Yes | Yes | No (only authentication) |
| **Provides non-repudiation?** | Yes | Yes | Limited (organizational, not personal) |
| **Protects message body?** | Yes (encrypts) | Yes (encrypts) | No (only signs headers + body hash) |
| **Standard** | OpenPGP RFC 4880 | RFC 5751 | RFC 6376, 7208, 7489 |
| **Primary use case** | Personal / activist / open-source | Enterprise / corporate | Anti-spoofing / anti-phishing |
| **Key distribution** | Public key servers (e.g., keys.openpgp.org) | LDAP / SCEP / manual exchange | DNS TXT records |
| **Algorithm agility** | Highly flexible (plugin ciphers) | Restricted to IETF-mandated suites | Signature-based, agility limited |
| **Revocation mechanism** | Trust signatures, revocation rings | OCSP, CRL | DNS policy (p=reject, p=quarantine) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Radix-64 Expansion

Suppose the concatenated ciphertext $C$ has length $n = 45$ bytes.

Each Base64 character encodes 6 bits. 4 characters encode 3 bytes.

$$\text{Output length} = 4 \times \left\lceil \frac{n}{3} \right\rceil = 4 \times \left\lceil \frac{45}{3} \right\rceil = 4 \times 15 = 60 \text{ characters}$$

If $n$ is not a multiple of 3, padding `=` characters are appended. Expansion ratio:
$$\eta = \frac{\text{output bits}}{\text{input bits}} = \frac{6}{8} \times \frac{4}{3} = 1.0$$

Wait — the effective expansion is $4/3 \approx 1.333$, i.e., a **33.3% bandwidth overhead**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — PGP Message Transmission (Alice → Bob)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plaintext Message M] --> B[Compute Hash H = SHA-256 of M]
    B --> C[Sign Hash using Alice Private Key to form Sigma]
    C --> D[Concatenate M and Sigma to form M prime]
    D --> E[Compress M prime using ZIP to form M double prime]
    E --> F[Generate random 128 bit session key Ks]
    F --> G[Encrypt M double prime with Ks using AES to form C1]
    F --> H[Encrypt Ks with Bob Public Key using RSA to form C2]
    G --> I[Concatenate C1 and C2]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Radix-64 Base64 Encoding]
    J --> K[Wrap in SMTP MIME Headers and Send to Bob]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — PGP Message Reception (Bob → Verify)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Receive SMTP Message from Alice] --> Q[Radix-64 Decode to recover C]
    Q --> R[Split C into C1 and C2]
    R --> S[Decrypt C2 with Bob Private Key to recover Ks]
    R --> T[Decrypt C1 with Ks using AES to recover M double prime]
    S --> T
    T --> U[Decompress M double prime to recover M prime]
    U --> V[Split M prime into M and Sigma]
    V --> W[Compute Hash of M to get H prime]
    V --> X[Decrypt Sigma with Alice Public Key to get H]
    W --> Y{Compare H prime with H}
    X --> Y
    Y -- Match --> Z[Signature Valid Authenticated Integrity OK]
    Y -- Mismatch --> AA[Signature Invalid Reject Message]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — S/MIME Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sender MUA
        S1[Application Content] --> S2[S/MIME Sign Module using Sender Private Key]
        S2 --> S3[S/MIME Encrypt Module using Recipient Public Key]
        S3 --> S4[Base64 Encode and Build PKCS7 CMS Object]
    end
    S4 --> NET((SMTP TLS Channel))
    NET --> R1[Base64 Decode and Parse PKCS7 CMS Object]
    subgraph Receiver MUA
        R1 --> R2[S/MIME Decrypt Module using Recipient Private Key]
        R2 --> R3[S/MIME Verify Module using Sender Public Key]
        R3 --> R4[Deliver Original Content]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing Between PGP and S/MIME

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{Who is the audience} -->|Personal and small group| WOT[Use PGP Web of Trust]
    START{Who is the audience} -->|Enterprise and corporate| PKI[Use S/MIME with X.509 PKI]
    START{Who is the audience} -->|Server to server validation| DNSCHK[Use DKIM plus SPF plus DMARC]
    WOT --> W1[Distribute public key via keyserver or fingerprint]
    PKI --> P1[Issue certificate from internal CA or public CA]
    DNSCHK --> D1[Publish DNS TXT records at domain registrar]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Sequence — DKIM Signing and Verification

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SenderMTA as Sender MTA
    participant DKIM as DKIM Signer Module
    participant RecvMTA as Receiver MTA
    participant DKIMV as DKIM Verifier
    participant DNS as Public DNS

    SenderMTA->>DKIM: Pass full message
    DKIM->>DKIM: Compute hash over signed headers plus body
    DKIM->>DKIM: Sign hash with domain private key
    DKIM->>SenderMTA: Add DKIM Signature header
    SenderMTA->>RecvMTA: Forward via SMTP
    RecvMTA->>DKIMV: Strip DKIM Signature header
    DKIMV->>DNS: Query TXT record for selector plus domain
    DNS-->>DKIMV: Return public key
    DKIMV->>DKIMV: Verify signature using public key
    DKIMV-->>RecvMTA: Return pass fail or policy result
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**List any four security services that must be provided by a secure email system. Briefly explain any two of them.**

**Model Answer:**

The four essential security services for email are:

1. **Confidentiality** — Protection of message content from unauthorized disclosure. Achieved via symmetric encryption (e.g., AES) of the message body, with the symmetric key securely exchanged using public-key encryption (e.g., RSA).

2. **Integrity** — Assurance that the message has not been altered in transit. Implemented by computing a cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-256) of the message and signing it; the receiver re-computes and compares.

3. **Authentication** — Verification that the sender is who they claim to be. Achieved through digital signatures using the sender's private key, verifiable by anyone with the corresponding public key.

4. **Non-Repudiation** — Prevention of the sender falsely denying having sent the message. A valid digital signature tied to a private key held solely by the sender is the standard mechanism.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Differentiate between PGP and S/MIME in terms of trust model and certificate format.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PGP | S/MIME |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Model** | Decentralized **Web of Trust** — users sign each other's public keys | Centralized **X.509 PKI** — trust is delegated to a Certificate Authority (CA) |
| **Certificate Format** | OpenPGP-specific key packets (RFC 4880) with user IDs and self-signatures | X.509 v3 certificates issued by CAs with defined fields (Subject, Issuer, Validity, Public Key) |
| **Revocation** | Revocation signatures and trust rings | Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) and Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) |
| **Distribution** | Public keyservers (e.g., keys.openpgp.org) | LDAP directories, SCEP, manual out-of-band exchange |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operational sequence of PGP for sending a message. List all five PGP services. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Alice sends the following 24-byte message to Bob after PGP processing: `M = "SECURE EMAIL 2024!!!"`. The SHA-256 hash of M is the value `H`. Alice signs H using RSA-2048 producing a 256-byte signature σ. After compression, the message plus signature has size 60 bytes. AES-128 encryption is used with a 16-byte session key, and the session key is RSA-2048-encrypted to 256 bytes. Compute the final Radix-64 output size. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) PGP Operational Sequence (7 Marks):**

The five PGP services are:
1. Digital Signature (Authentication)
2. Message Encryption (Confidentiality)
3. Compression
4. Radix-64 Email Compatibility Encoding
5. Segmentation & Reassembly

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Message M]
     |
     v
[SHA-256 Hash] ---> [RSA Sign with Pr_A] ---> Sigma
     |                                          |
     v                                          v
[Concatenate M || Sigma]  ---------------->  M'
     |
     v
[ZIP Compression]
     |
     v
[M'' = Compressed Data]
     |
     v
[Generate random 128-bit session key Ks]
     |                          |
     v                          v
[AES-128 Encrypt M'']    [RSA Encrypt Ks with Pu_B]
     |                          |
     v                          v
       C1                       C2
            |          |
            v          v
       [Concatenate C1 || C2 = C]
                  |
                  v
       [Radix-64 Encode of C]
                  |
                  v
       [SMTP Transmission to Bob]
```

**Explanation:** Alice first signs the hash of M using her private key, appends the signature, compresses the result, generates a random session key, encrypts the compressed data with AES, encrypts the session key with Bob's public RSA key, concatenates both ciphertexts, and finally Radix-64-encodes the result for safe SMTP transport. **(Full 7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $M$ = 24 bytes
- Signature $\sigma$ = 256 bytes
- $(M \parallel \sigma)$ before compression = 24 + 256 = **280 bytes**
- After ZIP compression, $|M''| = 60$ bytes
- AES-128 encrypted size $C_1$ = 60 bytes (AES is a stream-preserving block cipher with PKCS#7 padding; output size grows to next multiple of 16: $\lceil 60/16 \rceil \times 16 = 64$ bytes, but standard PGP uses prefix length, so we treat $C_1 \approx 60$ bytes for the purpose of this model question).

**Step 1 — Compute total ciphertext size before encoding:**
$$|C| = |C_1| + |C_2| = 60 + 256 = 316 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Radix-64 expansion:**
$$|C_{\text{base64}}| = 4 \times \left\lceil \frac{316}{3} \right\rceil = 4 \times \lceil 105.33 \rceil = 4 \times 106 = 424 \text{ characters}$$

Equivalently, since each base64 character is 1 byte:
$$|C_{\text{base64}}| = 424 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute expansion overhead:**
$$\eta = \frac{424}{316} = 1.3417 \approx 1.34$$

So the final Radix-64 output is **424 ASCII characters**, representing a 34% expansion over the 316-byte raw ciphertext.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the S/MIME protocol. List the four main functions of S/MIME and describe the "EnvelopedData" content type with a flow diagram. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast PGP and S/MIME on ten distinct parameters. Why is S/MIME preferred in enterprise environments while PGP is preferred by individuals? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) S/MIME Protocol (7 Marks):**

**S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)** is the IETF-standardized protocol (RFC 5751, v3.2) for adding cryptographic security services to MIME-format email. It is the enterprise counterpart to PGP and is natively supported in Microsoft Outlook, Apple Mail, Mozilla Thunderbird, and most modern MTAs.

**Four Main Functions of S/MIME:**

1. **Enveloped Data** — Provides confidentiality by encrypting the content with a symmetric CEK (Content Encryption Key), and encrypting the CEK with each recipient's public key.
2. **Signed Data** — Provides integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation via digital signature over the content using the sender's private key.
3. **Clear-Signed Data** — A digital signature appended to the message while the original content remains in plaintext (readable without S/MIME).
4. **Signed and Enveloped Data** — Both protections applied in sequence (sign-then-encrypt or sign-wrapped-encrypt).

**EnvelopedData Flow Diagram:**

```
[S/MIME Sender]
   |
   v
[Generate random 128/192/256-bit CEK]
   |                    |
   v                    v
[AES-Encrypt Content] [RSA-Encrypt CEK with each Recipient's Public Key]
   |                    |
   v                    v
   C_content     [C_CEK_1, C_CEK_2, ..., C_CEK_n]
        \         /
         \       /
          v     v
[Build ASN.1 DER-encoded CMS EnvelopedData structure]
          |
          v
[Base64-encode and embed in MIME body]
          |
          v
[SMTP Transmission]

[S/MIME Receiver]
   |
   v
[Parse CMS object, locate recipient's C_CEK_i]
   |
   v
[RSA-Decrypt C_CEK_i using recipient's private key to recover CEK]
   |
   v
[AES-Decrypt C_content using CEK to recover plaintext]
```

**(b) Comparison and Justification (7 Marks):**

Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.4** above for the full 10-parameter comparison. Key contrasts:

- **Trust Model** — PGP uses decentralized Web of Trust; S/MIME uses centralized X.509 PKI.
- **Certificate Format** — PGP uses OpenPGP packets; S/MIME uses X.509 v3.
- **Integration** — S/MIME is built into enterprise email clients (Outlook, Exchange); PGP requires external plugins (GPG, Enigmail).
- **Automation** — S/MIME supports automatic certificate enrollment via SCEP; PGP requires manual key signing.
- **Compliance** — S/MIME meets regulatory requirements (HIPAA, SOX) that demand auditable CA-based trust chains.
- **Cost** — S/MIME certificates from public CAs are paid; PGP is free.
- **User Control** — PGP gives users full custody of their keys; S/MIME keys are CA-managed.
- **Algorithm Agility** — PGP supports user-defined cipher preferences; S/MIME uses IETF-mandated suites.
- **Adoption** — PGP in journalist/activist/open-source circles; S/MIME in Fortune-500/government.
- **Revocation** — S/MIME uses OCSP/CRL infrastructure; PGP uses informal trust signatures.

**Why enterprises prefer S/MIME:** Centralized PKI integrates with Active Directory, supports automated provisioning, provides auditable certificate issuance, and meets compliance mandates. **Why individuals prefer PGP:** Free, no CA needed, full control over key custody, censorship-resistant, and supports the journalist/activist ethos of self-sovereign identity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong PGP order (5 marks lost):** Many students compress *before* signing. This is incorrect and exposes the system to compression-oracle attacks. The correct order is **Sign → Compress → Encrypt**.
> 2. **Confusing session key vs. message key (3 marks lost):** The session key $K_s$ is encrypted with the **recipient's public key** ($Pu_B$), not the sender's. The sender encrypts the *message* with $K_s$, not the other way around.
> 3. **Forgetting Radix-64 is mandatory (2 marks lost):** Skipping the Base64 step is a frequent omission. SMTP is a 7-bit text channel; raw binary ciphertext will be silently corrupted.
> 4. **Mixing PGP and S/MIME trust models (4 marks lost):** Stating "PGP uses PKI" or "S/MIME uses Web of Trust" is the most common factual error. Memorize the distinction.
> 5. **Wrong hash domain (2 marks lost):** The signature is generated over the **hash of M**, not over M directly. Always write $H(M)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Email Security**

- 🔑 Email Security delivers **Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication + Non-Repudiation** (the "CIA-N" tetrad).
- 🔑 PGP = **Phil Zimmermann (1991)**, hybrid cryptosystem, decentralized **Web of Trust**.
- 🔑 S/MIME = **RFC 5751**, X.509 PKI, **centralized CA**, enterprise-grade.
- 🔑 PGP order: **Sign (over $H(M)$) → Compress → Encrypt (with $K_s$) → Encrypt $K_s$ (with $Pu_B$) → Radix-64**.
- 🔑 AES is used for **bulk data** (fast); RSA is used for **session-key wrapping** (secure key exchange).
- 🔑 SHA-256 produces a **256-bit (32-byte)** hash; RSA-2048 produces a **2048-bit (256-byte)** signature.
- 🔑 **Radix-64** expands data by **33.3%** (ratio $4/3$) and is required because SMTP is 7-bit ASCII.
- 🔑 PGP **does not** require a CA; users directly sign each other's keys.
- 🔑 S/MIME uses **PKCS#7 / CMS** (Cryptographic Message Syntax) ASN.1 structures.
- 🔑 **DKIM signs the message body hash + selected headers** using a private key published via DNS TXT.
- 🔑 **SPF** authorizes sending MTAs by IP/CIDR in a DNS TXT record (mechanism only, no signing).
- 🔑 **DMARC** aligns SPF and DKIM results and publishes a policy (`p=none`, `p=quarantine`, `p=reject`).
- 🔑 Email threats: **Phishing, Spam, Spoofing, Malware attachment, MITM, Eavesdropping**.
- 🔑 MIME headers: `Content-Type: application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=enveloped-data` and `multipart/signed`.
- 🔑 PGP packet tags: 1 (PK-ESK), 2 (Signature), 9 (SED), 11 (Literal), 12 (Trust), 13 (User ID).
- 🔑 Non-repudiation in PGP/S/MIME comes from binding the signature to a **private key held only by the sender**.
- 🔑 Always **Sign first, then Compress, then Encrypt** — never change this order in your exam answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptography in Network Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cryptography in Network Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cryptography in Network Security** is the scientific discipline of designing and analyzing mathematical techniques that enable secure communication over an untrusted network by transforming intelligible data (plaintext) into an unintelligible form (ciphertext), and vice versa, using algorithms and cryptographic keys. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST863 – Topics in Security)**, cryptography forms the foundational pillar of modern network security, providing four essential security services:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Cryptographic Security Services**
> 1. **Confidentiality** – Ensuring that data is accessible only to authorized parties.
> 2. **Integrity** – Guaranteeing that data has not been altered during transmission.
> 3. **Authentication** – Verifying the identity of the communicating entities.
> 4. **Non-Repudiation** – Preventing a sender from denying the sending of a message.

## 1.2 Fundamental Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Plaintext (P)** | The original, readable message or data before encryption. |
| **Ciphertext (C)** | The scrambled, unreadable output produced by an encryption algorithm. |
| **Encryption (E)** | The process of converting plaintext into ciphertext using a key. |
| **Decryption (D)** | The reverse process of converting ciphertext back to plaintext. |
| **Key (K)** | A secret parameter (number, word, or string) used by the cryptographic algorithm. |
| **Algorithm (Cipher)** | The mathematical function used for encryption and decryption. |
| **Cryptanalysis** | The art of breaking ciphers or finding weaknesses in cryptographic systems. |
| **Cryptology** | The combined study of cryptography and cryptanalysis. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Time Capsule"

Imagine you wish to send a personal diary to your best friend through a public courier service. To protect the diary:

1. You place it inside a **steel box (encryption algorithm)**.
2. You lock the box with a **physical padlock (key)**.
3. The courier (untrusted network) carries the box across the city.
4. Only your friend possesses the matching key to unlock and read the diary (decryption).

In this analogy:
- The **diary = Plaintext**
- The **locked box = Ciphertext**
- The **padlock and key = Cryptographic key**
- The **locking mechanism = Encryption algorithm**
- The **unlocking mechanism = Decryption algorithm**
- The **courier = Untrusted network (Internet)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In modern cryptography, the security of the system must depend ONLY on the secrecy of the **key**, NOT on the secrecy of the algorithm. This is known as **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883)**, which remains the bedrock of KTU-aligned network security design.

## 1.4 The General Cryptographic Model

A network cryptographic system is a quintuple **(P, C, K, E, D)** where:

- **P** = set of all possible plaintexts
- **C** = set of all possible ciphertexts
- **K** = set of all possible keys
- **E** = encryption function family: $E_k : P \rightarrow C$
- **D** = decryption function family: $D_k : C \rightarrow P$

The fundamental relationship that must always hold for correctness is:

$$D_k(E_k(P)) = P$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Branches of Cryptography (KTU High-Yield Classification)

Cryptography in network security is broadly classified into **three main branches**. Every KTU exam question on this topic maps directly to one of these branches.

### 2.1.1 Symmetric-Key Cryptography (Secret-Key Cryptography)

The **same secret key K** is used for both encryption and decryption.

$$C = E_K(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_K(C)$$

**Sub-classification:**

- **Block Ciphers** — Encrypt fixed-size blocks of plaintext (e.g., **64 bits for DES**, **128 bits for AES**). Examples: **DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish**.
- **Stream Ciphers** — Encrypt one bit or one byte at a time. Examples: **RC4, ChaCha20, Salsa20**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Highlight:** The standard block cipher design follows the **Feistel Structure (Horst Feistel, 1973)**, which is the basis of **DES**. AES (Rijndael) uses a **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)** instead.

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric-Key Cryptography (Public-Key Cryptography)

A **pair of mathematically related keys** is used: a **Public Key (e)** for encryption and a **Private Key (d)** for decryption.

$$C = E_{e}(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_{d}(C)$$

The most foundational algorithms are:
- **RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman, 1977)** — based on the difficulty of factoring large primes.
- **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (1976)** — based on the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP).
- **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** — based on the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP).

### 2.1.3 Cryptographic Hash Functions

A one-way mathematical function that converts an arbitrary-length input into a **fixed-length output** called a *message digest* or *hash value*.

$$h = H(M)$$

Properties of a secure hash function:
1. **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same output.
2. **Pre-image resistance** — Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$.
3. **Second pre-image resistance** — Given $M_1$, it is hard to find $M_2 \ne M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
4. **Collision resistance** — It is hard to find any two inputs $M_1 \ne M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

Standard hash functions: **MD5 (128-bit, broken)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit, deprecated)**, **SHA-256, SHA-3 (Keccak)**.

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix — Symmetric vs Asymmetric Cryptography

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Keys** | Single shared key | Key pair (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slower (100–1000x slower) |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult (must be shared secretly) | Easy (public key is open) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (n users need n(n-1)/2 keys) | Excellent (n users need only 2n keys) |
| **Confidentiality** | Yes | Yes |
| **Authentication** | No (in pure form) | Yes (via digital signatures) |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, RC4 | RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECC |
| **Key Size (Secure)** | 128–256 bits | 2048–4096 bits (RSA) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Essential Equations)

| Equation | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $C \equiv P^e \pmod{n}$ | RSA Encryption | $P$ = plaintext, $e$ = public exponent |
| $P \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | RSA Decryption | $d$ = private exponent |
| $n = p \cdot q$ | RSA Modulus | $p, q$ = large prime numbers |
| $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's Totient for RSA | $\phi$ = Euler's totient function |
| $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Key Pair Relation | $e, d$ are modular inverses |
| $A \equiv g^a \pmod{p}$ | Diffie-Hellman Alice's Public Key | $g$ = generator, $a$ = Alice's private key |
| $B \equiv g^b \pmod{p}$ | Diffie-Hellman Bob's Public Key | $b$ = Bob's private key |
| $S \equiv B^a \equiv A^b \pmod{p}$ | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $S$ = shared secret |
| $N_b = \log_2 \left(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\right)$ | Symmetric Key Count | $N_b$ = keys for $n$ users |
| $N_a = 2n$ | Asymmetric Key Count | $N_a$ = keys for $n$ users |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Symmetric ciphers like **AES-256** secure HTTPS bulk traffic; **RSA-2048** is used in the TLS handshake to safely exchange the symmetric key. Hash functions like **SHA-256** underpin digital signatures, blockchain (Bitcoin), and password storage. Cryptography protects e-banking, WhatsApp (Signal Protocol), UPI transactions, and Aadhaar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Foundational Math: Modular Arithmetic Refresher

Before tackling real cryptographic algorithms, KTU expects fluency in modular arithmetic. The modulo operation returns the remainder after division.

$$a \equiv b \pmod{m} \iff m \mid (a-b)$$

**Worked Example:** Compute $17 \bmod 5$.
- Divide 17 by 5: $17 = 5 \cdot 3 + 2$.
- The remainder is **2**.
- Therefore: $17 \equiv 2 \pmod{5}$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: RSA Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

**RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977)** is the most tested public-key algorithm in KTU exams. Below is the full derivation logic, exactly as a KTU external examiner would expect.

### Step 1 — Key Generation

Choose **two distinct large primes** $p$ and $q$. For KTU problems, we use small primes to make manual computation feasible.

Let $p = 61$ and $q = 53$.

**Step 1.1 — Compute the modulus $n$:**

$$n = p \cdot q = 61 \cdot 53$$

$$\begin{aligned}
61 \cdot 53 &= 61 \cdot (50 + 3) \\
&= 3050 + 183 \\
&= 3233
\end{aligned}$$

So $n = 3233$.

**Step 1.2 — Compute Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$:**

For a product of two distinct primes, $\phi(n)$ is:

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(3233) &= (61 - 1)(53 - 1) \\
&= 60 \cdot 52 \\
&= 3120
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1.3 — Choose public exponent $e$:**

$e$ must satisfy two conditions:
- $1 < e < \phi(n)$, i.e., $1 < e < 3120$.
- $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, i.e., $e$ is coprime to $3120$.

A standard choice is $e = 17$. Verify: $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$. ✓

**Step 1.4 — Compute private exponent $d$:**

$d$ is the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

$$17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
3120 &= 17 \cdot 183 + 9 \\
17 &= 9 \cdot 1 + 8 \\
9 &= 8 \cdot 1 + 1 \\
8 &= 1 \cdot 8 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

Back-substitution to find $1 = 9 - 8 \cdot 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 9 - (17 - 9 \cdot 1) \cdot 1 \\
&= 2 \cdot 9 - 1 \cdot 17 \\
&= 2 \cdot (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) - 17 \\
&= 2 \cdot 3120 - 367 \cdot 17
\end{aligned}$$

So $-367 \cdot 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$, meaning $d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 \equiv 2753 \pmod{3120}$.

**Verify:** $17 \cdot 2753 = 46801 = 15 \cdot 3120 + 1 = 46800 + 1$. ✓

**Public Key:** $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$. **Private Key:** $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$.

### Step 2 — Encryption

Suppose Alice wants to send plaintext message $M = 65$ to Bob. She uses Bob's public key $(17, 3233)$:

$$C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$$

$$C \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233}$$

**Modular exponentiation using Square-and-Multiply:**
- $65^1 = 65$
- $65^2 = 4225 \equiv 4225 - 1 \cdot 3233 = 992 \pmod{3233}$
- $65^4 \equiv 992^2 = 984064 \equiv 984064 \bmod 3233$. $984064 / 3233 \approx 304.4$. $304 \cdot 3233 = 982832$. $984064 - 982832 = 1232$. So $65^4 \equiv 1232$.
- $65^8 \equiv 1232^2 = 1517824 \pmod{3233}$. $1517824 / 3233 \approx 469.5$. $469 \cdot 3233 = 1516277$. $1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$. So $65^8 \equiv 1547$.
- $65^{16} \equiv 1547^2 = 2393209 \pmod{3233}$. $2393209 / 3233 \approx 740.3$. $740 \cdot 3233 = 2392420$. $2393209 - 2392420 = 789$. So $65^{16} \equiv 789$.

Now expand $17 = 16 + 1$:

$$65^{17} = 65^{16} \cdot 65^1 \equiv 789 \cdot 65 = 51285 \pmod{3233}$$

$$51285 / 3233 \approx 15.86 \implies 15 \cdot 3233 = 48495$$

$$51285 - 48495 = 2790$$

$$\boxed{C = 2790}$$

### Step 3 — Decryption

Bob receives $C = 2790$ and uses his private key $(2753, 3233)$:

$$M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$$

$$M \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$

Applying the same Square-and-Multiply technique and CRT (Chinese Remainder Theorem) acceleration, the result is $M \equiv 65 \pmod{3233}$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Proof of RSA Correctness (KTU Theory Question):** RSA works because Euler's theorem guarantees that for $M$ coprime to $n$:
> $$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$
> Since $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$:
> $$C^d \equiv (M^e)^d = M^{ed} = M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv M \cdot 1^k = M \pmod{n}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange

**Scenario:** Alice and Bob want to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel.

**Public Parameters (known to all):** Prime $p = 23$, primitive root $g = 5$.

**Step 1 — Alice picks private key** $a = 6$.

**Step 2 — Bob picks private key** $b = 15$.

**Step 3 — Alice computes her public key $A$:**

$$A \equiv g^a \pmod{p} = 5^6 \pmod{23}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
5^1 &= 5 \\
5^2 &= 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \\
5^3 &= 5^2 \cdot 5 \equiv 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \pmod{23} \\
5^4 &\equiv 10 \cdot 5 = 50 \equiv 4 \pmod{23} \\
5^5 &\equiv 4 \cdot 5 = 20 \pmod{23} \\
5^6 &\equiv 20 \cdot 5 = 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \cdot 23 = 8 \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}$$

So $A = 8$.

**Step 4 — Bob computes his public key $B$:**

$$B \equiv g^b \pmod{p} = 5^{15} \pmod{23}$$

Continuing the chain: $5^7 \equiv 40 \equiv 17$, $5^8 \equiv 85 \equiv 16$, $5^9 \equiv 80 \equiv 11$, $5^{10} \equiv 55 \equiv 9$, $5^{11} \equiv 45 \equiv 22$, $5^{12} \equiv 110 \equiv 18$, $5^{13} \equiv 90 \equiv 21$, $5^{14} \equiv 105 \equiv 13$, $5^{15} \equiv 65 \equiv 19$.

So $B = 19$.

**Step 5 — Exchange $A$ and $B$ over the public channel.**

**Step 6 — Alice computes shared secret:**

$$S_A \equiv B^a \pmod{p} = 19^6 \pmod{23}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
19^1 &\equiv 19 \\
19^2 &\equiv 361 \equiv 361 - 15 \cdot 23 = 16 \\
19^3 &\equiv 16 \cdot 19 = 304 \equiv 304 - 13 \cdot 23 = 5 \\
19^4 &\equiv 5 \cdot 19 = 95 \equiv 95 - 4 \cdot 23 = 3 \\
19^5 &\equiv 3 \cdot 19 = 57 \equiv 57 - 2 \cdot 23 = 11 \\
19^6 &\equiv 11 \cdot 19 = 209 \equiv 209 - 9 \cdot 23 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

So $S_A = 2$.

**Step 7 — Bob computes shared secret:**

$$S_B \equiv A^b \pmod{p} = 8^{15} \pmod{23}$$

Using $8^1 = 8$, $8^2 = 64 \equiv 18$, $8^3 \equiv 18 \cdot 8 = 144 \equiv 144 - 6 \cdot 23 = 6$, $8^4 \equiv 6 \cdot 8 = 48 \equiv 2$, $8^5 \equiv 16$, $8^6 \equiv 16 \cdot 8 = 128 \equiv 128 - 5 \cdot 23 = 13$, $8^7 \equiv 13 \cdot 8 = 104 \equiv 12$, $8^8 \equiv 12 \cdot 8 = 96 \equiv 4$, $8^9 \equiv 32 \equiv 9$, $8^{10} \equiv 72 \equiv 3$, $8^{11} \equiv 24 \equiv 1$, $8^{12} \equiv 8$, $8^{13} \equiv 18$, $8^{14} \equiv 6$, $8^{15} \equiv 2$.

So $S_B = 2$. ✓ **Both parties share the secret $S = 2$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Eve (the eavesdropper) sees only $p=23$, $g=5$, $A=8$, $B=19$.** To find $a$ from $A$, she must solve the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**: find $a$ such that $5^a \equiv 8 \pmod{23}$. For $p=23$ this is easy by brute force, but for a 2048-bit prime, the DLP is computationally infeasible (requires ~$2^{112}$ operations).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Cryptographic Attacks

| Attack Type | Attacker Capability | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA)** | Has only ciphertext(s) | Frequency analysis on Caesar cipher |
| **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA)** | Has pairs of (P, C) | Breaking WWII Enigma with known cribs |
| **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA)** | Can encrypt chosen plaintexts | Differential cryptanalysis on DES |
| **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA)** | Can decrypt chosen ciphertexts | RSA-OAEP padding oracle attack |
| **Brute-Force Attack** | Tries all possible keys | AES-128 has $2^{128}$ key space |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric Key Cryptography — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext Message P] --> B[Encryption Algorithm E]
    K1[Shared Secret Key K] --> B
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Transmission over Untrusted Network]
    D --> E[Decryption Algorithm D]
    K2[Shared Secret Key K] --> E
    E --> F[Recovered Plaintext P]
```

## 4.2 Asymmetric Key Cryptography (RSA) — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sender Plaintext P] --> B[Encryption using Public Key e of Receiver]
    PK1[Public Key e, n] --> B
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Insecure Channel Transmission]
    D --> E[Decryption using Private Key d of Receiver]
    PK2[Private Key d, n] --> E
    E --> F[Receiver Plaintext P]
```

## 4.3 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Block Level Interaction Map

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Channel
    participant Bob

    Channel->>Alice: Public p, g
    Channel->>Bob: Public p, g
    Alice->>Alice: Choose private a; Compute A = g^a mod p
    Bob->>Bob: Choose private b; Compute B = g^b mod p
    Alice->>Channel: Send A
    Channel->>Bob: Forward A
    Bob->>Channel: Send B
    Channel->>Alice: Forward B
    Alice->>Alice: Compute S = B^a mod p
    Bob->>Bob: Compute S = A^b mod p
    Note over Alice,Bob: Shared Secret S Established
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hybrid Cryptosystem (TLS Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SenderSide[Sender System]
        A1[Plaintext Bulk Data] --> A2[SHA-256 Hashing for Integrity]
        A1 --> A3[AES-256 Symmetric Encryption]
        KEY1[Random AES Session Key Ks] --> A3
        A2 --> A4[RSA-2048 Encrypt Ks using Receivers Public Key]
        KEY2[Receivers Public Key e, n] --> A4
        A3 --> A5[Ciphertext C1]
        A4 --> A6[Encrypted Session Key C2]
    end

    A5 --> NET{Insecure Network}
    A6 --> NET

    NET --> B1[Received Ciphertext C1]
    NET --> B2[Received Encrypted Key C2]

    subgraph ReceiverSide[Receiver System]
        B2 --> B3[RSA Private Key Decryption to Recover Ks]
        KEY3[Receivers Private Key d, n] --> B3
        B1 --> B4[AES-256 Decryption with Ks]
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B5[Decrypted Plaintext]
        B5 --> B6[Hash Verification for Integrity]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** The above block diagram represents the **TLS 1.3 Handshake** used by HTTPS, e-banking, and UPI. RSA securely transmits an AES key, and AES then encrypts the bulk data — combining the speed of symmetric ciphers with the key-distribution convenience of asymmetric ciphers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Symmetric and Asymmetric key cryptography. List any two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| Keys Used | Single shared secret key | Key pair (public + private) |
| Speed | Faster (bulk data) | Slower (~1000x) |
| Key Distribution | Difficult | Easy (public key is open) |
| Scalability | $O(n^2)$ keys needed | $O(n)$ keys needed |

- **Symmetric examples:** AES, DES, 3DES, RC4, Blowfish.
- **Asymmetric examples:** RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECC, ElGamal.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a cryptographic hash function. List its three essential security properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a mathematical algorithm that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size bit string (the hash value or message digest).

**Three essential properties:**
1. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Second pre-image resistance:** Given $M_1$, it is hard to find $M_2 \ne M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
3. **Collision resistance:** It is hard to find any two distinct inputs $M_1, M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Choice (Internal):** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from this module (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RSA algorithm in detail. Perform RSA encryption and decryption for $P = 7$, $p = 11$, $q = 13$, $e = 7$.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm. Identify the mathematical problem on which its security relies and discuss the **Man-in-the-Middle attack** on Diffie-Hellman.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**RSA Key Generation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Compute $n = p \cdot q = 11 \cdot 13 = 143$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 10 \cdot 12 = 120$.

**Step 3:** Verify $e = 7$: Check $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$. ✓ (7 is coprime to 120).

**Step 4:** Compute $d$ such that $7 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
- $120 = 7 \cdot 17 + 1$
- Back-substitute: $1 = 120 - 7 \cdot 17$
- So $-17 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$, meaning $d \equiv -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$.

**Verify:** $7 \cdot 103 = 721 = 6 \cdot 120 + 1$. ✓

**Encryption of $P = 7$:**

$$C \equiv P^e \pmod{n} = 7^7 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^2 = 49, \quad 7^3 = 343 \equiv 343 - 2 \cdot 143 = 57, \quad 7^4 \equiv 57 \cdot 7 = 399 \equiv 399 - 2 \cdot 143 = 113$$

$$7^7 = 7^4 \cdot 7^2 \cdot 7^1 \equiv 113 \cdot 49 \cdot 7 \pmod{143}$$

$$113 \cdot 49 = 5537 \pmod{143}$$

$5537 / 143 \approx 38.7$, $38 \cdot 143 = 5434$, $5537 - 5434 = 103$. So $113 \cdot 49 \equiv 103$.

$$103 \cdot 7 = 721 \pmod{143} = 721 - 5 \cdot 143 = 6$$

$$\boxed{C = 6}$$

**Decryption of $C = 6$:**

$$P \equiv C^d \pmod{n} = 6^{103} \pmod{143}$$

Use Euler's theorem shortcut: $6^{120} \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$, so $6^{103} \equiv 6^{-17} \pmod{143}$.

First compute $6^{17} \pmod{143}$:
$6^2 = 36$, $6^4 = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 9 \cdot 143 = 9$, $6^8 \equiv 9^2 = 81$, $6^{16} \equiv 81^2 = 6561 \equiv 6561 - 45 \cdot 143 = 126$.

$6^{17} = 6^{16} \cdot 6 \equiv 126 \cdot 6 = 756 \equiv 756 - 5 \cdot 143 = 41$.

So $6^{17} \equiv 41 \pmod{143}$. Need inverse of 41 mod 143:
$41 \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$. Solve: $143 = 3 \cdot 41 + 20$, $41 = 2 \cdot 20 + 1$, so $1 = 41 - 2 \cdot 20 = 41 - 2(143 - 3 \cdot 41) = 7 \cdot 41 - 2 \cdot 143$.

Thus $41^{-1} \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$. Check: $41 \cdot 7 = 287 = 2 \cdot 143 + 1$. ✓

$$6^{-17} \equiv 41^{-1} \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$$

$$\boxed{P = 7} \quad \text{(Decryption successful)}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Diffie-Hellman Algorithm Steps:**
1. Global public parameters are chosen: a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$.
2. Alice picks a private key $a$ and computes public key $A = g^a \pmod{p}$.
3. Bob picks a private key $b$ and computes public key $B = g^b \pmod{p}$.
4. Alice and Bob exchange $A$ and $B$ over the insecure channel.
5. Alice computes shared secret $S = B^a \pmod{p}$.
6. Bob computes shared secret $S = A^b \pmod{p}$.
7. Both arrive at the same $S$ because $(g^b)^a \equiv (g^a)^b \pmod{p}$.

**Mathematical Foundation — Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):**
Given $p$, $g$, $A$, an attacker must find $a$ such that $g^a \equiv A \pmod{p}$. For large primes (≥ 2048 bits), no polynomial-time algorithm is known, making DLP computationally hard.

**Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack on Diffie-Hellman:**
The protocol has no built-in authentication. An attacker **Mallory** can intercept the channel:
- Mallory intercepts Alice's $A$ and sends her own $A' = g^{a'} \pmod{p}$ to Bob.
- Mallory intercepts Bob's $B$ and sends her own $B' = g^{b'} \pmod{p}$ to Alice.
- Alice computes $S_1 = (B')^a$ (shared with Mallory).
- Bob computes $S_2 = (A')^b$ (shared with Mallory).
- Mallory can now decrypt, modify, and re-encrypt all traffic between Alice and Bob.

**Countermeasure:** Use **digital signatures** or **certificates (X.509)** to authenticate the parties before key exchange (as done in TLS via Certificate Authority chains).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **DES (Data Encryption Standard)** algorithm with a neat block diagram. Describe the **Feistel Structure** and discuss the significance of the **S-Boxes**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** algorithm. Compare AES with DES in terms of key size, block size, rounds, and security.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Data Encryption Standard (DES):**
- **Type:** Symmetric block cipher.
- **Block size:** 64 bits.
- **Key size:** 64 bits (56 effective + 8 parity bits).
- **Rounds:** 16 Feistel rounds.
- **Adopted by NIST in 1977** (now deprecated due to short key length).

**Block Diagram of DES:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[64-bit Plaintext] --> IP[Initial Permutation IP]
    IP --> L0[Left Half L0 32 bits]
    IP --> R0[Right Half R0 32 bits]
    L0 --> L1[L1]
    R0 --> F1[Feistel Function with Round Key K1]
    R0 --> L1
    F1 --> R1[R1]
    L1 --> L2[L2]
    R1 --> F2[Feistel Function with Round Key K2]
    R1 --> L2
    F2 --> R2[R2]
    L2 --> LN[...continue 16 rounds...]
    RN --> SW[Swap and Combine]
    LN --> SW
    SW --> FP[Final Permutation FP inverse of IP]
    FP --> C[64-bit Ciphertext]
```

**Feistel Structure (The Core of DES):**
Each round splits the 64-bit block into two halves (L, R) and applies:

$$L_{i} = R_{i-1}$$
$$R_{i} = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

where $F$ is the round function and $K_i$ is the round key derived from the main 56-bit key via **key scheduling**. The beauty of Feistel is that **decryption uses the same algorithm with reversed round keys**.

**Significance of S-Boxes (Substitution Boxes):**
- S-Boxes are the **only non-linear component** in DES, providing **confusion** (Shannon's principle).
- They substitute 6-bit inputs into 4-bit outputs using lookup tables.
- They are the **primary source of cryptographic strength** in DES. Without S-Boxes, DES would be trivially breakable via linear algebra.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (DES):** Students often forget that the **swap at the end of round 16 is undone** before applying the final permutation. Failing to mention this loses **1 mark** in the diagram explanation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Advanced Encryption Standard (AES):**
- **Type:** Symmetric block cipher (Substitution-Permutation Network).
- **Standard:** FIPS 197 (2001), winner of NIST competition — algorithm **Rijndael** by Vincent Rijmen and Joan Daemen.
- **Block size:** 128 bits (fixed).
- **Key sizes:** 128, 192, or 256 bits.
- **Rounds:** 10, 12, or 14 respectively.

**AES Round Operations (per round, except the last):**
1. **SubBytes** — Byte-wise non-linear substitution using S-Box.
2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclic row shift of the 4×4 state matrix.
3. **MixColumns** — Linear mixing of columns using GF(2^8) arithmetic.
4. **AddRoundKey** — XOR with the round key.

The **final round** skips MixColumns.

**DES vs AES Comparison:**

| Parameter | DES | AES |
|---|---|---|
| **Year Adopted** | 1977 | 2001 |
| **Algorithm Origin** | IBM / NSA | Rijmen & Daemen |
| **Structure** | Feistel Network | Substitution-Permutation Network |
| **Block Size** | 64 bits | 128 bits |
| **Key Size** | 56 bits (effective) | 128 / 192 / 256 bits |
| **Rounds** | 16 | 10 / 12 / 14 |
| **Security Today** | Broken (brute force feasible) | Secure (no known practical attack) |
| **Speed** | Slower | Faster (even in software) |
| **S-Boxes** | 8 different S-Boxes | 1 S-Box (16×16 lookup) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (AES):** Students often wrongly state that AES has 16 rounds for *all* key sizes. Correct: **10 rounds for 128-bit, 12 for 192-bit, 14 for 256-bit** keys. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**
> - **Define cryptography** as the science of securing communication by transforming plaintext into ciphertext using keys.
> - **Three branches:** Symmetric, Asymmetric, Hash functions. Know one example and one property for each.
> - **Four security services:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation.
> - **Kerckhoffs's Principle:** The algorithm can be public; only the key must be secret.
> - **RSA Key Generation:** $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, choose $e$ with $\gcd(e,\phi)=1$, compute $d$ as modular inverse.
> - **RSA Encryption/Decryption:** $C = P^e \bmod n$ and $P = C^d \bmod n$.
> - **Diffie-Hellman:** Public parameters $(p, g)$, private keys $(a, b)$, public keys $(A, B)$, shared secret $S = B^a \equiv A^b \pmod{p}$.
> - **DLP & MITM:** Security rests on Discrete Logarithm Problem; vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle without authentication.
> - **DES:** 64-bit block, 56-bit key, 16 Feistel rounds, S-Boxes provide confusion.
> - **AES:** 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit keys, 10/12/14 rounds, SPN structure.
> - **Hash properties:** Pre-image resistance, Second pre-image resistance, Collision resistance.
> - **Numerical Skills:** Practice Square-and-Multiply modular exponentiation — it appears in **almost every RSA/DF question**.
> - **Symmetric key count** for $n$ users: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$. **Asymmetric key count:** $2n$.
> - **Remember to state units and security bits** in every answer; KTU examiners reward precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Encryption** is the cryptographic process of encoding data streams traversing a communication network (LAN, WAN, Internet, wireless) using mathematical algorithms, ensuring that intercepted transmissions remain unintelligible to unauthorized parties. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, network encryption is broadly classified into:

1. **Symmetric (Secret-Key) Encryption** – Same key used for encryption and decryption (e.g., **AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish**).
2. **Asymmetric (Public-Key) Encryption** – A mathematically paired key pair (`public` and `private`) is used (e.g., **RSA, ElGamal, ECC**).
3. **Hybrid Encryption** – Combines both (e.g., **TLS Handshake** uses RSA to exchange an AES session key).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Network Encryption covers classical and modern symmetric ciphers, public-key cryptosystems, key distribution mechanisms, message authentication codes, hash functions, digital signatures, and applied network security protocols (SSL/TLS, IPsec, PGP).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Locked Mailbox" Analogy:**
> Imagine Alice wants to send a confidential letter to Bob across a city full of strangers.
> - **Symmetric Encryption** = Alice and Bob own **one identical physical key** to a lockbox. They must meet secretly beforehand to copy the key. Fast, but key exchange is risky.
> - **Asymmetric Encryption** = Bob installs a **mailbox with two slots**: anyone can drop a letter in (using the public slot, which is open), but only Bob's **private key** can open it and read. Brilliant for unknown senders, but slow.
> - **Hybrid Encryption (TLS)** = Alice uses Bob's slow but secure **public mailbox** to send a fast **shared key**, then both switch to the fast lockbox for the rest of the conversation.

### 1.3 Core Metrics of a Secure Cipher

- **Confidentiality** – Only authorized parties can read the plaintext.
- **Integrity** – Data has not been tampered with in transit.
- **Authentication** – The sender's identity is verified.
- **Non-repudiation** – The sender cannot later deny sending the message.
- **Key Length**: Modern standard is **128-bit (AES)** to **256-bit** minimum. RSA keys are typically **2048-bit** or higher.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Cryptographic Goals — "CIA + N":** **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication, **N**on-repudiation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symmetric Encryption (Block Ciphers)

### 2.1.1 Data Encryption Standard (DES)
- **Block Size:** 64 bits
- **Key Size:** **56 bits** (effective, after 8 parity bits stripped from 64-bit input)
- **Rounds:** 16 Feistel rounds
- **Status:** **Obsolete** (cracked in hours by modern hardware)

### 2.1.2 Triple DES (3DES / TDEA)
- **Operation:** Apply DES three times using 2 or 3 keys → `C = E_{K3}(D_{K2}(E_{K1}(P)))`
- **Effective Key Size:** **112 bits** (2-key) or **168 bits** (3-key)
- **Status:** Being phased out; slow due to triple encryption.

### 2.1.3 Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)
- **Block Size:** **128 bits**
- **Key Sizes:** **128, 192, or 256 bits**
- **Rounds:** 10 (128-bit), 12 (192-bit), 14 (256-bit)
- **Structure:** Substitution–Permutation Network (SPN), NOT Feistel.

### 2.1.4 Block Cipher Modes of Operation

| Mode | Full Name | Initialization Vector (IV) | Error Propagation | Parallelizable? | KTU Use Case |
|------|-----------|----------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **ECB** | Electronic Codebook | No | None (per block) | Yes | Insecure for images (Tux logo problem) |
| **CBC** | Cipher Block Chaining | Required (unique per msg) | Yes (one block affects next) | Decrypt only | TLS, IPsec |
| **CFB** | Cipher Feedback | Required | Yes | No | Stream conversion |
| **OFB** | Output Feedback | Required | No | No | Stream conversion (no error prop.) |
| **CTR** | Counter | Required (nonce + counter) | No | Yes (full) | Modern standard (GCM uses CTR) |

## 2.2 Asymmetric (Public-Key) Encryption

### 2.2.1 RSA Algorithm
Based on the computational hardness of factoring the product of two large primes.
- **Key Generation:**
  1. Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.
  2. Compute $n = p \times q$ (modulus).
  3. Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ (Euler's totient).
  4. Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
  5. Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- **Public Key:** $(e, n)$
- **Private Key:** $(d, n)$
- **Encryption:** $C = M^e \bmod n$
- **Decryption:** $M = C^d \bmod n$

### 2.2.2 Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange
Allows two parties to derive a **shared secret over an insecure channel** without prior key sharing. Based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

- **Public parameters:** Prime $p$, generator $g$.
- Alice chooses secret $a$, sends $A = g^a \bmod p$.
- Bob chooses secret $b$, sends $B = g^b \bmod p$.
- **Shared secret:** $K = B^a \bmod p = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Vulnerability:** Diffie-Hellman is vulnerable to **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks without authentication. Mitigated by **authenticated DH** (e.g., ECDHE with digital certificates in TLS 1.3).

### 2.2.3 ElGamal Encryption
- **Public Key:** $(p, g, y)$ where $y = g^x \bmod p$
- **Private Key:** $x$
- **Encryption** of message $M$: choose random $k$, send $(c_1, c_2) = (g^k \bmod p,\; M \cdot y^k \bmod p)$
- **Decryption:** $M = c_2 \cdot (c_1^x)^{-1} \bmod p$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Domain | Notes |
|---|-------------------|--------|-------|
| 1 | $C = M^e \bmod n$ | RSA Encryption | $M < n$ |
| 2 | $M = C^d \bmod n$ | RSA Decryption | |
| 3 | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | RSA Key Relation | |
| 4 | $\phi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's Totient (RSA) | Only for distinct primes |
| 5 | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret | |
| 6 | $h_H(M) = H(K \oplus \text{opad} \,\vert\vert\, H(K \oplus \text{ipad} \,\vert\vert\, M))$ | HMAC | Inner/Outer padding |
| 7 | Feistel Round: $L_i = R_{i-1}$, $R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$ | DES Structure | Swapped halves |
| 8 | AES S-Box: $b' = b \oplus (b \lll 1) \oplus (b \lll 2) \oplus (b \lll 3) \oplus (b \lll 4) \oplus 0x63$ | Affine Transform | Used in SubBytes step |
| 9 | CTR Mode: $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ | Stream Mode | $T_i$ = counter block |
| 10 | CBC Mode: $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ | Chaining Mode | $C_0 = IV$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Network encryption underpins **HTTPS (TLS 1.3)**, **VPNs (IPsec/SSL)**, **Wi-Fi (WPA3)**, **SSH**, **PGP email**, and **blockchain wallets**. A B.Tech CS graduate must understand these to design secure distributed systems and pass KTU's ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

## 2.4 Hash Functions & Message Authentication

| Algorithm | Output Size | Status |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| MD5 | 128 bits | **Broken** (collisions found) |
| SHA-1 | 160 bits | **Deprecated** by NIST (2017) |
| SHA-256 | 256 bits | **Secure & Recommended** |
| SHA-3 (Keccak) | 224/256/384/512 | Modern alternative |

**HMAC (Hash-based MAC):** Provides integrity + authentication by hashing `H((K ⊕ opad) || H((K ⊕ ipad) || M))`.
- `ipad = 0x36` repeated to block size
- `opad = 0x5C` repeated to block size

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always specify *what* is being authenticated. Digital signatures authenticate the **sender's identity + message integrity**, while MACs only authenticate **message integrity (and a shared secret holder)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA End-to-End Worked Example

**Problem:** Alice wants to send plaintext $M = 7$ to Bob. Bob chooses primes $p = 11$, $q = 13$, and $e = 7$. Demonstrate the full RSA encryption and decryption cycle.

### Step 1: Compute the modulus $n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= p \times q \\
  &= 11 \times 13 \\
  &= 143
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The modulus $n$ is published in the public key. Its size dictates the strength of RSA — factoring $n$ back into $p$ and $q$ must be computationally infeasible.

### Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) &= (p - 1)(q - 1) \\
        &= (11 - 1)(13 - 1) \\
        &= 10 \times 12 \\
        &= 120
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $\phi(n)$ counts integers $< n$ that are coprime with $n$. For $n = pq$, only the multiples of $p$ and $q$ are excluded, giving the product above.

### Step 3: Verify that $e$ is coprime with $\phi(n)$

$$
\gcd(7, 120) = 1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

> **Logic:** If $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) \neq 1$, no modular inverse exists, and the chosen $e$ is invalid.

### Step 4: Compute the private exponent $d$

We need $d$ such that:

$$
e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}
$$

Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
120 &= 7 \cdot 17 + 1 \\
1   &= 120 - 7 \cdot 17
\end{aligned}
$$

Rewriting in modular form:

$$
7 \cdot (-17) \equiv 1 \pmod{120}
$$

$$
7 \cdot 103 \equiv 1 \pmod{120}
$$

Therefore: $d = 103$.

### Step 5: Encryption (Alice encrypts $M = 7$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= M^e \bmod n \\
  &= 7^7 \bmod 143
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute step-by-step using successive squaring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
7^1 &= 7 \\
7^2 &= 49 \\
7^4 &= 49^2 = 2401 \equiv 2401 - 16(143) = 2401 - 2288 = 113 \pmod{143} \\
7^7 &= 7^4 \cdot 7^2 \cdot 7^1 = 113 \cdot 49 \cdot 7 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

Intermediate calculation:

$$
113 \cdot 49 = 5537 \equiv 5537 - 38(143) = 5537 - 5434 = 103 \pmod{143}
$$

Final multiplication:

$$
C = 103 \cdot 7 = 721 \equiv 721 - 5(143) = 721 - 715 = 6 \pmod{143}
$$

**Ciphertext $C = 6$.**

### Step 6: Decryption (Bob recovers $M$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= C^d \bmod n \\
  &= 6^{103} \bmod 143
\end{aligned}
$$

We use Fermat/Euler's theorem: $M = C^d \equiv M^{ed} \pmod{n}$, and $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so $M^{ed} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ by Euler's theorem.

Decompose $103$ in binary: $103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1$.

Repeated squaring mod 143:

$$
\begin{aligned}
6^1   &= 6 \\
6^2   &= 36 \\
6^4   &= 36^2 = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 9(143) = 1296 - 1287 = 9 \pmod{143} \\
6^8   &= 9^2 = 81 \pmod{143} \\
6^{16} &= 81^2 = 6561 \equiv 6561 - 45(143) = 6561 - 6435 = 126 \equiv -17 \pmod{143} \\
6^{32} &= (-17)^2 = 289 \equiv 289 - 2(143) = 3 \pmod{143} \\
6^{64} &= 3^2 = 9 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combine:

$$
\begin{aligned}
6^{103} &= 6^{64} \cdot 6^{32} \cdot 6^4 \cdot 6^2 \cdot 6^1 \\
        &= 9 \cdot 3 \cdot 9 \cdot 36 \cdot 6 \pmod{143} \\
        &= (9 \cdot 9) \cdot (3 \cdot 36) \cdot 6 \\
        &= 81 \cdot 108 \cdot 6 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final reduction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
81 \cdot 108 &= 8748 \equiv 8748 - 61(143) = 8748 - 8723 = 25 \pmod{143} \\
25 \cdot 6 &= 150 \equiv 150 - 143 = 7 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recovered plaintext: $M = 7$ ✓** — The RSA round-trip is verified.

## 3.2 Diffie–Hellman Analytical Walkthrough

**Problem:** $p = 23$, $g = 5$. Alice picks $a = 6$, Bob picks $b = 15$. Compute the shared secret.

### Step 1: Compute Alice's public value

$$
A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23
$$

Step by step:

$$
5^2 = 25 \equiv 2,\quad 5^4 \equiv 4,\quad 5^6 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}
$$

So $A = 8$.

### Step 2: Compute Bob's public value

$$
B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23
$$

Using $5^{11} \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$ (Fermat), so $5^{15} = 5^{11} \cdot 5^4 \equiv 1 \cdot 4 = 4 \pmod{23}$.

So $B = 4$.

### Step 3: Both compute shared secret

$$
K_{Alice} = B^a \bmod p = 4^6 \bmod 23
$$

$$
4^2 = 16,\quad 4^3 = 64 \equiv 18,\quad 4^6 = 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14(23) = 324 - 322 = 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
K_{Bob} = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23
$$

Note $8 \equiv 2^3$, so $8^{15} = 2^{45}$. Since $2^{11} \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$, $2^{45} = 2^{44} \cdot 2 = (2^{11})^4 \cdot 2 \equiv 1 \cdot 2 = 2 \pmod{23}$.

**Shared secret: $K = 2$** — Both parties agree. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Justification |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| Bulk data encryption (files, disks) | **AES-256 (CBC or GCM)** | Speed + security; GCM adds authentication |
| Secure key exchange | **RSA-2048 or ECDH** | Leverages hardness of factoring / DLP |
| Digital signatures on documents | **RSA-PSS or ECDSA** | Non-repudiation + integrity |
| Web HTTPS handshake | **TLS 1.3 with X25519 (ECDH) + AES-256-GCM** | Modern best practice |
| Password hashing | **bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt** | Slow + salted; resists brute force |
| Message integrity check | **SHA-256 or HMAC-SHA256** | Collision resistance |
| Email encryption (PGP/GPG) | **Hybrid: RSA + AES** | Combines strengths of both |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Encryption Algorithm E]
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Network Transmission]
    D --> E[Decryption Algorithm D]
    E --> F[Recovered Plaintext M]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture: RSA Encryption-Decryption Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Plaintext M] --> B{M less than n?}
    B -- No --> Z[Apply OAEP Padding / Split into blocks]
    Z --> B
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute Ciphertext C = M^e mod n]
    C --> D[Transmit C over Network]
    D --> E[Receiver obtains C]
    E --> F[Apply Private Key d: M = C^d mod n]
    F --> G[Output Recovered M]
    H[Key Generation Module] --> I[Choose primes p, q]
    I --> J[Compute n = pq, phi n]
    J --> K[Choose e, compute d]
    K --> L[Publish Public Key e, n]
    K --> M[Keep Private Key d Secret]
```

## 4.3 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Public Parameters: Prime p, Generator g] --> A1[Alice picks secret a]
    P1 --> B1[Bob picks secret b]
    A1 --> A2[Alice computes A = g^a mod p]
    B1 --> B2[Bob computes B = g^b mod p]
    A2 --> X[Exchange A and B over insecure channel]
    B2 --> X
    X --> A3[Alice computes K = B^a mod p]
    X --> B3[Bob computes K = A^b mod p]
    A3 --> S[Shared Secret K = g^ab mod p]
    B3 --> S
```

## 4.4 TLS 1.3 Hybrid Handshake Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Client Hello with Key Share ECDHE] --> S1[Server Hello with Key Share]
    S1 --> S2[Server Certificate and Signature]
    S2 --> S3[Server Finished - MAC over handshake]
    S3 --> C2[Client Finished - MAC over handshake]
    C2 --> ENC[Both sides derive symmetric session keys]
    ENC --> DATA[Encrypted Application Data via AES-256-GCM]
```

## 4.5 Block Cipher Modes Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    PT[Plaintext Blocks P1 P2 P3] --> M{Select Mode}
    M -- ECB --> ECB[Each block encrypted independently: Ci = E(Pi)]
    M -- CBC --> CBC[C1 = E(P1 XOR IV), Ci = E(Pi XOR C(i-1))]
    M -- CTR --> CTR[Ci = Pi XOR E(Nonce + Counter)]
    M -- OFB --> OFB[Ci = Pi XOR Oi, where Oi = E(O(i-1))]
    M -- CFB --> CFB[Ci = Pi XOR E(C(i-1))]
    ECB --> OUT[Ciphertext Stream]
    CBC --> OUT
    CTR --> OUT
    OFB --> OUT
    CFB --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between Symmetric and Asymmetric key encryption. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Keys used** | Single shared secret key | Public key + mathematically linked private key |
| **Speed** | Fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slow (100–1000× slower) |
| **Key Distribution** | Challenging (must be shared secretly) | Easy (public key is published) |
| **Scalability** | Poor: $n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users | Excellent: only $2n$ keys for $n$ users |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | RSA, ElGamal, ECC, Diffie–Hellman |
| **Primary Use** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |

**Examples (1 mark):** Symmetric → **AES-128**; Asymmetric → **RSA-2048**.

---
