| **Cloud Computing** | AWS Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway using IPSec tunnels |
| **Remote Workforce** | Windows DirectAccess, macOS native IPSec for corporate network access |
| **Industrial IoT** | SCADA system protection between RTUs and control centers |
| **Defense & Government** | Classified network interconnects (e.g., NIC/GIG networks) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** IPSec is **stateless at the application layer**. It is processed by the OS kernel (Linux: `xfrm` framework; Windows: IPSec Driver) before the packet reaches the socket buffer, making it application-transparent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Tunnel Packet Size with ESP-AES-128-SHA1

**Problem Context (Typical KTU Numerical):** A host sends an original IPv4 packet of payload $P_{orig} = 1380$ bytes (TCP segment of 1360 bytes + 20 bytes TCP header). The network uses **ESP Tunnel Mode** with:
- Encryption: AES-128-CBC
- Integrity: HMAC-SHA1-96 (ICV = 12 bytes)
- No explicit IV (CBC uses 16-byte IV from ESP trailer)
- Anti-replay: Sequence number (4 bytes) + SPI (4 bytes)
- Padding to AES block boundary (16 bytes)
- New outer IP header: 20 bytes

**Derivation Walkthrough:**

### Step 1 — Identify each ESP component byte size
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{SPI} &= 4 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{SeqNo} &= 4 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{ICV} &= 12 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{HMAC-SHA1-96 truncation}) \\
L_{IV} &= 16 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{AES-CBC block size}) \\
L_{ESP\ Trailer} &= 2 \text{ bytes (PadLength + NextHeader)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute raw payload to encrypt
The original packet ($P_{orig} = 1380$ bytes) is the ESP payload.

### Step 3 — Compute padding to AES block boundary
The encrypted region must be a multiple of **16 bytes** (AES block size). The encrypted span = Payload + Padding + ESP Trailer (2 bytes).

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{encrypted} &= P_{orig} + L_{Pad} + L_{ESP\ Trailer} \\
L_{encrypted} &= 1380 + L_{Pad} + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Find smallest $L_{Pad} \geq 0$ such that $L_{encrypted} \equiv 0 \pmod{16}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1380 + L_{Pad} + 2 &\equiv 0 \pmod{16} \\
1382 + L_{Pad} &\equiv 0 \pmod{16} \\
1382 \bmod 16 &= 6 \quad (\text{since } 16 \times 86 = 1376, \text{ and } 1382 - 1376 = 6) \\
\therefore L_{Pad} &= 16 - 6 = 10 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute total ESP-protected size
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{ESP\ total} &= L_{SPI} + L_{SeqNo} + L_{encrypted} + L_{ICV} \\
L_{ESP\ total} &= 4 + 4 + (1380 + 10 + 2) + 12 \\
L_{ESP\ total} &= 4 + 4 + 1392 + 12 \\
L_{ESP\ total} &= 1412 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Add new outer IP header (Tunnel Mode)
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Tun} &= L_{ESP\ total} + 20 \\
P_{Tun} &= 1412 + 20 \\
P_{Tun} &= 1432 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify against Ethernet MTU
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTU}_{Ethernet} &= 1500 \text{ bytes} \\
P_{Tun} = 1432 &\leq 1500 \quad \checkmark \text{ (no fragmentation needed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $P_{Tun} > 1500$, the packet must either be fragmented (performance penalty) or **PMTUD (Path MTU Discovery)** must reduce the original MSS.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode — Bit-by-Bit Layout

### 3.2.1 ESP Transport Mode Layout (for IPv4)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Packet:} \quad & [IP_Hdr \parallel TCP_Hdr \parallel Data] \\
\text{ESP Transport:} \quad & [IP_Hdr \parallel ESP_Hdr \parallel TCP_Hdr \parallel Data \parallel ESP_Trailer \parallel ESP_ICV]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 ESP Tunnel Mode Layout (for IPv4)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Packet:} \quad & [IP_Hdr \parallel TCP_Hdr \parallel Data] \\
\text{ESP Tunnel:} \quad & [NewIP_Hdr \parallel ESP_Hdr \parallel IP_Hdr \parallel TCP_Hdr \parallel Data \parallel ESP_Trailer \parallel ESP_ICV]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 AH Transport Mode Layout (for IPv4)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Packet:} \quad & [IP_Hdr \parallel TCP_Hdr \parallel Data] \\
\text{AH Transport:} \quad & [IP_Hdr \parallel AH_Hdr \parallel TCP_Hdr \parallel Data]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **AH authenticates the IP_Hdr (with mutable fields zeroed)**, whereas **ESP leaves the IP_Hdr unauthenticated**. This is why AH alone fails behind NAT — the source IP changes.

## 3.3 IKE Phase 1 Exchange — Exhaustive Message-by-Message Derivation

The KTU examiner frequently asks for the **complete IKEv1 Main Mode exchange (6 messages)**. Here is the exhaustive derivation of each step.

### 3.3.1 IKE Phase 1 Goal
Produce a mutually authenticated **ISAKMP SA (IKE SA)** that protects subsequent Phase 2 exchanges, and derive **SKEYID, SKEYID_e, SKEYID_a, SKEYID_d**.

### 3.3.2 Notation
- $\text{HDR}$: ISAKMP Header (with cookies $C_I, C_R$)
- $SA$: Security Association payload
- $KE$: Key Exchange payload (Diffie-Hellman public value $g^x, g^y$)
- $N_i, N_r$: Nonces from Initiator and Responder
- $ID_{ii}, ID_{ir}$: Identities

### 3.3.3 Message Exchange (Main Mode with Pre-Shared Key)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M1: Initiator} &\rightarrow \text{Responder: } HDR, SA \\
\text{M2: Responder} &\rightarrow \text{Initiator: } HDR, SA \\
\text{M3: Initiator} &\rightarrow \text{Responder: } HDR, KE, N_i \\
\text{M4: Responder} &\rightarrow \text{Initiator: } HDR, KE, N_r \\
\text{M5: Initiator} &\rightarrow \text{Responder: } HDR, ID_{ii}, \text{HASH\_I} \\
\text{M6: Responder} &\rightarrow \text{Initiator: } HDR, ID_{ir}, \text{HASH\_R}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Key Derivation Chain (PSK variant)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A — Shared Secret (Diffie-Hellman):} \quad & g^{xy} \bmod p \\
\text{Step B — SKEYID (using PSK):} \quad & \text{SKEYID} = \text{prf}(PSK, N_i \parallel N_r) \\
\text{Step C — SKEYID\_d (Key Material for Phase 2):} \quad & \text{SKEYID\_d} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, g^{xy} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel 2) \\
\text{Step D — SKEYID\_a (Authentication Key):} \quad & \text{SKEYID\_a} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{SKEYID\_d} \parallel g^{xy} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel 3) \\
\text{Step E — SKEYID\_e (Encryption Key):} \quad & \text{SKEYID\_e} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{SKEYID\_a} \parallel g^{xy} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel 1) \\
\text{Step F — HASH\_I (Initiator Proof):} \quad & \text{HASH\_I} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, g^{xi} \parallel g^{xr} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel SA_i \parallel ID_{ii}) \\
\text{Step G — HASH\_R (Responder Proof):} \quad & \text{HASH\_R} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, g^{xr} \parallel g^{xi} \parallel C_R \parallel C_I \parallel SA_i \parallel ID_{ir})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.5 IKEv2 Simplification (RFC 7296)

IKEv2 reduces the exchange to **4 messages** total, combining authentication and SA setup:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M1: I} &\rightarrow \text{R: } HDR, SA_{i1}, KE_i, N_i \\
\text{M2: R} &\rightarrow \text{I: } HDR, SA_{r1}, KE_r, N_r \\
\text{M3: I} &\rightarrow \text{R: } HDR, \{ID_i, \text{AUTH}\}, SA_{i2}, TS_i, N_i', KE_i, [CERTREQ] \\
\text{M4: R} &\rightarrow \text{I: } HDR, \{ID_r, \text{AUTH}\}, SA_{r2}, TS_r, N_r', KE_r, [CERT]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $\{\cdot\}$ indicates **encryption under SKEYID_e**, derived in M1–M2.

## 3.4 ICV Computation Logic (Step-by-Step)

The ICV is the **Integrity Check Value** appended to AH or ESP packets. Using HMAC-SHA1-96 as the canonical KTU example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & K' = K \text{ if } \vert K \vert = 20 \text{ bytes; else } K' = \text{SHA1}(K) \text{ padded to } 64 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & ipad = 0x36 \text{ repeated 64 times} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & opad = 0x5C \text{ repeated 64 times} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Inner Hash} = \text{SHA1}((K' \oplus ipad) \parallel M) \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{HMAC} = \text{SHA1}((K' \oplus opad) \parallel \text{Inner Hash}) \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}[0..95] \quad (\text{truncate to 96 bits} = 12 \text{ bytes})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why truncate?** RFC 2404 mandates 96-bit ICV for HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA1 to maintain fixed header sizes and resist length-extension attacks while preserving cryptographic strength.

## 3.5 Anti-Replay Sliding Window — Mathematical Model

Define:
- $W$ = window size (32 or 64)
- $W_{left}$ = left edge of window
- $W_{right}$ = right edge = highest authenticated Sequence Number (SN) seen
- Incoming packet has $SN_{new}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} &= \begin{cases}
\text{DROP} & \text{if } SN_{new} < W_{left} \\
\text{ACCEPT \& SHIFT WINDOW} & \text{if } SN_{new} > W_{right} \\
\text{ACCEPT if bitmap unset, else DROP} & \text{if } W_{left} \leq SN_{new} \leq W_{right}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Window shift when $SN_{new} > W_{right}$:**
$$
W_{right} \leftarrow SN_{new}, \quad W_{left} \leftarrow SN_{new} - W + 1
$$

This mechanism guarantees that an attacker cannot replay any packet older than $W$ positions without detection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPSec Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["Application Layer TCP UDP Data"]
    NET["Network Layer IP Packet Outgoing"]
    SPD["SPD Security Policy Database"]
    SAD["SAD Security Association Database"]
    PAD["PAD Peer Authentication Database"]
    AH["AH Authentication Header Engine"]
    ESP["ESP Encapsulating Security Payload Engine"]
    IKE["IKE Internet Key Exchange Protocol"]
    OUT["Outgoing IP Datagram on Wire"]
    IN["Incoming IP Datagram from Wire"]

    APP --> NET
    NET --> SPD
    SPD -->|"PROTECT"| SAD
    SPD -->|"BYPASS"| OUT
    SPD -->|"DISCARD"| END1["Packet Dropped"]
    SAD -->|"SA Exists"| AH
    SAD -->|"SA Exists"| ESP
    SAD -->|"No SA Found"| IKE
    PAD --> IKE
    IKE --> SAD
    AH --> OUT
    ESP --> OUT
    IN --> SPD
    SPD -->|"Protected Match"| SAD
    SAD -->|"AH Verify"| AH
    SAD -->|"ESP Decrypt"| ESP
    ESP --> APP
    AH --> APP
```

## 4.2 ESP Packet Header Structure — Field-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ESP_PKT["ESP Packet Layout Bit-Level"]
        A["SPI 32 bits"]
        B["Sequence Number 32 bits"]
        C["Payload Data Variable Length Encrypted"]
        D["Padding 0 to 255 bytes"]
        E["Pad Length 8 bits"]
        F["Next Header 8 bits"]
        G["ICV Integrity Check Value 96 to 128 bits"]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When drawing the ESP header on the answer sheet, **always include the 2-byte ESP Trailer (PadLength + NextHeader)** and the **ICV at the tail**. A common mistake is to omit the ESP Trailer, which costs **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.

## 4.3 AH Header Structure — Field-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AH_PKT["AH Header Layout"]
        A1["Next Header 8 bits"]
        A2["Payload Length 8 bits"]
        A3["Reserved 16 bits"]
        A4["SPI 32 bits"]
        A5["Sequence Number 32 bits"]
        A6["ICV Integrity Check Value Variable Length 96 to 128 bits"]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Payload Length field in AH** is encoded as $\text{Payload Length} = (\text{AH Header Length in 32-bit words}) - 2$. For default ICV of 96 bits, Payload Length = 4 (i.e., $12$ bytes $\rightarrow 3$ words, plus 1 word fixed $\Rightarrow 4$).

## 4.4 Transport vs Tunnel Mode — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORIG["Original IPv4 Packet"]
        O1["Original IP Header 20 bytes"]
        O2["TCP Header 20 bytes"]
        O3["Application Data Variable"]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3
    end

    subgraph TRANS["ESP Transport Mode"]
        T1["IP Header 20 bytes Original"]
        T2["ESP Header SPI plus SeqNo 8 bytes"]
        T3["TCP Header 20 bytes"]
        T4["Application Data Variable"]
        T5["ESP Trailer 2 bytes"]
        T6["ESP ICV 12 bytes"]
        T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6
    end

    subgraph TUNNEL["ESP Tunnel Mode"]
        U1["New Outer IP Header 20 bytes"]
        U2["ESP Header SPI plus SeqNo 8 bytes"]
        U3["Original IP Header 20 bytes"]
        U4["TCP Header 20 bytes"]
        U5["Application Data Variable"]
        U6["ESP Trailer 2 bytes"]
        U7["ESP ICV 12 bytes"]
        U1 --> U2 --> U3 --> U4 --> U5 --> U6 --> U7
    end
```

## 4.5 IKEv1 Phase 1 Main Mode — Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant R as Responder

    Note over I,R: Message 1 SA Negotiation Start
    I->>R: HDR SA proposals and transforms
    Note over I,R: Message 2 SA Selection
    R->>I: HDR chosen SA
    Note over I,R: Message 3 DH and Nonce Exchange
    I->>R: HDR KE g to the xi and N_i
    Note over I,R: Message 4 DH and Nonce Exchange
    R->>I: HDR KE g to the xr and N_r
    Note over I,R: Both Compute SKEYID
    Note over I,R: Message 5 Authenticated Identity
    I->>R: HDR ID_ii and HASH_I encrypted
    Note over I,R: Message 6 Authenticated Identity
    R->>I: HDR ID_ir and HASH_R encrypted
    Note over I,R: IKE SA Established
```

## 4.6 IPSec Processing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Outgoing IP Packet"] --> Q1{"SPD Lookup"}
    Q1 -->|"DISCARD"| DROP["Drop Packet Silently"]
    Q1 -->|"BYPASS"| SEND1["Send Without Protection"]
    Q1 -->|"PROTECT"| Q2{"SA Exists in SAD"}
    Q2 -->|"NO"| KICK["Invoke IKE Phase 1 and Phase 2"]
    KICK --> Q2
    Q2 -->|"YES"| Q3{"Protocol AH or ESP"}
    Q3 -->|"AH"| APPLY_AH["Apply AH Generate ICV Add SeqNo"]
    Q3 -->|"ESP"| APPLY_ESP["Apply ESP Encrypt Payload Generate ICV Add SeqNo"]
    APPLY_AH --> SEND2["Send Protected Packet"]
    APPLY_ESP --> SEND2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *IKEv1* with *IKEv2* in answers. The 6-message Main Mode is **IKEv1 only**. IKEv2 uses 4 messages.
> - **Do NOT** claim AH provides confidentiality. If the question says "confidentiality," you **must** answer ESP, or your marks will be **deducted to zero** for that sub-part.
> - **Do NOT** forget the ESP Trailer (PadLength + NextHeader) when drawing packet formats — a 2-mark loss in every 14-mark question.
> - **Do NOT** mix up Transport and Tunnel modes. Transport mode is for **host-to-host**; Tunnel mode is for **gateway-to-gateway** VPN. The new IP header is a *giveaway* for Tunnel mode.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"Define Security Association (SA) in IPSec. Why is an SA unidirectional, and how is it uniquely identified?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Security Association (SA)** is a one-way logical connection between an IPSec sender and receiver that defines the security parameters (algorithms, keys, anti-replay window, lifetime, mode) used to protect traffic flowing in one direction. Since each direction of communication requires its own set of cryptographic parameters, an SA is **unidirectional** — bidirectional communication requires **two SAs**, one in each direction.

It is uniquely identified by a **triple** of values known as the **SPI (Security Parameters Index)**, the **Destination IP Address**, and the **Security Protocol identifier (AH = 51 or ESP = 50)**. This triple is stored in the SAD and is referenced by every IPSec packet header.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Distinguish between Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode in IPSec. Give one practical scenario for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Header Insertion | Between IP header and payload | A new IP header is prepended |
| Original IP Header | Retained, visible | Encapsulated (hidden) |
| Overhead | Lower (no new IP header) | Higher (adds 20/40 bytes) |
| Typical Use | Host-to-host within same network | Gateway-to-gateway VPN across Internet |

**Practical Scenarios:**
- **Transport Mode:** Two Linux servers in the same datacenter running an IPSec-aware application communicating directly without VPN gateways.
- **Tunnel Mode:** Branch office VPN router connecting to the HQ gateway across the public Internet, encapsulating all branch traffic inside a new IP packet addressed to the HQ gateway.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** protocol in detail. Describe its packet format, the security services it provides, and the exact position of each field in transport and tunnel modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the complete **IKEv1 Phase 1 Main Mode** exchange (6 messages). Show how the **SKEYID family of keys** is derived from the pre-shared key and Diffie-Hellman exchange. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** ESP, defined in **RFC 4303**, is the IPSec protocol that provides **confidentiality, integrity, data-origin authentication, and anti-replay protection** for IP packets. Unlike AH, it encrypts the payload.

**Security Services Provided (2 marks):**
- **Confidentiality** via symmetric encryption (3DES-CBC, AES-CBC, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)
- **Integrity & Authentication** via ICV (HMAC-SHA1-96, AES-XCBC-96, AES-GMAC)
- **Anti-Replay** via 32-bit Sequence Number and sliding window

**Packet Format — Transport Mode (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ESP Transport Mode:} \quad & [IP_Hdr \parallel SPI(4) \parallel SeqNo(4) \parallel Payload \parallel Pad(0..255) \parallel PadLen(1) \parallel NextHdr(1) \parallel ICV(\text{12 or 16})]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Packet Format — Tunnel Mode (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ESP Tunnel Mode:} \quad & [NewIP_Hdr \parallel SPI(4) \parallel SeqNo(4) \parallel OrigIP_Hdr \parallel Payload \parallel Pad(0..255) \parallel PadLen(1) \parallel NextHdr(1) \parallel ICV]
\end{aligned}
$$

The key difference is the presence of a **new outer IP header** in tunnel mode, which hides the original source and destination from intermediate routers — essential for VPN gateways.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (3 marks):** See Section 4.5 Mermaid sequence diagram. Draw the 6-message exchange with HDR, SA, KE, Nonce, ID, and HASH payloads.

**Message Walkthrough (2 marks):**

| Msg | Direction | Payload | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| M1 | I → R | HDR, SA | Propose crypto suites |
| M2 | R → I | HDR, SA | Accept one suite |
| M3 | I → R | HDR, KE, N_i | Send DH public value + nonce |
| M4 | R → I | HDR, KE, N_r | Send DH public value + nonce |
| M5 | I → R | HDR\*, ID_ii, HASH_I | Encrypted authenticated identity |
| M6 | R → I | HDR\*, ID_ir, HASH_R | Encrypted authenticated identity |

(\* indicates encryption under SKEYID_e from M3–M4 derivation)

**SKEYID Family Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SKEYID} &= \text{prf}(PSK, N_i \parallel N_r) \\
\text{SKEYID\_d} &= \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, g^{xy} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel 2) \\
\text{SKEYID\_a} &= \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{SKEYID\_d} \parallel g^{xy} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel 3) \\
\text{SKEYID\_e} &= \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{SKEYID\_a} \parallel g^{xy} \parallel C_I \parallel C_R \parallel 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Roles:**
- **SKEYID_e** → encrypts Phase 2 traffic
- **SKEYID_a** → authenticates Phase 2 messages
- **SKEYID_d** → derives IPSec SA keys for ESP/AH

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **IPSec architecture** in detail, covering the roles of **SPD, SAD, PAD**, and the outbound and inbound packet processing logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **AH (Authentication Header)** protocol — its packet format, the security services it provides, and explain **why AH alone cannot pass through a NAT** boundary. Compare AH and ESP in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**IPSec Architecture Overview (1 mark):**
IPSec is a framework of protocols and databases that provides network-layer security. The three core databases are SPD, SAD, and PAD, while AH, ESP, and IKE are the operational protocols.

**SPD — Security Policy Database (2 marks):**
The SPD stores *rules* that classify all traffic into three actions:
- **PROTECT** → apply IPSec (AH/ESP)
- **BYPASS** → forward without protection
- **DISCARD** → drop the packet

**SAD — Security Association Database (1 mark):**
The SAD stores *active SAs*. Each entry contains SPI, destination IP, protocol, sequence number, anti-replay window, algorithm IDs, keys, lifetime, and mode.

**PAD — Peer Authentication Database (1 mark):**
The PAD holds peer credentials — pre-shared keys, X.509 certificates, or public keys — used by IKE during authentication.

**Outbound Processing (1 mark):**
SPD lookup → action determination → SAD lookup → if no SA, invoke IKE → apply ESP/AH transform → transmit.

**Inbound Processing (1 mark):**
Parse AH/ESP header → extract SPI, Dest IP, Protocol → SAD lookup → anti-replay check → ICV verify → decrypt (if ESP) → SPD cross-check → deliver to upper layer.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**AH Packet Format (2 marks):** See Section 4.3. AH = [NextHdr (1) | PayloadLen (1) | Reserved (2) | SPI (4) | SeqNo (4) | ICV (12 or 16)].

**Security Services (1 mark):** Data integrity, data-origin authentication, anti-replay. **NO confidentiality.**

**Why AH Fails Behind NAT (2 marks):**
AH computes its ICV over the **entire IP header** (with mutable fields like TTL, Header Checksum, and — critically — **Source/Destination IP addresses for NAT translations** zeroed). When a NAT device translates the IP address, the receiver's recomputed ICV **does not match** the transmitted ICV, causing packet drop. ESP does not include the outer IP header in its ICV, so it survives NAT — making NAT-Traversal (NAT-T) feasible with ESP encapsulation.

**AH vs ESP Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | AH | ESP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Encryption | ❌ | ✅ |
| Authentication | ✅ (full packet incl. IP) | ✅ (payload only) |
| Integrity | ✅ | ✅ |
| Anti-Replay | ✅ | ✅ |
| NAT Compatibility | ❌ | ✅ (with NAT-T) |
| Protocol Number | 51 | 50 |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **IPSec** = Layer 3 security suite (RFC 4301–4309) providing confidentiality, integrity, authentication, anti-replay.
- **AH** = Protocol 51 — Integrity + Authentication only, no encryption.
- **ESP** = Protocol 50 — Integrity + Authentication + Encryption.
- **IKE** = Protocol for SA negotiation; IKEv1 (RFC 2409) vs IKEv2 (RFC 7296).
- **SA** = Unidirectional logical contract identified by *(SPI, Dest IP, Protocol)*.
- **SPI** = 32-bit SA selector in AH/ESP headers.
- **ICV** = 96-bit MAC for integrity, computed by HMAC or AES-XCBC.

### 🔢 Must-Know Numeric Constants
- **SPI size:** 32 bits = 4 bytes
- **Sequence Number:** 32 bits = 4 bytes
- **AH fixed overhead:** 24 bytes (header) + ICV (12 or 16)
- **ESP fixed overhead:** 10 bytes (header 8 + trailer 2) + ICV (12 or 16) + padding + optional IV
- **Anti-Replay Window:** 32 or 64 packets
- **ICV size:** Always truncated to 96 bits = 12 bytes
- **AES Block:** 128 bits = 16 bytes (for CBC mode)
- **IPv6 Min MTU:** 1280 bytes

### 🧠 Must-Know Comparisons
- **AH vs ESP:** AH no encryption; ESP provides encryption.
- **Transport vs Tunnel:** Transport = host-to-host, no new IP header; Tunnel = gateway-to-gateway, new IP header added.
- **IKEv1 vs IKEv2:** IKEv1 Main Mode = 6 messages; IKEv2 = 4 messages.
- **Aggressive vs Main Mode (IKEv1):** Aggressive = 3 messages, less secure, exposes identity in clear.

### ⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid
1. Writing "AH provides confidentiality" — **WRONG**.
2. Drawing ESP packet without the 2-byte trailer — **loses 2 marks**.
3. Saying "Tunnel mode is used for host-to-host" — **WRONG**, it's for gateway-to-gateway.
4. Forgetting that IKEv2 has no Aggressive Mode — **only IKEv1** does.
5. Confusing SAD (active SAs) with SPD (rules) — they are **different databases**.
6. Using IKE port 500 implicitly without mentioning **NAT-T UDP 4500** when ESP is behind NAT.
7. Claiming AH works with NAT — **does not**; only ESP with NAT-T works.
8. Mixing up SKEYID_e (encryption), SKEYID_a (authentication), SKEYID_d (derivation key).

### 📐 Must-Know Formula Quick-Reference
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{AH} &= 24 + \text{ICV} \\
L_{ESP} &= 10 + \text{ICV} + L_{Pad} + L_{IV}(\text{optional}) \\
L_{Tunnel} &= L_{ESP} + 20 \text{ (IPv4) or } 40 \text{ (IPv6)} \\
P_{Tun} &= P_{orig} + L_{Tunnel} \\
\text{ICV} &= \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, M)[0..95] \\
W_{left}^{new} &= W_{right}^{old} + 1 \quad \text{when } SN_{new} > W_{right}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy for IPSec Questions:**
> 1. Always start with the **three pillars** (AH, ESP, IKE) and the **two modes** (Transport, Tunnel).
> 2. Use **tables** liberally in your answer sheet — they score higher than paragraphs.
> 3. Whenever asked to "explain ESP/AH," always include: **services, packet format, mode differences**.
> 4. For IKE questions, **always draw the message diagram** — it is worth 3 of 7 marks.
> 5. Mention **RFC numbers** (4301, 4302, 4303, 7296) to demonstrate depth — examiners reward this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VPN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** VPN ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Virtual Private Network (VPN)** is a secure communication framework that creates an encrypted, logically isolated tunnel across a shared or untrusted public network (typically the Internet), enabling authorized remote endpoints to exchange data with the same confidentiality, integrity, and authentication guarantees as a private dedicated leased line. Formally, a VPN extends a private network across a public infrastructure through the combined use of **tunneling protocols**, **encryption**, **authentication**, and **access control policies**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Board Definition:** A VPN is *"a network constructed using public telecommunication infrastructure (such as the Internet) but maintaining privacy through the use of tunneling protocols and security procedures."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you want to send a sealed, valuable letter from your home in Kerala to your friend in Delhi through a busy, unpredictable courier network (the public Internet). Instead of handing over the open letter (plaintext), you: 1. Place the letter inside a **tamper-proof locked steel box** (encryption). 2. Address the box to your friend using a **secret code known only to them** (authentication). 3. Route the box through a **private underground tunnel** that bypasses the unsafe surface roads (tunneling). 4. Seal the box with a **tamper-evident seal** that breaks if anyone opens it (integrity check via hashing). The **steel box + tunnel + seal** combination is exactly what a VPN does for your digital packets. Even if a thief intercepts the courier truck, they see nothing but an unreadable, sealed container traveling along a hidden pathway. ### 1.3 Primary Design Objectives of a VPN A well-engineered VPN must satisfy **four security pillars** (the CIA-Triad extended): | Objective | Meaning in VPN Context | |---|---| | **Confidentiality** | Data must be unreadable to eavesdroppers (achieved via symmetric encryption: AES-256, 3DES). | | **Integrity** | Data must not be altered in transit (achieved via HMAC-SHA256, MD5). | | **Authentication** | Both sender and receiver must prove their identities (achieved via certificates, pre-shared keys, digital signatures). | | **Tunneling** | Packets must be encapsulated to traverse foreign networks seamlessly. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Term:** The "Virtual" in VPN means the connection is **logical (software-defined)**, not physical. No actual dedicated wires are laid — the privacy is **virtual**, enforced entirely by cryptographic protocols. ### 1.4 Why VPNs Are Essential — Engineering Motivation - **Remote Workforce Enablement**: Engineers and employees working from home need secure access to corporate intranets. - **Geo-Restriction Bypass**: Legitimate use-cases include cross-border academic research and cloud access. - **Defense-in-Depth Layer**: VPNs add an additional cryptographic layer on top of firewalls, IDS/IPS, and anti-malware systems. - **Cost Reduction**: VPN over Internet costs a fraction compared to leased lines (MPLS, Frame Relay). --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Core Architecture of a VPN — The Three Functional Layers A VPN is architecturally decomposed into **three logical layers**, each addressing a specific concern. Understanding these layers is mandatory for KTU board questions. #### **Layer 1: The Tunneling Layer (Encapsulation)** Tunneling is the **process of wrapping an entire packet inside another packet**. The original packet (which may belong to a private internal protocol suite) becomes the **payload** of a new packet that uses a public-network-compatible protocol. **Why tunneling?** Private networks frequently use non-routable IP ranges (e.g., `10.0.0.0/8`, `192.168.0.0/16`). These addresses cannot traverse the public Internet directly. Tunneling **re-encapsulates** them into routable public IP packets. **Generic Tunneling Mechanism:** $$ \text{Original Packet} = [\text{Inner Header} \mid \text{Payload} \mid \text{Inner Trailer}] $$ $$ \text{Encapsulated Packet} = [\text{Outer Header} \mid \text{Original Packet} \mid \text{Outer Trailer}] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Outer Header** uses public-routable IPs (VPN gateways), while the **Inner Header** retains the original private IPs (end hosts). This dual-header technique is called **double encapsulation** and is the heart of every VPN. #### **Layer 2: The Encryption & Authentication Layer** This layer applies cryptographic primitives to the encapsulated payload: - **Symmetric Encryption** (Bulk Data Encryption): Uses a single shared session key. - Algorithms: **AES-256**, **3DES**, **Blowfish** - Used because symmetric encryption is approximately **1000× faster** than asymmetric encryption. - **Asymmetric Encryption** (Key Exchange & Authentication): Uses public-private key pairs. - Algorithms: **RSA-2048/4096**, **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)**, **Diffie-Hellman** - Used to securely exchange the symmetric session key without prior shared secret. - **Hashing for Integrity**: **SHA-256**, **SHA-3**, or **HMAC-SHA256**. #### **Layer 3: The Access Control & Policy Layer** This layer governs **who** can connect, **from where**, **to which resources**, and **under what authentication strength**. It includes: - RADIUS / TACACS+ server integration - Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) - IP whitelisting and split-tunneling rules - Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL) and OCSP checks ### 2.2 Classification of VPNs — KTU High-Yield Taxonomy #### **A. Based on Deployment Architecture** | VPN Type | Full Form | Use Case | Topology | |---|---|---|---| | **Remote Access VPN** | Host-to-Gateway | Individual remote users accessing corporate network | Client-Server (Host → VPN Gateway) | | **Site-to-Site VPN** | Gateway-to-Gateway | Connecting two branch offices over Internet | Gateway ↔ Gateway | | **Intranet VPN** | Internal Site-to-Site | Linking multiple offices of the same organization | Hub-and-Spoke or Full Mesh | | **Extranet VPN** | External Site-to-Site | Limited access for business partners | Restricted Gateway-to-Gateway | #### **B. Based on the Protocol Layer Used** | Layer | VPN Protocol | Operates On | |---|---|---| | **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | PPTP, L2TP, L2F | Frames | | **Layer 3 (Network)** | IPSec, GRE, MPLS | IP Packets | | **Layer 4 (Transport)** | SSTP | TCP/UDP segments | | **Layer 7 (Application)** | SSL/TLS VPN, OpenVPN, SSH Tunnel | Application data | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | VPN Tunnel Overhead | $\text{Overhead} = H_{outer} + H_{inner} + E_{block} + P_{auth}$ | Bytes | | MTU Reduction | $MTU_{effective} = 1500 - \text{TunnelOverhead}$ | Bytes | | MSS Reduction | $MSS_{effective} = MTU_{effective} - 40$ | Bytes | | Encryption Strength | $\text{Key Length} \geq 128\text{ bits}$ (NIST minimum) | Bits | | HMAC Output | $HMAC = H(K \oplus opad \mid H(K \oplus ipad \mid M))$ | Bits | | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K = g^{ab} \mod p$ | Scalar | | IPSec SA Lifetime | Typical: $28800 \text{ seconds}$ or $4608000 \text{ KB}$ | Seconds / KB | | AES Block Size | $128 \text{ bits}$ | Bits | | 3DES Effective Key | $168 \text{ bits}$ (112 bits effective due to meet-in-the-middle) | Bits | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** Always state both **key length in bits** and the **algorithm name**. KTU examiners award marks for precision (e.g., "AES-256 in GCM mode" rather than just "AES"). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Corporate SD-WAN Backbones**: Modern enterprises replace MPLS with IPSec VPN tunnels over SD-WAN for cost savings. - **Cloud Connectivity**: AWS Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway, and Google Cloud VPN all use IPSec/IKEv2. - **IoT Security**: Lightweight VPN protocols like WireGuard secure IoT device fleets. - **Defense & Government**: High-assurance VPNs (Suite B cryptography) protect classified communications. - **Academic Research**: Researchers use SSH-tunnel-based VPNs to bypass institutional firewalls legitimately. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Deep Dive: The IPSec Protocol Suite (KTU Highest-Weight Topic) **IPSec (Internet Protocol Security)** is the de facto standard for Layer 3 VPN security. It is not a single protocol but a **suite of protocols** working together. #### **3.1.1 IPSec Architecture Components** | Component | Full Form | Function | |---|---|---| | **AH** | Authentication Header | Provides authentication + integrity (NO encryption) | | **ESP** | Encapsulating Security Payload | Provides encryption + authentication + integrity | | **IKE** | Internet Key Exchange | Automated key management (uses ISAKMP/Oakley) | | **SA** | Security Association | A one-way logical channel between two peers | | **SPD** | Security Policy Database | Defines which traffic to protect, bypass, or drop | | **SAD** | Security Association Database | Stores active SAs and their parameters | | **PAD** | Peer Authentication Database | Stores authentication credentials per peer | #### **3.1.2 IPSec Modes of Operation** **Mode 1: Transport Mode** Used for **end-to-end** communication between two hosts (Host-to-Host). $$ \text{Original Packet} = [\text{IP Header} \mid \text{TCP Header} \mid \text{Data}] $$ $$ \text{Transport Mode Encrypted} = [\text{IP Header} \mid \text{ESP Header} \mid \text{TCP Header} \mid \text{Encrypted Data} \mid \text{ESP Trailer} \mid \text{ESP Auth}] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In Transport Mode, **only the payload is encrypted**, the original IP header is preserved. Overhead ≈ 20-30 bytes. **Mode 2: Tunnel Mode** Used for **Gateway-to-Gateway** VPN (the typical corporate VPN scenario). $$ \text{Original Packet} = [\text{IP}_A \mid \text{TCP} \mid \text{Data}] $$ $$ \text{Tunnel Mode Encrypted} = [\text{IP}_B \mid \text{ESP Header} \mid \text{IP}_A \mid \text{TCP} \mid \text{Encrypted Data} \mid \text{ESP Trailer} \mid \text{ESP Auth}] $$ Where $\text{IP}_B$ is the VPN Gateway's public address and $\text{IP}_A$ is the original private host address. > [!IMPORTANT] > In Tunnel Mode, **the entire original IP packet is encrypted AND a new IP header is added**. Overhead ≈ 50-70 bytes. This is the standard VPN mode. #### **3.1.3 Comparison Matrix: Transport vs Tunnel Mode** | Parameter | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode | |---|---|---| | **IP Header Protection** | Not encrypted | Fully encrypted | | **Payload Protection** | Encrypted | Encrypted | | **Primary Use** | Host-to-Host | Gateway-to-Gateway | | **Overhead** | Lower (~20-30 bytes) | Higher (~50-70 bytes) | | **NAT Traversal** | Difficult | Easier with NAT-T | | **KTU Frequency** | Common in 3-mark questions | Very common in 14-mark questions | #### **3.1.4 Security Association (SA) — Mathematical Model** An SA is uniquely identified by a **triple**: $$ SA = \{SPI, Destination\ IP, Protocol\} $$ Where: - **SPI** = Security Parameter Index (32-bit identifier in AH/ESP header) - **Destination IP** = The endpoint IP address - **Protocol** = AH (51) or ESP (50) Since an SA is **unidirectional**, bidirectional communication requires **two SAs** (one in each direction). #### **3.1.5 IKE Phases (Key Exchange Derivation)** IKE negotiates SAs in **two phases**: **Phase 1: Establish ISAKMP SA (IKE SA)** Goal: Create a secure, authenticated channel to protect Phase 2 negotiations. $$ \text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Initiator} \to \text{Responder}: \text{SA Payload, KE, Nonce}_i $$ $$ \text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Responder} \to \text{Initiator}: \text{SA Payload, KE, Nonce}_r $$ $$ \text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Diffie-Hellman Exchange}: \quad g^a \mod p, \ g^b \mod p $$ $$ \text{Shared Secret Derivation:} \quad SKEYID = PRF(Ni \mid Nr, \ g^{ab} \mod p) $$ **Phase 2: Establish IPSec SA (for AH/ESP)** Goal: Negotiate actual IPSec parameters (encryption algo, hash algo, lifetime). $$ \text{Quick Mode Negotiation:} \quad \text{SA Proposal} \to \text{SA Acceptance} \to \text{Key Refresh} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** "What is the role of Diffie-Hellman in IPSec?" > **Answer:** Provides **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** — even if long-term private keys are compromised, past session keys remain secure because each session uses a fresh DH exchange. ### 3.2 Detailed VPN Protocol Analysis (Analytical Matrix) | Protocol | Layer | Encryption | Authentication | Weakness | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **PPTP** | Layer 2 | RC4 (40/128-bit) | MS-CHAPv1/v2 | Easily cracked | Historical only | | **L2TP** | Layer 2 | None (uses IPSec combo) | L2TP itself | No native crypto | Always paired with IPSec → **L2TP/IPSec** | | **IPSec** | Layer 3 | AES, 3DES | HMAC, Cert/PSK | Complex config | **Highest weight in KTU** | | **SSL/TLS VPN** | Layer 7 | AES-GCM | X.509 Certificates | Requires HTTPS-aware apps | Common for clientless VPN | | **OpenVPN** | Layer 4 (UDP/TCP) | AES-256-CBC/GCM | TLS-based | Slower than WireGuard | Open-source gold standard | | **WireGuard** | Layer 3 | ChaCha20 | Curve25519 | New, less audited | Modern, ultra-fast | | **SSH Tunnel** | Layer 7 | AES (configurable) | Public key | Not a full VPN | Used for port forwarding | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — MTU Calculation **Problem:** An organization uses IPSec Tunnel Mode with AES-256 (block size = 16 bytes), ESP Header (8 bytes), ESP Trailer (2 bytes padding + 2 bytes pad length + 2 bytes next header), and ESP Authentication (12 bytes for HMAC-SHA256 truncated). What is the effective MTU and MSS if the standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes? **Solution:** Step 1: Calculate ESP Trailer length. $$ T_{ESP} = 2 \text{ (padding)} + 2 \text{ (pad length)} + 2 \text{ (next header)} = 6 \text{ bytes} $$ Step 2: Calculate new IP Header overhead. $$ H_{new} = 20 \text{ bytes (standard IPv4 header)} $$ Step 3: Total IPSec overhead. $$ O_{total} = H_{new} + H_{ESP} + T_{ESP} + A_{ESP} = 20 + 8 + 6 + 12 = 46 \text{ bytes} $$ Step 4: Effective MTU. $$ MTU_{effective} = 1500 - 46 = 1454 \text{ bytes} $$ Step 5: Effective MSS (subtract TCP header 20 + IP header 20 = 40). $$ MSS_{effective} = 1454 - 40 = 1414 \text{ bytes} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world consequence:** If the application sends a 1500-byte packet, IPSec fragmentation will occur, increasing latency. Network admins must configure TCP MSS clamping at the firewall to force endpoints to use the smaller MSS value. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step VPN Establishment Lifecycle A complete VPN session progresses through **six stages**: **Stage 1: Tunnel Establishment** - IKE Phase 1 begins → ISAKMP SA created → mutual authentication via certificates or pre-shared keys. **Stage 2: Key Exchange** - Diffie-Hellman exchange → SKEYID derived → encryption keys generated for Phase 2. **Stage 3: SA Negotiation** - IKE Phase 2 → IPSec SAs negotiated → encryption algorithm and HMAC algorithm agreed upon. **Stage 4: Data Transfer** - User data is encapsulated → encrypted → authenticated → transmitted through tunnel. **Stage 5: Tunnel Rekeying** - Before SA lifetime expires → new keys negotiated → seamless transition (typically at 80% of lifetime). **Stage 6: Tunnel Termination** - DELETE payload exchanged → SAs purged from SAD → resources freed on both VPN gateways. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 VPN High-Level Architecture (Site-to-Site IPSec VPN) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Branch Office Host
192.168.1.10] --> B[Branch VPN Gateway
203.0.113.1] B -->|Encrypted ESP Tunnel
across Internet| C[HQ VPN Gateway
198.51.100.1] C --> D[HQ Internal Server
10.0.0.50] E[Remote Employee Laptop] -->|SSL VPN Tunnel| F[VPN Concentrator
DMZ] F --> G[Corporate Intranet] H[Threat Actor
Eavesdropper] -.X Cannot Read.-> B H -.X Cannot Tamper.-> C classDef secure fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gateway fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef host fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef threat fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF class A,D,E,G host class B,C,F gateway class H threat ``` ### 4.2 IPSec Protocol Stack — Functional Layer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Application Layer
HTTP, SMTP, FTP] --> L2[Transport Layer
TCP or UDP] L2 --> L3[Network Layer
Original IP Header
Private IP 10.x.x.x] L3 --> L4[IPSec Layer
AH or ESP Header] L4 --> L5[Outer Network Layer
New IP Header
Public VPN Gateway IP] L5 --> L6[Data Link Layer
Ethernet, WiFi] L6 --> L7[Physical Layer
Copper, Fiber, Wireless] L4 -.Encrypts.-> L3 L4 -.Authenticates.-> L2 L5 -.Routable.-> L7 classDef app fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef encrypt fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef route fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phys fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class L1,L2 app class L3,L4 encrypt class L5 route class L6,L7 phys ``` ### 4.3 VPN Protocol Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB VPN[VPN Technologies] --> L2[Layer 2 VPNs] VPN --> L3[Layer 3 VPNs] VPN --> L4[Layer 4 VPNs] VPN --> L7[Layer 7 Application VPNs] L2 --> PPTP[PPTP
Legacy Insecure] L2 --> L2TP[L2TP
Combined with IPSec] L2 --> L2F[L2F
Cisco L2 Forwarding] L3 --> IPSec[IPSec
AH and ESP] L3 --> GRE[GRE
Generic Routing Encapsulation] L3 --> WG[WireGuard
Modern Lightweight] L4 --> SSTP[SSTP
Microsoft SSL over TCP 443] L7 --> SSL[SSL TLS VPN
Clientless HTTPS] L7 --> OpenVPN[OpenVPN
Open Source TLS] L7 --> SSH[SSH Tunnel
Port Forwarding] classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef layer2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef legacy fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class VPN root class L2,L2TP,L2F,PPTP layer2 class L3,IPSec,GRE,WG layer3 class L4,SSTP layer4 class L7,SSL,OpenVPN,SSH layer7 ``` ### 4.4 IPSec Packet Encapsulation — Transport vs Tunnel Mode ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TM[Transport Mode Packet Structure] T1[IP Header
Original Host IP] --> T2[ESP Header
SPI, Seq Num] T2 --> T3[TCP UDP Header] T3 --> T4[Encrypted Payload
Original Data] T4 --> T5[ESP Trailer
Padding, PadLen, NextHdr] T5 --> T6[ESP Auth
HMAC-SHA256] end subgraph TUM[Tunnel Mode Packet Structure] U1[New IP Header
VPN Gateway Public IP] --> U2[ESP Header
SPI, Seq Num] U2 --> U3[Original IP Header
Private Host IP] U3 --> U4[TCP UDP Header] U4 --> U5[Encrypted Payload
Original Data] U5 --> U6[ESP Trailer
Padding, PadLen, NextHdr] U6 --> U7[ESP Auth
HMAC-SHA256] end classDef new fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef orig fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef crypto fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef meta fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class T1,U1 new class T3,T4,U3,U4,U5 orig class T2,U2,T5,U6,T6,U7 crypto class U5,U6 meta ``` ### 4.5 VPN Connection Lifecycle — IKE Negotiation Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant I as Initiator
Branch Gateway participant R as Responder
HQ Gateway Note over I,R: IKE Phase 1 Main Mode I->>R: MM1 SA Proposal, KE, Nonce_i R->>I: MM2 SA Accepted, KE, Nonce_r I->>R: MM3 Identity Protected R->>I: MM4 Identity Protected I->>R: MM5 Authentication Hash R->>I: MM6 Authentication Hash Note over I,R: ISAKMP SA Established Note over I,R: IKE Phase 2 Quick Mode I->>R: QM1 SA Proposal, KE, Nonce R->>I: QM2 SA Accepted, KE, Nonce I->>R: QM3 Authentication Hash Note over I,R: IPSec SA Established Note over I,R: Encrypted Data Transfer I->>R: ESP Encrypted Packets R->>I: ESP Encrypted Packets Note over I,R: Rekeying or Termination I->>R: DELETE Payload R->>I: DELETE Acknowledgement ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define VPN. List any two advantages of using a VPN.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** A **Virtual Private Network (VPN)** is a secure communication technology that uses tunneling protocols and encryption to create a private, encrypted connection over a public network infrastructure such as the Internet, allowing remote users to securely access a private network. **Advantages (any two):** 1. **Confidentiality and Security:** VPNs encrypt data using strong cryptographic algorithms (AES-256), making it unreadable to eavesdroppers on the public Internet. 2. **Cost-Effectiveness:** VPNs leverage existing Internet infrastructure, eliminating the need for expensive dedicated leased lines or MPLS circuits. 3. **Remote Access:** Enables employees to securely access corporate resources from any geographic location. 4. **IP Masking:** Hides the user's real IP address, enhancing privacy against tracking. --- **Q2. Differentiate between Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode in IPSec.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | End-to-End (Host-to-Host) | Gateway-to-Gateway | | **Encryption Scope** | Only the payload (TCP/UDP + data) is encrypted | The entire original IP packet is encrypted | | **IP Header** | Original IP header is preserved | A new outer IP header (VPN gateway IP) is added | | **Overhead** | Lower (~20-30 bytes) | Higher (~50-70 bytes) | | **Typical Use** | Securing communication between two hosts on a private network | Site-to-Site VPN between two corporate offices | | **Encapsulation Layers** | Single header insertion | Double encapsulation | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A: Explain the IPSec protocol suite in detail. Discuss the role of AH and ESP, and describe the two modes of IPSec operation with suitable diagrams. (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]` **Model Answer Structure (Mark Allocation Specified):** **Part (a) — IPSec Architecture and AH/ESP (7 Marks)** **Definition of IPSec:** IPSec (Internet Protocol Security) is a suite of protocols standardized by the IETF (RFC 4301-4309) that provides confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay protection for IP-level communications. IPSec operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model and can protect any application traffic without modifying the application itself. **Authentication Header (AH):** The Authentication Header provides **data origin authentication, integrity, and anti-replay** services. However, it does **NOT provide confidentiality** (no encryption). AH protects: - Most of the original IP header (mutable fields excluded) - AH header itself - Upper-layer protocol payload AH Format: $$ \text{AH Header} = [\text{Next Header (8 bits)} \mid \text{Payload Len (8 bits)} \mid \text{Reserved (16 bits)} \mid \text{SPI (32 bits)} \mid \text{Sequence Number (32 bits)} \mid \text{ICV Variable Length Hash}] $$ ICV (Integrity Check Value) is computed using HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA256 over the payload. **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP):** ESP provides **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay** — it is the more commonly used IPSec protocol. ESP Format: $$ \text{ESP Packet} = [\text{ESP Header} \mid \text{Encrypted Payload} \mid \text{ESP Trailer} \mid \text{ESP Authentication}] $$ ESP Header contains SPI and Sequence Number. The trailer provides padding for block ciphers. ESP encrypts payload using AES, 3DES, or ChaCha20 and authenticates using HMAC-SHA256. **Difference between AH and ESP:** | Feature | AH | ESP | |---|---|---| | **Confidentiality** | No | Yes | | **Integrity** | Yes | Yes | | **Authentication** | Yes | Yes | | **Anti-Replay** | Yes | Yes | | **IP Header Protection** | Yes (partial) | No (only in tunnel mode) | | **Algorithm** | HMAC only | Encryption + HMAC | --- **Part (b) — IPSec Modes of Operation (7 Marks)** **Transport Mode:** In transport mode, only the **payload of the IP packet is protected** (encrypted/ authenticated). The original IP header remains intact. This mode is used for **host-to-host** communication where both endpoints support IPSec natively. Packet Structure in Transport Mode with ESP: $$ [\text{Original IP Header}] \mid [\text{ESP Header}] \mid [\text{TCP/UDP Header}] \mid [\text{Encrypted Data}] \mid [\text{ESP Trailer}] \mid [\text{ESP Auth}] $$ **Tunnel Mode:** In tunnel mode, the **entire original IP packet is encapsulated** within a new IP packet that has a new outer IP header. This mode is used for **gateway-to-gateway VPN** scenarios where the endpoints are VPN gateways (not the actual hosts). Packet Structure in Tunnel Mode with ESP: $$ [\text{New IP Header}] \mid [\text{ESP Header}] \mid [\text{Original IP Header}] \mid [\text{TCP/UDP Header}] \mid [\text{Encrypted Data}] \mid [\text{ESP Trailer}] \mid [\text{ESP Auth}] $$ **Use Cases:** - **Transport Mode**: Securing communication between two servers within the same private network (e.g., database server to web server). - **Tunnel Mode**: Site-to-site VPN between two branch offices; remote access VPN where a VPN concentrator terminates tunnels. **Conclusion:** IPSec remains the **gold standard** for Layer 3 VPN security. Modern IPSec implementations favor ESP in Tunnel Mode due to its comprehensive protection and gateway-based scalability. --- #### **Question B: With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Virtual Private Network. Discuss the various tunneling protocols used in VPN with their relative advantages and disadvantages. (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]` **Model Answer Structure (Mark Allocation Specified):** **Part (a) — VPN Architecture (7 Marks)** A VPN architecture consists of **three core components**: the **tunnel**, the **VPN gateway/concentrator**, and the **authentication server**. **Components Explained:** 1. **Tunnel**: The encrypted virtual pathway through which packets travel. The tunnel is established using tunneling protocols such as PPTP, L2TP, IPSec, or SSL/TLS. 2. **VPN Gateway / Concentrator**: A specialized network device (hardware appliance or software) that terminates VPN tunnels, handles encryption/decryption, and enforces access policies. Examples include Cisco ASA, pfSense, OpenVPN Access Server, and AWS VPN Gateway. 3. **Authentication Server**: Validates user credentials using RADIUS, TACACS+, LDAP, or Active Directory. Modern deployments add Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). 4. **VPN Client Software**: Installed on remote devices to initiate and manage VPN connections (e.g., Cisco AnyConnect, OpenVPN GUI, WireGuard). **Architectural Flow (Site-to-Site IPSec VPN):** - Branch office host (192.168.1.10) sends packet to HQ server (10.0.0.50). - Branch VPN gateway encrypts the packet, encapsulates with new IP header. - Encrypted packet traverses the Internet. - HQ VPN gateway receives, decapsulates, decrypts. - HQ server receives plaintext packet. **Architectural Diagram (text representation):** $$ \text{[Branch Host]} \to \text{[Branch VPN Gateway]} \xleftrightarrow{\text{Encrypted Tunnel}} \text{[HQ VPN Gateway]} \to \text{[HQ Server]} $$ --- **Part (b) — Tunneling Protocols Comparison (7 Marks)** **PPTP (Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol):** - **Developed by:** Microsoft, 3Com, Ascend (RFC 2637) - **Layer:** Layer 2 - **Encryption:** RC4 (40 or 128-bit) - **Advantages:** Easy to set up, native support in Windows, low CPU overhead. - **Disadvantages:** Severely broken; MS-CHAPv2 has been cracked; no native integrity; **deprecated and unsafe**. **L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol):** - **Developed by:** IETF (RFC 2661), merger of Microsoft L2F and Cisco L2TP. - **Layer:** Layer 2 - **Encryption:** None natively — always paired with IPSec → **L2TP/IPSec** - **Advantages:** Supports multiple protocols (IP, IPX, AppleTalk); widely supported. - **Disadvantages:** Double encapsulation overhead; can be blocked by firewalls (uses UDP 500, 4500); slower than standalone IPSec. **IPSec (Internet Protocol Security):** - **Developed by:** IETF (RFC 4301-4309) - **Layer:** Layer 3 - **Encryption:** AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 - **Advantages:** Strongest Layer 3 security; transparent to applications; supports PFS; standardized. - **Disadvantages:** Complex configuration; NAT traversal issues (solved by NAT-T); can be blocked by firewalls. **SSL/TLS VPN (OpenVPN, Cisco AnyConnect):** - **Developed by:** Netscape (SSL), OpenVPN project - **Layer:** Layer 4-7 (Application) - **Encryption:** AES-256-GCM via TLS 1.3 - **Advantages:** Traverses NAT and firewalls easily (uses TCP 443); clientless mode for browser-based access; granular application-level access control. - **Disadvantages:** Requires application-aware proxy or client; can be slower; potential Web application vulnerabilities. **WireGuard:** - **Developed by:** Jason Donenfeld, 2020 (merged into Linux kernel 5.6) - **Layer:** Layer 3 - **Encryption:** ChaCha20-Poly1305, Curve25519, BLAKE2s - **Advantages:** Lean codebase (~4000 lines vs OpenVPN's 100,000+); blazing fast; modern cryptography; seamless roaming. - **Disadvantages:** Newer, less mature enterprise tooling; no built-in dynamic IP address allocation. **Comparison Summary Table:** | Protocol | Security | Speed | Ease of Use | Modern Recommendation | |---|---|---|---|---| | PPTP | ❌ Weak | ⚡ Fast | ✅ Easy | ❌ Never use | | L2TP/IPSec | ⚠️ Moderate | 🐢 Slower | ✅ Easy | ✅ Acceptable | | IPSec | ✅ Strong | ⚡ Fast | ❌ Complex | ✅ Enterprise choice | | SSL/TLS VPN | ✅ Strong | 🐢 Moderate | ✅ Easy | ✅ Clientless access | | OpenVPN | ✅ Strong | ⚡ Fast | ✅ Moderate | ✅ Gold standard open-source | | WireGuard | ✅ Strong | ⚡⚡ Very Fast | ✅ Easy | ✅ Future standard | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These at All Costs:** > > 1. **Confusing AH with ESP**: Many students write that AH provides encryption. **It does NOT.** AH only provides integrity and authentication. ESP provides encryption + integrity + authentication. This single mistake costs 2-3 marks. > > 2. **Writing "IPSec is a protocol"**: IPSec is a **suite of protocols** (AH, ESP, IKE, ISAKMP, Oakley). Always use "suite" or "framework". > > 3. **Skipping the SPI in SA Definition**: An SA is identified by the triple **{SPI, Destination IP, Protocol}**. Forgetting SPI is a 1-mark deduction. > > 4. **Mixing Up Transport vs Tunnel Mode**: Transport mode = host-to-host; Tunnel mode = gateway-to-gateway. Many students reverse this. > > 5. **Not Mentioning Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**: When explaining Diffie-Hellman in IPSec, always mention PFS as the key benefit. > > 6. **Forgetting ESP Trailer**: When drawing ESP packet format, the **ESP Trailer** is often forgotten. Examiners specifically look for it. > > 7. **Using Weak Algorithms**: Never recommend MD5, SHA-1, RC4, or 3DES in 2024. KTU expects AES-256, SHA-256 minimum. > > 8. **Vague Encryption References**: Saying "uses encryption" without specifying algorithm, key length, and mode (e.g., "AES-256-GCM") loses marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **VPN Definition**: Secure, encrypted tunnel over public network; logical privacy, not physical. - **Four Pillars**: Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication + Tunneling. - **Two Major VPN Categories**: Remote Access VPN (Host-to-Gateway) and Site-to-Site VPN (Gateway-to-Gateway). - **Tunneling Mechanism**: Double encapsulation — outer header (public IP) + original packet (private IP). - **IPSec Suite Components**: AH (auth only), ESP (encrypt + auth), IKE (key exchange), SA (logical channel). - **IPSec Modes**: Transport (host-to-host, ~20-30 byte overhead) vs Tunnel (gateway-to-gateway, ~50-70 byte overhead). - **SA is Unidirectional**: Bidirectional communication = 2 SAs. - **SA Identified By**: {SPI, Destination IP, Protocol} triple. - **IKE Has 2 Phases**: Phase 1 = ISAKMP SA, Phase 2 = IPSec SA. - **Diffie-Hellman Provides PFS**: Even if long-term key compromised, session keys remain secure. - **MTU Reduction Formula**: $MTU_{effective} = 1500 - \text{TunnelOverhead}$ - **Modern Recommended Protocols**: IPSec with AES-256-GCM, WireGuard with ChaCha20, OpenVPN with AES-256-GCM. - **Avoid Deprecated Protocols**: PPTP (broken), 3DES (weak), MD5 (broken), SHA-1 (deprecated). - **ESP vs AH**: ESP = encryption + auth; AH = auth only. - **SSL VPN Advantage**: Traverses firewalls via TCP 443. - **WireGuard Advantage**: Lean codebase (~4000 lines), modern crypto, blazing fast. - **L2TP Always Paired with IPSec**: L2TP alone provides no encryption. - **Standard VPN Ports**: UDP 500 (IKE), UDP 4500 (NAT-T), TCP 443 (SSL VPN), UDP 1194 (OpenVPN). - **Defense-in-Depth**: VPN is one layer; combine with firewall, IDS/IPS, anti-malware. - **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**: Critical property for VPN key management. - **NAT-Traversal (NAT-T)**: Encapsulates ESP in UDP 4500 to traverse NAT devices. - **Split Tunneling**: Allows some traffic to bypass VPN — security risk but performance benefit. - **Common Algorithms**: AES-128/256-GCM (encryption), SHA-256/384 (hashing), ECDH-P384 (key exchange), RSA-3072 (signatures). - **Real-World Applications**: AWS Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway, Cisco AnyConnect, Cloudflare WARP. ---

topics.title: Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems: Types of IDSs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems: Types of IDSs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Intrusion Detection System (IDS)

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism — either hardware or software-based — that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access, and produces reports to a management station. It is fundamentally a **passive, detective control** in the security triad (Preventive → Detective → Corrective).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST863 – Module 2):**
> An IDS is a device or software application that monitors a network or systems for malicious activity or policy violations. Any detected activity or violation is typically reported either to an administrator or collected centrally using a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)

An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is an evolution of the IDS that not only *detects* suspicious activity but also takes **automated, proactive action** to block, drop, or reset the malicious traffic in real time. It is classified as an **active, preventive control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (High-Yield KTU Concept):**
> - **IDS = Detective + Passive** → Generates an *alert* when a threat is found.
> - **IPS = Preventive + Active** → Generates an alert *and* automatically blocks the traffic.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV Security Room

Imagine your college campus:
- The **CCTV cameras** continuously record everything. They do *not* stop a thief; they only *detect* and *record* the intrusion. This is exactly how an **IDS** functions — it watches and alerts.
- Now imagine a CCTV camera connected to an **automatic door-locking mechanism** that, the moment it spots a thief, locks all relevant doors and calls security. This is the **IPS** — detect-and-respond in real time.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:**
> **IDS = "I Detect & Shout"** (passive)
> **IPS = "I Prevent & Stop"** (active)

## 1.4 Why We Need IDSs/IPSs

| Threat Reality | Why IDS/IPS is Essential |
|---|---|
| Firewalls fail on application-layer attacks (Layer 7) | IDS sees what the firewall misses |
| Zero-day exploits have no signatures initially | Anomaly-based IDS catches behavioral deviation |
| Insider threats bypass perimeter security | Host-based IDS (HIDS) monitors internal behavior |
| Encryption blinds traditional packet filters | Protocol-based IDS reassembles and inspects payloads |

## 1.5 Place in the KTU Defense-in-Depth Model

$$\text{Defense in Depth} = \text{Firewall} \oplus \text{IDS/IPS} \oplus \text{Antivirus} \oplus \text{Access Control}$$

The IDS/IPS sits as the **second line of defense** behind the firewall, providing visibility into traffic the firewall has allowed through.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Detection Methodologies (Universal Across All IDS Types)

Regardless of whether an IDS is host-based, network-based, or hybrid, the underlying **detection engine** uses one or a combination of the following three methods.

### 2.1.1 Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)

- **Operational Logic:** Compares observed traffic/behavior against a database of known attack patterns called *signatures* (e.g., Snort rules).
- **Analogy:** Like a bouncer checking IDs against a list of known troublemakers.
- **Strengths:** Very low false-positive rate; excellent for known threats.
- **Weaknesses:** Useless against **zero-day** attacks; requires constant signature updates.

### 2.1.2 Anomaly-Based Detection

- **Operational Logic:** Builds a statistical/ML model of *normal* behavior; flags any statistical deviation as an intrusion.
- **Analogy:** Like noticing a student who usually arrives at 9:00 AM suddenly arriving at 3:00 AM — *behavioral anomaly*.
- **Techniques:** Statistical profiling, neural networks, clustering, Markov models.
- **Strengths:** Can detect zero-day attacks.
- **Weaknesses:** Higher false-positive rate; requires a "training period."

### 2.1.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis

- **Operational Logic:** Tracks the state of network protocols (e.g., TCP handshake) and flags deviations from the expected state machine.
- **Analogy:** Like a teacher who knows exactly how a proper classroom Q&A should flow and notices when a student skips the queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Most commercial IDSs (Snort, Suricata) are *hybrid* — they combine signature, anomaly, and stateful methods in a layered engine.

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Types of IDSs (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU syllabus explicitly groups IDSs by **scope of monitoring**. The following five types are mandatory for the exam.

### Type 1: Network-Based IDS (NIDS)

- **Placement:** At strategic chokepoints (e.g., just inside the firewall, on a SPAN port of a switch).
- **Monitors:** Entire network traffic segments in promiscuous mode.
- **Examples:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (formerly Bro).

### Type 2: Host-Based IDS (HIDS)

- **Placement:** Installed on individual endpoints/servers.
- **Monitors:** System calls, log files, file integrity, registry changes, running processes.
- **Examples:** OSSEC, Tripwire, Wazuh.

### Type 3: Protocol-Based IDS (PIDS)

- **Placement:** At the front-end of a server or at a network choke point.
- **Monitors:** The behavior of protocol states (e.g., HTTPS, SMB, DNS) at the application layer.
- **Specialty:** Often deployed where encryption terminates (e.g., front of a web server farm).

### Type 4: Application Protocol-Based IDS (APIDS)

- **Placement:** Inside a specific application or server group.
- **Monitors:** Application-layer protocols (HTTP, SQL, SMTP) for misuse at the protocol semantic level (e.g., SQL injection patterns).

### Type 5: Hybrid IDS

- **Operational Logic:** Combines NIDS + HIDS, often with a centralized management console, to overcome the blind spots of each.

## 2.3 The IPS Counterparts

| Property | NIDS | NIPS | HIDS | HIPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Network segment | Network segment | Single host | Single host |
| **Action** | Alerts only | Blocks/Drops/Resets | Alerts only | Terminates process / Blocks syscall |
| **Mode** | Promiscuous tap | Inline (in-path) | Agent | Agent |
| **KTU Note** | "Eyes" of the network | "Hands" of the network | "Eyes" of the host | "Hands" of the host |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inline Mode vs. Passive Mode (Critical for KTU):**
> An IDS operates in **passive (tap) mode** — it receives a copy of packets via port mirroring.
> An IPS operates in **inline mode** — all traffic *must* pass *through* the IPS device, giving it the power to drop packets.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — IDS Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the most frequently asked numerical/short-answer formulas in KTU exams for the IDS topic. Memorize the **confusion matrix** first, then derive every metric from it.

Let the four outcomes of an IDS decision be:
- **TP (True Positive):** Attack correctly detected.
- **TN (True Negative):** Benign traffic correctly allowed.
- **FP (False Positive):** Benign traffic falsely flagged (Type I error).
- **FN (False Negative):** Attack missed (Type II error — most dangerous).

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Fraction of actual attacks caught | Higher is better; must be **> 95%** in production |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | Fraction of benign events falsely flagged | Lower is better; target **< 1%** |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}} = 1 - \text{DR}$ | Fraction of attacks missed | Most critical metric for security |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | When IDS alerts, how often is it right? | Higher → fewer wasted analyst hours |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Overall correctness | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | Best single metric for IDS evaluation |
| **Bayes Posterior (Alert → Attack)** | $P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \dfrac{P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(\text{Alert})}$ | Probability attack is real given an alert | KTU favorite for **Bayesian IDS** questions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Rule:** In the table above, vertical bars were replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside formulas (e.g., $P(A \mid \text{Alert})$) to keep the markdown table valid. This is the **official KTU-style notation** for conditional probability.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise SOCs** (Security Operations Centers) deploy **hybrid IDS/IPS** as their primary detective control.
- **PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53** all mandate IDS/IPS deployment for compliance.
- **5G and IoT networks** are increasingly using **anomaly-based ML-driven IDSs** because signature-based methods cannot keep up with the device diversity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why F1-Score is the Right Metric for IDS

Most KTU Module-2 numerical problems will give you a confusion matrix and ask for these metrics. Below is the **exhaustive derivation** of the F1-Score, since it is the most commonly asked combined metric.

### Step 1 — Start With the Two Component Metrics

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$$

$$\text{Recall (Detection Rate)} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Problem

A high precision with a low recall (or vice-versa) gives a misleading picture of IDS performance. We need a **single metric** that balances both.

### Step 3 — Apply the Harmonic Mean

The **arithmetic mean** is sensitive to large values and ignores small ones. The **harmonic mean** penalizes extreme imbalance — exactly what we want in an IDS where missing an attack is catastrophic.

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{\text{Precision}} + \frac{1}{\text{Recall}}}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Algebraic Form

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Confusion-Matrix Forms

Substituting the confusion-matrix definitions back in:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}, \quad \text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \cdot \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}}{\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} + \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}}$$

### Step 6 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $(\text{TP} + \text{FP})(\text{TP} + \text{FN})$ to Clear Fractions

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{TP}^2}{\text{TP}(\text{TP} + \text{FN}) + \text{TP}(\text{TP} + \text{FP})}$$

### Step 7 — Final Simplified Form (KTU Board Favorite)

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{TP}}{2 \cdot \text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This final boxed formula is what you should write directly in the exam. It requires only **three values** (TP, FP, FN) and is the cleanest form.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** An IDS evaluated on a test corpus produced the following:
- $\text{TP} = 190$, $\text{FN} = 10$, $\text{FP} = 5$, $\text{TN} = 795$.

**Compute DR, FPR, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score.**

### Solution 1: Detection Rate

$$\text{DR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{190}{190 + 10} = \frac{190}{200} = 0.95 = 95\%$$

### Solution 2: False Positive Rate

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{5}{5 + 795} = \frac{5}{800} = 0.00625 = 0.625\%$$

### Solution 3: Precision

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{190}{190 + 5} = \frac{190}{195} \approx 0.974 = 97.4\%$$

### Solution 4: Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{190 + 795}{190 + 795 + 5 + 10} = \frac{985}{1000} = 0.985 = 98.5\%$$

### Solution 5: F1-Score (Using the Boxed Formula)

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 190}{2 \cdot 190 + 5 + 10} = \frac{380}{395} \approx 0.962 = 96.2\%$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Bayesian IDS Posterior Probability

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU exams.

### Step 1 — Define the Events

Let $A$ = attack occurred, $\bar{A}$ = no attack, $E$ = IDS raised an alert.

We want $P(A \mid E)$: "Given that the IDS raised an alert, what is the probability that an actual attack is in progress?"

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem

$$P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Denominator Using the Law of Total Probability

$$P(E) = P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(E \mid \bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{A})$$

### Step 4 — Final Bayesian IDS Formula

$$\boxed{P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(E \mid \bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{A})}}$$

Where:
- $P(A)$ = **base rate** of attacks (prior probability).
- $P(E \mid A)$ = **true positive rate** (sensitivity) of the IDS.
- $P(E \mid \bar{A})$ = **false positive rate** of the IDS.
- $1 - P(\bar{A}) = P(A)$ since events are exhaustive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If $P(A)$ is very low (e.g., 1 in 10,000 packets), even an IDS with 99% sensitivity and 1% FPR will produce a *low* $P(A \mid E)$. This is the **base-rate fallacy**, and is why SOC analysts suffer "alert fatigue."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — IDS/IPS Placement in a Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet((Internet)) --> fw[Firewall]
    fw --> ips1[NIPS Inline]
    ips1 --> coreSw{Core Switch}
    coreSw --> server1[Web Server with HIPS]
    coreSw --> server2[DB Server with HIDS]
    coreSw --> spanPort[SPAN Port]
    spanPort --> nids[NIDS Passive Sensor]
    nids --> soc[(SOC / SIEM Dashboard)]
    server1 --> soc
    server2 --> soc
    classDef defense fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef monitor fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px
    class fw,ips1,server1 defense
    class nids,soc,spanPort,server2 monitor
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **NIPS sits inline** (in the data path) right after the firewall.
- The **NIDS sits off a SPAN port** — it only sees a copy of traffic (passive).
- Each server has its own **HIDS or HIPS agent** reporting to the central SIEM.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Taxonomy of IDS Detection Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Intrusion Detection System]
    root --> sig[Signature Based Detection]
    root --> ano[Anomaly Based Detection]
    root --> state[Stateful Protocol Analysis]
    sig --> sigDesc[Matches traffic against known attack patterns in a signature database]
    ano --> anoDesc[Builds statistical model of normal behavior and flags deviations]
    state --> stateDesc[Tracks protocol state machines and flags protocol violations]
    sigDesc --> tools1[Example Tools Snort Suricata]
    anoDesc --> tools2[Example Tools ML based NIDS Zeek]
    stateDesc --> tools3[Example Tools Protocol decoders in Suricata]
    classDef method fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tool fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px
    class sig,ano,state method
    class tools1,tools2,tools3 tool
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture of an IDS Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pkt[Network Packets or Host Events] --> cap[Packet Capture Sensor]
    cap --> pre[Preprocessor Normalization TCP Reassembly Defragmentation]
    pre --> det[Detection Engine]
    det --> sig1[Signature Matcher]
    det --> ano1[Anomaly Profiler]
    det --> state1[Protocol State Tracker]
    sig1 --> out[Alert Output]
    ano1 --> out
    state1 --> out
    out --> log[Log File]
    out --> siem[SIEM Correlation]
    siem --> analyst[SOC Analyst Triage]
    classDef stage fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px
    class pkt,cap,pre,det,out,log,siem,analyst stage
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — IDS vs IPS Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Packet Arrives] --> checkInline{IPS in Inline Path}
    checkInline -- Yes --> blockDec{Action Required}
    blockDec -- Block --> drop[Drop Packet]
    blockDec -- Reset --> rst[Send TCP RST]
    blockDec -- Allow --> fwd1[Forward to Destination]
    blockDec -- Alert --> alert1[Raise Alert]
    checkInline -- No NIDS only --> copy[Copy via SPAN or TAP]
    copy --> analyze[Analyze Offline or Real time]
    analyze --> alert2[Raise Alert]
    alert2 --> socLog[Send to SOC]
    drop --> socLog
    rst --> socLog
    alert1 --> socLog
    classDef dec fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    class checkInline,blockDec dec
    class drop,rst,fwd1,alert1,alert2,analyze action
```
