<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) in terms of operational mode and response action.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Operation** | Passive (sniffing via SPAN/TAP) | Inline (in the data path) |
| **Response Action** | Generates alert; does **not** block | Actively **blocks, drops, or resets** the offending traffic |
| **Failure Impact** | Failure is silent; traffic continues | Failure can break connectivity (fail-open vs fail-closed) |
| **Analogy** | CCTV camera | CCTV + automatic door lock |

The IDS is purely **detective**, while the IPS is both **detective and preventive** (active response).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain the three primary detection methodologies used inside an IDS engine.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection):** Compares traffic against a database of known attack signatures. Highly accurate for known attacks but ineffective against zero-day threats.
2. **Anomaly-Based Detection:** Builds a statistical or ML profile of normal behavior; flags deviations. Capable of detecting zero-day attacks but prone to higher false-positive rates.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Tracks the legitimate state of protocols (e.g., TCP three-way handshake) and flags deviations from the protocol state machine.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

#### (a) **Explain the different types of Intrusion Detection Systems based on their scope of monitoring. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The classification of IDSs by **scope** is the KTU-prescribed taxonomy:

**1. Network-Based IDS (NIDS):**
- Placed at strategic points (e.g., between firewall and core switch).
- Monitors entire network segments in **promiscuous mode**.
- Uses a **tap or SPAN port** to receive a copy of packets.
- Examples: Snort, Suricata, Zeek.
- Detects: port scans, DoS, malware C2 traffic, worm propagation.
- *Limitation:* Encrypted traffic is opaque; high-speed networks (10 Gbps+) can drop packets.

**2. Host-Based IDS (HIDS):**
- Installed as an agent on the host whose activity must be monitored.
- Monitors: system calls, log files (syslog, Event Viewer), file integrity (Tripwire), running processes, registry edits.
- Examples: OSSEC, Wazuh, Tripwire.
- *Strength:* Can see encrypted traffic *after* decryption at the host.
- *Limitation:* Consumes host resources; no visibility into other hosts.

**3. Protocol-Based IDS (PIDS):**
- Sits at the front-end of a server (e.g., in front of a web server farm).
- Monitors and analyzes the **protocol state** between server and client (HTTPS, SMB, DNS).
- *Specialty:* Often deployed at the point where SSL/TLS terminates, allowing it to inspect encrypted payloads.

**4. Application Protocol-Based IDS (APIDS):**
- Lives inside a specific application server group (e.g., database servers).
- Interprets application-layer protocol semantics (e.g., HTTP request methods, SQL commands) to detect application-level attacks like **SQL injection** or **XSS**.

**5. Hybrid IDS:**
- The most common enterprise deployment.
- Combines NIDS + HIDS + APIDS under a centralized management console (SIEM).
- Eliminates the blind spots of any single type.

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram to draw on the exam board:** A simple table or block diagram showing each type, its placement, and what it monitors. KTU examiners reward well-labeled diagrams.

#### (b) **Discuss signature-based and anomaly-based detection methods in detail. Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Signature-Based Detection:**
- **How it works:** Maintains a database of unique patterns (signatures) extracted from known attacks. Each signature is a sequence of bytes, a regular expression, or a hash that uniquely identifies the attack.
- **Example:** Snort rule for detecting a SQL injection attempt:
  `alert tcp any any → any 80 (content:"' OR 1=1--"; msg:"SQL Injection Attempt"; sid:1000001;)`
- **Advantages:** Very high accuracy for known attacks; very low false-positive rate; easy for analysts to interpret.
- **Limitations:** Cannot detect **zero-day** attacks; requires constant signature updates; ineffective against **polymorphic malware** that mutates its byte pattern.

**Anomaly-Based Detection:**
- **How it works:** Builds a model of *normal* behavior during a training phase using statistics, machine learning, or rule-based heuristics. Any statistically significant deviation from this baseline is flagged.
- **Techniques used:**
  - Statistical methods (mean, variance of packet sizes, inter-arrival times).
  - Machine learning: clustering (k-means), classification (SVM, Random Forest), deep learning (autoencoders).
  - Rule-based heuristics (e.g., "CPU usage > 95% for > 60s is abnormal").
- **Advantages:** Can detect **zero-day** attacks; no signature database required; adapts to evolving threats.
- **Limitations:** Higher false-positive rate (the "boy-who-cried-wolf" problem); requires a clean training dataset; computationally expensive.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Target** | Known attacks | Unknown / zero-day attacks |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low | High |
| **False Negative Rate** | High for novel attacks | Low |
| **Update Requirement** | Frequent signature DB updates | Periodic model retraining |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | High |
| **Best Use Case** | Perimeter defense for known malware | Insider threat, APT detection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students often **write the same explanation for both methods**. Marks are split on clearly highlighting the *contrast* — make a side-by-side table to score full marks.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

#### (a) **Explain the architecture of an IDS. Describe the role of each component in detecting intrusions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A standard IDS architecture consists of **four functional components**:

**1. Sensor / Agent (Data Collection Layer):**
- The "eyes" of the IDS. Captures raw packets from the network (NIDS) or events from the host (HIDS).
- For NIDS: runs in promiscuous mode on a SPAN port or hardware tap.
- For HIDS: hooks into system call tables, log files, and the file system.

**2. Preprocessor / Normalization Engine:**
- Cleans and normalizes traffic before detection.
- **Functions:** IP defragmentation, TCP stream reassembly, decoding application-layer protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMTP), stripping evasion techniques (e.g., unicode encoding).

**3. Detection Engine (The Brain):**
- The core component that decides whether traffic is malicious.
- Uses one or a combination of:
  - **Signature matcher** (regex / pattern matching against attack DB).
  - **Anomaly profiler** (statistical or ML-based).
  - **Protocol state tracker** (state machine validation).
- Outputs: a verdict + confidence score for each event.

**4. Alert / Response Module:**
- When the detection engine flags an event, this module formats an alert (syslog, SNMP trap, JSON) and forwards it to:
  - Local log files.
  - SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) systems.
  - Email / SMS to SOC analysts.
- In an **IPS**, this module also takes blocking action (drop, reset, quarantine).

**Optional Component: Management Console**
- A centralized GUI to configure sensors, view alerts, update signatures, and generate reports.

**Block Diagram (to draw on answer sheet):**
```
[Sensor] → [Preprocessor] → [Detection Engine] → [Alert Module] → [SIEM / Console]
                                                       ↓ (IPS only)
                                                [Blocking Action]
```

#### (b) **The following confusion matrix was obtained from an IDS evaluation: TP=180, FN=20, FP=10, TN=790. Compute the Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** TP = 180, FN = 20, FP = 10, TN = 790. Total = 1000.

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity):**

$$\text{DR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate:**

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{10}{10 + 790} = \frac{10}{800} = 0.0125 = 1.25\%$$

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{180}{180 + 10} = \frac{180}{190} \approx 0.9474 = 94.74\%$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{Total}} = \frac{180 + 790}{1000} = \frac{970}{1000} = 0.97 = 97\%$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

Using the simplified boxed formula:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{TP}}{2 \cdot \text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{2 \cdot 180}{2 \cdot 180 + 10 + 20} = \frac{360}{370} \approx 0.9730 = 97.30\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> - **Do not** use Accuracy as your sole metric — it can be misleading when attacks are rare (e.g., 99% accuracy on a dataset with only 1% attacks is meaningless).
> - **Always** state the F1-Score for IDS performance — it is the KTU-expected composite metric.
> - **Do not** confuse FPR (False Positive Rate) with FDR (False Discovery Rate = 1 − Precision). Many students interchange them and lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Recap — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **IDS = Detective + Passive.** **IPS = Preventive + Active (Inline).**
- The **three detection methods** are: **Signature-based, Anomaly-based, Stateful Protocol Analysis.**
- The **five types of IDSs** (KTU syllabus): **NIDS, HIDS, PIDS, APIDS, Hybrid IDS.**
- An **NIDS** sniffs from a **SPAN port or TAP**; an **NIPS** is placed **inline** in the data path.
- **HIDS** monitors **system calls, logs, and file integrity** on a single host.
- **Signature-based** = low FP, cannot detect zero-days. **Anomaly-based** = detects zero-days, higher FP.
- The four outcomes: **TP, TN, FP, FN** — memorize the confusion matrix.
- **Detection Rate (Recall)** = TP / (TP + FN). Higher is better.
- **FPR** = FP / (FP + TN). Lower is better.
- **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP). Higher means alerts are trustworthy.
- **F1-Score** = 2·TP / (2·TP + FP + FN) → KTU-preferred composite metric.
- **Accuracy alone is misleading** in IDS due to class imbalance.
- **Bayesian IDS Posterior** $P(A \mid E)$ quantifies the probability an alert is a real attack — the basis of alert prioritization in SOCs.
- **Real-world examples:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (NIDS); OSSEC, Wazuh, Tripwire (HIDS).
- **Defense in Depth:** IDS/IPS is the **second line of defense** behind the firewall.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the IDS architecture — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a clean diagram.
- **Exam Tip:** For numericals, **show the substitution step** before finalizing the answer — partial marks are awarded even if the final value is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Intrusion Detection Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Other Intrusion Detection Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Other Intrusion Detection Technology — KTU Module 2

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the context of KTU's PECST863 syllabus, **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** are broadly classified into two primary paradigms: **Network-Based IDS (NIDS)** and **Host-Based IDS (HIDS)**. The module topic "Other Intrusion Detection Technology" refers to a sophisticated *third tier* of detection mechanisms that operate *orthogonal* or *complementary* to these traditional approaches.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**  
> *Other Intrusion Detection Technologies* encompass advanced, specialised, and hybrid detection methodologies—including **Anomaly-Based Detection (ABD)**, **Stateful Protocol Analysis (SPA)**, **Honeypots/Honeynets**, **Application Protocol Analysis**, **File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)**, **System Call Sequence Analysis**, **Wireless IDS (WIDS)**, and **Machine Learning–Driven IDS**—that augment conventional signature-based and rule-based engines by identifying novel, zero-day, and complex multi-stage attacks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Metaphor

Imagine a large international airport. There are several layers of security:

| Airport Layer | Cyber Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Metal detectors** (signature match — gun shape = alarm) | Signature-based IDS (SNORT rule match) |
| **Behavioural officers** (sweating, loitering, unusual gait) | Anomaly-based IDS (statistical deviation) |
| **Decoy VIP lounges** (intruder takes bait) | **Honeypots / Honeynets** |
| **X-ray baggage scanners** (protocol-aware inspection) | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
| **Bag-tag reconciliation** (file hashes) | File Integrity Monitoring (Tripwire) |
| **AI facial recognition** (learns over time) | ML-based IDS (supervised/unsupervised) |
| **Drone-detection radar** (wireless spectrum) | Wireless IDS (WIDS) |

> [!NOTE]  
> Just as an airport does not rely on *one* security layer, modern enterprise networks deploy **defence-in-depth** using these "other" detection technologies alongside the primary NIDS/HIDS.

### 1.3 Why These Technologies Matter

Traditional signature-based NIDS suffers from three well-documented limitations:

1. **Zero-Day Blindness** — cannot detect attacks without prior signatures.
2. **Encrypted Traffic Blindness** — payload inspection fails on TLS 1.3.
3. **Polymorphic Malware** — code mutation defeats static patterns.

The "other" technologies listed in this module specifically address these gaps. The standard performance metrics you must memorise for KTU are:

- **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate (DR):** **TPR = TP / (TP + FN)**
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** **FPR = FP / (FP + TN)**
- **Precision:** **P = TP / (TP + FP)**
- **F1-Score:** **F1 = 2·P·R / (P + R)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of "Other" Intrusion Detection Technologies

The KTU 2024 scheme recognises the following categorical breakdown:

### 2.1.1 Anomaly-Based Detection (ABD)
- **How it works:** Builds a *profile* of normal system/network behaviour. Any statistically significant deviation flags an intrusion.
- **Techniques:** Statistical (mean, variance, multivariate), clustering (k-means, DBSCAN), classification (SVM, Random Forest, Deep Neural Networks).
- **Strengths:** Detects **zero-day attacks**, polymorphic malware, insider threats.
- **Weakness:** Higher **False Positive Rate (FPR)**.
- **Mathematical core:** Uses hypothesis testing — H₀ (normal) vs H₁ (intrusion).

### 2.1.2 Stateful Protocol Analysis (SPA)
- **How it works:** Tracks protocol state machines (e.g., TCP three-way handshake, DNS query–response pairing, SMTP command sequencing). An attack is flagged when a transition violates the expected state.
- **Example:** A TCP packet with both SYN and FIN flags set violates RFC 793 and is a classic **Christmas Tree / Xmas scan**.
- **Analogy:** A train ticket inspector who knows the *exact sequence* of stations — a passenger appearing at Station 5 without passing through Stations 1–4 is flagged.

### 2.1.3 Application Protocol Analysis / Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)
- **How it works:** Parses application-layer protocols (HTTP, SIP, SQL, SMB) and validates commands against RFC specifications.
- **Use case:** Detecting SQL injection (looking for `' OR 1=1 --`), HTTP request smuggling, malformed SIP INVITE floods.

### 2.1.4 Honeypots and Honeynets
- **Honeypot:** A decoy system with **no production value** — any interaction is, by definition, suspicious.
- **Honeynet:** A network of honeypots simulating a full enterprise.
- **Modes:**
  - **Low-interaction:** Emulates services (e.g., Honeyd, Cowrie) — safer, less data.
  - **High-interaction:** Real OS/services (e.g., Gen-III honeynets) — richer intelligence, higher risk.
- **Analogy:** A fake jewellery box left in a museum — only a thief touches it.

### 2.1.5 File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)
- **How it works:** Computes cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) of critical files (binaries, configs, /etc/passwd) and alerts on any change.
- **Canonical example:** **Tripwire** — the de-facto FIM tool.
- **Limitation:** Detects *post-exploitation* tampering, not the attack vector itself.

### 2.1.6 System Call Sequence Analysis
- **How it works:** Uses **system call sequences** (e.g., `open → read → write → execve`) as a behavioural fingerprint. Models trained on normal traces flag anomalous calls.
- **Foundational work:** Forrest et al. (1996) — sequence-time-delay embedding (stide).
- **Analogy:** A dance choreography — each ballet move in order is normal; an unexpected pirouette signals trouble.

### 2.1.7 Wireless Intrusion Detection System (WIDS)
- **How it works:** Monitors 802.11 frames for rogue APs, evil-twin attacks, deauth floods, MAC spoofing.
- **Tools:** Kismet, Aircrack-ng suite, Snort WiFi plugins.

### 2.1.8 Machine Learning / AI-Driven IDS
- **Supervised:** Random Forest, SVM, CNN-LSTM on labelled datasets (NSL-KDD, CICIDS2017, UNSW-NB15).
- **Unsupervised:** Autoencoders, Isolation Forest — used when labelled attack data is scarce.
- **Reinforcement learning:** Adaptive agents that evolve with the threat landscape.

### 2.1.9 Hybrid / Multi-Layer IDS
- Combines signature + anomaly + stateful + honeypot intelligence into a unified **SIEM-correlated** event stream.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> These formulas are **exam-critical**. Memorise the variable definitions and units.

| Metric / Equation | Symbol / Variable | Definition | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| True Positive Rate (Sensitivity) | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}}$ | Fraction of actual attacks correctly flagged | 0 to 1 (1 = perfect) |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP}+\text{TN}}$ | Fraction of normal traffic falsely flagged | 0 to 1 (0 = perfect) |
| True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN}+\text{FP}}$ | Complement of FPR | 0 to 1 |
| Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $P = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}}$ | Of all alerts, how many are real | 0 to 1 |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}}$ | Synonym of TPR | 0 to 1 |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | 0 to 1 |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{TP+TN}}{\text{TP+TN+FP+FN}}$ | Overall correctness | 0 to 1 |
| Matthews Correlation Coeff. | $\text{MCC} = \dfrac{\text{TP·TN}-\text{FP·FN}}{\sqrt{(\text{TP+FP})(\text{TP+FN})(\text{TN+FP})(\text{TN+FN})}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced data | −1 to +1 |
| Bayesian Detection Prob. | $P(I \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid I)\,P(I)}{P(A \mid I)\,P(I) + P(A \mid \neg I)\,P(\neg I)}$ | Posterior probability of intrusion given alert | 0 to 1 |
| ROC Curve | Plot of TPR vs FPR as threshold varies | Visualises classifier trade-off | AUC ∈ [0,1] |
| Anomaly Score (Z-Normal) | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | How many σ a sample deviates | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These technologies form the backbone of modern **Security Operations Centres (SOCs)**:

- **Anomaly detection** → used by **Darktrace**, **Vectra AI** for network behavioural analysis.
- **Honeypots** → deployed by **banking sector** to track Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs).
- **FIM** → mandated by **PCI-DSS Requirement 11.5** for payment card industry compliance.
- **ML-IDS** → used in **5G core networks** for real-time signalling attack detection.
- **WIDS** → used in **airports and hospitals** to detect rogue medical IoT devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to IDS Posterior Probability

The most important derivation in this module is the **Bayesian detection probability** — how to update our belief that an alert corresponds to a true intrusion.

### Step 1 — Define the Events
Let:
- $I$ = event that an **intrusion** is occurring.
- $\neg I$ = event that traffic is **normal**.
- $A$ = event that the **IDS raised an alert**.

### Step 2 — State Bayes' Theorem (Axiom of Conditional Probability)

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid I)\,P(I)}{P(A)}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Law of Total Probability to the Denominator

The denominator $P(A)$ is the probability of an alert under *all* mutually exclusive hypotheses. Only two exist (intrusion or normal):

$$
P(A) = P(A \mid I)\,P(I) + P(A \mid \neg I)\,P(\neg I)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back to Obtain the IDS Posterior Formula

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid I)\,P(I)}{P(A \mid I)\,P(I) + P(A \mid \neg I)\,P(\neg I)}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret Each Term

| Symbol | Meaning in IDS Context |
|---|---|
| $P(I \mid A)$ | **Posterior**: probability the alert is a *real* intrusion |
| $P(A \mid I)$ | **True Positive Rate (TPR)** — alert given attack |
| $P(A \mid \neg I)$ | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — false alarm |
| $P(I)$ | **Prior base-rate** of intrusions (typically very small, e.g., $10^{-6}$) |
| $P(\neg I)$ | $1 - P(I)$ |

### Step 6 — Numerical Example (Worked Out)

Suppose a network sees **1 intrusion per 1,000,000 packets** and the IDS has TPR = 0.95, FPR = 0.01. Find $P(I \mid A)$.

Given:
- $P(I) = 10^{-6}$
- $P(\neg I) = 1 - 10^{-6} = 0.999999$
- $P(A \mid I) = 0.95$
- $P(A \mid \neg I) = 0.01$

Numerator:
$$
P(A \mid I)\,P(I) = 0.95 \times 10^{-6} = 9.5 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Denominator:
$$
P(A \mid I)\,P(I) + P(A \mid \neg I)\,P(\neg I) = 9.5 \times 10^{-7} + 0.01 \times 0.999999
$$

$$
= 9.5 \times 10^{-7} + 9\,999.99 \times 10^{-6} = 9.5 \times 10^{-7} + 9\,999.99 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
= 0.00000095 + 0.00999999 = 0.01000094
$$

Posterior:
$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{9.5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.01000094} \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0095\%
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with 95% TPR and only 1% FPR, the *posterior* probability that any random alert is a real intrusion is **less than 0.01%**. This is the famous **base-rate fallacy** in IDS design — and it is why SOC analysts suffer from alert fatigue. The "other" technologies (e.g., correlation, context, ML scoring) exist precisely to push this posterior upward.

### Step 7 — Connection to ROC and Threshold Selection

By varying the IDS decision threshold $\tau$, we obtain pairs (FPR($\tau$), TPR($\tau$)) plotted as the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve**. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** summarises detector quality:

$$
\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})
$$

An AUC of 1.0 = perfect detector; 0.5 = random guess.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Technology | Detection Basis | Detects Zero-Day? | False Positive Rate | Encryption-Tolerant? | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Signature (NIDS/HIDS — context only) | Pattern match | ❌ No | Very Low | ❌ No | Low |
| Anomaly (Statistical) | Deviation from profile | ✅ Yes | High | ✅ Yes | Medium |
| Anomaly (ML-based) | Learned model | ✅ Yes | Medium | ✅ Yes | High |
| Stateful Protocol Analysis | RFC state machines | ⚠ Partial | Low | ⚠ Partial | Low |
| Application Protocol / DPI | Command semantics | ⚠ Partial | Low | ❌ No (needs decryption) | High |
| Honeypot | Any interaction | ✅ Yes | Very Low (almost 0) | ✅ Yes | Variable |
| File Integrity (FIM) | Hash comparison | ❌ Post-exploit only | Very Low | N/A | Low |
| System Call Sequence | Behavioural fingerprint | ✅ Yes | Medium | ✅ Yes | Medium |
| WIDS | 802.11 frame anomalies | ✅ Yes | Medium | N/A (radio) | Low |
| Hybrid SIEM | Correlation of all | ✅ Yes | Low (with tuning) | ✅ Yes | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hybrid Multi-Layer IDS Architecture

```mermaid

    E2 -- Yes --> F
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Detection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Packet Arrives]) --> P1[Capture via libpcap or AF_PACKET]:::proc
    P1 --> P2[Decode layers L2 to L7]:::proc
    P2 --> P3{Signature match?}:::dec
    P3 -- Yes --> ALERT1[Generate HIGH severity alert]:::alert
    P3 -- No --> P4{Anomaly score above tau?}:::dec
    P4 -- Yes --> ALERT2[MEDIUM severity alert with anomaly explanation]:::alert
    P4 -- No --> P5{State machine violation?}:::dec
    P5 -- Yes --> ALERT3[MEDIUM severity protocol alert]:::alert
    P5 -- No --> P6{Honeypot interaction logged?}:::dec
    P6 -- Yes --> ALERT4[CRITICAL honeypot breach]:::alert
    P6 -- No --> P7[Packet classified as BENIGN]:::ok

    classDef proc fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef alert fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between signature-based intrusion detection and anomaly-based intrusion detection. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection basis** | Matches traffic against a database of known attack patterns (signatures/rules) | Compares traffic against a learned "normal" profile; flags statistical deviation |
| **Detection of zero-day** | ❌ Cannot detect novel/unknown attacks | ✅ Can detect previously unseen attacks |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low (FPR ≈ 0.001) | High (FPR can be 0.05–0.20) |
| **Example tool** | **Snort** with rule `alert tcp any any → any 80 (msg:"ET SHELLCODE";)` | **Statistical** z-score detector or **ML-based** Random Forest trained on CICIDS2017 |

*(1 mark for crisp definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for example tool.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is a Honeypot? Explain the difference between low-interaction and high-interaction honeypots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **honeypot** is a decoy computing resource deployed within a network that has **no legitimate production purpose**; therefore, *any* access to it is treated as suspicious and is logged for analysis. It serves as an early-warning and intelligence-gathering mechanism.

| Property | Low-Interaction Honeypot | High-Interaction Honeypot |
|---|---|---|
| **Emulation depth** | Emulates only the *service banner* and limited commands (e.g., fake SSH port) | Runs a *real* OS and real vulnerable services |
| **Risk** | Very low — attacker is contained in emulation | High — attacker may compromise the honeypot and pivot |
| **Data collected** | Limited: scan attempts, banner grabs | Rich: full attack TTP (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures), malware samples, lateral movement |
| **Example tools** | Honeyd, Cowrie (low-mode) | Gen-III Honeynet, Cowrie (high-mode) |

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark contrast + 1 mark example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**  
> - Marks are deducted for **missing the formula derivation** — every 14-mark sub-question with a numerical aspect **must** show the substitution and arithmetic.  
> - Avoid writing *"Snort is a HIDS"* — it is a **NIDS**. Mixing HIDS and NIDS examples is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.  
> - When asked to *compare*, **tabular format** is awarded higher marks than running prose. Always draw a comparison table.  
> - Do **not** write only tool names without explaining *how* they detect — that is the most common 3-mark loss.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Anomaly Detection Focus*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and working of an **anomaly-based intrusion detection system**. With the help of suitable diagrams, describe how statistical methods (mean, variance) and machine learning methods (SVM, Random Forest) can be used to flag intrusions. Discuss the **base-rate fallacy** using Bayes' theorem.

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (2 marks):**
The anomaly-based IDS has four stages:

1. **Data Collection Module** — gathers network flow records (NetFlow, sFlow) or host audit logs.
2. **Profile / Model Building Module** — during a *training phase*, learns the statistical distribution (or ML model) of normal behaviour.
3. **Detection Module** — at runtime, computes an **anomaly score** for each new observation; if score > threshold $\tau$, an alert is raised.
4. **Response Module** — sends alert to SIEM, updates the profile via online learning.

**Statistical Method — Z-Score (1.5 marks):**
For each feature $x_i$ (e.g., packet length), the detector maintains running mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. An alert is raised when:
$$
Z = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma} > \tau \quad (\text{typically } \tau = 3)
$$
This detects outliers in *univariate* features. **Multivariate** extension uses Mahalanobis distance:
$$
D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}
$$

**Machine Learning Methods (1.5 marks):**
- **SVM (One-Class SVM):** Learns a hyperplane that separates normal data from origin in feature space; deviations across the hyperplane are anomalies.
- **Random Forest:** Ensemble of decision trees trained on labelled flows; final prediction = majority vote. Computes *anomaly score* as average path length (akin to Isolation Forest).

**Base-Rate Fallacy (2 marks):**
Even an IDS with TPR = 95% and FPR = 1% yields $P(I \mid A) < 0.01\%$ when intrusion base rate $P(I) = 10^{-6}$ (as shown in §3.1 Step 6). Hence analysts are overwhelmed by false positives. Mitigation: correlate alerts, incorporate context (asset criticality, threat intel).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A network IDS has the following confusion matrix from a test on **10,000 flows**:

| | Predicted Attack | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP = 180 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Normal** | FP = 90 | TN = 9,710 |

Compute **(i)** Detection Rate, **(ii)** False Positive Rate, **(iii)** Precision, **(iv)** F1-Score, **(v)** Accuracy. Comment on the detector's suitability for a high-security environment.

**Model Solution (step-by-step, 1.4 marks each formula):**

Given: TP = 180, FN = 20, FP = 90, TN = 9710, Total = 10,000.

**(i) Detection Rate (TPR):**
$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}} = \frac{180}{180+20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

**(ii) False Positive Rate (FPR):**
$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP}+\text{TN}} = \frac{90}{90+9710} = \frac{90}{9800} \approx 0.00918
$$

**(iii) Precision:**
$$
P = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}} = \frac{180}{180+90} = \frac{180}{270} \approx 0.6667
$$

**(iv) F1-Score:**
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.6667 \times 0.90}{0.6667 + 0.90} = \frac{1.200}{1.5667} \approx 0.7661
$$

**(v) Accuracy:**
$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{\text{TP}+\text{TN}}{\text{Total}} = \frac{180+9710}{10000} = \frac{9890}{10000} = 0.989
$$

**Commentary (towards the end of 7 marks):**
- TPR = 0.90 is acceptable but 10% of attacks still evade — **not suitable** for high-security environments without a secondary detector.
- FPR = 0.92% yields a low *but non-negligible* alert volume — at 1 million flows/day this is 9,200 false positives.
- Precision = 0.67 means **1 in 3 alerts is a false alarm** — analyst fatigue likely.
- F1 = 0.77 indicates a moderate detector; threshold tuning or hybrid approach recommended.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Honeypots & Stateful Protocol Focus*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Honeypots and Honeynets** as an "other intrusion detection technology". With a neat diagram, describe a **Gen-III Honeynet** architecture. List any **four advantages** and **three limitations**.

**Model Solution:**

**Role (2 marks):**
Honeypots serve three purposes:
1. **Detection** — every interaction is by definition malicious (zero false positives in the strict sense).
2. **Deflection** — diverts attackers from real production assets.
3. **Intelligence** — captures malware binaries, attacker TTPs, and command-and-control (C2) infrastructure.

**Gen-III Honeynet Architecture (2 marks diagram + 1 mark explanation):**

A Gen-III Honeynet (per the Honeynet Project) consists of three components:

- **Honeywall (gateway)** — a *transparent* Layer-2 bridge (e.g., `roo` Honeywall CD-ROM) that sits between the honeypots and the Internet. It performs **data control** (limit outbound traffic to prevent attacker abuse) and **data capture** (Sebek-style keystroke and process logging).
- **Honeypots** — multiple high-interaction VMs running diverse OS/services.
- **Centralised Log Server** — collects `p0f`, `Snort`, `argus`, and Sebek logs for forensic reconstruction.

```
[Internet] → [Honeywall CD-ROM Bridge] → [HP1 / HP2 / HP3 / …] → [Central SOC DB]
```

**Four Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Zero-false-positive detection (no production traffic).
2. Captures unknown / zero-day exploits.
3. Provides full attack lifecycle data.
4. Low maintenance cost for low-interaction variants.

**Three Limitations (0.5 marks):**
1. High-interaction honeypots can be turned against the network.
2. "Fingerprinting" — attackers can detect virtual environments.
3. Limited coverage — only catches attackers who reach the honeypot subnet.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Stateful Protocol Analysis (SPA)**. With examples, describe how an SPA engine detects **(i)** a TCP SYN-FIN scan, **(ii)** a DNS zone-transfer abuse, and **(iii)** an HTTP request smuggling attempt.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
SPA tracks the **state** of multi-step protocol exchanges (e.g., TCP connection states, DNS transaction IDs, HTTP request–response pairs) and flags any transition that violates the RFC-defined state machine.

**(i) TCP SYN-FIN Scan (2 marks):**
RFC 793 specifies that a SYN (synchronise) flag initiates a connection, and a FIN (finish) flag terminates it. A single packet with **both SYN and FIN set** is a logically impossible transition: the connection has not yet been established (SYN state), yet it is supposedly being closed (FIN state). Attackers (e.g., using `nmap -sS -sF`) use this to bypass stateless firewalls. The SPA engine flags the packet because the **state machine has no transition defined for SYN|FIN**.

**(ii) DNS Zone-Transfer Abuse (2 marks):**
A legitimate DNS zone transfer (AXFR) is requested **only by secondary name servers** of the same zone. An SPA engine tracks:
- The source IP of the AXFR requester.
- Whether the requester is in the NS record allow-list.
- The response size — a full zone transfer is unusually large.

If a client outside the NS list requests AXFR, or the response contains the entire zone, the SPA raises an alert (`Snort sid:255`). This detects attempts like `dig @ns1.example.com example.com AXFR` from an unauthorised IP.

**(iii) HTTP Request Smuggling (2 marks):**
HTTP/1.1 allows `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` headers in the *same* request. A malicious client can use mismatched CL/TE values to "smuggle" a second hidden request. The SPA engine validates that:
- Only **one** of CL or TE is present (RFC 7230 §3.3.3).
- The TE `chunk` size is a valid hexadecimal integer.
- The chunk terminator `0\r\n\r\n` is present.

A request with `Content-Length: 50` and `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` whose chunk size is `0` is detected as a smuggling attempt and blocked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield Bullet Recap — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ⭐ **Signature vs Anomaly** — signature = pattern match, low FPR, no zero-day; anomaly = statistical/ML, high FPR, detects zero-day.
- ⭐ **SPA** validates protocol state machines (TCP, DNS, HTTP) — flags impossible transitions.
- ⭐ **Honeypot** = decoy with no production value; **Honeynet** = network of honeypots; **Gen-III** uses transparent Honeywall bridge.
- ⭐ **FIM / Tripwire** uses cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-256) for tamper detection.
- ⭐ **System call sequence** analysis uses behavioural fingerprints (Forrest's stide).
- ⭐ **WIDS** monitors 802.11 — detects rogue APs, evil-twin, deauth floods.
- ⭐ **ML-IDS** datasets: NSL-KDD, CICIDS2017, UNSW-NB15.
- ⭐ **Confusion matrix** metrics: TPR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy, MCC.
- ⭐ **Bayes' formula** for IDS posterior — proves base-rate fallacy numerically.
- ⭐ **ROC curve** plots TPR vs FPR; **AUC** = 1.0 is perfect detector.
- ⭐ **Defence-in-depth** = combination of NIDS + HIDS + Honeypot + SIEM correlation.
- ⭐ Exam tip: always present **comparison tables** when asked *"differentiate"*.
- ⭐ Exam tip: every numerical answer must show the **substitution and arithmetic** to earn full marks.
- ⭐ Tool-to-architecture mapping: **Snort/Suricata** = NIDS; **OSSEC/Wazuh** = HIDS; **Tripwire** = FIM; **Honeyd/Cowrie** = Honeypot; **Kismet** = WIDS; **Splunk/ELK** = SIEM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Prevention Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Prevention Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is a network security appliance or software solution that monitors network and/or system activities for malicious or policy-violating behavior, and **proactively blocks** such activity in real-time. Unlike a passive Intrusion Detection System (IDS), an IPS is positioned **inline** within the traffic flow, granting it the authority to drop malicious packets, reset connections, and quarantine offending hosts before damage occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST863 Module 2):** An IPS is the *active, control-plane* evolution of the IDS. It combines **detection intelligence** with **automated response capability** to form a self-defending network node.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Officer

Imagine a busy international airport terminal. A **CCTV camera** that only *records* suspicious behavior and alerts a guard is analogous to an **IDS** — it detects but does not intervene. Now consider a **TSA officer standing right at the boarding gate** who not only scans every passenger but also **physically stops and detains** anyone carrying prohibited items before they board the plane. This officer is the **IPS** — placed directly in the path of flow, inspecting, deciding, and *acting* in real time.

| Component | Airport Analogy | Network Security Role |
|---|---|---|
| CCTV Camera | Records only | Intrusion Detection System (IDS) |
| TSA Officer at Gate | Stops threats in real time | **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** |
| X-Ray Machine | Examines contents | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) Engine |
| Wanted List Database | Reference for known threats | Signature Database |
| Behavioral Profiler | Detects unusual movement | Anomaly Detection Engine |

## 1.3 Core Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definitions to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Inline Placement:** The IPS is physically or logically inserted in the data path so that *all* traffic must traverse it.
> - **True Positive (TP):** Legitimate attack correctly blocked.
> - **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate traffic wrongly blocked.
> - **False Negative (FN):** Attack wrongly allowed through.
> - **Shunning:** The act of automatically blocking traffic from a source IP once an attack is confirmed.
> - **TCP Reset (RST):** Sending a forged TCP RST packet to terminate a malicious connection.

## 1.4 Classification of IPS

```
                        Intrusion Prevention System
                                   |
        +----------+-----------+-----------+----------+
        |          |           |           |          |
   NIPS      WIPS      HIPS      NBA      HBIPS
   (Network)  (Wireless) (Host)  (Network  (Host-Based
                                Behavior) Behavior)
```

- **NIPS (Network-based IPS):** Sits at network choke points (e.g., behind firewall).
- **WIPS (Wireless IPS):** Monitors 802.11 radio spectrum.
- **HIPS (Host-based IPS):** Installed on individual endpoints.
- **NBA (Network Behavior Analysis):** Detects traffic-flow anomalies.
- **HBIPS (Host-Based Behavioral IPS):** Monitors system calls and process behavior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an IPS

An IPS operates as a **closed-loop control system** with the following functional pipeline:

1. **Traffic Capture:** Packets are mirrored (port spanning) or physically forwarded (inline TAP/bump-in-the-wire) to the IPS engine.
2. **Normalization:** Packets are reassembled, defragmented, and decoded into a canonical form to evade evasion techniques.
3. **Detection Engine:** Applies multiple detection methodologies in parallel.
4. **Decision Module:** A policy-driven decision unit that classifies traffic as *malicious*, *suspicious*, or *benign*.
5. **Response Engine:** Executes the chosen action (drop, reset, quarantine, alert, log).
6. **Feedback Loop:** Updates signature databases, refines thresholds, and trains ML models.

## 2.2 Detection Methodologies

### A. Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)
- Matches traffic against a database of known attack **patterns (signatures)**.
- Examples: Snort rules, Suricata rules.
- **Strength:** Near-zero false positives for known threats.
- **Weakness:** Cannot detect *zero-day* attacks.

### B. Anomaly-Based Detection
- Establishes a **statistical baseline** of normal behavior (bandwidth, packet size, protocol frequency).
- Flags deviations beyond a threshold $\tau$.

$$ \text{Anomaly Score} = \frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma} $$

where $x$ is the observed value, $\mu$ is the baseline mean, and $\sigma$ is the baseline standard deviation.

- **Strength:** Detects novel attacks.
- **Weakness:** Higher false positive rate.

### C. Stateful Protocol Analysis
- Verifies that traffic conforms to the expected state machine of the relevant protocol (e.g., RFC-compliant TCP handshake, HTTP request grammar).

### D. Heuristic / Behavioral Analysis
- Uses expert rules, sandboxing, or machine learning to identify suspicious behavior patterns.

## 2.3 Inline vs. Passive Deployment

| Feature | Passive (IDS) | Inline (IPS) |
|---|---|---|
| Position | Out-of-band (TAP/SPAN) | In the data path |
| Action | Alert only | Block + Alert |
| Latency Impact | None | 50–500 $\mu$s typical |
| Failure Mode | Open (fails open) | Configurable (fail-open / fail-closed) |
| Bypass Risk | High | Low (single chokepoint) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** An IPS must be configured to **fail-open** in most enterprise networks, meaning if the device crashes, traffic flows through uninterrupted. This prevents the IPS itself from becoming a single point of failure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (Recall) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of real attacks caught | Ratio (0–1) |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fraction of benign traffic wrongly blocked | Ratio (0–1) |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Accuracy of alerts/block actions | Ratio (0–1) |
| F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | Ratio (0–1) |
| Anomaly Score (Z-Normal) | $Z = \dfrac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$ | Statistical deviation | Dimensionless |
| Throughput Requirement | $T = N_{pkt} \times S_{avg} \times 8$ | Bandwidth needed to inspect | bits per second (bps) |
| Latency Budget | $L_{IPS} \leq L_{SLA} - L_{link}$ | Max allowable IPS processing delay | milliseconds (ms) |

> **Note:** The pipe symbol has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.5 IPS Response Actions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favorite — List the Six Standard IPS Response Actions:**
> 1. **Drop Packet** — Silently discards the offending packet.
> 2. **Block / Shun Source** — Blocks all future traffic from source IP for $T$ seconds.
> 3. **TCP Reset** — Sends RST to both ends to terminate session.
> 4. **Quarantine Host** — Uses 802.1X or NAC to isolate the host on a remediation VLAN.
> 5. **Modify Packet** — Strips malicious payload (e.g., SQL keywords) and forwards sanitized packet.
> 6. **Alert / Log** — Forwards event to SIEM for correlation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers:** Perimeter NIPS defends east-west and north-south traffic.
- **Cloud Workloads:** Host-based IPS (HIPS) inside VMs (e.g., AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender).
- **OT/ICS Networks:** Protocol-aware IPS for Modbus, DNP3, and IEC 60870-5-104 in SCADA systems.
- **5G Core:** Service-Based IPS for SBI (Service-Based Interface) traffic between AMF, SMF, UPF.
- **SD-WAN Edges:** Inline IPS in branch appliances for distributed enterprise security.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Detection Latency of an IPS

In KTU numerical/analytical questions, students are often asked to compute the **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Prevent (MTTP)** for a given attack vector. We derive the formulas below.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let:
- $N$ = total number of packets in the attack session
- $p$ = probability that any single packet triggers a signature match
- $q = 1 - p$ = probability that a packet evades detection
- $T_{proc}$ = time to inspect one packet (seconds)
- $T_{resp}$ = time to execute response action (seconds)

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Expected Detection Packet Position

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{detect at packet } k) &= q^{k-1} \cdot p \\
\mathbb{E}[K] &= \sum_{k=1}^{N} k \cdot q^{k-1} \cdot p
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $p$ is constant and the sum is a truncated geometric series, we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[K] &= p \cdot \frac{1 - (N+1)q^{N} + N q^{N+1}}{(1-q)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a long attack session ($N \to \infty$), this simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[K] &= \frac{1}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the IPS detects the attack after $\frac{1}{p}$ packets. Higher signature coverage (larger $p$) yields faster detection.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Mean Time to Prevent (MTTP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{detect}} &= \mathbb{E}[K] \cdot T_{\text{proc}} \\
MTTP &= T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{resp}} \\
MTTP &= \frac{T_{\text{proc}}}{p} + T_{\text{resp}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** An IPS inspects packets at $T_{proc} = 0.2$ ms. A signature matches with probability $p = 0.25$. Response action takes $T_{resp} = 1.5$ ms. Find the Mean Time to Prevent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[K] &= \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ packets} \\
T_{\text{detect}} &= 4 \times 0.2 \text{ ms} = 0.8 \text{ ms} \\
MTTP &= 0.8 + 1.5 = 2.3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The IPS will block the attack on average within **2.3 milliseconds** of the first packet.

## 3.2 Derivation: ROC Curve and Optimal Threshold (Anomaly-Based IPS)

For anomaly-based detection, the decision threshold $\tau$ controls the trade-off between $TPR$ and $FPR$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR(\tau) &= P(X > \tau \mid \text{attack}) \\
FPR(\tau) &= P(X > \tau \mid \text{benign})
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming Gaussian distributions for both classes with means $\mu_0, \mu_1$ and equal variance $\sigma^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR(\tau) &= Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_1}{\sigma}\right) \\
FPR(\tau) &= Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}/2} \, du$ is the Q-function.

**Optimal Threshold (Youden's J Statistic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau^{*} &= \arg\max_{\tau} \left[ TPR(\tau) - FPR(\tau) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the operating point that maximizes the vertical distance from the ROC diagonal.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: IPS vs. IDS vs. Firewall

| Attribute | Firewall | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Function | Policy-based filtering | Threat detection | Threat detection + prevention |
| Layer of Operation | L3 / L4 (mostly) | L2 – L7 | L2 – L7 |
| Inline? | Yes | No (passive) | Yes (inline) |
| Evasion Resistance | Low (stateless) | High (offline analysis) | High (inline normalization) |
| Latency Added | Low | None | Moderate |
| Action on Threat | Drop by rule | Log + Alert | Drop / Reset / Quarantine |
| Best Use | Perimeter gate | Compliance / Forensics | Active defense |

## 3.4 Sample Throughput Calculation

**Problem:** An enterprise link carries $N_{pkt} = 1.2$ million packets/second with average packet size $S_{avg} = 750$ bytes. Calculate the line-rate the IPS must support.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= N_{pkt} \times S_{avg} \times 8 \text{ bits} \\
T &= 1{,}200{,}000 \times 750 \times 8 \\
T &= 7{,}200{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
T &= 7.2 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The IPS must sustain **at least 7.2 Gbps of line-rate inspection** to avoid becoming a bottleneck.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an IPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Traffic Input] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    B --> C[Protocol Normalizer]
    C --> D[Detection Engine]
    D --> D1[Signature Matcher]
    D --> D2[Anomaly Detector]
    D --> D3[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    D --> D4[Heuristic Engine]
    D1 --> E[Policy Decision Unit]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Action?}
    F -->|Drop| G[Silent Drop]
    F -->|Reset| H[TCP Reset Sender]
    F -->|Block| I[Shun List Updater]
    F -->|Quarantine| J[NAC / VLAN Isolator]
    F -->|Allow| K[Forward to Destination]
    G --> L[Logging and SIEM]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> M[End Host]
    L --> N[Admin Console]
```

## 4.2 Network Placement of IPS (Inline Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Internet[External Internet] --> FW[Perimeter Firewall]
    FW --> IPS1[IPS Engine 1]
    IPS1 --> Core[Core Router]
    Core --> SW[Distribution Switch]
    SW --> IPS2[Internal IPS]
    IPS2 --> VLAN10[Server VLAN 10]
    SW --> VLAN20[User VLAN 20]
    SW --> VLAN30[DMZ VLAN 30]
    IPS2 --> SIEM[(SIEM Database)]
    IPS1 --> SIEM
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The IPS is shown in **two strategic locations**:
1. **North-South Perimeter IPS** (IPS1) — Inspects all traffic entering the enterprise.
2. **East-West Internal IPS** (IPS2) — Inspects lateral movement within the data center to catch insider threats and compromised hosts.

## 4.3 IPS Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Packet Arrives]) --> Recv[Receive and Decode]
    Recv --> Norm[Normalize and Reassemble]
    Norm --> Sig{Signature Match?}
    Sig -->|Yes| Drop1[Drop Packet]
    Sig -->|No| Anom{Anomaly Score > Tau?}
    Anom -->|Yes| Susp[Flag as Suspicious]
    Anom -->|No| State{Stateful Check Pass?}
    Susp --> Deep[Deep Inspection]
    Deep --> Drop2[Drop / Reset]
    State -->|No| Drop3[Drop Packet]
    State -->|Yes| Fwd[Forward Packet]
    Drop1 --> Log[(Log to SIEM)]
    Drop2 --> Log
    Drop3 --> Log
    Fwd --> Out([Send to Destination])
```

## 4.4 Inline vs. Passive (TAP) Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLINE[Inline Bump-in-the-Wire]
        R1[Router] --> IPSX[IPS Engine] --> R2[Core Switch]
    end
    subgraph PASSIVE[Passive TAP Monitoring]
        R3[Router] --> T1[Network TAP]
        T1 --> R4[Core Switch]
        T1 -.mirror.-> IDSX[IDS Sensor]
    end
```

**Caption:** The **left** diagram shows an IPS that all traffic must pass through (active blocking). The **right** diagram shows an IDS receiving a mirrored copy via a TAP (passive observation only).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Intrusion Prevention System. How is it different from an Intrusion Detection System?**

**Model Answer:**
An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network traffic for malicious activity and **actively blocks** identified threats in real time. It is deployed **inline** within the data path.

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Passive | Active |
| Placement | Out-of-band | Inline |
| Action | Alerts only | Blocks / Resets / Quarantines |
| Failure Impact | Detection blind spot | Possible traffic disruption |

> **Note:** KTU expects at least 3 distinguishing points for full marks. Mentioning **inline placement** is mandatory.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List any three response actions performed by an IPS when it detects an attack.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Packet Drop** — Silently discards the malicious packet.
2. **TCP Reset Injection** — Sends a forged RST packet to terminate the session.
3. **IP Shunning / Blocking** — Adds the source IP to a temporary block list for a configurable duration (typically 300–3600 seconds).
4. *(Bonus)* **Host Quarantine** — Isolates the offending host via NAC.
5. *(Bonus)* **Alert Forwarding to SIEM** for centralized correlation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Network-based Intrusion Prevention System (NIPS) with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Compare and contrast signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis detection techniques used in IPS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of NIPS:**
A NIPS consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):
1. **Traffic Capture Module** — Receives packets via inline port or TAP.
2. **Packet Decoder** — Parses headers across OSI layers 2–7.
3. **Preprocessor / Normalizer** — Reassembles fragments, decodes HTTP URI, normalizes Unicode, and defeats evasion.
4. **Detection Engine** — Runs parallel detection modules (signature, anomaly, stateful, heuristic).
5. **Decision / Policy Engine** — Maps detection output to a verdict (benign / suspicious / malicious) using rules and thresholds.
6. **Response Engine** — Executes actions: drop, reset, shun, quarantine.
7. **Logging and Management Interface** — Forwards alerts to SIEM; receives policy updates from admin.

The NIPS operates as a **closed-loop control system** where detection results feed back into the rule base for continuous refinement.

**(b) Comparison of Detection Techniques:**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Basis | Known attack patterns | Deviation from baseline | RFC protocol compliance |
| Zero-Day Detection | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| False Positive Rate | Very Low | High | Moderate |
| Computational Cost | Low | Moderate–High | Moderate |
| Update Mechanism | Signature DB refresh | Periodic retraining | Protocol definition update |
| Example | Snort rules | Statistical Z-score | HTTP RFC 2616 parser |
| Best Suited For | Known malware, exploits | DDoS, worms, novel attacks | Protocol fuzzing, malformed traffic |

> **Valuation Tip:** Draw a comparison *table* in the exam — KTU examiners award 2 extra marks for structured tables versus running prose.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]
**(a)** An enterprise IPS processes packets at 0.15 ms per packet. A signature match probability is 0.20. The response action takes 2.0 ms. Calculate the Mean Time to Prevent (MTTP) the attack. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the various deployment modes of an IPS with their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Computation of MTTP:**

Step 1 — Identify the given values:
- $T_{proc} = 0.15$ ms
- $p = 0.20$
- $T_{resp} = 2.0$ ms

Step 2 — Apply the expected detection formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[K] &= \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5 \text{ packets}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute detection time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{detect}} &= \mathbb{E}[K] \times T_{\text{proc}} = 5 \times 0.15 = 0.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute MTTP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTP &= T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{resp}} = 0.75 + 2.0 = 2.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $MTTP = \mathbf{2.75 \text{ ms}}$

**(b) Deployment Modes of IPS:**

1. **Inline Bump-in-the-Wire (Most Common):**
   - *Advantage:* Full traffic visibility; immediate blocking capability.
   - *Limitation:* Single point of failure; must be sized for peak load.

2. **Tap + Span Mirror with Inline Tap:**
   - *Advantage:* Allows fail-open bypass during hardware failure.
   - *Limitation:* Bypass switches add cost and complexity.

3. **Clustered / High-Availability (Active-Passive or Active-Active):**
   - *Advantage:* Eliminates single point of failure; supports load sharing.
   - *Limitation:* Stateful synchronization overhead; double the hardware cost.

4. **Cloud-Native / Virtual IPS (e.g., Cisco NGFWv, Suricata on K8s):**
   - *Advantage:* Elastic scaling; integrates with cloud-native telemetry.
   - *Limitation:* East-west visibility limited in some virtualized topologies.

5. **Hybrid Edge IPS (in SD-WAN branch appliances):**
   - *Advantage:* Distributed enforcement close to the user.
   - *Limitation:* Centralized policy management required for consistency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS and IPS terminology** — Using "alerts" instead of "blocks" costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions — Always draw a labeled block diagram; even a hand-drawn version is mandatory.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention inline placement** in definitions — This is the single most important defining feature of an IPS.
> 4. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — In handwritten exams, the equivalent mistake is sloppy layout; examiners deduct marks for unaligned tables.
> 5. **In numericals, failing to convert units** — e.g., mixing ms and $\mu$s in MTTP calculations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for IPS (Module 2 — KTU PECST863):**
> - **Definition:** IPS = IDS + Inline Blocking Capability.
> - **Must-Mention Keywords:** Inline, Real-time, Active response, Fail-open, Deep packet inspection.
> - **Three Core Detection Methods:** Signature (known attacks), Anomaly (unknown attacks), Stateful (protocol compliance).
> - **Six Response Actions:** Drop, Reset, Shun, Quarantine, Modify, Alert.
> - **Two Key Architectures:** NIPS (perimeter / data center) and HIPS (endpoint agent).
> - **Key Performance Metrics:** $DR = TP/(TP+FN)$, $FPR = FP/(FP+TN)$, $F1$-score, MTTP, $Z$-score.
> - **Throughput Formula:** $T = N_{pkt} \times S_{avg} \times 8$ bps.
> - **Mathematical Foundations:** Geometric series for detection packet expectation; Q-function for anomaly thresholds; Youden's J for optimal ROC operating point.
> - **Real-World Tools:** Snort, Suricata, Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto NGFW, Fortinet FortiGate.
> - **Deployment Modes:** Inline, TAP, HA cluster, Virtual/cloud-native, SD-WAN edge.
> - **Common Confusions to Avoid:** IDS ≠ IPS; HIPS ≠ NIPS; Stateful ≠ Deep Packet Inspection alone; Signature ≠ Anomaly.
> - **Failure Mode Priority:** Most enterprise IPS deployments are configured *fail-open* to preserve availability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Response (IR)** is the set of pre-planned, systematic actions and countermeasures executed immediately after an intrusion is detected (or strongly suspected) to **contain**, **eradicate**, **recover from**, and **learn** from a security breach. It represents the *active reaction* phase of the broader security management lifecycle, transforming a detected security event into controlled defensive action.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, intrusion response is studied as a **post-detection discipline** that bridges the gap between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) raising an alert and the eventual restoration of normal, secure operations. It is governed by an *Incident Response Plan (IRP)* and is a core competency required for security analysts, SOC engineers, and incident handlers.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Standard)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Incident** | Any confirmed adverse event that violates security policy |
| **Alert** | A signal from an IDS/IPS indicating a possible incident |
| **False Positive** | Legitimate traffic wrongly flagged as malicious |
| **True Positive** | Correct identification of an actual intrusion |
| **False Negative** | A real intrusion missed by the detection system |
| **Triage** | Prioritization of alerts based on severity & impact |
| **Playbook** | Predefined, step-by-step response procedure for a known scenario |
| **SOC** | Security Operations Center – the team executing response |

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Intrusion Response as a Hospital's Emergency Department (ED) protocol after a patient arrives with a gunshot wound.**

The workflow maps beautifully:

1. **Triage Nurse (IDS/SIEM)** → Quickly classifies the severity of the incoming "patient" (alert). Is it a false alarm (someone with a paper cut) or a true critical case (gunshot wound)? This is *triage*.
2. **ER Doctors (Level-1 Responders)** → Perform **containment** — stop the bleeding, stabilize the patient. In cyber terms: isolate the affected host, block the attacker's IP, revoke compromised credentials.
3. **Surgeons (Forensics & IR Specialists)** → Perform **eradication** — remove the bullet, clean the wound, ensure no infection (malware) remains. In cyber terms: remove the backdoor, patch the vulnerability, wipe and re-image affected systems.
4. **Rehabilitation Team (Recovery)** → Help the patient recover to full health. In cyber terms: restore from clean backups, monitor for reinfection, return systems to production.
5. **Case Study Review (Post-Incident Activity)** → Medical board reviews the case to improve future protocols. In cyber terms: write a post-mortem, update the playbook, feed lessons back into detection rules.

Just as an ED cannot function with only diagnosis (triage) and no treatment, a security system without **Intrusion Response** is merely an observer. Response is what *converts information into action*.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold)

The following industry-standard metrics govern all quantitative response decisions in modern SOCs:

- **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)**: Average time from intrusion start to detection.
- **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond/Recover)**: Average time from detection to full recovery.
- **MTTC (Mean Time to Contain)**: Average time from detection to successful containment.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement)**: Contractual response time, e.g., critical incidents must be contained within **15 minutes**.
- **NIST Target SLA**: For federal-grade systems, critical incidents should be contained within **1 hour** of detection per NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2.

### Why Intrusion Response Matters (KTU CO Alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to IBM's 2024 Cost of a Data Breach Report, the global **average cost of a data breach is USD 4.88 million**, and breaches that took longer than **200 days to contain** cost on average **USD 5.46 million** versus **USD 3.86 million** for those contained under 200 days. The difference is essentially the *value of an effective intrusion response capability*. This is why CO2 of this module ("Apply appropriate response mechanisms for identified threats") carries high KTU exam weightage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Three Pillars of Intrusion Response (Theoretical Framework)

Intrusion response is structured around **three orthogonal response dimensions**. Every real-world response action can be classified along these axes:

### 2.1 Axis 1: Response Timing — Proactive vs Reactive vs Retrospective

1. **Proactive Response (Preemptive)**
   - Executed *before* an attack occurs.
   - Examples: Hardening systems, applying patches, deploying honeypots, blacklisting known malicious IPs, network segmentation.
   - **Goal**: Reduce the attack surface and increase the cost to the attacker.

2. **Reactive Response (Real-time / Active)**
   - Triggered *immediately* upon detection of an ongoing attack.
   - Examples: Killing a malicious process, blocking a TCP session via TCP RST, isolating a host via VLAN change, revoking a session token.
   - **Goal**: Stop the attack in progress and limit the blast radius.

3. **Retrospective Response (Post-Incident / Forensic)**
   - Executed *after* the attack is contained.
   - Examples: Forensic disk imaging, log correlation, root-cause analysis, legal evidence collection, lessons-learned reports.
   - **Goal**: Understand the attacker, prevent recurrence, support legal action.

### 2.2 Axis 2: Response Mode — Active vs Passive

- **Active Response (Intrusion Prevention Mode)**: The system *automatically* takes action that modifies the environment. Example: Snort in `iptables` mode drops malicious packets. **Trade-off**: Risk of self-inflicted DoS if detection is wrong.
- **Passive Response (Alert-Only Mode)**: The system only logs/alerts; a human operator decides. **Trade-off**: Slower but safer; no risk of automated false-positive damage.

### 2.3 Axis 3: Response Source — Manual vs Automated vs Hybrid

- **Manual Response**: Human SOC analyst triggers each action. Slow but context-aware.
- **Automated Response (SOAR-driven)**: Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response platforms execute playbooks. Fast, consistent, but rigid.
- **Hybrid (Human-on-the-Loop)**: System automates low-risk steps (e.g., enrichment, ticket creation) and pauses for human approval on high-risk steps (e.g., isolating a production server).

## The NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle (Core KTU Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** framework, which divides incident response into **four mandatory phases plus two supporting functions**:

| Phase | Objective | Key Activities |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Preparation** | Be ready before incidents occur | IR plan, team training, tools, communication channels |
| **2. Detection & Analysis** | Confirm whether an incident occurred | Alert triage, log review, IOC correlation, severity scoring |
| **3. Containment, Eradication & Recovery** | Stop the bleeding, clean up, restore | Short-term & long-term containment, malware removal, system restoration |
| **4. Post-Incident Activity** | Learn and improve | Lessons-learned report, evidence retention, playbook updates |

Supporting functions: **Communication** (throughout) and **Documentation** (throughout).

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are universally required for KTU Board Exam numerical/model answers. Memorize units, not just symbols.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Risk Score | $T$ = Threat likelihood, $V$ = Vulnerability, $I$ = Impact | dimensionless / qualitative |
| 2 | $EMV = \sum_{i} P_i \times C_i$ | Expected Monetary Value of a response option | $P_i$ = probability of outcome $i$, $C_i$ = cost of outcome $i$ | INR / USD |
| 3 | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} t_{rj}}{n}$ | Mean Time to Respond | $t_{rj}$ = response time of incident $j$, $n$ = number of incidents | minutes / hours |
| 4 | $MTTC = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} t_{cj}}{n}$ | Mean Time to Contain | $t_{cj}$ = containment time of incident $j$ | minutes / hours |
| 5 | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} t_{dj}}{n}$ | Mean Time to Detect | $t_{dj}$ = detection time of incident $j$ | minutes / hours |
| 6 | $SE = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Sensitivity (Recall) of detection | $TP$ = True Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives | dimensionless |
| 7 | $SP = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Specificity of detection | $TN$ = True Negatives, $FP$ = False Positives | dimensionless |
| 8 | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | $FP$ = False Positives, $TN$ = True Negatives | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{post} = \dfrac{SE \times P_{pre}}{SE \times P_{pre} + (1-SP) \times (1-P_{pre})}$ | Bayesian Posterior Probability (using Bayes' Theorem with sensitivity/specificity) | $P_{pre}$ = prior probability, $SE$ = sensitivity, $SP$ = specificity | probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| 10 | $C_{total} = C_{damage} + C_{response}$ | Total Cost of an Incident | $C_{damage}$ = business loss, $C_{response}$ = response effort cost | INR / USD |
| 11 | $C_{response} = C_{labor} \times h + C_{tools} + C_{downtime}$ | Cost of Response | $h$ = person-hours, $C_{labor}$ = hourly rate | INR / USD |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the Risk formula $R = T \times V \times I$, the three factors are usually **qualitative scores (1–5 or 1–10)**, not probabilities. Students who write probabilities lose marks. Always state the **scale** used.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Intrusion response is the **operational heart of every modern Security Operations Center (SOC)**. It is what differentiates a *defensive* security posture from a *reactive* one. Career roles that depend on this knowledge include:

- SOC Analyst (Tier 1, 2, 3)
- Incident Responder / CSIRT Engineer
- Threat Hunter
- Digital Forensic Investigator
- Security Automation Engineer (SOAR)
- Red Team / Blue Team operator

The same response taxonomy is used in **SCADA/ICS security** (power grids, water treatment), **cloud-native incident response** (AWS, Azure, GCP), and **OT security** in manufacturing plants — making it one of the most transferable skills in the cybersecurity domain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Bayesian Decision Rule for Automated Response

A core theoretical question in automated intrusion response is: *"Given a detection alert, should the system auto-contain the host (active response) or merely alert the analyst (passive response)?"* The answer uses **Bayes' theorem**.

**Step 1 — Define prior probability.** Let $P_{pre}$ be the prior probability that any given alert corresponds to a true intrusion. This is estimated from historical data: total true positives divided by total alerts over a window.

**Step 2 — Define detection performance metrics.** From the confusion matrix of the IDS:
- $SE$ = sensitivity (true positive rate)
- $SP$ = specificity (true negative rate)
- $FPR = 1 - SP$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.** The posterior probability that an alert is a true intrusion, *given that the alert fired*, is:

$$
P_{post} = \frac{SE \times P_{pre}}{SE \times P_{pre} + (1-SP) \times (1-P_{pre})}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the decision rule.** The system should auto-contain only if the **expected loss of NOT containing** exceeds the **expected cost of containing**.

Define:
- $L_{NC}$ = expected loss if a true positive is not contained (data exfiltration, ransomware spread)
- $C_{C}$ = cost of containment (downtime, false-positive damage)

$$
\text{Auto-contain} \iff P_{post} \times L_{NC} > C_{C}
$$

Rearranging for the threshold prior:

$$
P_{pre}^{*} = \frac{C_{C} \times (1 - SP)}{(L_{NC} - C_{C}) \times SE + C_{C} \times (1 - SP)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** If the running $P_{pre}$ in the SOC exceeds $P_{pre}^{*}$, automated containment is the EMV-minimizing decision. If not, alert-only is preferred. This is the mathematical basis of every modern SOAR playbook.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark question**. Memorize the threshold formula and be ready to compute $P_{post}$ for a numerical problem.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Risk & Response Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Question (KTU-style):** A SOC observes the following over a month: 10,000 alerts generated. Of these, 200 are true intrusions (confirmed). The IDS misclassified 50 of those true intrusions as benign and raised 100 false alarms. An analyst takes on average **45 minutes** per incident to contain. The SLA mandates containment in **30 minutes**.
>
> Compute: (a) Sensitivity, Specificity, FPR. (b) The posterior probability that the *next* alert is a true intrusion. (c) Whether the SLA is being met, and recommend a response action.

**Given Data:**
- Total alerts $= 10{,}000$
- True intrusions in dataset $= 200$
- $FN$ (missed intrusions) $= 50$
- $FP$ (false alarms) $= 100$
- MTTC (observed) $= 45$ minutes
- SLA target $= 30$ minutes

**Step 1 — Build the Confusion Matrix.**

- $TP = \text{Total true intrusions} - FN = 200 - 50 = 150$
- $FN = 50$
- $FP = 100$
- $TN = \text{Total alerts} - TP - FP - FN = 10{,}000 - 150 - 100 - 50 = 9{,}700$

**Step 2 — Compute Sensitivity $SE$.**

$$
SE = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 50} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75
$$

So $SE = 75\%$.

**Step 3 — Compute Specificity $SP$.**

$$
SP = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{9{,}700}{9{,}700 + 100} = \frac{9{,}700}{9{,}800} \approx 0.9898
$$

So $SP \approx 98.98\%$.

**Step 4 — Compute False Positive Rate $FPR$.**

$$
FPR = 1 - SP = 1 - 0.9898 = 0.0102
$$

So $FPR \approx 1.02\%$.

**Step 5 — Compute the prior $P_{pre}$.**

$$
P_{pre} = \frac{\text{True intrusions}}{\text{Total alerts}} = \frac{200}{10{,}000} = 0.02
$$

So $P_{pre} = 2\%$.

**Step 6 — Apply Bayes' theorem to get the posterior $P_{post}$.**

$$
P_{post} = \frac{SE \times P_{pre}}{SE \times P_{pre} + (1-SP) \times (1-P_{pre})}
$$

$$
P_{post} = \frac{0.75 \times 0.02}{(0.75 \times 0.02) + (0.0102 \times 0.98)}
$$

$$
P_{post} = \frac{0.0150}{0.0150 + 0.009996}
$$

$$
P_{post} = \frac{0.0150}{0.024996} \approx 0.6002
$$

So $P_{post} \approx 60\%$. This means **once an alert fires**, the chance it is a real intrusion is roughly **60%** — substantially higher than the base rate of 2%, validating that the IDS is informative.

**Step 7 — SLA Compliance Check.**

$$
MTTC_{observed} = 45 \text{ min} \;>\; SLA_{target} = 30 \text{ min}
$$

The SLA is **NOT met**. The SOC has a containment latency of 15 minutes over the SLA.

**Step 8 — Recommended Response Action.**

Because the SLA is breached and the posterior probability of a true incident is high (60%), the recommendation is:

1. **Adopt an automated SOAR playbook** to reduce MTTC by auto-isolating hosts and blocking C2 IPs (reducing analyst fatigue on tier-1 alerts).
2. **Tune the IDS to reduce $FPR$** (from 1.02% toward 0.5%) by adding suppression rules for known-good traffic.
3. **Introduce a tiered response:** Auto-contain alerts with $P_{post} > 0.7$, manual review for $0.3 \leq P_{post} \leq 0.7$, log-only for $P_{post} < 0.3$.

This **hybrid response** meets the SLA while keeping the false-positive cost low.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Response Type

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU 14-mark template** that examiners love:

| Intrusion Severity | Asset Criticality | False-Positive Cost | Recommended Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low | Low | Low | Passive: log + alert |
| Low | High | Low | Passive: alert + log + watchlist |
| High | Low | Low | Active: auto-block IP / kill session |
| High | High | Low | Active: auto-isolate host + revoke tokens |
| Low | Low | High | Passive: log only (avoid disruption) |
| Low | High | High | Passive: alert SOC; do not auto-respond |
| High | Low | High | Manual: page on-call analyst for decision |
| High | High | High | Manual: activate CSIRT; preserve evidence first |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule:** Active automated response is justified when the *cost of the false positive* is much lower than the *cost of a missed true positive*. In production-critical systems, this is rarely true, hence the dominance of **manual / hybrid** response in mature SOCs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Intrusion Response Lifecycle (NIST-Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram renders the complete NIST SP 800-61 incident response lifecycle with all four phases, supporting functions, and the feedback loop into detection engineering. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are clean per KTU diagram guidelines.

```mermaid
    A1 --- A1b[Tools SIEM EDR SOAR]

    A2 --- A2b[IOC Correlation]
```

## 4.2 Automated Response Decision Flow (Active vs Passive)

```mermaid

    B1[Alert Received from IDS or SIEM] --> B2[Enrichment: WHOIS, GeoIP, Threat Intel]

    B4 -- Yes --> B10[P_active: Auto Contain Host]
```

## 4.3 Three-Dimensional Response Taxonomy (Block View)

```mermaid

```

## 4.4 SOAR Architecture Block Diagram (Intrusion Response Engine)

```mermaid

    D1[SIEM and IDS Alert Feed] --> D2[SOAR Ingestion Layer]
    D2 --> D3[Enrichment Service: Threat Intel, GeoIP, Asset DB]

    D5 -- High Confidence --> D6[Active Response Module]
    D5 -- Medium Confidence --> D7[Human Approval Queue]
    D5 -- Low Confidence --> D8[Passive Logging Module]

    D6 --> D6a[Network ACL Update]

    D6 --> D6c[Identity Token Revocation]

    D9 -- No --> D8

    D8 --> D8a[SIEM Long Term Storage]
    D8 --> D8b[Daily Report Generation]
```
