<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Intrusion Response" with "Intrusion Prevention".** Prevention is *pre-attack*; response is *post-detection*. Mixing them loses 2-3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Always mention both Containment AND Eradication** in any IR question. Students often write only one and lose half the marks.
> 3. **Quantitative answers demand units.** Writing "$MTTR = 45$" without "minutes" is marked as incomplete.
> 4. **For Bayes-theorem questions**, show the formula substitution step explicitly. A bare final number with no work gets zero.
> 5. **Avoid giving legal evidence advice** (chain of custody, admissible in court) unless specifically asked; examiners deduct for out-of-syllabus content.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Intrusion Response. List the four phases of the NIST Incident Response Lifecycle.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Intrusion Response is the set of pre-planned, systematic actions taken after an intrusion is detected, in order to contain, eradicate, recover from, and learn from the security incident. It is the active reaction phase that bridges detection and restoration of normal operations.

**Four NIST Phases (1.5 marks):**
1. **Preparation** — establishing the IR plan, team, and tools before incidents.
2. **Detection and Analysis** — confirming whether an alert represents a real incident.
3. **Containment, Eradication and Recovery** — stopping the attack, removing the threat, and restoring systems.
4. **Post-Incident Activity** — lessons learned, evidence retention, and playbook updates.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Active Response and Passive Response. Give one example for each.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Response | Passive Response |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The system *automatically* modifies the environment to stop an attack. | The system *only* logs and alerts; a human decides the action. |
| **Speed** | Very fast (milliseconds to seconds). | Slower (minutes to hours). |
| **False-Positive Risk** | High — may damage legitimate traffic. | Negligible — no automated side effect. |
| **Example** | Snort in IPS mode drops malicious packets automatically. | Snort in IDS mode writes to a log and sends an email to the SOC. |

**(1 mark each for definition + example, 1 mark for clear differentiation.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Question (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Containment, Eradication and Recovery** phase of incident response. Distinguish clearly between **short-term containment** and **long-term containment** strategies with suitable examples.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a flowchart, describe the **automated intrusion response decision process** using a Bayesian approach. Derive the posterior probability formula used to decide between active and passive response.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**1. Containment (2.5 marks):** Containment is the act of *limiting the scope and impact* of an ongoing incident. It is the **first response action** after an intrusion is confirmed.

- **Short-term Containment (1.5 marks):** Immediate, often disruptive actions taken to *stop the bleeding* without yet addressing the root cause.
  - Examples: Disconnect the affected host from the network, block the attacker's IP at the perimeter firewall, disable the compromised user account, kill the malicious process via EDR, take the affected server offline.
  - Goal: Stop further damage **right now**; preserve volatile evidence first (memory dump, running processes, open connections).

- **Long-term Containment (1 mark):** Sustained measures that allow partial business continuity while the system is being cleaned.
  - Examples: Patching the vulnerable service while keeping it online, applying a virtual patch via WAF, segmenting the affected VLAN, restoring from a clean backup and running it on an isolated subnet.

**2. Eradication (2 marks):** Complete removal of the attacker's artefacts and root cause.
- Remove malware, rootkits, backdoors, and webshells.
- Close the exploited vulnerability (patch, configuration change, code fix).
- Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed.
- Rebuild compromised systems from known-good media (do not just delete the malware — re-image).

**3. Recovery (1.5 marks):** Bringing the cleaned systems back into production safely.
- Restore data from clean, verified backups.
- Monitor restored systems intensively (enhanced logging, network sensors) for signs of reinfection.
- Validate that all services are functioning normally.
- Declare the incident "closed" only after a defined monitoring period with no recurrence.

**4. Key trade-off discussed (1 mark):** *Evidence preservation vs business continuity.* Short-term containment may destroy volatile memory evidence; the IR team must capture forensic state **before** isolating the host.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**1. Flowchart Description (2.5 marks):**

The automated response process can be described in 6 steps:
1. **Alert ingestion** — SIEM/IDS forwards an alert to the SOAR engine.
2. **Enrichment** — The alert is enriched with threat intel, asset criticality, and user context.
3. **Bayesian scoring** — Compute the posterior probability $P_{post}$ that the alert is a true intrusion.
4. **Threshold comparison** — If $P_{post} \geq \theta$, trigger active response; else passive alert.
5. **Active response actions** — Block IP, isolate host, revoke tokens, snapshot for forensics.
6. **Passive response actions** — Log to long-term storage, create low-priority ticket, daily summary.

**2. Derivation of the Posterior Formula (3 marks):**

Let $A$ = "alert fired" and $I$ = "true intrusion". We want $P(I \mid A)$.

By Bayes' theorem:

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid I) \times P(I)}{P(A)}
$$

We know $P(A \mid I) = SE$ (sensitivity), $P(I) = P_{pre}$ (prior), and by the law of total probability:

$$
P(A) = P(A \mid I) \times P(I) + P(A \mid \neg I) \times P(\neg I) = SE \times P_{pre} + (1-SP) \times (1-P_{pre})
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{post} = \frac{SE \times P_{pre}}{SE \times P_{pre} + (1-SP) \times (1-P_{pre})}
$$

**3. Decision Rule (1.5 marks):** The system should trigger active automated containment iff:

$$
P_{post} \times L_{NC} > C_C
$$

where $L_{NC}$ is the expected loss from *not* containing, and $C_C$ is the cost of containment. This is the EMV-minimizing rule for response selection.

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three dimensions of the intrusion response taxonomy** (timing, mode, source). For each dimension, list the two or three categories and give a real-world example.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SOC observed 8,000 alerts in a month. Of these, 120 were true intrusions. The IDS missed 30 of those intrusions and raised 80 false alarms. The average response time was 50 minutes against an SLA of 30 minutes. Compute the Sensitivity, Specificity, FPR, and the Bayesian posterior probability. Recommend one improvement.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Dimension 1: Timing (2.5 marks)**

| Category | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Proactive** | Action *before* an attack. | Applying the MS17-010 patch a week after disclosure to prevent EternalBlue. |
| **Reactive** | Action *during* an attack. | EDR kills the ransomware process the moment encryption starts. |
| **Retrospective** | Action *after* containment. | Forensic analysis identifies the attacker's C2 server, and an IOC is added to the SIEM. |

**Dimension 2: Mode (2 marks)**

| Category | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Active** | System automatically modifies the environment. | Snort-inline (NFQ mode) drops a malicious HTTP request at the gateway. |
| **Passive** | System only logs/alerts; no automatic change. | Splunk indexes the alert and emails the SOC for review. |

**Dimension 3: Source (2.5 marks)**

| Category | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual** | Human analyst drives the entire response. | A Tier-2 analyst manually revokes a session token via Okta admin console. |
| **Automated** | SOAR playbook executes end-to-end. | Cortex XSOAR auto-isolates an endpoint and opens a Jira ticket. |
| **Hybrid** | System automates low-risk steps, human approves high-risk steps. | SOAR auto-blocks an external IP but pauses for human approval before isolating an internal host. |

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Total alerts $= 8{,}000$
- True intrusions $= 120$
- $FN = 30$, $FP = 80$
- MTTC $= 50$ min, SLA $= 30$ min

**Step 1 — Confusion Matrix (1 mark).**
- $TP = 120 - 30 = 90$
- $FN = 30$
- $FP = 80$
- $TN = 8{,}000 - 90 - 80 - 30 = 7{,}800$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity (1 mark).**

$$
SE = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 30} = \frac{90}{120} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3 — Specificity (1 mark).**

$$
SP = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{7{,}800}{7{,}800 + 80} = \frac{7{,}800}{7{,}880} \approx 0.9898
$$

**Step 4 — FPR (1 mark).**

$$
FPR = 1 - SP = 1 - 0.9898 = 0.0102 \approx 1.02\%
$$

**Step 5 — Prior Probability (0.5 mark).**

$$
P_{pre} = \frac{120}{8{,}000} = 0.015
$$

**Step 6 — Posterior Probability using Bayes (1.5 marks).**

$$
P_{post} = \frac{SE \times P_{pre}}{SE \times P_{pre} + (1-SP) \times (1-P_{pre})}
$$

$$
P_{post} = \frac{0.75 \times 0.015}{(0.75 \times 0.015) + (0.0102 \times 0.985)}
$$

$$
P_{post} = \frac{0.01125}{0.01125 + 0.010047} = \frac{0.01125}{0.021297} \approx 0.528
$$

So $P_{post} \approx 52.8\%$.

**Step 7 — Improvement Recommendation (1 mark).**
Since MTTC (50 min) exceeds the SLA (30 min) by 20 minutes, the SOC should **deploy an automated SOAR playbook** for tier-1 alert containment to reduce analyst handling time. Additionally, the relatively high $FPR$ (1.02%) increases analyst fatigue, so **tuning the IDS rules** (e.g., suppressing scan traffic from internal vulnerability scanners) is recommended to lower $FP$ and free analyst bandwidth for genuine high-severity incidents.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Intrusion Response):**

- **Definition:** Intrusion Response = post-detection action; it is *not* prevention.
- **NIST 4-phase lifecycle:** Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity.
- **Two supporting functions (throughout):** Communication + Documentation.
- **Three response timing categories:** Proactive, Reactive, Retrospective.
- **Two response modes:** Active (modifies environment) vs Passive (alerts only).
- **Three response sources:** Manual, Automated, Hybrid (most common in mature SOCs).
- **Containment split:** Short-term (immediate, disruptive) vs Long-term (sustained, business-continuity friendly).
- **Trade-off to always mention:** Evidence preservation (volatile memory) vs business continuity.
- **Key metrics to memorize with units:** MTTD, MTTR, MTTC, MTTA (mean time to acknowledge), Sensitivity, Specificity, FPR.
- **Bayes' formula (must memorize):** $P_{post} = \dfrac{SE \cdot P_{pre}}{SE \cdot P_{pre} + (1-SP) \cdot (1-P_{pre})}$.
- **EMV decision rule:** Auto-contain if $P_{post} \cdot L_{NC} > C_C$.
- **Risk formula:** $R = T \times V \times I$ (qualitative scores, not probabilities).
- **Confusion matrix identities (memorize):** $SE = TP/(TP+FN)$, $SP = TN/(TN+FP)$, $FPR = 1 - SP$.
- **NIST target SLA:** Critical incidents contained within 1 hour of detection.
- **Industry benchmark:** Mean data breach cost ≈ USD 4.88 M (IBM 2024); breaches contained <200 days cost ~USD 1.6 M less.
- **Common exam pitfall:** Do not confuse Intrusion Detection, Intrusion Prevention, and Intrusion Response — three distinct concepts.
- **Modern trend:** SOAR-driven hybrid response (Human-on-the-Loop) is the industry default.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers and **always state the scale** in qualitative risk formulas.
- **One-line KTU one-liner for the answer sheet:** *"Detection without response is observation; response is what turns information into security."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goals for Intrusion Detection Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Goals for Intrusion Detection Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism — hardware or software-based — that monitors network traffic and/or host activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts. The **Goals for Intrusion Detection Systems** define the operational, functional, and performance-oriented objectives that an IDS is engineered to satisfy in order to provide reliable, real-time, and accurate threat identification within a computer system or network.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with *Stallings' Cryptography and Network Security* and *Northcutt's IDS principles*), the primary goals of an IDS can be classified into **seven canonical objectives**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — The Seven Canonical IDS Goals**
> 1. **Functionality** — correctly detecting intrusions
> 2. **Performance** — operating at line speed without bottlenecking traffic
> 3. **Transparency** — functioning without affecting legitimate user activity
> 4. **Reliability** — fault-tolerant operation with no single point of failure
> 5. **Scalability** — adapting to growing networks and traffic volumes
> 6. **Adaptability** — handling new (zero-day) attacks via updates/learning
> 7. **Maintainability** — easy to configure, update, and patch

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **Intrusion Detection System** like the **CCTV surveillance and security guard combination at a large shopping mall**:

- **Functionality** → The cameras must actually capture clear footage of shoplifters. (An IDS must detect real attacks.)
- **Performance** → The cameras must record in *real-time* without lagging, even during festival sales. (An IDS must process packets at wire speed.)
- **Transparency** → Shoppers should not feel watched or hindered. (An IDS must not slow down normal browsing.)
- **Reliability** → If one camera fails, another must take over. (An IDS must have failover mechanisms.)
- **Scalability** → When the mall expands a new wing, more cameras must be added seamlessly. (An IDS must scale with the network.)
- **Adaptability** → Guards learn new tricks thieves use (e.g., wearing disguises). (An IDS signature/anomaly engine must update.)
- **Maintainability** → A technician should easily swap or upgrade a faulty camera. (An IDS must support modular updates.)

## 1.3 Key IDS Performance Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

The quantitative evaluation of IDS goals rests on four fundamental metrics:

- **True Positive (TP)** = Intrusion correctly flagged as intrusion
- **False Positive (FP)** = Legitimate activity wrongly flagged as intrusion (Type I Error)
- **True Negative (TN)** = Legitimate activity correctly identified as benign
- **False Negative (FN)** = Intrusion wrongly classified as benign (Type II Error — *the most dangerous error*)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FN is "Fatal"** — A False Negative means an actual attack slipped past the IDS undetected. While False Positives cause analyst fatigue, False Negatives cause actual breaches. The primary engineering goal is to **minimize False Negatives** while keeping False Positives operationally manageable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Seven Goals

### 2.1.1 Functionality
The IDS must **correctly classify** all observed events into one of two categories: *normal* or *intrusive*. This is the foundational goal — without it, all other goals are meaningless. Functionality is achieved through:
- **Signature-based detection** (misuse detection): pattern matching against known attack signatures.
- **Anomaly-based detection**: statistical deviation from established baseline behavior.
- **Stateful protocol analysis**: tracking protocol state transitions.

### 2.1.2 Performance
An IDS must operate at the **full data rate** of the network segment it monitors. Performance metrics include *throughput* (packets/second), *latency* (time-to-alert), and *packet drop rate* under load.

### 2.1.3 Transparency
The IDS must be **invisible to legitimate users**. A poorly designed inline IDS (like an IPS in blocking mode) that introduces latency is non-transparent. Transparency requires *passive monitoring* (taps/SPAN ports) or *transparent in-line bridging*.

### 2.1.4 Reliability
An IDS must remain operational under:
- **Hardware failure** (redundant NICs, hot-standby sensors)
- **Software crash** (watchdog timers, automatic restart)
- **Attack against itself** (anti-evasion, log integrity protection, secure communication channel)

### 2.1.5 Scalability
As network bandwidth grows (from **100 Mbps → 10 Gbps → 100 Gbps**), the IDS must scale via:
- **Hierarchical/distributed sensor deployment**
- **Load balancing** across multiple analysis engines
- **Sampling and flow-based aggregation** (e.g., NetFlow, sFlow)

### 2.1.6 Adaptability
Modern threats evolve rapidly (zero-day exploits, polymorphic malware). The IDS must:
- Receive **frequent signature updates** (e.g., Snort VRT rules)
- Employ **machine learning** for unknown anomaly detection
- Support **threat intelligence feeds** (STIX/TAXII)

### 2.1.7 Maintainability
Operations teams must easily:
- **Update signature databases**
- **Tune detection thresholds**
- **Generate reports** for compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA)
- **Integrate** with SIEM, SOAR, and ticketing systems

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — IDS Performance Evaluation

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (DR) / Sensitivity / Recall | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of actual intrusions correctly caught | $DR \to 1$ (100%) |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fraction of benign events wrongly alarmed | $FPR \to 0$ (0%) |
| False Negative Rate (FNR) | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN} = 1 - DR$ | Fraction of intrusions missed | $FNR \to 0$ (0%) |
| Precision | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts, how many were true attacks | $Precision \to 1$ |
| Accuracy | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | $Accuracy \to 1$ |
| F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Detection Rate | $F1 \to 1$ |
| ROC-AUC | Area under TPR vs FPR curve | Classifier discrimination ability | $AUC \to 1$ |
| Base Rate Fallacy | $P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \dfrac{DR \cdot P(A)}{DR \cdot P(A) + FPR \cdot (1 - P(A))}$ | Probability that an alert is a true attack | Should be high |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip** — Always express DR and FPR as decimals between 0 and 1 unless explicitly asked for percentages. The Base Rate Fallacy formula is frequently tested because it shows why high FPR is catastrophic in low-attack-traffic environments.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These IDS goals are not academic — they directly determine whether an organization can survive a real breach:

- **Banking sector**: A False Negative means a fraudulent transaction completes → direct monetary loss.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA)**: A False Positive means a legitimate doctor's access is blocked → life-threatening delay.
- **E-commerce**: A non-scalable IDS becomes the bottleneck during Black Friday sales → revenue loss.
- **Defense/Military**: A non-reliable IDS that crashes under DDoS leaves the network blind during an active attack.

Thus, the seven goals must be **jointly optimized** — improving one (e.g., lowering FPR) may worsen another (e.g., increasing FNR).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Base Rate Fallacy for IDS

Let us derive the fundamental Bayesian formula that explains why low base-rate environments (most production networks) make False Positives especially damaging.

**Step 1 — Define the Events**
- Let $A$ = an actual intrusion occurring
- Let $B$ = the IDS raises an alert

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Marginal Probability $P(B)$**

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \lnot A) \cdot P(\lnot A)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Components in IDS Context**
- $P(B \mid A) = DR$ (Detection Rate — probability of alert given a real attack)
- $P(B \mid \lnot A) = FPR$ (False Positive Rate — probability of alert given benign traffic)
- $P(A) = p$ (base rate of intrusions in total traffic)
- $P(\lnot A) = 1 - p$

**Step 5 — Substitute**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{DR \cdot p}{DR \cdot p + FPR \cdot (1 - p)}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret**
Even with a very good IDS (say $DR = 0.99$, $FPR = 0.01$), in a network where only $p = 0.0001$ (1 attack per 10,000 events):

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{0.99 \cdot 0.0001}{0.99 \cdot 0.0001 + 0.01 \cdot 0.9999}$$

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{0.000099}{0.000099 + 0.009999} = \frac{0.000099}{0.010098} \approx 0.0098$$

**Conclusion** — Only **~0.98%** of alerts represent true attacks. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**, and it explains why IDS operators suffer from *alert fatigue*.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs Between IDS Goals

| Goal Improved | Consequence on Other Goals | Trade-off Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Increase DR (catch more attacks) | FPR typically rises → alert fatigue | High negative impact on Transparency & Maintainability |
| Decrease FPR (fewer false alarms) | DR may drop → more False Negatives | Critical security risk |
| Increase Scalability via sampling | Anomaly detection accuracy degrades | Medium impact on Functionality |
| Increase Reliability via redundancy | Cost & complexity rise | Operational trade-off |
| Increase Adaptability (more ML) | Explainability drops, false alarms rise | Medium-high impact |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** An IDS monitored 10,000 events. Of these, 100 were actual intrusions. The IDS raised 150 alerts, of which 80 corresponded to real intrusions. Compute DR, FPR, FNR, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score.

**Step 1 — Build the Confusion Matrix**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Events} &= TP + TN + FP + FN = 10000 \\
\text{Actual Intrusions} &= TP + FN = 100 \\
\text{Alerts Raised} &= TP + FP = 150 \\
\text{True Alerts (Correct)} &= TP = 80
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Each Cell**

$$\begin{aligned}
FN &= 100 - 80 = 20 \\
FP &= 150 - 80 = 70 \\
TN &= 10000 - 80 - 70 - 20 = 9830
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Detection Rate**

$$DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 4 — Compute False Positive Rate**

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{70}{70 + 9830} = \frac{70}{9900} \approx 0.00707$$

**Step 5 — Compute False Negative Rate**

$$FNR = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20$$

**Step 6 — Compute Precision**

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 70} = \frac{80}{150} \approx 0.5333$$

**Step 7 — Compute Accuracy**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{10000} = \frac{80 + 9830}{10000} = \frac{9910}{10000} = 0.9910$$

**Step 8 — Compute F1-Score**

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5333 \cdot 0.80}{0.5333 + 0.80}$$

$$F1 = \frac{0.8533}{1.3333} \approx 0.6400$$

**Summary Table**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| TP | **80** |
| FP | **70** |
| FN | **20** |
| TN | **9830** |
| DR | **0.80** |
| FPR | **0.00707** |
| FNR | **0.20** |
| Precision | **0.5333** |
| Accuracy | **0.9910** |
| F1-Score | **0.6400** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IDS Goals Hierarchy

The following block-level functional diagram maps the seven IDS goals and their interdependencies.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode[Intrusion Detection System] --> goal1Node[Functionality]
    rootNode --> goal2Node[Performance]
    rootNode --> goal3Node[Transparency]
    rootNode --> goal4Node[Reliability]
    rootNode --> goal5Node[Scalability]
    rootNode --> goal6Node[Adaptability]
    rootNode --> goal7Node[Maintainability]

    goal1Node --> sub1ANode[Signature Detection]
    goal1Node --> sub1BNode[Anomaly Detection]
    goal1Node --> sub1CNode[Stateful Protocol Analysis]

    goal2Node --> sub2ANode[Throughput Optimization]
    goal2Node --> sub2BNode[Low Latency Alerting]
    goal2Node --> sub2CNode[Packet Loss Minimization]

    goal3Node --> sub3ANode[Passive Tapping]
    goal3Node --> sub3BNode[Transparent Bridging]

    goal4Node --> sub4ANode[Hardware Redundancy]
    goal4Node --> sub4BNode[Watchdog Recovery]
    goal4Node --> sub4CNode[Anti Evasion Hardening]

    goal5Node --> sub5ANode[Distributed Sensors]
    goal5Node --> sub5BNode[Load Balancing]
    goal5Node --> sub5CNode[Flow Aggregation]

    goal6Node --> sub6ANode[Signature Update Feeds]
    goal6Node --> sub6BNode[Machine Learning Models]
    goal6Node --> sub6CNode[Threat Intelligence Integration]

    goal7Node --> sub7ANode[Modular Updates]
    goal7Node --> sub7BNode[Threshold Tuning]
    goal7Node --> sub7CNode[SIEM and SOAR Integration]

    classDef goalStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef subStyle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#0f172a;
```
