## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Define Supervised Learning for Misuse/Signature Detection. How does it differ from Anomaly-based Detection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Supervised Learning for Misuse/Signature Detection is a machine-learning approach to Intrusion Detection in which a classifier is trained on a **labeled dataset** containing known attack signatures and normal traffic. At inference, the trained model classifies new events as belonging to one of the known signatures or to normal.

**Difference from Anomaly Detection:**

| Aspect | Misuse (Signature) | Anomaly |
|---|---|---|
| Training data | Labeled (normal + attack) | Often unlabeled (only normal) |
| Detects | Known attacks only | Novel / zero-day attacks |
| False alarms | Low | High |
| Approach | Pattern matching via classifier | Deviation from learned "normal" model |

*(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for difference, 0.5 mark for concluding statement.)*

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`
**List and briefly explain any three evaluation metrics used for a supervised misuse detector.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Precision (1 mark):** $TP/(TP+FP)$ — fraction of flagged attacks that are real. High precision = few false alarms.
2. **Recall / Detection Rate (1 mark):** $TP/(TP+FN)$ — fraction of real attacks that are caught. High recall = few missed attacks.
3. **F1-Score (1 mark):** $2 \cdot \text{Pre} \cdot \text{Rec} / (\text{Pre} + \text{Rec})$ — harmonic mean that balances precision and recall, especially useful on imbalanced datasets.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Many students write a generic answer on "machine learning" and forget to anchor it to **intrusion detection / network security context**. Always tie every concept back to IDS — *features = network traffic attributes*, *labels = attack types*, *output = alert or pass*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** When asked to "compare" algorithms, students list pros and cons without a **tabular comparison**. KTU examiners award 2-3 extra marks for a clean comparative table.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end pipeline of supervised learning for misuse/signature detection**, clearly stating the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a labeled test set with $TP=80$, $FP=20$, $FN=10$, $TN=890$. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, and False Alarm Rate. **Interpret the results** from a security analyst's perspective. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

1. **Data Acquisition (1 mark):** Collect network traces from sources like NSL-KDD, CICIDS 2017, or a corporate honeypot. A human expert (or vendor feed like CVE + Snort) labels each instance as `normal` or one of the attack classes.

2. **Feature Engineering (1 mark):** Convert raw pcap data into numeric feature vectors. For NSL-KDD, this yields 41 features per connection: `duration, protocol_type, service, flag, src_bytes, dst_bytes, …`.

3. **Preprocessing (1 mark):** Handle missing values, encode categorical features (one-hot or label encoding), normalize numerical features (z-score or min-max) to prevent scale dominance.

4. **Model Training (1.5 marks):** Feed $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ pairs into a classifier (e.g., Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM). The algorithm minimizes a loss function — typically cross-entropy — by adjusting its parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ via gradient-based optimization.

5. **Validation & Testing (1.5 marks):** Use $k$-fold cross-validation (commonly $k=10$) on the training set to tune hyperparameters and detect overfitting. The held-out test set is then used to obtain unbiased performance estimates.

6. **Deployment & Inference (1 mark):** The trained model is exported and integrated into a live IDS pipeline. For each new event, the model outputs a class label (and a confidence score) which the IDS engine uses to raise an alert or pass the traffic.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1, Figure 4.1 should be redrawn on paper for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $TP=80$, $FP=20$, $FN=10$, $TN=890$, Total $= 1000$.

**Step 1 — Accuracy (1 mark):**
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{80 + 890}{1000} = \frac{970}{1000} = 0.97 = 97\%$$

**Step 2 — Precision (1 mark):**
$$\text{Pre} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Step 3 — Recall (1 mark):**
$$\text{Rec} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} \approx 0.889 = 88.9\%$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (1 mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.889}{0.80 + 0.889} = \frac{1.4222}{1.689} \approx 0.842 = 84.2\%$$

**Step 5 — False Alarm Rate (1 mark):**
$$\text{FAR} = \frac{20}{20 + 890} = \frac{20}{910} \approx 0.022 = 2.2\%$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- The IDS catches **88.9% of all attacks** (good recall) — a strong security posture.
- Out of every 100 alerts raised, **80 are real attacks** (good precision) — manageable for SOC analysts.
- The **2.2% false alarm rate** is low, minimizing alert fatigue.
- The **F1 of 84.2%** indicates a balanced classifier.
- **Verdict:** This is a deployment-ready misuse detector; however, the 11.1% miss rate ($FN=10$) means some attacks slip through, motivating the need for an additional anomaly-detection layer to catch zero-day threats.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes classifier** with its underlying mathematical formulation. Show how the **naive independence assumption** is applied and why this classifier is popular for signature-based intrusion detection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Decision Tree, SVM, and Random Forest** for misuse detection in terms of: (i) handling of non-linear boundaries, (ii) interpretability, (iii) training time, (iv) robustness to noise. Use a tabular comparison and justify which one you would deploy in a high-throughput SOC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Bayes' Theorem Foundation (1.5 marks):**

$$P(y = c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y = c) \cdot P(y = c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

- $P(y = c)$ = **prior** probability of class $c$ in the training set.
- $P(\mathbf{x} \mid y = c)$ = **likelihood** of observing features $\mathbf{x}$ given class $c$.
- $P(y = c \mid \mathbf{x})$ = **posterior** probability we seek at inference time.

**2. The Naive Independence Assumption (2 marks):**
Computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid y = c) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d \mid y = c)$ directly requires estimating a $d$-dimensional joint distribution — infeasible. The Naive Bayes assumption simplifies this by stating that **all features are conditionally independent given the class**:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid y = c) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = c)$$

This reduces the estimation to $d \times C$ one-dimensional distributions (continuous → Gaussian PDF; categorical → frequency count).

**3. Decision Rule (1.5 marks):**
Dropping the constant denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$, the predicted class is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{Y}} \; P(y = c) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = c)$$

**4. Why Suitable for Signature Detection (2 marks):**
- **Speed:** Both training and inference are O($d \cdot N$) — ideal for real-time IDS.
- **Small-sample efficiency:** Works well even with limited labeled data per attack class.
- **Probabilistic output:** Confidence scores can be thresholded for alert prioritization.
- **Incremental learning:** Priors and likelihoods can be updated as new labeled attacks are observed.
- **Limitation:** The independence assumption is often violated in network traffic (e.g., `src_bytes` and `dst_bytes` are correlated). This is why Random Forest and SVM are often preferred in practice.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Decision Tree (C4.5) | SVM (RBF kernel) | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Non-linear boundaries | Yes — via recursive axis-aligned splits | Yes — via kernel trick $\ K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = e^{-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2}$ | Yes — ensemble of deep trees |
| (ii) Interpretability | **High** — produces human-readable IF-THEN rules that mirror Snort signatures | Low — black-box hyperplane in kernel-induced space | Moderate — feature importance available, but not single rules |
| (iii) Training time | **Fast** — $O(N \log N)$ typical | Slow — $O(N^2)$ to $O(N^3)$ for quadratic programming | Moderate — $T$ trees trained in parallel |
| (iv) Robustness to noise | Moderate — prone to overfitting noisy data | **High** — max-margin principle tolerates noise via slack variables | **Very High** — bagging reduces variance |

**Deployment Justification for a High-Throughput SOC (3 marks):**

For a high-throughput Security Operations Center processing millions of flows per second, the **recommended choice is Random Forest**, justified as follows:

- **Throughput:** At inference, a Random Forest makes a single pass through $T$ trees (each evaluating a few feature splits). Predictions can be batched and parallelized on multi-core CPUs, easily sustaining 100k+ flows/sec.
- **Accuracy:** Its ensemble nature (bagging + random feature subsets) yields the highest F1-scores on imbalanced IDS datasets like NSL-KDD and CICIDS 2017, typically 99%+.
- **Robustness:** Bagging makes it inherently resilient to label noise and concept drift — critical when attack patterns evolve daily.
- **Interpretability trade-off:** While a single Decision Tree is more interpretable, modern SHAP/LIME post-hoc explanations make Random Forests acceptably explainable for compliance audits.
- **SVM is rejected** for SOC deployment because its $O(N^2)$-order training and large memory footprint do not scale to millions of training samples; inference is also slower than Random Forest at high throughput.
- **Decision Tree is rejected** as the primary model because its single-tree predictions are highly unstable (high variance) and overfit noisy traffic data.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Supervised Learning for Misuse/Signature Detection):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Supervised learning for misuse detection = training a classifier on **labeled** network traffic to recognize **known attack signatures**.
- ✅ **Key prerequisite:** A **labeled dataset** is essential — distinguish from anomaly detection, which often uses unlabeled data.
- ✅ **Pipeline:** Acquisition → Feature Engineering → Preprocessing → Training → Validation → Deployment.
- ✅ **Feature vector:** Each network connection is represented by a numeric vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g., 41 features in NSL-KDD).
- ✅ **Algorithms covered:** Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, k-NN, SVM, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, MLP.
- ✅ **Loss function:** Cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ is the standard for classification.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) P(y) / P(\mathbf{x})$ — foundation of Naive Bayes.
- ✅ **Naive assumption:** Features are conditionally independent given the class — the source of both speed and limitations.
- ✅ **Confusion matrix:** 4 cells — TP, FP, FN, TN. Know each by definition.
- ✅ **Memorize formulas:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, FAR, and ROC-AUC. **F1 = $2PR/(P+R)$** is a KTU favorite.
- ✅ **Interpret metrics:** High recall = few missed attacks; high precision = few false alarms; F1 balances both.
- ✅ **Class imbalance:** IDS datasets are highly imbalanced; accuracy alone is misleading. Always report F1 and AUC.
- ✅ **Overfitting vs. Underfitting:** Use $k$-fold cross-validation and regularization ($\lambda \Vert \boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^2$) to control it.
- ✅ **Deployment insight:** Random Forest is the most popular choice in modern SOCs for its accuracy–throughput trade-off.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot detect **zero-day attacks** (no signature in training set) — pair with anomaly detection.
- ✅ **Standard datasets (cite by name in answers):** NSL-KDD, KDD Cup 99, CICIDS 2017, UNSW-NB15.
- ✅ **Examiner's final tip:** Always include a **diagram** (pipeline / confusion matrix / decision boundary) in 14-mark answers for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner KTU answer template (for Part A definitions):**
> *"Supervised Learning for Misuse/Signature Detection is a knowledge-based IDS technique that trains a classifier on labeled instances of known attack signatures to recognize and flag matching traffic at inference time, achieving high detection rates on known threats but unable to detect novel zero-day attacks."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anomaly Detection Using Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anomaly Detection Using Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anomaly Detection (Outlier Detection)** is the identification of rare items, events, or observations which deviate significantly from the majority of the data and do not conform to a well-defined notion of normal behavior. In the context of **Machine Learning (ML)**, it refers to the set of computational techniques that build models of *normality* from historical data and flag observations whose likelihood under the learned distribution falls below a configured threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST863 / Module 3):**
> *Anomaly detection using machine learning is a data-driven security paradigm that leverages statistical learning, neural networks, and clustering algorithms to identify patterns in network traffic, system calls, or user behavior that deviate from an established baseline, signaling potential intrusions, fraud, or malware.*

### 1.2 Types of Anomalies (Rigorous Classification)

| Anomaly Type | Formal Description | Real-World Security Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Point (Global) Anomaly** | A single data instance $x_i$ that is anomalous w.r.t. the rest of the dataset $D$ | A single login attempt from an unknown geo-location at 3 AM |
| **Contextual (Conditional) Anomaly** | An instance anomalous in a *specific context* (e.g., time, location) but normal otherwise | A $1{,}000$ login attempts in 5 minutes *only* at midnight |
| **Collective Anomaly** | A collection of related instances anomalous *as a group*, even if individually normal | A sequence of 6 low-port scans from the same source IP (each scan is normal) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bouncer at a Nightclub" Analogy:**
> Imagine a bouncer (the ML model) at a regular nightclub. For months, he has learned (training phase) that customers walk in between 8 PM and 1 AM, wear casual clothes, and enter in small groups of 1–4. One night, at 4 AM, three individuals wearing ski masks and carrying large bags try to enter. Each individual signal (4 AM entry, masks, group of 3) might be mildly unusual, but **collectively** they form an *anomaly score* that exceeds the bouncer's threshold. He denies entry — this is exactly how an ML-based anomaly detector flags an intrusion or attack pattern, without ever having been explicitly taught what an "attack" looks like.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in the Field

The following performance metrics are **mandatory** for evaluating any anomaly detection system in KTU examinations:

- **True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity):** $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate:** $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Precision:** $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **F1-Score:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
- **Area Under ROC Curve (AUC):** scalar value in $[0, 1]$, with **0.5 = random**, **1.0 = perfect**
- **Anomaly Score Threshold ($\tau$):** decision boundary; $x$ is anomalous if $s(x) \geq \tau$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In cybersecurity datasets (e.g., NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017), the class distribution is highly **imbalanced** (attacks $\approx 1$–$5\%$ of total traffic). Therefore, **accuracy is a misleading metric** and KTU examiners specifically look for F1-Score, AUC-ROC, and the confusion matrix as the standard evaluation triad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Machine Learning Paradigms for Anomaly Detection

Anomaly detection systems are classified strictly by the **labeling regime** of the training data:

1. **Supervised Anomaly Detection**
   - Requires fully labeled training set: $\{ (x_i, y_i) \}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (normal / attack).
   - Treated as a binary classification problem (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost, Deep Neural Networks).
   - **Limitation:** Attack labels are scarce; zero-day attacks are by definition unseen.

2. **Semi-Supervised (One-Class) Anomaly Detection**
   - Training data contains **only normal samples**; the model learns the boundary of normality.
   - At inference, samples falling outside the boundary are flagged.
   - Algorithms: One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest (when trained on $\mathcal{N}$), Autoencoders.

3. **Unsupervised Anomaly Detection**
   - No labels used. The algorithm infers anomalies from intrinsic data structure.
   - Algorithms: K-Means, DBSCAN, Local Outlier Factor (LOF), Isolation Forest (default mode).

### 2.2 Operational Concept Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" and "How" behind every anomaly detection pipeline:**

- **Why use ML?** Signature-based detection fails against zero-day attacks and polymorphic malware. ML captures *behavioral patterns* rather than static byte sequences.
- **How does it work?** Four operational steps:
  1. **Feature Engineering:** Convert raw network/system logs into numerical vectors (packet size, flow duration, protocol type, port number, flags).
  2. **Model Training:** Fit a statistical or neural model to the feature space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$.
  3. **Scoring:** Compute an anomaly score $s(x) \in \mathbb{R}$ for new observations.
  4. **Thresholding:** Apply $\tau$; if $s(x) \geq \tau$, raise an alert.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Use Case / Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| Z-Score (Statistical) | $z(x) = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Outlier detection in 1-D Gaussian-distributed features (e.g., packet length) |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M(x) = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}$ | Multivariate anomaly detection accounting for feature correlation |
| Euclidean Distance (k-NN) | $d(x, x_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - x_{i,j})^2}$ | Distance-based scoring in LOF and k-NN detectors |
| Isolation Forest Score | $s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | Tree-based path-length scoring (see §3 for derivation) |
| Local Outlier Factor (LOF) | $LOF_k(x) = \frac{\sum_{i \in N_k(x)} \frac{lrd(k)}{lrd(i)}}{k}$ | Density-based local deviation (flags points in sparser regions) |
| Reconstruction Error (AE) | $\mathcal{L}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$ | Autoencoder — high error $\Rightarrow$ anomalous |
| One-Class SVM Decision | $f(x) = \text{sign}\big( (w \cdot \Phi(x)) - \rho \big)$ | Hyperplane separation in kernel-induced space |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Primary evaluation metric for imbalanced security datasets |
| AUC-ROC | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt$ | Threshold-independent classifier quality measure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Convention:** Throughout this sheet, all absolute-value and norm operators use $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ — **never** the bare ASCII pipe character — to preserve the markdown table integrity required by the KTU typesetting pipeline.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS):** Snort + ML plugin, Zeek with ML backends.
- **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR):** CrowdStrike, SentinelOne use process-behavior ML models.
- **Credit Card Fraud:** PayPal, Stripe — autoencoder-based scoring on transaction streams.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA):** Detecting Modbus/TCP protocol anomalies in power grids.
- **Cloud Workload Protection:** AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender — ML on VPC flow logs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Isolation Forest Anomaly Score

The Isolation Forest algorithm (Liu, Ting, Zhou — 2008; re-derived here for KTU analytical purposes) assigns an anomaly score based on the average path length required to isolate a sample in a random partitioning tree.

**Step 1 — Define the average path length for a binary search tree of $n$ external nodes:**

The mathematically expected number of comparisons to isolate a sample $x$ in a Binary Search Tree (BST) is given by the harmonic formulation:

$$
c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}
$$

where $H(k)$ is the $k$-th harmonic number:

$$
H(k) = \ln(k) + \gamma \quad \text{with Euler-Mascheroni constant } \gamma \approx 0.5772
$$

**Step 2 — Express the anomaly score:**

The Isolation Forest score $s(x, n)$ is a monotonically decreasing function of the expected path length $E(h(x))$:

$$
s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the scoring range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(h(x)) \to 0 &\implies s \to 1 &\text{(highly anomalous, isolated quickly)} \\
E(h(x)) \to c(n) &\implies s \to 0.5 &\text{(normal, behaves like average)} \\
E(h(x)) \to n-1 &\implies s \to 0 &\text{(inconclusive, not isolated)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Anomalous if $s(x, n) \geq \tau$** where $\tau$ is typically chosen in the range $[0.5, 0.7]$ depending on the desired false positive budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Anomalies are *few* and *different*, so they require **fewer random partitions** to be isolated. This is the geometric intuition that makes Isolation Forest both fast (linear complexity $O(n \log n)$) and effective for high-dimensional security data.

### 3.2 Derivation: Local Outlier Factor (LOF) — Density Ratio

The LOF algorithm quantifies how *locally* isolated a point is, relative to its $k$-nearest neighbors.

**Step 1 — Compute the $k$-distance:**

For a point $x$ and integer $k$, define $d_k(x)$ as the distance from $x$ to its $k$-th nearest neighbor. The $k$-distance neighborhood is:

$$
N_k(x) = \{ x_i \in D \setminus \{x\} \mid d(x, x_i) \leq d_k(x) \}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the reachability distance:**

For any $x_i \in N_k(x)$:

$$
\text{reach-dist}_k(x, x_i) = \max\{ d_k(x_i), \, d(x, x_i) \}
$$

This stabilizes distance estimates for points that are *very close* to $x$ (smoothing the local density estimate).

**Step 3 — Compute the local reachability density (LRD):**

$$
\text{lrd}_k(x) = \frac{\lVert N_k(x) \rVert}{\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} \text{reach-dist}_k(x, x_i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Final LOF score:**

$$
LOF_k(x) = \frac{\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} \frac{\text{lrd}_k(x_i)}{\text{lrd}_k(x)}}{\lVert N_k(x) \rVert}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LOF_k(x) \approx 1 &\implies \text{point lies in a region of similar density (normal)} \\
LOF_k(x) \gg 1 &\implies \text{point is in a much sparser region (anomaly)} \\
LOF_k(x) \ll 1 &\implies \text{point is in a much denser region (possible inlier cluster)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LOF derivation above is a **high-frequency KTU question**. Examiners specifically test whether students understand (a) the smoothing role of `reach-dist` and (b) why LOF is a *local* measure — it can detect anomalies that a global Gaussian model would miss.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection for Security Applications

| Security Use Case | Best ML Algorithm | Justification (Why this algorithm?) |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time packet inspection (>1 Gbps) | **Isolation Forest** | Sub-linear average time, scales to high-dimensional flow features |
| User Behavior Analytics (UEBA) | **Autoencoder** | Captures nonlinear behavioral baselines, robust to noise |
| Credit card fraud (tabular, imbalanced) | **XGBoost (supervised)** | Handles missing features, strong F1 on structured fraud data |
| Insider threat detection | **LOF / DBSCAN** | Local density deviation catches subtle behavior shifts |
| Zero-day exploit identification | **One-Class SVM** | No attack labels required; learns only normal behavior |
| Malware API-call sequence | **LSTM Autoencoder** | Sequential reconstruction error flags unknown call patterns |
| SCADA / ICS protocol anomaly | **Mahalanobis + Statistical** | Low-dimensional, Gaussian-distributed sensor data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Anomaly Detection Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Raw Security Data Sources] --> A1[Log Collector]
    A1 --> A2[Network Packets]
    A1 --> A3[System Calls]
    A1 --> A4[User Activity Logs]
    A1 --> A5[Authentication Records]
    A2 --> B0[Feature Extraction Layer]
    A3 --> B0
    A4 --> B0
    A5 --> B0
    B0 --> B1[Flow Based Features]
    B0 --> B2[Statistical Features]
    B0 --> B3[Temporal Features]
    B1 --> C0[Preprocessing Module]
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0
    C0 --> C1[Normalization]
    C0 --> C2[Encoding]
    C0 --> C3[Feature Selection]
    C1 --> D0[ML Model Layer]
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    D0 --> D1[Supervised Model]
    D0 --> D2[Unsupervised Model]
    D0 --> D3[Semi Supervised Model]
    D1 --> E0[Anomaly Score Generator]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    E0 --> E1[Score Normalization]

    F0 --> F2[Alert Suspicious]
    F0 --> F3[Alert Anomalous]
    F1 --> G0[SIEM Dashboard]
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Security Analyst Review]
    H0 --> I0[Feedback Loop to Model]
    I0 --> D0
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow of the Isolation Forest Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J0[Start: New Sample x] --> J1[Initialize Score Zero]
    J1 --> J2[For each of t Trees]
    J2 --> J3[Random Split Feature q]
    J3 --> J4[Random Split Value p]
    J4 --> J5{Is x Isolated}
    J5 -->|Yes| J6[Record Path Length h]
    J5 -->|No| J3
    J6 --> J7[Compute Average Path E h]
    J7 --> J8[Compute Average c of n]
    J8 --> J9[Calculate Score s x n]
    J9 --> J10{Score Greater Than Tau}
    J10 -->|Yes| J11[Flag as Anomaly]
    J10 -->|No| J12[Flag as Normal]
    J11 --> J13[End Decision]
    J12 --> J13
```

### 4.3 Taxonomy of Anomaly Detection Methods (KTU High-Yield Map)

```mermaid

    K0[Anomaly Detection Methods] --> K1[Statistical]
    K0 --> K2[Machine Learning Based]

    K0 --> K6[Deep Learning Based]

    K2 --> K2c[Isolation Forest]
```
