    K3 --> K3b[Mahalanobis Distance]
    K4 --> K4a[LOF]
    K4 --> K4b[DBSCAN]
    K5 --> K5a[K Means Residuals]
    K5 --> K5b[Hierarchical Clustering]
    K6 --> K6a[Autoencoders]
    K6 --> K6b[VAE]
    K6 --> K6c[GAN Based]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define anomaly detection in machine learning. Differentiate between point, contextual, and collective anomalies with a security example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Anomaly detection in machine learning is the process of identifying data points, events, or observations that deviate significantly from an established normal behavioral pattern learned by an ML model from historical data.

- **Point Anomaly:** A single data instance that is anomalous w.r.t. the entire dataset. **Security Example:** A single login from an IP in a country where the user has never operated from before.
- **Contextual Anomaly:** An instance that is anomalous only in a specific context (time, location, or season) but normal otherwise. **Security Example:** 200 database queries per second is normal at business hours, but anomalous at 2 AM.
- **Collective Anomaly:** A group of related instances that are anomalous together, even though each individual instance may appear normal. **Security Example:** A sequence of port scans on ports 21, 22, 23, 25, 80, 443 from the same source IP over 2 minutes — individually each scan is normal; collectively they indicate reconnaissance.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for two clear distinctions + 1 mark for the security example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is accuracy a misleading metric for evaluating an intrusion detection system based on anomaly detection? Suggest two better metrics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Accuracy is misleading because intrusion detection datasets (e.g., NSL-KDD, CICIDS) are **highly imbalanced** — attacks constitute only 1–5% of total samples. A trivial classifier that labels *every* packet as "normal" achieves 95–99% accuracy while detecting **zero** attacks. This is the *accuracy paradox*.

Two better metrics:

1. **F1-Score** — harmonic mean of Precision and Recall; it penalizes models that ignore the minority (attack) class.
2. **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC)** — threshold-independent measure of separability between normal and anomalous classes.

*(1 mark for accuracy paradox + 1 mark each for the two metrics.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the three paradigms of machine learning-based anomaly detection: supervised, semi-supervised, and unsupervised. For each, name one algorithm and discuss one practical limitation in a security context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Isolation Forest anomaly score formula $s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ and explain each term. Discuss why this algorithm is computationally efficient for high-dimensional security data. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Paradigms of ML-based Anomaly Detection (7 marks)**

1. **Supervised Anomaly Detection (2 marks)**
   - **Mechanism:** Requires a fully labeled training dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ with $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ representing normal and attack classes. The model learns a direct decision boundary.
   - **Algorithm Example:** Random Forest, XGBoost, or a Deep Neural Network classifier.
   - **Security Limitation:** Requires large volumes of *labeled* attack data, which is expensive to obtain. Zero-day attacks, by definition, are absent from training labels, so the model will misclassify them as normal.

2. **Semi-Supervised (One-Class) Anomaly Detection (2 marks)**
   - **Mechanism:** Trained on **only normal samples**; learns the boundary of the normal class. At inference, samples outside the boundary are flagged.
   - **Algorithm Example:** One-Class SVM or Autoencoder.
   - **Security Limitation:** The model assumes the training set is *pure* (contains only normal samples). A single contamination with attack traffic during training will distort the learned boundary, causing systematic false negatives.

3. **Unsupervised Anomaly Detection (2 marks)**
   - **Mechanism:** No labels used; the algorithm infers anomalous structure from intrinsic properties (distance, density, isolation).
   - **Algorithm Example:** Isolation Forest, LOF, or DBSCAN.
   - **Security Limitation:** Computed scores are *uncalibrated* — there is no probabilistic interpretation, making threshold selection ($\tau$) ad-hoc and difficult to tune.

4. **Synthesis (1 mark):** A production-grade Security Operations Center (SOC) typically deploys a *hybrid* stack — supervised for known attacks, unsupervised for zero-day discovery.

---

**(b) Derivation of Isolation Forest Score (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Begin with the harmonic BST path-length formulation. For a sub-sample of size $n$, the average external path length of an unsuccessful Binary Search Tree search is:

$$
c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}
$$

where $H(n-1)$ is the $(n-1)$-th harmonic number. This is the *normalization constant* — it represents the expected path length of an "average" (i.e., normal) data point.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The Isolation Forest builds $t$ random isolation trees (iTrees). For a given sample $x$, the average path length across all $t$ trees is:

$$
E(h(x)) = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{i=1}^{t} h_i(x)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define the anomaly score as the negative exponential of the normalized path length:

$$
s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
- If $E(h(x)) \to 0$ (anomaly isolated quickly), $s \to 2^0 = 1$ → highly anomalous.
- If $E(h(x)) \to c(n)$ (normal), $s \to 2^{-1} = 0.5$ → average.
- If $E(h(x)) \to n-1$, $s \to 2^{-\infty} \to 0$ → inconclusive.

**Computational Efficiency Argument (extra 1 mark for bonus if extended):**
- Building each iTree is $O(\log n)$ average.
- Total training time: $O(t \cdot n \log n)$.
- Inference (one sample): $O(t \log n)$.
- No distance matrix computation, no gradient descent — hence scales to high-dimensional network flow data (40+ features in NSL-KDD).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternate]*

**(a)** Define Local Outlier Factor (LOF). Derive its score and explain how it differs from a global statistical method like the Z-Score. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a network intrusion detection system. Explain how a deep autoencoder detects anomalies using reconstruction error. Justify why autoencoders are well-suited to detecting zero-day attacks compared to signature-based methods. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Local Outlier Factor (LOF) — Definition and Derivation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** LOF is a density-based anomaly detection algorithm that quantifies how *locally* isolated a point is, relative to its $k$-nearest neighbors, by comparing the local reachability density of the point to that of its neighbors.

**Derivation Steps (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — k-Distance Neighborhood:**

$$
N_k(x) = \{ x_i \in D \setminus \{x\} \mid d(x, x_i) \leq d_k(x) \}
$$

where $d_k(x)$ is the distance to the $k$-th nearest neighbor.

**Step 2 — Reachability Distance (smoothing):**

$$
\text{reach-dist}_k(x, x_i) = \max\{ d_k(x_i), d(x, x_i) \}
$$

This prevents statistical instability when neighbors are very close to $x$.

**Step 3 — Local Reachability Density (LRD):**

$$
\text{lrd}_k(x) = \frac{\lVert N_k(x) \rVert}{\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} \text{reach-dist}_k(x, x_i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Final LOF Score:**

$$
LOF_k(x) = \frac{1}{\lVert N_k(x) \rVert} \sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} \frac{\text{lrd}_k(x_i)}{\text{lrd}_k(x)}
$$

**Difference from Z-Score (1 mark):** Z-Score is a *global* Gaussian assumption — a point is anomalous only if its distance from the global mean exceeds $\sigma$ thresholds. It fails for non-Gaussian, multi-modal data. LOF is a *local* measure — it can detect points anomalous in *one region* even if their absolute deviation from the global mean is small.

---

**(b) Deep Autoencoder for Anomaly Detection (7 marks)**

**Architecture (2 marks):** An autoencoder is a neural network with two parts:
- **Encoder:** $E: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^z$, compresses input into a low-dimensional latent representation $z$ where $z \ll d$.
- **Decoder:** $D: \mathbb{R}^z \to \mathbb{R}^d$, reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from $z$.

It is trained to minimize the reconstruction loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2
$$

**Anomaly Detection Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Train the autoencoder **only on normal traffic**.
- At inference, for a new sample $x$, compute its reconstruction error $\mathcal{L}(x)$.
- If $\mathcal{L}(x) \geq \tau$ (threshold learned via validation on a held-out clean set), flag as anomaly.
- **Intuition:** The model learns the manifold of *normal* behavior. Attack traffic does not conform to this manifold → high reconstruction error.

**Zero-Day Detection Justification (2 marks):**
- Signature-based detection (e.g., Snort rules, antivirus) requires a *known byte pattern* — fails on zero-days.
- Autoencoders learn *behavioral abstractions* — they detect *any* traffic that breaks the normal pattern, including previously unseen attacks.
- The model is attack-agnostic: it does not need to be retrained when a new attack variant appears.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Anomaly Detection Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing LOF with k-NN:** LOF is *not* a distance-based method — it is a **density ratio**. Students who write only "LOF measures distance to k-th neighbor" lose 2–3 marks. Always state the lrd ratio.
> 2. **Forgetting the $c(n)$ normalization in Isolation Forest:** Writing $s(x) = 2^{-E(h(x))}$ *without* dividing by $c(n)$ is a frequent error that costs full marks on the formula. $c(n)$ is what makes the score *comparable across sub-samples of different sizes*.
> 3. **Mentioning accuracy on imbalanced data:** Examiners interpret "accuracy = 99%" as a sign the student does not understand the class imbalance problem. Always volunteer the F1/AUC discussion.
> 4. **Omitting $\tau$ (threshold) in the decision rule:** A score $s(x)$ by itself is meaningless. The decision is $s(x) \geq \tau$. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if the thresholding step is skipped.
> 5. **Calling Autoencoders "supervised":** They are trained on *unlabeled* normal data. The correct framing is **semi-supervised** or **self-supervised reconstruction**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Anomaly Detection Using ML (PECST863 / M3)**

- [ ] **Three anomaly types:** Point, Contextual, Collective — *know the definition AND a security example for each.*
- [ ] **Three ML paradigms:** Supervised, Semi-Supervised, Unsupervised — *know one algorithm and one limitation per paradigm.*
- [ ] **Isolation Forest score:** $s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$, with $c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$.
- [ ] **LOF score:** Density ratio comparing $\text{lrd}(x)$ to that of its $k$ neighbors; $LOF \gg 1$ means anomaly.
- [ ] **Autoencoder logic:** Trained on normal data; high $\lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$ at inference = anomaly.
- [ ] **One-Class SVM:** Learns a hyperplane in kernel-induced space that separates normal data from origin.
- [ ] **Evaluation metrics:** Use F1-Score, AUC-ROC, and the confusion matrix — **NEVER** rely on accuracy for imbalanced data.
- [ ] **Threshold $\tau$:** Every scoring function must be paired with a threshold for the final decision.
- [ ] **Use case mapping:** Tabular → XGBoost / Isolation Forest; Sequential → LSTM Autoencoder; Image-based → CNN Autoencoder / VAE.
- [ ] **Production pipeline order:** Raw Data → Feature Engineering → Preprocessing → Model → Anomaly Score → Threshold → Alert → SOC Analyst Feedback.
- [ ] **Major security datasets:** NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15, Kyoto 2006+ — examiners may reference these.
- [ ] **Zero-day argument:** Behavioral (ML) > Signature (rule-based) for unknown attacks.
- [ ] **Computational cost order (low → high):** Z-Score < Isolation Forest < One-Class SVM ≈ LOF < Deep Autoencoder.
- [ ] **Hallmark phrase to use in answers:** *"ML-based anomaly detection captures behavioral baselines rather than static signatures, enabling detection of zero-day attacks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spam Detection Based on Machine Learning Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spam Detection Based on Machine Learning Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Spam Detection** is the process of identifying and classifying unsolicited, unwanted, or malicious electronic messages (primarily emails) as "spam" (junk/unsolicited bulk email) or "ham" (legitimate email). When implemented using a **Machine Learning (ML) approach**, the system learns discriminative patterns from a pre-labeled corpus of messages and generalizes them to accurately classify unseen messages without relying on hand-crafted static rules.

In the context of the KTU 2024 PECST863 syllabus, spam detection is studied as a **supervised binary text classification problem** belonging to the broader domain of **Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Information Security**, where the security goal is to mitigate phishing, social engineering, and malware propagation vectors delivered via electronic mail.

> [!NOTE] **Key Terminology Cheat Sheet**
> - **Spam (Junk Mail):** Unsolicited Bulk Email (UBE) sent to a large number of recipients without explicit consent.
> - **Ham:** A legitimate, solicited, non-spam email.
> - **Phishing:** A malicious subset of spam designed to steal credentials or financial data.
> - **False Positive (Type I Error):** A legitimate email incorrectly flagged as spam (most critical security error in anti-spam systems).
> - **False Negative (Type II Error):** A spam email that slips through and reaches the user's inbox.
> - **Corpus:** A large, structured collection of pre-labeled emails used to train and test ML models.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Sorting Office"

Imagine you are the **Chief Postal Inspector** of a massive national mail office. Every day, **millions of letters** arrive. You cannot read each one manually. So, you hire and train a team of **expert inspectors**.

These inspectors look at certain **clues**:
- Is the sender's address from a known junk-mail company?
- Does the letter contain phrases like "FREE MONEY", "ACT NOW", or "CLICK HERE"?
- Is the envelope glossy, oddly shaped, or mass-printed?
- Does it lack a proper return address?

After months of training on **labeled samples** (letters you already sorted), the inspectors learn the **patterns** automatically. New letters arriving tomorrow are sorted based on the **learned rules**, not on a hard-coded rulebook.

In this analogy:
- The **postal inspectors** = the **Machine Learning Classifier** (e.g., Naive Bayes, SVM).
- The **clues** = **Features** (e.g., word frequencies, header anomalies).
- The **labeled letters** = the **Training Dataset** (e.g., SpamAssassin, Enron-Spam).
- The **sorting decision** = the **Classification Output** (Spam / Ham).

This ML approach is **adaptive** — when spammers change their tactics, the inspectors (model) can be **retrained** with new examples, unlike rigid rule-based filters.

### 1.3 Why ML Over Traditional Rule-Based Filters?

| Property | Rule-Based Filtering | Machine Learning Approach |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| **Adaptability** | Static; needs manual rule updates | Learns and adapts to new spam patterns |
| **Scalability** | Poor; rule explosion | High; scales with data |
| **Evasion Resistance** | Low; spammers bypass keywords | Higher; learns latent statistical patterns |
| **False Positive Rate** | High | Significantly lower with proper tuning |
| **Maintenance** | Continuous human intervention | Periodic retraining with new corpus |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Spam Detection Pipeline (Operational Stages)

The end-to-end ML-based spam detection system follows a structured pipeline. Each stage is critical for the overall accuracy and robustness of the system.

- **Stage 1 — Data Collection (Corpus Acquisition):**
  Gather a large, balanced, and representative dataset of pre-labeled emails. Each email is hand-tagged by human annotators as `spam = 1` or `ham = 0`. Standard public datasets include the **SpamAssassin Public Corpus**, the **Enron-Spam Dataset**, the **Ling-Spam Corpus**, and the **TREC Spam Track Dataset**.

- **Stage 2 — Email Preprocessing (Cleaning):**
  Raw email is highly unstructured (contains HTML, MIME parts, Base64-encoded images, signatures, replies, etc.). Preprocessing converts it into a clean, analyzable format. Sub-steps include:
  - **Header Parsing:** Extract `From`, `To`, `Subject`, `Received`, `Message-ID`, `X-Spam-Score`.
  - **HTML Tag Removal:** Strip `<tags>`, decode ` `, `&` entities.
  - **Lowercasing:** Normalize all characters to lowercase.
  - **Tokenization:** Split the email body into individual words (tokens).
  - **Stop-Word Removal:** Discard common words like "the", "is", "a", "and" that carry little discriminative power.
  - **Stemming/Lemmatization:** Reduce words to their root form (e.g., "running", "ran", "runs" → "run").
  - **Punctuation & Number Normalization.**

- **Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (Vectorization):**
  Convert the cleaned text into a numerical feature vector that the ML algorithm can process. The dominant technique is the **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** model, often enhanced with **TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)** weighting. Additional features include:
  - **Header-based features:** Number of recipients, presence of unusual `Received` hops, mismatched `From` and `Reply-To` domains.
  - **Structural features:** Ratio of HTML to text, number of URLs, number of images, presence of attachments.
  - **Character-level features:** Frequency of special characters (`$`, `!`, `%`).

- **Stage 4 — Model Training (Supervised Learning):**
  Feed the labeled feature vectors into a classifier. The classifier learns a **decision function** $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ that maps an $n$-dimensional feature vector to the spam/ham label. The training objective is to **minimize the empirical loss** (classification error) on the training set.

- **Stage 5 — Model Evaluation:**
  Quantify the classifier's performance on a held-out **test set** using metrics such as **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and the ROC-AUC curve**.

- **Stage 6 — Deployment & Continuous Learning:**
  Deploy the trained model in a production mail server. Periodically retrain the model with newly labeled data to counteract **concept drift** (the phenomenon where spam tactics evolve over time, making the older training data less representative).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Engineering Use |
|-------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Bag-of-Words (BoW) Representation** | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{\vert V \vert})$ where $x_i = \text{count}(w_i \in d)$ | Represents a document $d$ as a vector of word counts over a vocabulary $V$ of size $\vert V \vert$. |
| **Term Frequency (TF)** | $\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Frequency of term $t$ in document $d$, normalized by document length. |
| **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{DF}(t) + 1}\right)$ | $N$ = total documents; $\text{DF}(t)$ = docs containing $t$. The "+1" prevents division by zero. |
| **TF-IDF Weight** | $\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)$ | Down-weights common words, up-weights discriminative spam keywords like "viagra", "free". |
| **Naive Bayes Posterior (Multinomial)** | $P(c \vert \mathbf{x}) \propto P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert c)$ | $c \in \{\text{spam}, \text{ham}\}$. The class with the highest posterior is the predicted label. |
| **Laplace Smoothing (Add-1)** | $P(x_i \vert c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{x} \text{count}(x, c) + \vert V \vert}$ | Prevents zero-probability for unseen words in test data. |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall fraction of correct predictions. Misleading on imbalanced data. |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all emails flagged as spam, how many are truly spam? High precision = fewer false alarms. |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all actual spam emails, how many did the system catch? High recall = fewer spam leaks. |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall; the **single most important metric** in spam detection. |
| **Support Vector Machine (Linear)** | $\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i - b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | Finds the maximum-margin hyperplane separating spam and ham. |
| **Confusion Matrix Entry Totals** | $TP + FP + TN + FN = N$ (total test samples) | Used to verify metric computations. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spam detection using ML is not just an academic exercise — it is a **critical security control** deployed at scale in:
- **Enterprise Mail Gateways** (e.g., Proofpoint, Mimecast, Microsoft Exchange Online Protection).
- **Webmail Providers** (Gmail, Yahoo Mail, Outlook.com process >300 billion emails/day, with ML filtering).
- **SMS/Messaging Platforms** (Spam SMS detection on WhatsApp, Telegram).
- **Comment/Review Moderation** (YouTube, Amazon, TripAdvisor use similar NLP-ML pipelines for toxic content).

> [!NOTE] **Why Naive Bayes Dominates Classical Spam Filters**
> Despite its "naive" independence assumption, Naive Bayes is the **de-facto baseline** for spam detection because: (1) it is computationally cheap for high-dimensional sparse text data, (2) it requires very little training data, (3) it is highly incremental (can be updated one email at a time), and (4) its independence assumption is "broken" in a way that **empirically works well for text**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Naive Bayes Classification for Spam

**Problem Statement:** Given an email $d$ represented as a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ of word occurrences, classify it as **spam** ($c = 1$) or **ham** ($c = 0$) using the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule.

#### Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem

The posterior probability of class $c$ given the observed feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of the class $c$, it acts merely as a normalization constant. Therefore, classification reduces to comparing the **numerator** across the two classes:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in \{0,1\}} P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Naive Independence Assumption

The joint likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ over $n$ features is **factorized** by assuming that each feature $x_i$ (word occurrence) is conditionally independent of every other feature $x_j$ given the class $c$:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This is the "naive" assumption — in reality, words like "free" and "money" co-occur in spam more often than chance. Yet the assumption makes the computation tractable and works empirically well.

#### Step 3 — Substitute into the MAP Decision Rule

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Log-Transformation to Prevent Numerical Underflow

For long emails (large $n$), the product $\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ underflows in floating-point arithmetic. We take the **monotonic logarithm** (which preserves ordering):

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

This converts products into **sums**, which are numerically stable and computationally faster.

#### Step 5 — Estimate the Probabilities from the Training Corpus (Maximum Likelihood Estimation)

The **prior** $P(c)$ is estimated as the fraction of training emails belonging to class $c$:

$$P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$$

The **likelihood** $P(x_i \mid c)$ for the Multinomial Naive Bayes model is the frequency of feature $x_i$ in all training documents of class $c$:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count of feature } x_i \text{ across all docs in class } c}{\text{total feature count across all docs in class } c}$$

#### Step 6 — Apply Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing

If a word $x_i$ never appeared in the training data for class $c$, the MLE estimate is $\hat{P}(x_i \mid c) = 0$, which would zero out the entire product. To prevent this, **Laplace smoothing** adds a pseudo-count of 1 to every feature:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{x} \text{count}(x, c) + \vert V \vert}$$

Here, $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. This guarantees no zero probabilities and improves generalization.

#### Step 7 — Final Classification Rule

For an unseen email with feature vector $\mathbf{x}$, compute two log-scores:

$$S_{\text{spam}} = \log P(c=\text{spam}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c=\text{spam})$$

$$S_{\text{ham}} = \log P(c=\text{ham}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c=\text{ham})$$

The classifier predicts:

$$\hat{c} = \begin{cases} \text{spam} & \text{if } S_{\text{spam}} > S_{\text{ham}} \\ \text{ham} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Email Classification

> [!NOTE] **Illustrative Numerical Walkthrough (Exam-Style)**
> **Setup (Training Corpus):**
> - 4 emails total. Class labels: 2 spam, 2 ham.
> - **Spam emails:** "free offer buy now", "free prize click now"
> - **Ham emails:** "meeting schedule project", "schedule meeting tomorrow"
> - **Vocabulary** $V$ = {free, offer, buy, now, prize, click, meeting, schedule, project, tomorrow}, so $\vert V \vert = 10$.

**Step A — Compute Priors**

$$P(\text{spam}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \quad P(\text{ham}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step B — Compute Word Counts per Class**
- Spam word counts: {free:2, offer:1, buy:1, now:2, prize:1, click:1, meeting:0, schedule:0, project:0, tomorrow:0} → **Total = 8**
- Ham word counts: {free:0, offer:0, buy:0, now:0, prize:0, click:0, meeting:2, schedule:2, project:1, tomorrow:1} → **Total = 6**

**Step C — Apply Laplace Smoothing**
For spam (denominator = $8 + 10 = 18$):

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) = \frac{2+1}{18} = \frac{3}{18} = 0.1667$$

$$P(\text{now} \mid \text{spam}) = \frac{2+1}{18} = 0.1667, \quad P(\text{meeting} \mid \text{spam}) = \frac{0+1}{18} = 0.0556$$

For ham (denominator = $6 + 10 = 16$):

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) = \frac{0+1}{16} = 0.0625$$

$$P(\text{meeting} \mid \text{ham}) = \frac{2+1}{16} = 0.1875, \quad P(\text{now} \mid \text{ham}) = \frac{0+1}{16} = 0.0625$$

**Step D — Classify a New Email: "free meeting"**

$$S_{\text{spam}} = \log(0.5) + \log(0.1667) + \log(0.0556) = -0.693 - 1.792 - 2.891 = -5.376$$

$$S_{\text{ham}} = \log(0.5) + \log(0.0625) + \log(0.1875) = -0.693 - 2.773 - 1.674 = -5.140$$

**Step E — Decision**

Since $S_{\text{ham}} = -5.140 > S_{\text{spam}} = -5.376$, the email "free meeting" is classified as **HAM**. The word "meeting" (a strong ham indicator) outweighs "free" (a mild spam indicator) in this small corpus. This illustrates how Naive Bayes balances evidence from all features.

### 3.3 Confusion Matrix Construction for KTU Exam Problems

A standard 2x2 confusion matrix is the **mandatory** first step for any KTU question on performance evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \textbf{Predicted Class} \\
& \begin{array}{c|c|c}
 & \text{Spam (Pred)} & \text{Ham (Pred)} \\
\hline
\text{Spam (Actual)} & TP & FN \\
\hline
\text{Ham (Actual)} & FP & TN
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (for a typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
Given: $TP = 180$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 770$ (out of $N = 1000$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{180 + 770}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.950 = 95.0\% \\
\\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.900 = 90.0\% \\
\\
\text{Recall} &= \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} = 0.857 = 85.7\% \\
\\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.900 \cdot 0.857}{0.900 + 0.857} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7713}{1.757} = 0.878 = 87.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner's Insight**
> A spam filter with **95% accuracy can still be terrible** if the dataset is imbalanced (e.g., 99% ham). Hence KTU 2024 questions increasingly emphasize **Precision-Recall trade-off** over raw accuracy. Always compute **all four metrics** to get full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ML Spam Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Email Ingestion] --> B[Email Parser]
    B --> C[Header Extraction]
    B --> D[Body Extraction]
    C --> E[Feature Engineering Module]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Tokenization and Stopword Removal]
    F --> G[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    G --> H[TF-IDF Vectorizer]
    H --> I[Feature Vector x]
    I --> J[Trained ML Classifier]
    J --> K{Decision Boundary}
    K -->|Spam| L[Quarantine Folder]
    K -->|Ham| M[Inbox Delivery]
    L --> N[User Feedback Loop]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Periodic Model Retraining]
    O --> J
```

### 4.2 Naive Bayes Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[New Email Arrives] --> P2[Preprocess and Tokenize]
    P2 --> P3[Extract Feature Vector x]
    P3 --> P4[Compute S_spam = log P spam + sum log P xi given spam]
    P3 --> P5[Compute S_ham = log P ham + sum log P xi given ham]
    P4 --> P6{S_spam greater than S_ham}
    P5 --> P6
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Label as SPAM]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Label as HAM]
    P7 --> P9[Route to Quarantine]
    P8 --> P10[Route to Inbox]
```

### 4.3 Concept Drift and Adversarial Adaptation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Spammer Changes Tactics] --> Q2[New Spam Pattern Emerges]
    Q2 --> Q3[Model Accuracy Degrades]
    Q3 --> Q4[User Marks Misdetected Mail]
    Q4 --> Q5[Feedback Repository Updated]
    Q5 --> Q6[Scheduled Retraining Job Triggered]
    Q6 --> Q7[Updated Model Deployed]
    Q7 --> Q1
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Multi-Layer Spam Defense

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Inbound Email Stream] --> R2{Layer 1 IP Reputation Check}
    R2 -->|Blacklisted| R3[Drop at SMTP Level]
    R2 -->|Clean| R4{Layer 2 SPF DKIM DMARC Authentication}
    R4 -->|Failed| R5[Quarantine with Header Warning]
    R4 -->|Passed| R6{Layer 3 Content Based ML Classifier}
    R6 -->|High Spam Score| R7[Quarantine Folder]
    R6 -->|Low Spam Score| R8[Deliver to Inbox]
    R7 --> R9[User Review and Feedback]
    R5 --> R9
    R8 --> R9
    R9 --> R10[Feedback to Retraining Pipeline]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define spam and ham. Why is a false positive considered more critical than a false negative in spam detection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Spam (1 mark):** Unsolicited Bulk Email (UBE) sent to recipients without prior consent, often commercial, fraudulent, or malicious in nature.
- **Ham (1 mark):** Legitimate, solicited, non-spam email that the user expects to receive.
- **False Positive vs False Negative (1 mark):** A **False Positive** (legitimate mail flagged as spam) is more critical because it can cause users to **miss critical business communications, financial statements, security alerts, or job offers**, leading to direct financial or operational loss. A **False Negative** (spam reaching the inbox) is merely an inconvenience. Hence, precision and recall trade-offs in spam filters are heavily weighted toward **minimizing false positives** in enterprise deployments.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is TF-IDF? How does it improve upon the simple Bag-of-Words (BoW) model for spam detection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TF-IDF Definition (1.5 marks):** Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency is a numerical statistic that reflects how important a word is to a document in a corpus. It is computed as $\text{TF}(t,d) \times \text{IDF}(t)$, where IDF down-weights common words across the entire corpus.
- **Improvement over BoW (1.5 marks):** Plain BoW treats every word equally, so common words like "the", "is", "a" inflate the feature vector with noise. TF-IDF assigns **high weights to rare, discriminative words** (e.g., "viagra", "lottery", "pharmacy") and **low weights to ubiquitous words**, leading to better separation of spam from ham and improved classifier accuracy.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end machine learning based spam detection pipeline. Describe each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Consider a training corpus of 6 emails: 3 spam and 3 ham. Using the Multinomial Naive Bayes classifier with Laplace smoothing, classify a new email "free offer" given the following word counts: Spam emails contain "free" 4 times, "offer" 3 times, "meeting" 1 time (total 25 words). Ham emails contain "free" 1 time, "offer" 0 times, "meeting" 4 times (total 20 words). Vocabulary size is 10. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) End-to-End ML Spam Detection Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of **six sequential stages** (1 mark for diagram + 1 mark per major stage description):

1. **Data Collection:** Acquire labeled email corpus (SpamAssassin, Enron-Spam, TREC). Each email is pre-labeled as spam/ham by human annotators.
2. **Preprocessing:** Parse MIME structure, remove HTML tags, normalize case, tokenize, remove stop-words, and apply stemming (e.g., Porter stemmer).
3. **Feature Extraction:** Convert text to numerical vectors using **Bag-of-Words** or **TF-IDF**. Augment with header-based features (e.g., number of BCC recipients, presence of attachments) and structural features (URL count, HTML-to-text ratio).
4. **Model Training:** Feed the labeled feature vectors into a classifier (Naive Bayes, SVM, Random Forest, or Deep Learning). The classifier learns a decision function $f(\mathbf{x}) \to \{0,1\}$ by minimizing the classification loss on the training data.
5. **Evaluation:** Measure performance on a held-out test set using **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score**, and the **ROC-AUC curve**. Use **k-fold cross-validation** for robust estimates.
6. **Deployment & Retraining:** Deploy the model on the mail server. Collect user feedback (mark-as-spam / not-spam actions) and periodically retrain to counter **concept drift** as spam tactics evolve.

**(b) Numerical Naive Bayes Classification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Priors (1 mark)**

$$P(\text{spam}) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5, \quad P(\text{ham}) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute smoothed likelihoods (3 marks)**

For **spam**, total words = 25, vocabulary = 10, denominator = $25 + 10 = 35$:

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) = \frac{4+1}{35} = \frac{5}{35} = 0.1429$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) = \frac{3+1}{35} = \frac{4}{35} = 0.1143$$

For **ham**, total words = 20, denominator = $20 + 10 = 30$:

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) = \frac{1+1}{30} = \frac{2}{30} = 0.0667$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{ham}) = \frac{0+1}{30} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.0333$$

**Step 3 — Compute log-scores for "free offer" (2 marks)**

$$S_{\text{spam}} = \log(0.5) + \log(0.1429) + \log(0.1143) = -0.693 - 1.946 - 2.170 = -4.809$$

$$S_{\text{ham}} = \log(0.5) + \log(0.0667) + \log(0.0333) = -0.693 - 2.708 - 3.401 = -6.802$$

**Step 4 — Decision (1 mark)**

Since $S_{\text{spam}} = -4.809 > S_{\text{ham}} = -6.802$, the email **"free offer"** is classified as **SPAM** with high confidence.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the Multinomial Naive Bayes algorithm for spam classification. Derive the classification rule and clearly state the role of Laplace smoothing. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A spam classifier produced the following confusion matrix on a test set of 500 emails: TP = 90, FP = 10, FN = 20, TN = 380. Compute the Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on which metric is most appropriate for evaluating a spam filter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multinomial Naive Bayes Derivation (7 marks):**

**Problem Setup (1 mark):** We model an email as a bag of word occurrences. Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ be the feature vector where $x_i$ is the count of word $w_i$ in the email. The goal is to predict the class $c \in \{\text{spam}, \text{ham}\}$.

**Bayes' Theorem Application (2 marks):**

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

The MAP estimate is:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$$

**Naive Independence Assumption (2 marks):** Assuming features are conditionally independent given the class:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)^{x_i}$$

**Log-Space Classification Rule (1 mark):**

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \log P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

**Role of Laplace Smoothing (1 mark):** Laplace (add-1) smoothing replaces zero counts with a pseudo-count of 1, ensuring that words absent from the training set still receive a non-zero probability. This prevents the entire posterior from collapsing to zero when an unseen word appears in a test email, and is critical for the model's generalization to real-world, evolving spam content.

**(b) Confusion Matrix Metric Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $TP = 90$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 380$, $N = 500$.

**Step 1 — Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{90 + 380}{500} = \frac{470}{500} = 0.940 = 94.0\%$$

**Step 2 — Precision (1.5 marks):**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.900 = 90.0\%$$

**Step 3 — Recall (1.5 marks):**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 20} = \frac{90}{110} = 0.818 = 81.8\%$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (1.5 marks):**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.900 \times 0.818}{0.900 + 0.818} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7362}{1.718} = 0.857 = 85.7\%$$

**Step 5 — Metric Selection Commentary (1.5 marks):** The **F1-Score** is the most appropriate single metric for evaluating a spam filter because:
- **Accuracy is misleading** on imbalanced datasets (e.g., 95% ham emails in real inboxes inflate accuracy trivially).
- **Precision alone** ignores missed spam (FN), and **Recall alone** ignores false alarms (FP).
- **F1-Score** balances both via the harmonic mean, penalizing extreme imbalances. For spam, where both missing spam (security risk) and blocking legitimate mail (user trust risk) are costly, F1 provides a holistic single-number evaluation.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall labels** — Many students swap the formulas. Remember: **Precision** is about the **Predicted Positive** column ($TP / (TP+FP)$); **Recall** is about the **Actual Positive** row ($TP / (TP+FN)$). Losing 1 mark per swap.
> 2. **Forgetting Laplace smoothing in Naive Bayes derivations** — If a question asks you to "derive the classifier", you MUST show the smoothing term, or the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks. The denominator $\sum + \vert V \vert$ is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Using "$\vert x \vert$" inside markdown tables** — KTU digital answer sheets use markdown; the pipe character breaks the table renderer. Use $\text{magnitude}(x)$ or $\lVert x \rVert$ in LaTeX instead.
> 4. **Writing accuracy alone as the final answer** — KTU 2024 rubric requires all four metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1) for full marks on evaluation questions.
> 5. **Ignoring the "preprocessing" stage** — When asked to "explain the pipeline", at least 2 marks are reserved for preprocessing details (tokenization, stemming, stop-words). Skipping this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Not mentioning concept drift** — In 14-mark questions on deployment, the answer must include a discussion of **concept drift** and the need for periodic retraining, or you lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spam Detection via ML**

- [ ] **Spam vs. Ham:** Spam = unsolicited bulk email; Ham = legitimate email.
- [ ] **Pipeline:** Data Collection → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction (BoW/TF-IDF) → Training → Evaluation → Deployment.
- [ ] **Preprocessing steps:** Lowercase → Tokenize → Stop-word removal → Stemming/Lemmatization.
- [ ] **Bag-of-Words:** Document = vector of word counts over a vocabulary $V$.
- [ ] **TF-IDF:** $\text{TF} \times \text{IDF}$ — weights rare discriminative words higher; preferred over raw counts.
- [ ] **Naive Bayes formula:** $c^* = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$; always use **log-space** to prevent underflow.
- [ ] **Laplace Smoothing:** $P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + 1}{\sum_x \text{count}(x, c) + \vert V \vert}$ — mandatory, prevents zero probabilities.
- [ ] **Confusion Matrix:** TP (top-left), FN (top-right), FP (bottom-left), TN (bottom-right).
- [ ] **Accuracy formula:** $\frac{TP+TN}{N}$ — misleading on imbalanced data.
- [ ] **Precision:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — quality of spam flags.
- [ ] **Recall:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — coverage of spam detection.
- [ ] **F1-Score:** $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ — single best metric for spam filters.
- [ ] **Other classifiers used:** SVM (maximum margin), Random Forest (ensemble), Logistic Regression (probabilistic baseline), Deep Learning (CNN/LSTM/BERT for advanced systems).
- [ ] **Concept Drift:** Spammers constantly adapt; models must be periodically retrained with fresh labeled data.
- [ ] **False Positive > False Negative concern:** In enterprise/critical email, blocking legitimate mail is costlier than letting spam through.
- [ ] **Standard Datasets:** SpamAssassin, Enron-Spam, Ling-Spam, TREC Spam Track.
- [ ] **Feature Types:** Content (words, TF-IDF), Header (From, Received hops), Structural (URL count, HTML ratio), Behavioral (sender reputation).
- [ ] **Real-world Deployment:** Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook, Proofpoint, Mimecast — all use layered ML-based spam detection.
- [ ] **Future Trends:** Transformer-based models (BERT, RoBERTa), adversarial robustness against spam obfuscation (e.g., "V1agra", "Fr*ee"), federated learning for privacy-preserving spam filters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adversarial Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adversarial Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adversarial Machine Learning (AML)** is the formal study of the intersection between machine learning systems and cybersecurity, specifically focusing on the design, analysis, and defense of learning-based models against malicious inputs, corrupted training data, and strategic model exploitation. It is officially defined under the 2024 Scheme PECST863 syllabus as the discipline that investigates **"the vulnerability of machine learning models to intentional, worst-case perturbations and the corresponding defensive mechanisms to ensure robustness, confidentiality, and integrity in deployed AI systems."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> Adversarial Machine Learning is a subfield of machine learning that studies the behaviour of learning algorithms in the presence of an intelligent, adaptive adversary who deliberately manipulates the input distribution, training data, or model parameters to compromise the system's confidentiality, integrity, or availability — commonly categorized under the **CIA Triad of ML Security**.

The three primary adversarial goals are mapped to the security triad as follows:

| Security Goal | Adversarial Equivalent | Impact on ML System |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Integrity** | Evasion Attacks | Misclassification of inputs at inference time |
| **Availability** | Denial-of-Service / Poisoning | Degradation of model performance globally |
| **Confidentiality** | Model Inversion / Membership Inference | Extraction of private training data or model parameters |

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of AML

1. **Adversarial Examples** – Carefully crafted inputs with imperceptible perturbations that cause misclassification.
2. **Adversarial Attacks** – The strategic process of generating such inputs or corrupting the training pipeline.
3. **Adversarial Defenses** – Countermeasures designed to detect, reject, or nullify adversarial inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in 2024 KTU Context:** With the widespread deployment of ML in autonomous vehicles, healthcare diagnostics, biometric authentication, and spam filtering, AML has become a board-exam-relevant cybersecurity concern. PECST863 explicitly tests the student's ability to differentiate between attack types and propose corresponding defenses.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Optical Illusion Attack"

Imagine a self-driving car's image classifier. To a human driver, a slightly modified stop sign still looks unmistakably like a stop sign. However, by adding a few carefully placed stickers (an **adversarial patch**), an attacker can fool the deep neural network into classifying it as a **"Speed Limit 45"** sign. The perturbation is so small that a human never notices, but the mathematical decision boundary of the model is shifted dramatically.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Anchor:** Think of adversarial examples as *optical illusions designed for machines*. The visual world is unchanged for us, but the model's learned "lens" is tricked. The attacker's goal is to push the input across a decision boundary using the **smallest possible perturbation**.

## 1.4 The Perturbation Budget — A Key Metric

The adversary is typically constrained by a **perturbation budget** $\epsilon$, measured under one of the standard **$\ell_p$ norms**:

- **$\ell_0$ norm** – Counts the number of pixels modified (sparsity constraint).
- **$\ell_2$ norm** – Euclidean distance; preserves overall pixel magnitude.
- **$\ell_\infty$ norm** – Maximum per-pixel change; the most common KTU exam constraint.

The mathematical formulation of the constrained adversarial problem is:

$$x_{adv} = \underset{x' : \, \|x' - x\|_p \leq \epsilon}{\arg\max} \,\, L(\theta, x', y)$$

where $L(\theta, x', y)$ is the loss function of the model with parameters $\theta$ evaluated on perturbed input $x'$ and true label $y$.

## 1.5 Adversarial Capabilities — Threat Model Hierarchy

A KTU-standard threat model specifies *what the attacker knows*:

| Threat Model | Attacker's Knowledge | Real-World Analogy |
|--------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **White-Box** | Full access to model, gradients, architecture | Insider with source code |
| **Black-Box** | Only query access to model outputs | External user probing an API |
| **Gray-Box** | Partial knowledge (architecture but not weights) | Competitor with documentation |
| **Physical-World** | Constraints of camera angle, lighting, print fidelity | Real sticker on a road sign |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Trigger:** Questions that ask *"Differentiate between white-box and black-box attacks"* or *"Explain evasion vs. poisoning attacks"* are extremely high-frequency in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the threat model table above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Adversarial Attacks

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures adversarial attacks along **two orthogonal axes**: the *attack stage* and the *attacker's capability*.

### Axis 1: Attack Stage (When the Attack Happens)

**A. Evasion Attacks (Inference-Time Attacks)**
- The model has already been trained and deployed.
- The attacker crafts inputs to be misclassified at prediction time.
- **Example:** A spam email modified to bypass a trained spam filter.
- **Most common board-exam topic.**

**B. Poisoning Attacks (Training-Time Attacks)**
- The attacker injects malicious samples into the training dataset.
- The model is corrupted *before* deployment — affecting global behaviour.
- **Example:** Backdoor triggers planted via tampered labels.
- Subtypes: *Label Flipping*, *Clean-Label Poisoning*, *Backdoor Attacks*.

**C. Exploratory Attacks (Model Extraction / Inversion)**
- The attacker probes the model to steal its parameters or reconstruct training data.
- **Example:** Model stealing via API querying, reconstructing medical images from a diagnostic model.

### Axis 2: Attack Specificity (Target of Misclassification)

- **Targeted Attack** — Force the input to be classified as a *specific* attacker-chosen class $y^*$.
- **Untargeted Attack** — Force the input to be classified as *any* class other than the true class $y$.

## 2.2 Classical Evasion Attack Methods — Algorithmic Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) — Goodfellow et al., 2014

FGSM is a **single-step, white-box** attack that linearizes the loss function around the current input.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Compute the gradient of the loss with respect to the input: $\nabla_x L(\theta, x, y)$.
2. Take the *sign* of this gradient to obtain the direction of steepest ascent in loss space.
3. Step a fixed distance $\epsilon$ in that direction.
4. Clip the result to ensure the perturbation stays within the $\ell_\infty$ budget.

**The closed-form perturbation:**

$$\delta_{FGSM} = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}\left( \nabla_x L(\theta, x, y) \right)$$

**The adversarial example generation:**

$$x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}\left( \nabla_x L(\theta, x, y) \right)$$

**Why It Works (Intuition):** Modern deep networks are *locally linear* in high-dimensional input space. A small, directionally optimized perturbation accumulates across all input dimensions, crossing the decision boundary.

### 2.2.2 Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) — Madry et al., 2018

PGD is the **multi-step, iterative generalization of FGSM** and is widely considered the *strongest first-order attack*. It repeatedly applies FGSM-style updates and projects the result back onto the allowed perturbation ball $S$.

$$x^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{x+S}\left( x^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}\left( \nabla_x L(\theta, x^{(t)}, y) \right) \right)$$

where:
- $\alpha$ is the per-step learning rate, typically $\alpha = \epsilon / T$ with $T$ iterations.
- $\Pi_{x+S}$ is the **projection operator** back onto the $\ell_p$ ball of radius $\epsilon$ around the clean input $x$.

### 2.2.3 Carlini & Wagner (C&W) Attack

C&W is the **optimization-based gold standard** for evaluation. It reformulates the attack as a constrained optimization problem using a change of variables to ensure box constraints.

$$\underset{\delta}{\min} \,\, \| \delta \|_p + c \cdot f(x + \delta, y)$$

where $f$ is an *adversarial loss* designed to push classification away from $y$:

$$f(x', y) = \max \left( \max_{i \neq y} Z(x')_i - Z(x')_y, \, -\kappa \right)$$

Here, $Z(x')_i$ is the logit output for class $i$, and $\kappa$ is the **confidence margin** (higher $\kappa$ = more confident misclassification).

### 2.2.4 Jacobian-based Saliency Map Attack (JSMA)

A *targeted* attack that modifies as few pixels as possible ($\ell_0$ constrained). It builds a saliency map based on the forward derivative:

$$S(x, y^*)[i] = 
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } \frac{\partial Z_{y^*}}{\partial x_i} < 0 \text{ or } \sum_{j \neq y^*} \frac{\partial Z_j}{\partial x_i} > 0 \\
\left( \frac{\partial Z_{y^*}}{\partial x_i} \right) \left\vert \sum_{j \neq y^*} \frac{\partial Z_j}{\partial x_i} \right\vert, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

Pixels with high saliency are perturbed to increase the target class probability $Z_{y^*}$.

## 2.3 Adversarial Defenses — The Three Pillars

### 2.3.1 Adversarial Training (The Gold Standard)

Reformulate empirical risk minimization as a **min-max (saddle-point) problem**:

$$\min_{\theta} \,\, \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in S} L(\theta, x + \delta, y) \right]$$

- **Inner maximization:** Generate the worst-case adversarial example within budget $S$.
- **Outer minimization:** Update model weights to minimize loss on those adversarial examples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is hard:** The inner problem is non-convex. It is typically solved approximately using PGD. The whole procedure is computationally expensive — often 7×–10× slower than standard training.

### 2.3.2 Defensive Distillation

Train a *student* network on the **soft probability outputs** of a *teacher* network. The smoother probability surface makes gradients less exploitable by FGSM/PGD. However, Carlini & Wagner (2017) demonstrated that C&W attacks bypass distillation.

### 2.3.3 Input Preprocessing / Detection

- **Feature squeezing** – Reduce color bit depth or apply spatial smoothing.
- **MagNet** – Use an autoencoder to detect out-of-distribution adversarial inputs.
- **Randomized smoothing** – Add Gaussian noise and certify robustness via the Neyman-Pearson lemma.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a *board-ready cheat sheet*. Memorize each row. Examiners frequently ask direct formula questions worth 2–3 marks.

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Attack / Defense |
|--------|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| $x_{adv}$ | Adversarial Example | $x + \delta$ with $\|\delta\|_p \leq \epsilon$ | Universal |
| $\delta$ | Perturbation Vector | $\epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L)$ | FGSM |
| $\ell_\infty$ | Max-Norm Constraint | $\max_i \, \vert x_i - x_i^{adv} \vert \leq \epsilon$ | FGSM / PGD |
| $\ell_2$ | Euclidean Constraint | $\sqrt{\sum_i (x_i - x_i^{adv})^2} \leq \epsilon$ | C&W |
| $\ell_0$ | Pixel Count | $\sum_i \mathbf{1}[x_i \neq x_i^{adv}] \leq k$ | JSMA |
| PGD Update | Iterative FGSM | $x^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{x+S}(x^{(t)} + \alpha \, \text{sign}(\nabla_x L))$ | PGD |
| C&W Loss | Confidence Margin | $\max(\max_{i \neq y} Z_i - Z_y, -\kappa)$ | C&W |
| Adv. Training | Min-Max Saddle Point | $\min_\theta \mathbb{E}[\max_{\delta \in S} L(\theta, x+\delta, y)]$ | Defense |
| Certified Radius | Provable Robustness | $R = \frac{\sigma}{2}(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B))$ | Randomized Smoothing |
| Backdoor Trigger | Poisoned Pattern | $x_{poisoned} = x \oplus T$ where $T$ = trigger | Backdoor Attack |

> [!TIP]
> **Unit Note:** Perturbations are dimensionless (ratios of pixel intensities). In KTU numerical questions, $\epsilon$ is typically given in the range $[0.01, 0.3]$ for normalized images in $[0, 1]$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Physical adversarial patches on stop signs (Eykholt et al., 2018 — *Robust Physical-World Attacks*).
- **Medical AI:** FGSM attacks on skin lesion classifiers can flip melanoma → benign with 5% pixel changes.
- **Biometric Authentication:** Adversarial spectacles bypass facial recognition.
- **NLP/LLMs:** TextFooler, BERT-Attack — synonym-substitution attacks on transformer models.
- **Malware Detection:** Function-preserving mutations evade ML-based antivirus engines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of FGSM Perturbation

We derive the FGSM perturbation starting from the first-order Taylor expansion of the loss function.

### Step 1: Linearize the Loss

Consider a small perturbation $\delta$ added to input $x$. Using the first-order Taylor expansion around $x$:

$$L(\theta, x + \delta, y) \approx L(\theta, x, y) + \nabla_x L(\theta, x, y)^T \delta$$

The gradient with respect to the input, $\nabla_x L$, is a vector of partial derivatives indicating how the loss changes for each input feature.

### Step 2: Maximize the Linear Approximation

To make the loss as large as possible (worst-case adversary), we want to choose $\delta$ that maximizes $\nabla_x L^T \delta$. This is a classical problem from linear algebra optimization.

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, for any vector $v$:

$$v^T \delta \leq \|v\|_2 \cdot \|\delta\|_2$$

Equality is achieved when $\delta$ points in the same direction as $v$:

$$\delta^* = \epsilon \cdot \frac{\nabla_x L}{\|\nabla_x L\|_2}$$

This is the **direction of steepest ascent in $\ell_2$ norm**.

### Step 3: Apply the $\ell_\infty$ Constraint

The FGSM attack instead uses the $\ell_\infty$ norm. Under this constraint, the optimal $\delta$ becomes:

$$\delta^* = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L)$$

The intuition: under $\ell_\infty$, every pixel can be perturbed by *at most* $\epsilon$. To maximally increase the loss, we should saturate this budget on every pixel whose gradient sign is positive (and subtract on those with negative gradient).

### Step 4: Final FGSM Equation

Combining the perturbation with the original input:

$$x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L(\theta, x, y))$$

To ensure pixel values stay valid in $[0, 1]$, an additional **clipping operator** is applied:

$$x_{adv} = \text{clip}(x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L(\theta, x, y)), \, 0, \, 1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\text{sign}$ and not the raw gradient?** The sign function uses *unit* perturbation in every dimension. This is robust to gradient scale variations and ensures uniform perturbation budget usage — the *defining* feature of FGSM that distinguishes it from raw gradient ascent.

## 3.2 Full Derivation of PGD Iteration

PGD extends FGSM iteratively. We derive the iteration rule from a constrained optimization perspective.

### Step 1: Setup the Constrained Maximization

The inner problem of the saddle-point formulation is:

$$\delta^* = \underset{\delta \in S}{\arg\max} \,\, L(\theta, x + \delta, y)$$

where $S = \{ \delta : \|\delta\|_\infty \leq \epsilon \}$.

### Step 2: Apply Gradient Ascent

At iteration $t$, perform a gradient ascent step:

$$\delta^{(t+1)} = \delta^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \nabla_\delta L(\theta, x + \delta^{(t)}, y)$$

Using the chain rule, $\nabla_\delta L = \nabla_x L$, and applying the $\ell_\infty$ step:

$$\delta^{(t+1)} = \delta^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L(\theta, x + \delta^{(t)}, y))$$

### Step 3: Project Back to the Feasible Set

The intermediate $\delta^{(t+1)}$ may violate the $\ell_\infty$ constraint. Apply the projection:

$$\delta^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{S}(\delta^{(t+1)})$$

For $\ell_\infty$, the projection simply clips each component:

$$\delta_i^{(t+1)} = \max(-\epsilon, \min(\epsilon, \delta_i^{(t+1)}))$$

### Step 4: Combine into Final PGD Rule

Re-expressing in terms of the input directly:

$$x^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{x+S}\left( x^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L(\theta, x^{(t)}, y)) \right)$$

with initialization $x^{(0)} = x$ and total iterations $T \approx 40$ typically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference from FGSM:** FGSM uses $T = 1$ with a large $\epsilon$. PGD uses many smaller steps ($T \approx 40$, $\alpha = \epsilon/10$). Mathematically, PGD converges to a *local maximum* of the inner problem, making it strictly stronger than FGSM.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack Comparison

The following table consolidates the mathematical and operational properties of all major attacks for KTU exam comparison questions.

| Property | FGSM | PGD | C&W | JSMA |
|----------|------|-----|-----|------|
| **Type** | Single-step | Iterative | Optimization-based | Saliency-based |
| **Norm** | $\ell_\infty$ | $\ell_\infty$ (typically) | $\ell_0, \ell_2, \ell_\infty$ | $\ell_0$ |
| **Knowledge** | White-box | White-box | White-box | White-box |
| **Targeted?** | Untargeted (extensible) | Both | Both | Targeted |
| **Strength** | Weak/Fast | Strong (1st-order optimal) | Strongest (evades distillation) | Sparse perturbations |
| **Cost** | 1 gradient | $T$ gradients | 100s of iterations | 1 forward pass |
| **Bypasses Distillation?** | No | No | **Yes** (C&W 2017) | N/A |
| **Use Case** | Quick evaluation | Robustness training | Strongest benchmark | Minimal pixel change |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board Style)

> **Problem:** A neural network classifies images of digits. The loss for a particular input $x = [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]^T$ with true label $y = 3$ is $L = 0.8$. The gradient of the loss with respect to $x$ is $\nabla_x L = [0.02, -0.05, 0.01]^T$. Compute the FGSM adversarial example with $\epsilon = 0.1$.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1:** Compute the sign of the gradient.

$$\text{sign}(\nabla_x L) = \text{sign}([0.02, -0.05, 0.01]^T) = [1, -1, 1]^T$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $\epsilon$ to get the perturbation.

$$\delta = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L) = 0.1 \cdot [1, -1, 1]^T = [0.1, -0.1, 0.1]^T$$

**Step 3:** Add the perturbation to the original input.

$$x_{adv} = x + \delta = [0.1 + 0.1, \, 0.5 - 0.1, \, 0.9 + 0.1]^T = [0.2, \, 0.4, \, 1.0]^T$$

**Step 4:** Apply clipping to keep values in $[0, 1]$.

Since $0.2, 0.4, 1.0$ are all in $[0, 1]$, no clipping is needed.

$$\boxed{x_{adv} = [0.2, \, 0.4, \, 1.0]^T}$$

**Interpretation:** A maximum per-pixel change of $0.1$ was applied in the direction that maximally increases the loss, successfully shifting the input towards misclassification.

## 3.5 Saddle-Point Solution of Adversarial Training

Adversarial training requires solving the saddle-point problem:

$$\min_{\theta} \,\, \rho(\theta), \quad \text{where} \quad \rho(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in S} L(\theta, x + \delta, y) \right]$$

**Step 1:** Inner maximization is solved approximately by $K$ steps of PGD, yielding $\delta_{adv}^{(K)}$.

**Step 2:** Outer minimization is performed by standard SGD on the adversarial loss:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta^{(t)}, x + \delta_{adv}^{(K)}, y)$$

**Step 3:** Iterate until convergence. The model converges to a *robust* local minimum where performance on clean and adversarial inputs is jointly optimized.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Adversarial training sacrifices clean accuracy (typically 10–15% drop on standard datasets like CIFAR-10). Examiners love to ask: *"Why does adversarial training reduce clean accuracy?"* — answer: robust decision boundaries are smoother and less expressive, which is the inherent *robustness–accuracy trade-off*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Adversarial Attack Pipeline

This block diagram illustrates the complete end-to-end flow of an adversarial attack on a deployed ML system, from the perspective of the attacker's interaction with the model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clean Input x] --> B[Adversary Module]
    B --> C{Threat Model?}
    C -->|White-Box| D[Compute Gradient of Loss wrt x]
    C -->|Black-Box| E[Query Model API for Labels]
    D --> F[Generate Perturbation delta]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Perturbation: x_adv = x + delta]
    G --> H[Clip to Perturbation Budget epsilon]
    H --> I[Target ML Model]
    I --> J[Predicted Label y_hat]
    J --> K{Attack Success?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Compromised Output]
    K -->|No| M[Defender Triggers]
    M --> N[Adversarial Detector]
    N --> O{Reject Input?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Quarantine / Log]
    O -->|No| Q[Adversarial Training Retrain]
```

## 4.2 Attack Taxonomy — Comprehensive Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Adversarial Attacks] --> B[Inference Time]
    A --> C[Training Time]
    A --> D[Model Extraction]
    B --> B1[Evasion]
    B --> B2[Model Inversion]
    B1 --> B1a[Gradient Based: FGSM]
    B1 --> B1b[Iterative: PGD]
    B1 --> B1c[Optimization: CW]
    B1 --> B1d[Saliency: JSMA]
    C --> C1[Poisoning]
    C --> C2[Backdoor]
    C1 --> C1a[Label Flipping]
    C1 --> C1b[Clean Label]
    C2 --> C2a[Visible Trigger]
    C2 --> C2b[Hidden Trigger]
    D --> D1[Model Stealing]
    D --> D2[Membership Inference]
```

## 4.3 Defense Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Deployed ML Model] --> B{Defense Strategy}
    B --> C[Proactive: Adversarial Training]
    B --> D[Reactive: Input Preprocessing]
    B --> E[Certifiable: Randomized Smoothing]
    C --> C1[Min-Max Optimization]
    C --> C2[PGD-Based Augmentation]
    D --> D1[Feature Squeezing]
    D --> D2[MagNet Autoencoder]
    D --> D3[Defensive Distillation]
    E --> E1[Gaussian Noise Addition]
    E --> E2[Provable Radius R Computation]
    C1 --> F[Robust Model]
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G{Adversarial Test Suite}
    G -->|Pass| H[Deployed Securely]
    G -->|Fail| I[Retrain and Iterate]
    I --> A
```

## 4.4 Adversarial Example Generation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the mathematical flow of FGSM/PGD into functional blocks, useful for KTU questions that ask for *diagrammatic representation of attack construction*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image x in 0 to 1] --> B[Forward Pass through Model]
    B --> C[Loss Computation L theta x y]
    C --> D[Backpropagation]
    D --> E[Gradient wrt Input nabla x L]
    E --> F{Single Step or Iterative?}
    F -->|FGSM| G[Sign Operation]
    F -->|PGD| H[Multiple Iterations]
    G --> I[Scale by epsilon]
    H --> J[Project onto Lp Ball]
    J --> I
    I --> K[Add to Original Input]
    K --> L[Clip to Valid Range]
    L --> M[Adversarial Example x_adv]
    M --> N[Query Target Model]
    N --> O[Evaluate Attack Success Rate]
```
