# Database Disclosure — Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantifying Disclosure Risk via ALE

The **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** is the cornerstone metric for assessing the financial impact of database disclosure incidents. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define Asset Value (AV)

Let $AV$ represent the total monetary value of the database asset. For example, a customer database containing 1 million records valued at ₹500 per record.

$$AV = N \times V_{\text{record}}$$

where $N$ is the number of records and $V_{\text{record}}$ is the per-record value.

Substituting the example values:

$$AV = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 500 = \text{₹ } 500{,}000{,}000$$

### Step 2 — Define the Exposure Factor (EF)

The **Exposure Factor** $EF \in [0, 1]$ represents the *fraction of asset value lost* in a single disclosure event. A full database leak has $EF = 1.0$; a partial leak of 10% of records has $EF = 0.10$.

### Step 3 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$\text{SLE} = AV \times EF$$

For a full disclosure with $EF = 1.0$:

$$\text{SLE} = 500{,}000{,}000 \times 1.0 = \text{₹ } 500{,}000{,}000$$

### Step 4 — Estimate Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)

The **ARO** is determined empirically from historical incident data. Suppose the organization experiences, on average, **0.3 disclosure incidents per year** (one every ~3.3 years).

### Step 5 — Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

$$\text{ALE} = 500{,}000{,}000 \times 0.3 = \text{₹ } 150{,}000{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The organization should ideally spend *up to ₹15 crore annually* on database disclosure controls (encryption, DLP, training, monitoring) to maintain a positive return on security investment. This is the **ALE-based security budgeting principle**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Information-Theoretic Disclosure Measurement

Shannon's information theory provides a **rigorous mathematical foundation** for measuring how much information is disclosed in a breach.

### Step 1 — Define Entropy of the Original Database

Let the database contain $n$ distinct records, each with probability $p(x_i)$ of being the target of disclosure.

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

For a uniform distribution across 1 million records:

$$p(x_i) = \frac{1}{1{,}000{,}000}$$

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{1{,}000{,}000} \frac{1}{10^6} \log_2 \frac{1}{10^6}$$

$$H(X) = -\log_2 \frac{1}{10^6} = \log_2 10^6 \approx 19.93 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Define Conditional Entropy After Disclosure

After an attacker observes $Y$ (e.g., a query response), the conditional entropy $H(X \vert Y)$ represents the residual uncertainty.

$$H(X \vert Y) = -\sum_{y \in Y} p(y) \sum_{x \in X} p(x \vert y) \log_2 p(x \vert y)$$

### Step 3 — Compute Mutual Information (Information Leakage)

$$I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$$

If the attacker confirms a specific record (e.g., "user 42's password is 'qwerty'"), then $H(X \vert Y) = 0$, and:

$$I(X;Y) = 19.93 - 0 = 19.93 \text{ bits}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This means that **perfect disclosure** of a single record from a uniformly distributed 1-million-record database leaks approximately **20 bits of information**. Information-theoretic metrics like this are now part of the KTU 2024 advanced security analytics module.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hash Collision Probability for Stolen Hash Databases

When password databases are disclosed, attackers often obtain only **hashed passwords**. The probability that two passwords share the same hash (collision) is given by the **Birthday Paradox approximation**.

### Step 1 — Define Parameters

Let $k$ = hash bit-length (e.g., SHA-256 has $k = 256$ bits), and $n$ = number of hashed values.

### Step 2 — Apply Birthday Bound Approximation

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^k)}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation for Common Cases

For $n = 100{,}000$ hashes and $k = 128$ (MD5):

$$P \approx 1 - e^{-(10^5)^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{128})}$$

$$P \approx 1 - e^{-10^{10} / 6.8 \times 10^{38}}$$

$$P \approx 0 \text{ (negligibly small)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For strong hashes (SHA-256), collisions are computationally infeasible, but **precomputed rainbow tables** for weak hashes (MD5, SHA-1) can reverse common passwords, defeating the hash protection after disclosure.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Disclosure Attack Vector Catalogue

| Attack Vector | Disclosure Mechanism | Detection Difficulty | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SQL Injection** | Direct UNION-based data extraction | Low (signatures exist) | Parameterized queries, WAF |
| **Blind SQL Injection** | Boolean/time-based inference | High | Input validation, least privilege |
| **Backup Theft** | Off-site unencrypted backups | Very High (often unnoticed) | AES-256 encryption, secure vaults |
| **Insider Threat** | Authorized user copies data | Very High | DLP, audit logging, separation of duties |
| **Misconfigured Cloud Storage** | Public S3/Azure Blob exposure | Low (cloud scanners detect) | Default-deny IAM policies |
| **Verbose Error Messages** | Stack traces reveal schema | Low | Custom error handlers |
| **DNS Exfiltration** | Data encoded in DNS queries | High | DNS monitoring, egress filtering |
| **Side-Channel Attacks** | Timing/caching leaks | Very High | Constant-time algorithms |

# Database Disclosure — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define Database Disclosure. List any FOUR causes of database disclosure.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Database Disclosure is the unauthorized exposure, leakage, or revelation of sensitive, confidential, or personally identifiable information stored within a database system to entities lacking legitimate access rights.

**Four Causes:**

1. **SQL Injection Attacks** — Malicious SQL statements are injected into application input fields, allowing attackers to extract data directly from database tables.
2. **Insider Threats** — Authorized database administrators or employees with legitimate access deliberately or accidentally leak sensitive records.
3. **Misconfigured Cloud Storage** — Improperly secured cloud databases (e.g., public S3 buckets, open MongoDB instances) expose data to the public internet.
4. **Insecure Backup Storage** — Unencrypted backup tapes or drives lost or stolen from off-site storage locations.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always give a *one-line definition* followed by a *numbered list* with brief one-line explanations of each cause. Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 marks per cause with explanation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Direct Disclosure and Indirect Disclosure with one example each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Direct Disclosure | Indirect Disclosure |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Raw data exposed in plain form | Data inferred through patterns/errors |
| **Mechanism** | Direct query returns the data | Boolean/timing responses reveal data |
| **Example** | `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --` returns actual credentials | `' AND SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1)='5' --` returns TRUE/FALSE, revealing version character by character |
| **Detectability** | Easier (logs visible query) | Harder (queries appear normal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Use a *comparison table* in your answer. This visually segregates your points and helps the evaluator award partial marks efficiently.

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **phases of a Database Disclosure attack** with relevant techniques used in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **technical and administrative controls** that an organization can implement to prevent database disclosure. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

Database Disclosure attacks typically follow a structured **five-phase methodology**, often referred to as the *Database Disclosure Kill Chain*:

**Phase 1 — Reconnaissance and Fingerprinting:**
The attacker identifies the DBMS vendor and version by exploiting verbose error messages, HTTP headers, or default ports. Tools like `sqlmap`, `Nmap`, and `Havij` automate this. The goal is to construct DBMS-specific exploit payloads.

**Phase 2 — Schema Enumeration:**
Once the DBMS is identified, the attacker queries system tables such as `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES`, `pg_catalog`, or `sys.tables` to enumerate table names, column names, and data types. For instance, the payload `' UNION SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public' --` reveals all PostgreSQL table names.

**Phase 3 — Privilege Escalation:**
The attacker attempts to elevate privileges from the application's restricted database user to a DBA-level account. Techniques include exploiting SQL functions, misconfigured roles, and stored procedure injection.

**Phase 4 — Data Exfiltration:**
Sensitive records are extracted in bulk — passwords, PII, credit card numbers, medical records. Methods include `UNION SELECT` attacks, stacked queries, out-of-band DNS tunneling, and HTTP-based chunked transfers.

**Phase 5 — Cover Tracks and Persistence:**
The attacker deletes or modifies audit logs, plants web shells, creates rogue stored procedures, or establishes alternative access channels for future re-entry.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Reward:** Diagrams (even hand-drawn in the answer script) of the kill chain fetch an additional 1–2 marks. Always draw a *flowchart of the 5 phases*.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

Database Disclosure prevention requires a **defense-in-depth** strategy combining technical and administrative controls.

**Technical Controls:**

1. **Encryption at Rest and in Transit:** AES-256 encryption for stored data; TLS 1.3 for data in transit.
2. **Parameterized Queries and Stored Procedures:** Eliminates SQL injection by separating code from data.
3. **Principle of Least Privilege:** Application database users should have only the minimum required permissions — no `SELECT *` on user tables.
4. **Database Activity Monitoring (DAM):** Real-time monitoring of all SQL queries with anomaly detection.
5. **Data Masking and Tokenization:** Replace sensitive fields (e.g., credit card numbers) with masked or tokenized values in non-production environments.
6. **Web Application Firewalls (WAF):** Filter malicious SQL payloads before they reach the database.
7. **Database Firewalls:** Proxy-based filtering of database traffic at the protocol level.

**Administrative Controls:**

1. **Access Control Policies:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with periodic access reviews.
2. **Security Awareness Training:** Educate developers, DBAs, and end-users on disclosure risks.
3. **Incident Response Plans:** Documented procedures for detection, containment, and notification.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Adherence to IT Act 2000, GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS standards.
5. **Vendor Risk Management:** Third-party database providers must comply with security SLAs.
6. **Data Classification:** Tag data as Public, Internal, Confidential, or Restricted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CIA Triad and Parkerian Hexad**. How does Database Disclosure violate each attribute? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Information-Theoretic approach to measuring Database Disclosure**. Derive the entropy and mutual information equations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

Refer to Section 2.2 of these notes for the full table. The student should present:

- A diagram of the CIA Triad (3 circles).
- A diagram of the Parkerian Hexad (6 attributes).
- A 6-row table mapping each attribute to how Database Disclosure violates it.

**Sample Answer Excerpt:**

> *Confidentiality is directly violated because unauthorized parties gain access to protected records. Integrity is violated when disclosure is followed by tampering. Authenticity is undermined when leaked data is republished under false attribution. Utility decreases as leaked credit card numbers must be cancelled. Possession is lost when physical backup media are stolen. Availability is affected when exfiltration precedes a denial-of-service attack.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students list only the CIA triad and skip the Parkerian Hexad. Under the KTU 2024 NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, the Parkerian Hexad carries **explicit 2-mark weightage**. Failing to mention it will cap your score at 5/7 on this sub-part.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. The student should:

1. Define Shannon entropy $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$.
2. Compute entropy for a uniformly distributed database of $n$ records: $H(X) = \log_2 n$ bits.
3. Define conditional entropy $H(X \vert Y)$ representing residual uncertainty after disclosure.
4. Derive mutual information $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ as the measure of disclosed information.
5. Apply to a numerical example: a 1-million-record database has $H(X) \approx 19.93$ bits; full disclosure of one record leaks 19.93 bits.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Database Disclosure Questions:**
> 1. **Students forget to name the violated security principle** (Confidentiality, or Parkerian Hexad attribute) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **They confuse SQL Injection with Database Disclosure** — SQL Injection is a *vector*; Database Disclosure is the *outcome*. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for treating them as synonyms.
> 3. **They omit quantitative examples** (e.g., ALE calculation, entropy in bits). KTU 2024 values numerical literacy.
> 4. **They skip diagrams** in 14-mark answers. Even a basic flowchart of the 5-phase attack earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 5. **They write prevention measures without categorization** (technical vs administrative). Always use sub-headings.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** Database Disclosure = unauthorized exposure of database contents to parties without legitimate access.
- **Violated Principle:** Primarily **Confidentiality** in CIA; extends to multiple attributes in the **Parkerian Hexad**.
- **Five Phases of Attack:** Reconnaissance → Schema Enumeration → Privilege Escalation → Data Exfiltration → Cover Tracks.
- **Key Metrics:** ALE = SLE × ARO; SLE = AV × EF. Entropy $H(X) = \log_2 n$ for uniform distribution.
- **Top Causes:** SQL Injection, insider threats, cloud misconfiguration, insecure backups, verbose errors.
- **Defense-in-Depth Layers:** Encryption, parameterized queries, least privilege, DAM, WAF, audit logging.
- **Information Theoretic View:** Mutual information $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ quantifies leakage in bits.
- **Real-World Relevance:** GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, IT Act 2000 mandate disclosure-prevention controls.
- **Examiner Heuristics:** Always (1) state violated principle, (2) use phased explanation, (3) categorize controls as technical/administrative, (4) include a diagram, (5) provide numerical examples where possible.
- **Parkerian Hexad Expansion:** Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability + Authenticity + Utility + Possession — KTU 2024 expects all six attributes mentioned in advanced answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confidentiality Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Confidentiality Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Confidentiality Policy** is a formal, mathematically-grounded set of rules, constraints, and access-control specifications that govern how classified, sensitive, or restricted information may be *accessed, observed, propagated, derived, and disclosed* among subjects (active entities such as users, processes, or programs) and objects (passive containers of information such as files, records, database tables, or memory segments) within a trusted computing system.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (drawing from Bishop's *Computer Security: Art and Science* and Gollmann's *Computer Security*), a confidentiality policy is the **abstract authorization framework** that constrains the system's behaviour so that information does not flow to principals (subjects) who are not authorized to receive it. The policy is expressed independently of any specific mechanism; the mechanisms (access control lists, capabilities, reference monitors) merely *enforce* the policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Often Tested in KTU):**
> - **Confidentiality Policy** → *Anti-disclosure* → controls **read** and **propagation** of sensitive data.
> - **Integrity Policy** → *Anti-modification* → controls **write** and **alteration** of trusted data.
> These are **dual concerns** and Biba's integrity model is the historical mirror image of Bell–LaPadula.

## 1.2 Components of a Confidentiality Policy

Every formal confidentiality policy, regardless of the model flavour (military, commercial, lattice-based, compartmentalized), must define the following five components:

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Subjects (S)** | Active entities that request access — users, processes, programs. |
| **Objects (O)** | Passive entities that hold information — files, directories, records, memory pages. |
| **Access Modes (A)** | The set of permitted operations (read, write, append, execute, etc.). |
| **Security Labels / Clearance Levels (L)** | Ordered sensitivity markers (e.g., Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret). |
| **Authorization Rules (R)** | Boolean predicates that decide, for any (s, o, a) triple, whether access is permitted. |

> [!NOTE]
> A confidentiality policy is **complete** only when all five components are explicitly enumerated. KTU examiners often award partial credit for identifying the *components* even when the *rules* are forgotten.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classified Office Building" 🏢

Imagine a four-storey government office building:

- **Floor 0** = *Unclassified* (public lobby, anyone may enter)
- **Floor 1** = *Confidential* (badge required)
- **Floor 2** = *Secret* (special clearance, biometric check)
- **Floor 3** = *Top Secret* (vault room, dual control, two-person integrity)

**Rules of the building:**
1. You may walk *down* the staircase to pick up a coffee, but you may not enter a *higher* floor than your badge allows (**No Read-Up / Simple Security Property**).
2. Once you are cleared for Floor 3, you may not photocopy a Top Secret document and leave it on Floor 1's desk, because that would leak sensitive data downward (**No Write-Down / *-Property**).
3. Your clearance cannot suddenly be *upgraded* or *downgraded* while you are inside the building (**Tranquility Principle**).
4. The building is divided into wings (compartments): *Nuclear*, *Cryptographic*, *Intelligence*. Even two Top Secret–cleared officers cannot share files across wings without explicit compartment membership (**Lattice / Categories**).

This building is precisely the **Bell–LaPadula (BLP) confidentiality policy model** formalized in 1973 by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula for the U.S. Department of Defense.

## 1.4 Why Confidentiality Policies Matter — Engineering Motivation

Confidentiality policies are foundational to:

- **Military / Defence Command-and-Control Systems** (the historical motivator of BLP).
- **Healthcare Information Systems** governed by *HIPAA* (U.S.), *DISHA* / *DPDP Act 2023* (India), and *GDPR* (EU).
- **Financial Systems** (PCI-DSS, SOX compliance) preventing insider data leakage.
- **Cloud Multi-Tenant Platforms** ensuring one tenant's data is invisible to another.
- **Intellectual Property Repositories** (R&D labs, semiconductor design houses) preventing trade-secret exfiltration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Confidentiality Triad (CIA Triad, KTU High-Yield):** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. Confidentiality policies specifically safeguard the **C** in the CIA triad.

## 1.5 High-Level Classification of Confidentiality Policies

Confidentiality policies are broadly classified into three families:

1. **Military / Government Security Policies** — hierarchical levels + compartments (e.g., BLP, lattice-based).
2. **Commercial / Civilian Policies** — broader, more flexible, less rigid, often based on roles and need-to-know (e.g., Clark–Wilson hybrid, Chinese Wall model).
3. **Privacy / Data-Protection Policies** — regulatory frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA) translated into technical rules.

This Module 4 focuses on **(1) — Military/Government** policies, with **Bell–LaPadula** as the flagship model.

---

## 1.6 Quick Self-Test (Concept Anchors)

| Anchor Question | One-Line Answer |
|---|---|
| What does a confidentiality policy control? | *Disclosure* (read / observe / infer) of sensitive information. |
| What is the *Simple Security Property*? | No subject may read an object at a higher level (**no read up**). |
| What is the **\*-property**? | No subject may write to an object at a lower level (**no write down**). |
| What is *tranquility*? | The security labels of subjects/objects do not change *during* a system operation. |
| What is a *lattice*? | A partially ordered set in which every pair of elements has a unique least upper bound and greatest lower bound. |
| What is a *covert channel*? | An unintended communication path that violates the policy. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bell–LaPadula (BLP) Model — Architectural Foundation

The BLP model is a **state-machine confidentiality model** introduced in 1973. The system is modelled as a finite-state machine whose state transitions are governed by security rules. A state is "secure" if and only if it satisfies all BLP properties.

### 2.1.1 The State of the System

A state $v \in V$ of the system is the 4-tuple:

$$
v = (b, M, f, H)
$$

where:

- $b \subseteq S \times O \times A$ — the set of *current accesses* (which subject has what kind of access to which object).
- $M$ — the *access permission matrix*, $M[s, o] \subseteq A$ specifies the access modes *permitted* for subject $s$ on object $o$.
- $f$ — the *level function*, $f = (f_S, f_O, f_C)$:
  - $f_S : S \rightarrow L$ assigns each subject a **security clearance**.
  - $f_O : O \rightarrow L$ assigns each object a **security classification**.
  - $f_C : S \rightarrow L$ assigns each subject a **current security level** (dynamic, can be lowered for *trusted subjects*).
- $H$ — the *history* (or *hierarchy of objects*), e.g., the tree of file-system directories.

> [!NOTE]
> $f_S(s) \geq f_C(s)$ always holds. The current level $f_C$ is what the subject is *currently* operating at; the clearance $f_S$ is the *maximum* level the subject is *cleared* for. Trusted subjects can write down by lowering $f_C$.

### 2.1.2 The Dominance Relation $\geq$

Given two security levels $L_1, L_2$, we say $L_1$ **dominates** $L_2$ (denoted $L_1 \geq L_2$) iff:

1. The hierarchical component of $L_1$ is $\geq$ that of $L_2$, **and**
2. The category set of $L_1$ is a **superset** of that of $L_2$.

Formally, if $L_i = (H_i, C_i)$ with $H_i$ = hierarchy rank and $C_i$ = set of compartments, then:

$$
L_1 \geq L_2 \iff H_1 \geq H_2 \;\land\; C_2 \subseteq C_1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dominance relation defines a **partial order** (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive). The set of all security levels under $\geq$ forms a **lattice** (see §2.3).

### 2.1.3 The Three BLP Security Properties

Let $s \in S$, $o \in O$. The state $v$ satisfies BLP iff:

**Property 1 — Simple Security Property (ss-property):**

$$
\forall (s, o, r) \in b.\;\;\; f_S(s) \geq f_O(o)
$$

A subject may *read* an object only if the subject's clearance dominates the object's classification. **"No Read Up" (NRU).**

**Property 2 — *-Property (Star Property):**

$$
\forall (s, o, w) \in b.\;\;\; f_C(s) \leq f_O(o)
$$

A subject may *write* an object only if the subject's *current* level is dominated by the object's classification. **"No Write Down" (NWD).**

**Property 3 — Discretionary Security Property (ds-property):**

$$
\forall (s, o, a) \in b.\;\;\; a \in M[s, o]
$$

Every current access must be one that the access matrix $M$ explicitly permits. This adds a discretionary-access-control (DAC) layer atop the mandatory rules.

### 2.1.4 Tranquility

The **tranquility principle** states that security labels do not change during a system operation. Two variants:

- **Strong Tranquility:** Labels of *all* subjects and objects are *fixed* for the lifetime of the system.
- **Weak Tranquility:** Labels are *fixed* during any single operation (they may change between operations, but not in the middle of one).

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: If strong tranquility is dropped, a subject's level can be *changed* to permit a violating operation in the same state transition. Most production systems use *weak* tranquility and rely on transaction-level atomicity to prevent this.

## 2.2 The Basic Security Theorem (BST) — The Crown Jewel

The **Basic Security Theorem** of BLP states:

> A system is *secure* (i.e., starts in a secure state and every state transition leads to a secure state) **if and only if** the initial state $v_0$ satisfies the ss-property and the *-property, and every state transition function preserves both properties.

Equivalently: *Secure state + secure transitions ⇒ the system is always secure.*

This is a *reachability theorem* about the state machine. The proof is by induction on the length of state-transition sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BST is the foundation of all formal security proofs in mandatory access control.** It is the direct analogue of the Biba integrity model's *Low-Water-Mark* theorem, and is examinable as a 7- or 14-mark question in KTU.

## 2.3 The Lattice of Security Labels

The set of all security levels forms a **mathematical lattice** under $\geq$ because:

- Any two security levels $L_1, L_2$ have a **unique least upper bound** (lub, or *join*): $L_1 \sqcup L_2 = (\max(H_1, H_2),\, C_1 \cup C_2)$.
- Any two security levels have a **unique greatest lower bound** (glb, or *meet*): $L_1 \sqcap L_2 = (\min(H_1, H_2),\, C_1 \cap C_2)$.

**Why a lattice?** Because the dominance relation must support "information flow upward" — combining two Top Secret compartments (say *Crypto* and *Intel*) gives a higher "combined" level, and combining them with a lower hierarchical rank must yield a *unique* level. The lattice structure guarantees this uniqueness.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question:** *"Is the set of security labels always a lattice?"* — Only if the lattice is **bounded** (has a system-low and system-high element) and the join/meet are *defined* for every pair. Arbitrary sets of categories can fail to be a lattice if not closed under union/intersection.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Property Sheet

| Property | Statement (Informal) | Symbol | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Security | No Read Up | NRU | $f_S(s) \geq f_O(o)$ for any read |
| Star Property | No Write Down | NWD | $f_C(s) \leq f_O(o)$ for any write |
| Dominance | Subject level $\geq$ object level | $\geq$ | $H_1 \geq H_2 \land C_2 \subseteq C_1$ |
| Discretionary | Access must be matrix-permitted | ds | $a \in M[s,o]$ |
| Tranquility | Labels do not change mid-operation | — | $\forall$ op, $f$ is constant |
| Basic Security | Secure state + secure transitions $\Rightarrow$ always secure | BST | Induction on transition path |
| Lattice Join | Least upper bound | $\sqcup$ | $(\max(H_i), \bigcup C_i)$ |
| Lattice Meet | Greatest lower bound | $\sqcap$ | $(\min(H_i), \bigcap C_i)$ |
| No Read Up (NRU) | $\lambda(s) \not< \lambda(o)$ on read | $\not<$ | N/A |
| No Write Down (NWD) | $\lambda_C(s) \not> \lambda(o)$ on write | $\not>$ | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit / Convention:** Security labels are *unitless* ordinals; compartments are *set-valued*. The lattice is finite iff both the hierarchy and the compartment universe are finite.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where BLP Is Actually Used

| Domain | Use of Confidentiality Policy |
|---|---|
| **Trusted OS (SELinux, MULTICS)** | Mandatory access control (MAC) at the kernel level. |
| **Database Systems (Oracle Label Security, MS SQL Row-Level Security)** | Each row tagged with a label; queries filtered by user clearance. |
| **Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI)** for Defence / Govt | Lattice-based access control across classification levels. |
| **Multi-Level Secure (MLS) Networks** | BLP enforced at network gateways (e.g., X.509 attribute certificates carrying clearance labels). |
| **Data Loss Prevention (DLP) Systems** | Implement *-property rules to prevent downgrading labelled data. |

## 2.6 Limitations of BLP (Critically Examinable)

| Limitation | Description |
|---|---|
| **Covert Channels** | Timing, storage, and steganographic channels can leak data even with BLP enforced. |
| **Trusted Subjects** | BLP must permit trusted subjects to write down (e.g., for declassification), breaking the *-property — and these subjects become a vulnerability. |
| **Static Policies** | BLP does not handle *dynamic* policy changes well unless tranquility is dropped. |
| **No Integrity** | BLP only addresses confidentiality; *Biba* is needed for integrity. |
| **Aggregate / Inference Attacks** | Combining non-sensitive data can infer sensitive data — BLP alone does not prevent this. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often pairs a question on **covert channels** with a BLP question. The interplay between *-property enforcement and *covert timing channels* is a classic 14-mark exam theme.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix

> *Because confidentiality policy is a formal-theoretic topic, the "derivation" sections here are dedicated to (a) the formal walkthrough of the Basic Security Theorem proof, (b) lattice operation derivations, and (c) sample security-label computations that students may be asked to perform in KTU 14-mark questions.*

## 3.1 Derivation: The Basic Security Theorem (BST) — Full Proof Walkthrough

### 3.1.1 Setup

Define:

- $V$ = set of all reachable system states.
- $v_0$ = initial state.
- $R$ = set of state-transition functions, $R = \{\rho_1, \rho_2, \ldots, \rho_n\}$.
- $\rho_i : V \times Q \rightarrow V$ where $Q$ = set of all possible requests.

A state $v \in V$ is **secure** iff it satisfies the ss-property and the *-property.

**Theorem (BST).** *If $v_0$ is secure and every $\rho_i$ preserves the security properties, then every reachable state $v \in V$ is secure.*

### 3.1.2 Proof by Induction on the Length of a Transition Sequence

**Base Case (k = 0):** The state is $v_0$, which is secure by hypothesis. The base case holds.

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume every state reachable in $\leq k$ transitions is secure.

**Inductive Step (k + 1):** Let $v_{k+1}$ be reached from $v_k$ by some transition $\rho_i$ in response to a request $q_k$. We must show $v_{k+1}$ is secure.

By the inductive hypothesis, $v_k$ is secure. By the theorem's premise, $\rho_i$ *preserves* the security properties. Therefore $v_{k+1}$ is secure.

Formally, using the principle of mathematical induction:

$$
\forall k \in \mathbb{N}_0.\;\; \text{State reachable in } k \text{ transitions is secure}.
$$

Equivalently, in predicate-logic form:

$$
\forall v.\;\;\big[\,v_0 \text{ secure} \;\land\; \forall \rho \in R.\;\rho \text{ preserves security}\,\big] \;\Rightarrow\; v \text{ is secure}
$$

### 3.1.3 Why the Discretionary Property (ds-property) is Orthogonal

The ds-property is enforced *separately* by the access matrix $M$. BLP's mandatory part (ss + *) operates *alongside* DAC. The proof above is unaffected because $M[s,o]$ is a precondition for the state transition; it is not modified by the security-property check itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examinable corollary:** BST applies to *mandatory* confidentiality. If a discretionary access (e.g., user chmods a file to world-readable) violates the policy, the transition is rejected by $M$ before BLP even runs. This is why BLP is said to be "augmentative" of DAC, not a replacement.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lattice Join and Meet from the Dominance Relation

Given two security levels $L_1 = (H_1, C_1)$ and $L_2 = (H_2, C_2)$, derive the join $L_1 \sqcup L_2$:

**Step 1.** Define dominance: $L_i \geq L_j$ iff $H_i \geq H_j$ and $C_j \subseteq C_i$.

**Step 2.** The *least upper bound* $L$ of $\{L_1, L_2\}$ must satisfy $L \geq L_1$ and $L \geq L_2$ and be minimal.

**Step 3.** $L \geq L_1$ requires $C_1 \subseteq C_L$. $L \geq L_2$ requires $C_2 \subseteq C_L$. Hence $C_L \supseteq C_1 \cup C_2$. Minimality forces $C_L = C_1 \cup C_2$.

**Step 4.** $L \geq L_1$ requires $H_L \geq H_1$. $L \geq L_2$ requires $H_L \geq H_2$. Minimality forces $H_L = \max(H_1, H_2)$.

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$
L_1 \sqcup L_2 = (\max(H_1, H_2),\, C_1 \cup C_2)
$$

By a symmetric argument:

$$
L_1 \sqcap L_2 = (\min(H_1, H_2),\, C_1 \cap C_2)
$$

**Closure property:** If the compartment universe is closed under $\cup$ and $\cap$, and the hierarchy is finite and totally ordered, the resulting set of security levels is a **bounded lattice** with:

- **System Low** $L_{\perp} = (\min H,\, \emptyset)$ — the level every unclassified subject/object carries.
- **System High** $L_{\top} = (\max H,\, \text{all compartments})$ — the absolute top.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examinable Trick:** If the lattice is *not* bounded (no system-low), then BLP fails on empty-compartment subjects. Most operating systems therefore define a "Public" compartment, ensuring closure.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample Security-Label Computations

### Worked Example 3.3.1 — NRU Check

**Given:**
- Subject $s$: clearance $(Secret, \{Crypto, Nuclear\})$.
- Object $o$: classification $(Confidential, \{Crypto\})$.
- Request: $s$ reads $o$.

**Step 1.** Compute $f_S(s) = (S, \{C_r, N\})$, $f_O(o) = (C, \{C_r\})$.

**Step 2.** Check $H_S \geq H_O$: $S > C$ ✓.

**Step 3.** Check $C_O \subseteq C_S$: $\{C_r\} \subseteq \{C_r, N\}$ ✓.

**Step 4.** $f_S(s) \geq f_O(o)$ ✓ → **Read permitted** (NRU satisfied).

### Worked Example 3.3.2 — NWD Check

**Given:** Same as above. Subject $s$ now writes to a *Top Secret* object $o'$ with classification $(TS, \{Crypto, Nuclear, Intel\})$.

**Step 1.** $f_C(s) = (S, \{C_r, N\})$ (current level same as clearance, untrusted subject).

**Step 2.** Check $f_C(s) \leq f_O(o')$: $(S, \{C_r, N\}) \leq (TS, \{C_r, N, I\})$?

**Step 3.** $S \leq TS$ ✓. $\{C_r, N, I\} \subseteq \{C_r, N\}$? **No** — $\{I\} \not\subseteq \{C_r, N\}$.

**Step 4.** Dominance fails → **Write rejected** (NWD violated).

### Worked Example 3.3.3 — Lattice Computation

**Given:** $L_1 = (Secret, \{Crypto, Nuclear\})$, $L_2 = (Top Secret, \{Crypto, Intel\})$.

**Step 1.** Join: $L_1 \sqcup L_2 = (\max(S, TS), \{C_r, N\} \cup \{C_r, I\}) = (TS, \{Crypto, Nuclear, Intel\})$.

**Step 2.** Meet: $L_1 \sqcap L_2 = (\min(S, TS), \{C_r, N\} \cap \{C_r, I\}) = (S, \{Crypto\})$.

**Step 3.** Verification: $L_1 \sqcup L_2$ dominates both $L_1$ and $L_2$ ✓. $L_1 \sqcap L_2$ is dominated by both ✓.

### Worked Example 3.3.4 — Trusted Subject (Declassification)

**Scenario:** Subject $s$ is a *declassification officer* with clearance $(Top Secret, \{Crypto, Nuclear, Intel\})$. She is to *declassify* a Secret document to Confidential for public release.

**Step 1.** Without lowering $f_C$, the *-property blocks the write: $f_C(s) = (TS, \{C_r, N, I\}) \not\leq (Confidential, \{\})$ because $TS \not\leq C$ hierarchically.

**Step 2.** The officer becomes a **trusted subject** and *relaxes* her current level to $f_C(s) = (Confidential, \{\})$.

**Step 3.** Now $f_C(s) \leq f_O(o)$ trivially (both are $C/\emptyset$) → write permitted.

**Step 4.** This is the *only* legitimate way to break the *-property, and it requires the officer to be explicitly designated as *trusted* (auditable, accountable, two-person-controlled in practice).

> [!WARNING]
> Trusted subjects are the single biggest attack surface in BLP. KTU examiners expect students to identify the trade-off — without trusted subjects, declassification is impossible; with them, the system inherits human frailty.

## 3.4 Covert Channels — Quantitative Analysis

A **covert channel** is a communication path not intended for information transfer that nevertheless leaks data. Two primary types:

| Type | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Channel** | Writes to a *shared resource* whose later observation reveals information. | Modulating a file's existence, length, or lock state. |
| **Timing Channel** | Modulates the *timing* of observable events. | CPU-busy signal at bit 1, idle at bit 0. |

### Channel Capacity Formula

The Shannon-capacity bound for a noise-free covert channel is:

$$
C = \log_2 N \;\; \text{bits per symbol}
$$

where $N$ = number of distinguishable symbol values. For a binary timing channel, $N = 2$ and $C = 1$ bit per timing interval.

> [!NOTE]
> BLP cannot prevent covert channels; it can only attempt to *limit* them via system noise injection, random padding, or by auditing trusted subjects. KTU frequently tests this gap.

## 3.5 The Analytical Matrix — BLP Properties × Real-World Scenarios

| Scenario | Property Invoked | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Analyst with Secret clearance reads Top Secret file | ss-property | **Deny** (no read up). |
| Process writes Secret output to a Public log file | *-property | **Deny** (no write down). |
| Two Secret-cleared users in different compartments exchange notes | Lattice dominance | **Deny** (compartments not equal). |
| Auditor downgrades her $f_C$ to log a Secret event publicly | Trusted subject | **Permit** (with audit trail). |
| Trojan encodes Secret data in the *creation timestamp* of Public files | Covert storage channel | **Permitted by BLP** (a known gap). |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> *Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10: the diagrams below use **block-level functional architecture** rather than complex physical drawings, with strict adherence to the Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside node labels).*

## 4.1 Bell–LaPadula State Machine — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef stateNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ruleNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef propNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputNode fill:#f9a825,stroke:#a36800,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px

    A[Initial State v0]:::stateNode
    B[Incoming Request q]:::stateNode
    C[Access Matrix M Check]:::stateNode
    D{Discretionary Permit?}:::stateNode
    E[ss-property No Read Up]:::ruleNode
    F[Star-property No Write Down]:::ruleNode
    G{Dominance Check}:::stateNode
    H{Star Check}:::stateNode
    I[Execute Access]:::propNode
    J[Reject Access]:::ruleNode
    K[Next State v k+1]:::stateNode
    L[Audit Log]:::outputNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> G
    D -- No --> J
    G -- NRU ok --> H
    G -- NRU fail --> J
    H -- NWD ok --> I
    H -- NWD fail --> J
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> A
    I --> L
    J --> L

    E -.enforces.-> G
    F -.enforces.-> H
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue** path is the secure transition; the **red** rules enforce BLP; the **green** node represents successful access.
- Both successful and failed accesses are recorded in the **Audit Log** (gold node) — a non-negotiable requirement for KTU-style designs.

## 4.2 Security Lattice — Block-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef lvlNode fill:#283593,stroke:#1a237e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef catNode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef compNode fill:#00695c,stroke:#004d40,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    L0[System Low Public]:::lvlNode
    L1[Confidential Crypto]:::lvlNode
    L2[Confidential Nuclear]:::lvlNode
    L3[Secret Crypto Nuclear]:::lvlNode
    L4[Top Secret Crypto Nuclear]:::lvlNode
    L5[Top Secret Crypto Nuclear Intel]:::lvlNode
    L6[System High All]:::lvlNode

    L0 --> L1
    L0 --> L2
    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L1 --> L5
    L2 --> L5
    L5 --> L6

    catA[Compartment Crypto]:::catNode
    catB[Compartment Nuclear]:::catNode
    catC[Compartment Intel]:::catNode

    catA -.labels.-> L1
    catA -.labels.-> L3
    catA -.labels.-> L5
    catB -.labels.-> L2
    catB -.labels.-> L3
    catB -.labels.-> L5
    catC -.labels.-> L5
```

**Interpretation:** Each node is a security level; edges represent the dominance relation $L_a \geq L_b$. A subject at the leaf cannot move *up* an edge for a read; a write flows *against* the edge direction (no write down). The **System Low** and **System High** bound the lattice.

## 4.3 Information Flow Direction — NRU and NWD at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef subjNode fill:#1565c0,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef objNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef flowNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef blockNode fill:#4a148c,stroke:#2a0854,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    S1[Subject Secret Clearance]:::subjNode
    S2[Subject Top Secret Clearance]:::subjNode
    O1[Object Confidential]:::objNode
    O2[Object Secret]:::objNode
    O3[Object Top Secret]:::objNode

    F1[Read Permitted]:::flowNode
    F2[Read Denied NRU]:::blockNode
    F3[Write Permitted]:::flowNode
    F4[Write Denied NWD]:::blockNode

    S1 -- read --> O1
    S1 -- read --> F1
    S1 -- read attempt --> O3
    S1 -- read attempt --> F2
    S2 -- write --> O3
    S2 -- write --> F3
    S2 -- write attempt --> O1
    S2 -- write attempt --> F4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Green arrows = allowed by BLP; Purple arrows = blocked by BLP. Note the asymmetry: a Secret subject can read Confidential, but a Top Secret subject cannot write to Confidential.

## 4.4 Confidentiality Policy Hierarchy — Classification of Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef rootNode fill:#000000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef milNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef commNode fill:#e65100,stroke:#bf360c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef modelNode fill:#37474f,stroke:#102027,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    R[Confidentiality Policies]:::rootNode
    M[Military Government]:::milNode
    C[Commercial Civilian]:::commNode
    P[Privacy Data Protection]:::commNode

    M --> BLP[Bell LaPadula 1973]:::modelNode
    M --> LAT[Lattice Model Denning 1976]:::modelNode
    M --> BRE[Brew and Nash Chinese Wall 1989]:::modelNode
    C --> CW[Clark Wilson 1987]:::modelNode
    C --> RBAC[Role Based Access Control]:::modelNode
    P --> GDPR[GDPR Art 32 Pseudonymisation]:::modelNode
    P --> HIPAA[HIPAA Safe Harbor 18 Identifiers]:::modelNode
```

**Key insight:** Bell–LaPadula is the **prototype** of the military branch; all subsequent confidentiality models (lattice, Chinese Wall, etc.) inherit or modify its core ideas.

## 4.5 Covert Channel Attack Path — BLP Bypass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef atkNode fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vicNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef chanNode fill:#4a148c,stroke:#2a0854,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detNode fill:#f9a825,stroke:#a36800,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px

    TS[Malicious Insider Top Secret]:::atkNode
    PUB[Public User Unclassified]:::vicNode
    SH[Shared Resource File Lock CPU Cache]:::chanNode
    TIM[Timing Modulation]:::chanNode
    STO[Storage Modulation]:::chanNode
    OBS[Observer Public User]:::detNode
    INF[Infer Secret Bits]:::detNode
    DC[Detection NOP by BLP]:::atkNode

    TS --> SH
    PUB --> SH
    SH --> TIM
    SH --> STO
    TIM --> OBS
    STO --> OBS
    OBS --> INF
    INF --> DC
```

**Reading the diagram:** BLP detects *legal* read/write violations, but a covert channel operates through a *shared resource* whose state is changed in a manner *legally allowed* by BLP. This is the irreducible gap of any MAC system.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
> Define a **confidentiality policy** and list its five essential components.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):**

A **confidentiality policy** is a formal, abstract specification of the rules that govern how sensitive information may be accessed and propagated within a system, ensuring that data is disclosed only to authorized principals.

Its five essential components are:
1. **Subjects (S)** — active entities that initiate access (users, processes).
2. **Objects (O)** — passive entities that hold information (files, records, memory).
3. **Access Modes (A)** — the permitted operations (read, write, append, execute).
4. **Security Labels (L)** — sensitivity markers (hierarchical rank + compartment set).
5. **Authorization Rules (R)** — predicates that decide whether a (s, o, a) triple is permitted.

A policy is *complete* only when all five components are explicitly defined. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the five components, 0.5 for completeness remark.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> State and explain the **Simple Security Property** and the ***-Property** of the Bell–LaPadula model.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):**

**Simple Security Property (ss-property, "No Read Up" — NRU):** A subject $s$ may read an object $o$ only if $s$'s clearance dominates $o$'s classification, i.e., $f_S(s) \geq f_O(o)$. This prevents unauthorized disclosure via direct reading.

**\*-Property (Star Property, "No Write Down" — NWD):** A subject $s$ may write an object $o$ only if $s$'s *current* security level is dominated by $o$'s classification, i.e., $f_C(s) \leq f_O(o)$. This prevents information leakage via *write* operations to lower-classified objects.

Together, ss and * form the mandatory core of the BLP confidentiality model; the **Basic Security Theorem** proves the system remains secure if both are preserved across all state transitions. *(3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *Per KTU ESE regulations: Part B Module 4 question carries 14 marks, with internal choice (a) or (b).*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**
> **(a)** With the aid of a state-machine diagram, describe the **Bell–LaPadula (BLP) confidentiality model**. Define the dominance relation, the two key security properties, and the state-transition rule. State the **Basic Security Theorem** and outline its proof. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following scenario. A military database has three security levels: *Public* (P), *Confidential* (C), and *Secret* (S); and two compartments: *Intel* (I) and *Operations* (O).
>
> - Subject $s_1$ has clearance $(S, \{I, O\})$ and current level $(S, \{I, O\})$.
> - Subject $s_2$ has clearance $(C, \{I\})$ and current level $(C, \{I\})$.
> - Object $o_1$ is classified $(C, \{I, O\})$.
> - Object $o_2$ is classified $(S, \{I\})$.
>
> Determine for each subject–object pair whether the following operations are permitted or denied by BLP, and justify each decision using the dominance relation:
>
> 1. $s_1$ reads $o_1$.
> 2. $s_2$ reads $o_2$.
> 3. $s_1$ writes to $o_2$.
> 4. $s_1$ reads $o_2$, then writes the *contents* of $o_2$ to $o_1$.
> 5. Compute $L_1 \sqcup L_2$ and $L_1 \sqcap L_2$ where $L_1 = (S, \{I, O\})$ and $L_2 = (C, \{I\})$.
>
> **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of BLP (1 mark):** BLP is a state-machine confidentiality model proposed by Bell and LaPadula (1973) for the U.S. DoD. A system is modelled as a finite-state automaton whose state $v = (b, M, f, H)$ comprises (i) current accesses $b$, (ii) the access permission matrix $M$, (iii) the level functions $f = (f_S, f_O, f_C)$, and (iv) the object hierarchy $H$.

**Dominance Relation (1 mark):** For two security levels $L_1 = (H_1, C_1)$ and $L_2 = (H_2, C_2)$, $L_1 \geq L_2$ iff $H_1 \geq H_2$ and $C_2 \subseteq C_1$. This is a partial order; the set of all labels under $\geq$ is a bounded lattice.

**Two Key Properties (2 marks):**
- **ss-property (No Read Up):** $f_S(s) \geq f_O(o)$ for any read access.
- **\*-property (No Write Down):** $f_C(s) \leq f_O(o)$ for any write access.

**State-Transition Rule (1 mark):** On receiving a request $q$, the system checks the discretionary matrix $M$, then the mandatory properties (ss and *); on success, the access is granted and the state updates; on failure, the request is denied. A state is "secure" iff it satisfies ss and *.

**Basic Security Theorem + Proof Outline (2 marks):** *The system is secure at all times iff the initial state is secure and every transition preserves the security properties.* Proof by induction on the length of the transition sequence — base case is $v_0$; inductive step uses the preservation hypothesis.

*(Total 7 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Setup (0.5 mark):** Levels are $P < C < S$; compartments are subsets of $\{I, O\}$.

**Step 2 — Operations Check (1.5 marks each = 6 marks):**

| # | Operation | Check | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s_1$ reads $o_1$ | $f_S(s_1) = (S, \{I,O\}) \geq f_O(o_1) = (C, \{I,O\})$? Hierarchy $S \geq C$ ✓, compartments $\{I,O\} \supseteq \{I,O\}$ ✓. | **Permitted** (NRU satisfied). |
| 2 | $s_2$ reads $o_2$ | $f_S(s_2) = (C, \{I\}) \geq f_O(o_2) = (S, \{I\})$? Hierarchy $C \not\geq S$ ✗. | **Denied** (NRU violated). |
| 3 | $s_1$ writes to $o_2$ | $f_C(s_1) = (S, \{I,O\}) \leq f_O(o_2) = (S, \{I\})$? Compartments: $\{I,O\} \subseteq \{I\}$? **No**, $\{O\} \not\subseteq \{I\}$. | **Denied** (*-property violated). |
| 4 | $s_1$ reads $o_2$ | Per #2, $s_1$ reading $o_2$: $f_S(s_1) = (S, \{I,O\}) \geq (S, \{I\})$? Compartments $\{I\} \subseteq \{I,O\}$ ✓. Hierarchy equal. | **Read permitted** ✓. |
| | $s_1$ writes the *contents* of $o_2$ to $o_1$ | The contents carry the *highest* level seen (Tranquility / *-property strictly applied). $o_2$ is at $(S, \{I\})$, so the *current* level for the data is at least $(S, \{I\})$. Write to $o_1 = (C, \{I,O\})$: $(S, \{I\}) \leq (C, \{I,O\})$? Hierarchy $S \not\leq C$ ✗. | **Write denied** (*-property violated — no write down). |
| 5 | Lattice ops | $L_1 = (S, \{I,O\})$, $L_2 = (C, \{I\})$. | $L_1 \sqcup L_2 = (\max(S,C), \{I,O\} \cup \{I\}) = (S, \{I,O\})$. $L_1 \sqcap L_2 = (\min(S,C), \{I,O\} \cap \{I\}) = (C, \{I\})$. |

*(0.5 mark for Step 1, 6 × 1.0 mark = 6 marks for the 5 sub-items + lattice, 0.5 mark for the final interpretation in #4.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Alternative)
> **(a)** Explain the **lattice model** of information flow (Denning, 1976). Show, with a worked example, why the set of security labels in BLP must be a **bounded lattice** (i.e., possess both a least element $L_\perp$ and a greatest element $L_\top$) for the model to be well-defined. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **covert channels** in the context of confidentiality policies. Classify them into storage and timing channels, derive the Shannon capacity of a binary timing channel, and explain why BLP cannot prevent them. Suggest two engineering mitigations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Lattice Model Overview (2 marks):** Dorothy Denning's 1976 lattice model generalises BLP by requiring that the set of security classes be a **lattice** — a partially ordered set closed under finite meet and join. Information can only flow *upward* along the partial order; this corresponds to BLP's NRU and NWD.

**Why a Lattice? (2 marks):** A lattice guarantees:
- For any two classes $L_1, L_2$, a unique least upper bound (join) and greatest lower bound (meet) exist.
- This allows the system to compute, deterministically, the "appropriate level" of *combined* information.
- Without lattice closure, two security labels could be combined into a *non-existent* class, breaking the partial order.

**Boundedness Requirement (2 marks):** The lattice must be bounded:
- $L_\perp$ (system-low) is required so every unclassified subject has a well-defined label.
- $L_\top$ (system-high) is required so aggregated/synthesized data has a sink.
- The *closure axiom*: $L \sqcup L' \in L$ and $L \sqcap L' \in L$ for all $L, L' \in L$.

**Worked Example (1 mark):** Hierarchy $\{P, C, S\}$ with compartments $\{I, O\}$. The 12-element set of levels includes $(C, \{I\})$ and $(S, \{I, O\})$; their join is $(S, \{I, O\})$, their meet is $(C, \{I\})$ — both in the lattice. $(S, \{I, O\})$ dominates $(C, \{I\})$ because $S \geq C$ and $\{I\} \subseteq \{I, O\}$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Covert Channel (1 mark):** A covert channel is a communication path *not intended* for information transfer that nevertheless leaks data across a security boundary. Covert channels exist in *every* mandatory access control system, including BLP.

**Classification (2 marks):**
- **Storage Channels:** Modulate information into a *shared resource's* state — e.g., file existence, lock state, allocation count. Detected by analysing *information flows* on non-data attributes.
- **Timing Channels:** Modulate information into the *timing* of observable events — e.g., CPU busy/idle cycles, response latency. Detected by analysing *temporal* patterns.

**Shannon Capacity of a Binary Timing Channel (2 marks):** For a noise-free binary channel, $N = 2$ distinguishable symbols per interval. The Shannon capacity is:

$$
C = \log_2 N = \log_2 2 = 1 \text{ bit per symbol}
$$

For an $N$-ary channel with symbol rate $r$ symbols/sec, the capacity is $C = r \log_2 N$ bits/sec.

**Why BLP Cannot Prevent Covert Channels (1 mark):** BLP governs only the *legal* read/write matrix. A covert channel uses a path that BLP's reference monitor does not inspect (e.g., shared cache state, scheduler timing). Hence, BLP is *necessary but not sufficient* for confidentiality.

**Two Engineering Mitigations (1 mark):**
1. **Noise injection / padding** (e.g., randomize I/O timing) to reduce effective channel capacity to near zero.
2. **Resource isolation / partitioning** (e.g., physically separate CPU caches for different security levels) to eliminate shared state.

*(1 mark distributed: 0.5 for mitigation listing + 0.5 for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $f_C$ vs $f_S$:** Students often use only the clearance in the *-property. The *-property uses the *current* level $f_C$, not the clearance $f_S$. This is a 2-mark loss in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Conflating compartments and hierarchy:** Compartments are *sets* (un-ordered, partial), hierarchy is *ordinal* (totally ordered). The dominance relation requires *both* components to satisfy the relation. Mixing them up is a 1.5-mark loss.
> 3. **Ignoring tranquility:** Forgetting to mention weak vs strong tranquility costs 1 mark in long answers.
> 4. **Skipping the audit trail:** KTU values security *designs* that include audit/logging. Omitting the audit log in diagrams loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Lattice closure:** Stating "labels form a lattice" without justifying closure (join/meet exist) is incomplete. Always prove or argue closure explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] **Confidentiality policy** = anti-disclosure rule set over (S, O, A, L, R).
- [ ] **BLP** = 1973 state-machine model by Bell & LaPadula for the U.S. DoD.
- [ ] **Dominance** $\geq$ = hierarchy $\geq$ *and* compartments $\supseteq$.
- [ ] **ss-property** = No Read Up: $f_S(s) \geq f_O(o)$.
- [ ] **\*-property** = No Write Down: $f_C(s) \leq f_O(o)$.
- [ ] **ds-property** = Discretionary: every access is matrix-permitted.

### ✅ Models & Theorems
- [ ] **Basic Security Theorem (BST):** secure start + secure transitions ⇒ always secure.
- [ ] **Lattice model (Denning 1976):** labels form a *bounded lattice* under dominance.
- [ ] **Tranquility:** labels constant during a single operation (weak) or lifetime (strong).
- [ ] **Trusted subjects:** can lower $f_C$ to declassify; primary attack surface.

### ✅ Engineering Concepts
- [ ] **Covert channels** = unintended communication paths; BLP cannot prevent.
- [ ] **Storage channels** = shared resource state; **Timing channels** = event timing.
- [ ] **Shannon capacity** of a binary channel = $\log_2 2 = 1$ bit per symbol.
- [ ] **Mitigations:** noise injection, resource partitioning, audit logging.

### ✅ Mnemonics
- **"BLP"** = **B**lock **L**eaks by **P**reventing **L**ateral and **P**rogressive disclosure.
- **"NRU + NWD"** = "**N**ever **R**ead **U**p, **N**ever **W**rite **D**own" — the two golden rules.
- **"CIA"** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability (the BLP addresses **C**).

### ✅ KTU-Favorite 14-Mark Themes
1. BLP state machine + state tuple + properties + BST proof outline.
2. Lattice operations (join/meet) on given security labels.
3. Covert channels — classification, capacity, mitigations.
4. Trusted subjects — necessity, attack surface, declassification.
5. Comparison of confidentiality models (BLP vs Biba vs Clark–Wilson vs Chinese Wall).

### ✅ Pitfalls to Avoid
- Do not use clearance $f_S$ where current level $f_C$ is required (and vice versa).
- Do not forget compartments in dominance checks.
- Do not assert "labels form a lattice" without proving closure.
- Do not skip audit logging in security architecture diagrams.

### ✅ One-Line Summary
> *The Bell–LaPadula confidentiality policy guarantees that information never flows *up* into a more-cleared subject via reading, and never flows *down* into a less-classified object via writing — provided the system starts secure, every transition preserves ss and *, and the labels form a bounded lattice under dominance.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model**: A formal **state machine** security model developed by **David Elliott Bell** and **Leonard J. LaPadula** in **1973** for the U.S. Department of Defense (originally as MITRE Technical Report MTR-2997). It is a **confidentiality-oriented** access control model that mathematically enforces multi-level security (MLS) policies through a set of access rules governing how **subjects** (active entities) may interact with **objects** (passive containers of information).

The BLP model is formally defined as a tuple of sets and functions:

$$
M = (S, O, A, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F}, \mathcal{H}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Set of **Subjects** (active users/processes)
- $O$ = Set of **Objects** (files, records, resources)
- $A = \{r, w, a, e\}$ = Set of **Access Modes** (read, write, append, execute)
- $\mathcal{L}$ = Set of **Security Levels** (lattice elements)
- $\mathcal{F} \subseteq \mathcal{L}^S$ = **Clearance function** assigning security levels to subjects
- $\mathcal{H} \subseteq \mathcal{L}^O$ = **Classification function** assigning security levels to objects
- $\mathcal{R}$ = Set of **Requests** (access attempts)
- $\mathcal{C}$ = Set of **Decisions** (yes/no/illegal/empty)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lattice Foundation**: The set $\mathcal{L}$ is mathematically structured as a **partially ordered set (poset)** with a unique greatest lower bound (meet, $\wedge$) and least upper bound (join, $\vee$) for every pair, ensuring that any two security classifications are comparable within a finite lattice hierarchy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Government Filing Cabinet

Imagine a highly secure government office where documents are stored in colour-coded cabinets:

| Cabinet Colour | Allowed Audience | Visual Cue |
|----------------|------------------|------------|
| 🟢 Green (Unclassified) | Any cleared employee | Open shelf |
| 🔵 Blue (Confidential) | Verified staff only | Locked door |
| 🟡 Yellow (Secret) | Senior officers | Locked + key |
| 🔴 Red (Top Secret) | Directors only | Biometric lock |

**The Rules of the Filing Room:**
1. 🟢➜🔴 You can **read down** (a Secret-cleared clerk can read Unclassified files freely). ❌ But you cannot **read up** (a Secret-cleared clerk cannot open a Top-Secret drawer).
2. 🟢➜🔴 You can **write up** only (a Confidential-cleared officer can draft a Secret memo and pass it upward). ❌ But you cannot **write down** (a Secret-cleared officer cannot copy secret content into a public report — that would leak classified data downward).

This "no leak downward, no peek upward" principle is the soul of the Bell-LaPadula model.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST863)**: The BLP model is a *mandatory* topic under Module 4. Examiners specifically test the **three security properties** (Simple Security, *-Property, Discretionary), the **tranquility principle**, and the **Basic Security Theorem**. Memorize the lattice notation — it appears in nearly every KTU question paper from this module.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Security Properties

> [!NOTE]
> The BLP model enforces confidentiality through **three hierarchical properties** that constrain every state transition $(s, o, a)$ where $s \in S$, $o \in O$, $a \in A$.

### Property 1: Simple Security Property (ss-Property) — *"No Read Up" (NRU)*

A subject $s$ may **read** an object $o$ **only if** the subject's clearance *dominates* the object's classification:

$$
\text{ss-Property:} \quad \forall (s, o, r) \in b \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)
$$

- **Intuition**: You cannot read documents classified *above* your clearance level.
- **Formal name**: Often called the "**No Read Up**" (NRU) rule.

### Property 2: *-Property (Star Property) — *"No Write Down" (NWD)*

A subject $s$ may **write** to an object $o$ **only if** the object's classification *dominates* the subject's clearance:

$$
\text{*-Property:} \quad \forall (s, o, w) \in b \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)
$$

- **Intuition**: You cannot write (or copy) information into a container at a *lower* security level — this would create a downward information leak.
- **Formal name**: Often called the "**No Write Down**" (NWD) rule.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception**: The *-Property is *not* "no write at all." A Top-Secret user *can* write into a Top-Secret object. The prohibition is *only* on writing to objects classified *below* the subject's clearance. The asterisk (*) in "*-Property" is a placeholder used in the original 1973 MITRE report to keep the property name generic.

### Property 3: Discretionary Security Property (ds-Property)

Access is also governed by an **access permission matrix** $M$ where $M[s,o]$ contains the explicitly authorized access modes for subject $s$ on object $o$:

$$
\text{ds-Property:} \quad (s, o, a) \in b \;\Longrightarrow\; a \in M[s,o]
$$

- **Intuition**: Even if the ss- and *-properties allow an access, the *discretionary* matrix must *also* authorize it. This is the "owner-controlled" layer.

## 1.4 Access Modes — The Four Primitives

The BLP model recognizes exactly **four** primitive access modes. Compound modes like *read-write* are constructed from these:

$$
A = \{ \underbrace{r}_{\text{read}}, \underbrace{w}_{\text{write}}, \underbrace{a}_{\text{append}}, \underbrace{e}_{\text{execute}} \}
$$

| Mode | Symbol | Meaning | Affected Property |
|------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| Read | $r$ | Observe the contents of the object | ss-Property (NRU) |
| Write | $w$ | Observe *and* modify the contents | Both ss- and *-Property |
| Append | $a$ | Modify contents *without* observing (write-only) | *-Property (NWD) |
| Execute | $e$ | Activate/invoke the object without observing | Neither (modeled as safe) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Append Mode ($\mathbf{a}$)** is a clever BLP invention: a subject at a *higher* level can *append* (write-only) data into a *lower-level* object **without** observing it, which preserves confidentiality while still allowing legitimate logging at lower levels. This single addition is what makes the BLP model **non-trivially consistent** (otherwise the no-read-up and no-write-down rules would forbid *all* useful activity).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lattice of Security Levels

The BLP model assumes that security levels form a **lattice** $(\mathcal{L}, \leq, \wedge, \vee)$ where:

- $\leq$ is the partial order (dominance relation).
- $\wedge$ is the **greatest lower bound (meet)** — the *highest* level a subject and object can both be at for safe read.
- $\vee$ is the **least upper bound (join)** — the *lowest* level that dominates both.

For a typical 4-level military lattice:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \{\text{Unclassified (U)} < \text{Confidential (C)} < \text{Secret (S)} < \text{Top Secret (TS)}\}
$$

For any two levels $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{L}$:

$$
L_1 \wedge L_2 = \min(L_1, L_2) \quad \text{and} \quad L_1 \vee L_2 = \max(L_1, L_2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Lattice?** A lattice guarantees that *every pair* of security levels has a unique greatest lower bound and least upper bound. This means that for *any* subject clearance and *any* object classification, the system can always compute a deterministic "safe comparison" without ambiguity. Without a lattice structure, the dominance relation would not be well-defined.

## 2.2 The Tranquility Principle

The Tranquility Principle addresses a crucial question: **Can a subject's clearance or an object's classification change at runtime?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong Tranquility**: The clearance $\mathcal{F}(s)$ and classification $\mathcal{H}(o)$ are **fixed for the lifetime** of the subject/object. They never change. This guarantees that the security properties hold at *all* states.
>
> **Weak Tranquility**: The clearance/classification *may* change, but **only in ways that preserve** the security properties (i.e., the system's "security level" never decreases for a subject, and never increases for an object in a way that violates the rules).

| Tranquility Type | Allows Runtime Changes? | Guarantees Properties? | Used In |
|------------------|------------------------|------------------------|---------|
| **Strong** | ❌ No | ✅ Always | Highly classified military systems |
| **Weak** | ✅ Yes (with restrictions) | ✅ Only if transition is "safe" | Practical MLS systems |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table and keep it beside your exam desk.** These are the exact formulas you will need to reproduce in any KTU question on the Bell-LaPadula model.

| # | Property / Concept | Mathematical Formulation | Verbal Form | Exam Weight |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Simple Security (ss) | $\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)$ when $r \in b$ | No Read Up (NRU) | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| 2 | Star Property (*) | $\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)$ when $w \in b$ | No Write Down (NWD) | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| 3 | ds-Property | $a \in M[s,o]$ | Discretionary access matrix check | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 4 | ds-Property (read) | $r \in M[s,o]$ | Discretionary read permission | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 5 | ds-Property (append) | $a \in M[s,o]$ | Discretionary append permission | ⭐ Medium |
| 6 | State Definition | $v = (b, M, f, h)$ | Current access set, matrix, and level functions | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 7 | State Transition | $X \vdash Y$ | State $X$ transitions to $Y$ via a request | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 8 | Secure State | $\Sigma(R, D, W, z_0) \models \phi$ | System $\Sigma$ satisfies policy $\phi$ | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| 9 | Lattice Meet | $L_1 \wedge L_2 = \min(L_1, L_2)$ | Greatest lower bound | ⭐ Low |
| 10 | Lattice Join | $L_1 \vee L_2 = \max(L_1, L_2)$ | Least upper bound | ⭐ Low |
| 11 | Strong Tranquility | $\forall t,\; f_t(s) = f_0(s) \land h_t(o) = h_0(o)$ | Levels never change | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 12 | Weak Tranquility | Levels may change if security preserved | Levels change safely | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 13 | Access Modes Set | $A = \{r, w, a, e\}$ | The four primitive modes | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| 14 | Basic Security Theorem | If $z_0$ is secure and every state transition preserves security, then all reachable states are secure | Induction on state sequence | ⭐⭐⭐ High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Exams**: The original BLP paper uses the greek letter $\lambda$ (lambda) for "current security level" of a subject. Many KTU textbooks abbreviate this as $\mathcal{L}$. Be prepared to recognize *both* notations in the question paper. The relationship is:
>
> $$\mathcal{L}(s) = \text{current security level of subject } s = f_c(s)$$
>
> where $f_c$ is the *current* clearance function (as opposed to the *maximum* clearance $f_{\max}$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BLP model is **not merely academic** — it has been deployed in several real systems:

| Real System | Year | BLP Application |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| **Multics** (MIT/Bell Labs) | 1974 | Original BLP implementation on a mainframe OS |
| **SCOMP** (Honeywell) | 1983 | Trusted UNIX variant for the U.S. Navy (NSA-certified) |
| **Trusted Solaris** | 1999 | Sun's MLS-enabled Solaris (B1-rated) |
| **SELinux** (partial) | 2000s | Modern Linux MLS mode (uses BLP-flavored policies) |
| **AWS GovCloud** | 2010s | Multi-tenant cloud uses BLP-style lattice for region segregation |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interview Tip**: If asked in a KTU viva or placement interview *"Where is BLP used today?"*, cite **Trusted Solaris / SELinux MLS mode** and explain that modern BLP-flavored policies are also embedded in **Kubernetes namespace isolation** and **AWS IAM role-based access with clearance levels**.

## 2.5 Strengths and Limitations of BLP

| Aspect | Strength | Limitation |
|--------|----------|------------|
| **Confidentiality** | ✅ Mathematically rigorous | ❌ No integrity guarantees |
| **Formal Verification** | ✅ Can be model-checked | ❌ Vulnerable to covert channels (timing, storage) |
| **Multi-Level Security** | ✅ Native MLS support | ❌ No "read-write" within same level in some variants |
| **Discretionary Layer** | ✅ Owner-controlled | ❌ Trojan horse attacks still possible |
| **Simplicity** | ✅ Three clear rules | ❌ Tranquility issues in dynamic environments |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point**: BLP **does not address integrity**. This is the famous reason why Kenneth J. Biba later proposed the **Biba Model** in 1977, which is essentially the "dual" of BLP — protecting integrity via "No Write Up" and "No Read Down". Students confuse these two; remember: **BLP = Confidentiality (NWD), Biba = Integrity (NRD)**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal State and System Definition

The BLP model defines a **state** $v$ as a 4-tuple:

$$
v = (b, M, f, h)
$$

Where:
- $b \subseteq S \times O \times A$ is the **current access set** (who has which access right to which object *right now*).
- $M$ is the **access permission matrix** (discretionary rights).
- $f : S \to \mathcal{L}$ is the **clearance function** for subjects.
- $h : O \to \mathcal{L}$ is the **classification function** for objects.

A **system state sequence** is a sequence of states $z = (v_0, v_1, v_2, \dots)$ where each consecutive pair represents a transition caused by a request.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: The Access Mode × Property Matrix

The BLP paper carefully tabulates which security property applies to which access mode. Let us derive this matrix from first principles.

**Step 1**: The `read` mode allows the subject to **observe** the object's contents. Observation requires the subject's clearance to dominate the object's classification. Therefore:

$$
\text{read access} \implies \text{ss-Property} \implies \mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)
$$

**Step 2**: The `write` mode allows the subject to **modify** the object's contents. This means the new content is at the subject's *current level*. To prevent leakage, the object's classification must dominate the subject's clearance. Therefore:

$$
\text{write access} \implies \text{*-Property} \implies \mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)
$$

**Step 3**: The `append` mode is write-only (no observation). It only needs the *-Property:

$$
\text{append access} \implies \text{*-Property} \implies \mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)
$$

**Step 4**: The `execute` mode is a no-op from a confidentiality perspective. Neither property is checked:

$$
\text{execute access} \implies \text{no property check} \implies \text{always safe}
$$

The full analytical matrix:

$$
\text{Access Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
a & \text{ss-Property} & \text{*-Property} & \text{ds-Property} \\
\hline
r & \text{Required} & \text{N/A} & \text{Required} \\
w & \text{Required} & \text{Required} & \text{Required} \\
a & \text{N/A} & \text{Required} & \text{Required} \\
e & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} & \text{Required}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: The Basic Security Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Basic Security Theorem)**: A system $\Sigma(R, D, W, z_0)$ is *secure* (i.e., satisfies the security policy $\phi$) if and only if:
>
> 1. The **initial state** $z_0$ is a secure state.
> 2. For every state transition $z_i \to z_{i+1}$ caused by a request in $R$, the resulting state $z_{i+1}$ is also secure.

**Proof Sketch (Induction on State Sequence):**

**Base Case ($i = 0$)**: We are given that $z_0$ is secure. ✅

**Inductive Hypothesis**: Assume $z_k$ is secure for some $k \geq 0$.

**Inductive Step**: We must show that $z_{k+1}$ is secure. A state transition $z_k \to z_{k+1}$ is triggered by a request $R_k$. The transition rule $\mathcal{W}$ is *defined* in the BLP model to produce only secure successor states (i.e., $\mathcal{W}$ is "security-preserving"). Therefore, since $z_k$ is secure (by hypothesis) and $\mathcal{W}$ is security-preserving (by construction):

$$
\text{Secure}(z_k) \;\land\; \mathcal{W} \text{ is security-preserving} \;\implies\; \text{Secure}(z_{k+1})
$$

By the **principle of mathematical induction**:

$$
\forall k \geq 0: \text{Secure}(z_k)
$$

Therefore, the system is secure for *all* reachable states. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Significance**: This theorem is powerful because it reduces the problem of verifying an *infinite* sequence of states to verifying just **two things** — the initial state and the transition function. In real-world auditing (e.g., **Common Criteria EAL7** evaluation), this is exactly the technique used: model-check the initial configuration and the kernel's state transition rules.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Append Mode Resolves the BLP Paradox

A naive reading of BLP seems to forbid all activity. Consider a Secret-cleared subject $s$ and a Confidential object $o$:

- **ss-Property** says: $s$ can read $o$ only if $\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)$, i.e., Secret $\geq$ Confidential. ✅ This holds.
- **\*-Property** says: $s$ can write $o$ only if $\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)$, i.e., Confidential $\geq$ Secret. ❌ This fails.

So the Secret user can *read* a Confidential object but cannot *write* into it. This is the **BLP paradox**.

**Solution via Append Mode** (the `a` access mode): The subject uses `append` instead of `write`. The `append` mode does *not* require the ss-Property (no observation) and *does* require the *-Property. So the check is:

$$
\text{append } (s, o) \implies \mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s) \implies \text{Confidential} \geq \text{Secret} \;\text{?}
$$

This *still* fails. So the **actual** resolution is to allow Secret-to-Confidential append under **specific** system rules (a relaxation of the *-Property known as the *-Property *for append*). Many BLP implementations define a **modified *-property** where appending downward is allowed, provided the *current* level of the subject is high enough to "bless" the appended data.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

Consider the following security lattice (using KTU's typical 4-level example):

$$
\mathcal{L} = \{\text{Unclassified (0)} < \text{Confidential (1)} < \text{Secret (2)} < \text{Top Secret (3)}\}
$$

A system has three subjects and three objects with the following clearance/classification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \{s_1, s_2, s_3\} \\
O &= \{o_1, o_2, o_3\} \\
\mathcal{F}(s_1) &= \text{Secret (2)} \\
\mathcal{F}(s_2) &= \text{Confidential (1)} \\
\mathcal{F}(s_3) &= \text{Top Secret (3)} \\
\mathcal{H}(o_1) &= \text{Confidential (1)} \\
\mathcal{H}(o_2) &= \text{Secret (2)} \\
\mathcal{H}(o_3) &= \text{Top Secret (3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Question 1**: Can $s_1$ *read* $o_2$?

Apply ss-Property: $\mathcal{F}(s_1) \geq \mathcal{H}(o_2)$ ? $\text{Secret (2)} \geq \text{Secret (2)}$ ✅ **Yes**.

**Question 2**: Can $s_1$ *write* into $o_1$?

Apply *-Property: $\mathcal{H}(o_1) \geq \mathcal{F}(s_1)$ ? $\text{Confidential (1)} \geq \text{Secret (2)}$ ❌ **No** (violates NWD).

**Question 3**: Can $s_3$ *write* into $o_2$?

Apply *-Property: $\mathcal{H}(o_2) \geq \mathcal{F}(s_3)$ ? $\text{Secret (2)} \geq \text{Top Secret (3)}$ ❌ **No**.

**Question 4**: Can $s_2$ *append* to $o_2$?

Apply *-Property (append): $\mathcal{H}(o_2) \geq \mathcal{F}(s_2)$ ? $\text{Secret (2)} \geq \text{Confidential (1)}$ ✅ **Yes** (write-only downward into higher object is fine).

**Question 5**: Can $s_2$ *read* $o_3$?

Apply ss-Property: $\mathcal{F}(s_2) \geq \mathcal{H}(o_3)$ ? $\text{Confidential (1)} \geq \text{Top Secret (3)}$ ❌ **No** (NRU violation).

## 3.6 Summary of Analytical Verifications

For the above scenario, the full access verdict table is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
& o_1 \text{ (C)} & o_2 \text{ (S)} & o_3 \text{ (TS)} \\
\hline
s_1 \text{ (S)} & \text{Read: ✅, Write: ❌} & \text{Read: ✅, Write: ✅} & \text{Read: ❌, Write: ❌} \\
s_2 \text{ (C)} & \text{Read: ✅, Write: ✅} & \text{Read: ❌, Write: ❌} & \text{Read: ❌, Write: ❌} \\
s_3 \text{ (TS)} & \text{Read: ✅, Append: ✅} & \text{Read: ✅, Append: ✅} & \text{Read: ✅, Write: ✅}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack**: When you see a lattice-style question on the KTU paper, **always check the access mode first**, then apply the corresponding property. Mixing up `read` (use ss-Property) with `write` (use *-Property) is the #1 reason students lose marks in this module.

        TRAN["State Transition Function W"]
    end

    subgraph LATTICE["Security Lattice Module"]
