```mermaid
        L2["Confidential (C)"]
        L3["Secret (S)"]
        L4["Top Secret (TS)"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Decision Module"]
        DEC{"All Checks Pass?"}
        YES["Grant Access\n(v becomes v')"]
        NO["Deny Access\n(return 'no')"]
    end

    R1 --> SS
    R1 --> ST
    R1 --> DS

    SS --> DEC
    ST --> DEC
    DS --> DEC

    STV --> TRAN
    TRAN --> SS
    TRAN --> ST
    TRAN --> DS

    L1 --> SS
    L2 --> SS
    L3 --> SS
    L4 --> SS
    L1 --> ST
    L2 --> ST
    L3 --> ST
    L4 --> ST

    DEC --> YES
    DEC --> NO

    YES --> STV
```

## 4.2 Lattice Hierarchy of Security Levels

```mermaid
graph TD
    TS["Top Secret (TS) - Level 3"]
    S["Secret (S) - Level 2"]
    C["Confidential (C) - Level 1"]
    U["Unclassified (U) - Level 0"]

    TS --- S
    S --- C
    C --- U

    TS -.->|dominates| S
    S -.->|dominates| C
    C -.->|dominates| U

    TS2["Any TS user"] -.->|read allowed| U3["U documents"]
    TS3["Any TS user"] -.->|NO write down| U4["U documents"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Lattice**: The solid lines (`---`) show the lattice's partial-order edges. The dotted lines (`-.->`) show the **information flow** allowed by BLP — strictly upward only, with read also allowed at the same level (horizontal move on the lattice).

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SecureInit: z0 (secure initial state)

    SecureInit --> CheckRequest: Receive request R
    CheckRequest --> ApplyS: Apply Simple Security
    ApplyS --> ApplyStar: Pass? Apply Star Property
    ApplyStar --> ApplyDS: Pass? Apply Discretionary Check
    ApplyDS --> Grant: All pass? New state v'
    ApplyDS --> Deny: Any fail? Reject, state unchanged
    Grant --> SecureNext: v' is also secure
    SecureNext --> CheckRequest: Next request
    Deny --> CheckRequest: Next request

    state Grant {
        direction LR
        G1["Update b: add (s,o,a)"]
        G2["Update M if discretionary change"]
        G3["Persist f, h (tranquility)"]
    }
```

## 4.4 Three-Property Enforcement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Access Attempt (s, o, a)"] --> B{"a = read?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Check ss-Property:\n F(s) >= H(o)"]
    B -- No --> D{"a = write?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Check ss-Property AND *-Property:\n F(s) >= H(o) AND H(o) >= F(s)"]
    D -- No --> F{"a = append?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Check *-Property:\n H(o) >= F(s)"]
    F -- No --> H["a = execute\n(No property check)"]

    C --> I["ds-Property: a in M[s,o]"]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J{"All Required\nChecks Pass?"}
    J -- Yes --> K["GRANT"]
    J -- No --> L["DENY"]
```

> [!TIP]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Part A contains 5–6 short questions of 3 marks each. Two are reproduced below with model answers. Use 4–5 lines for each answer; *no diagrams required* in Part A.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the Bell-LaPadula model. State and explain the Simple Security Property.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model** is a state-machine-based formal security model proposed by **David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in 1973** to enforce **confidentiality** in multi-level security (MLS) systems. It defines subjects, objects, and four access modes $\{r, w, a, e\}$ on a security lattice.

**Simple Security Property (ss-Property / "No Read Up")**: A subject $s$ is permitted to read an object $o$ only if the subject's clearance dominates the object's classification:

$$
\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)
$$

This prevents unauthorized information disclosure from higher-classified objects to lower-cleared subjects.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is the *-Property? Why is it sometimes called the "No Write Down" rule?

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **\*-Property (Star Property)** states that a subject $s$ may write into an object $o$ only if the object's classification *dominates* the subject's clearance:

$$
\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)
$$

It is called "**No Write Down**" because writing to a lower-classified object would cause classified information to flow *downward* in the lattice, violating confidentiality. The rule ensures that data never migrates from a high-clearance subject to a low-classification object, thereby preventing leaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A)**: Students often confuse *which* direction the inequality points. The *-Property is $\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)$ — **object on the left, subject on the right**, opposite of the ss-Property. Get this wrong and you lose 2 of 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Part B carries 14-mark questions with an *internal choice*. Below, Question A and Question B are two alternative choices. Each is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with full model solutions.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — *BLP Properties + Worked Example*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three security properties enforced by the Bell-LaPadula model. For each property, state the formal condition, the verbal rule, and a real-world analogy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a BLP system with the following configuration:

- Security lattice: $\mathcal{L} = \{U < C < S < TS\}$
- Subjects and clearances: $s_1 = TS$, $s_2 = S$, $s_3 = C$, $s_4 = U$
- Objects and classifications: $o_1 = TS$, $o_2 = S$, $o_3 = C$, $o_4 = U$

For each of the following access requests, decide whether the request should be **granted** or **denied** under the ss- and *-properties. Justify each decision. **(7 marks)**

| # | Request | Verdict |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $s_1$ reads $o_3$ | ? |
| 2 | $s_2$ reads $o_1$ | ? |
| 3 | $s_3$ writes to $o_2$ | ? |
| 4 | $s_1$ appends to $o_4$ | ? |
| 5 | $s_2$ appends to $o_3$ | ? |
| 6 | $s_4$ reads $o_2$ | ? |
| 7 | $s_1$ writes to $o_2$ | ? |

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — The Three Properties (7 marks)

> The three security properties of the BLP model are the **Simple Security Property (ss-Property)**, the **\*-Property**, and the **Discretionary Security Property (ds-Property)**.

**1. Simple Security Property (ss-Property)** — "**No Read Up (NRU)**" (2 marks)

$$
\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o) \quad \text{whenever } (s, o, r) \in b
$$

A subject may read an object only if the subject's clearance level is at least as high as the object's classification level. *Real-world analogy*: A *Confidential*-cleared military officer cannot read a *Top Secret* file in the archive — they lack sufficient clearance.

**2. \*-Property (Star Property)** — "**No Write Down (NWD)**" (2 marks)

$$
\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s) \quad \text{whenever } (s, o, w) \in b
$$

A subject may write into an object only if the object's classification dominates the subject's clearance. *Real-world analogy*: A *Secret*-cleared employee cannot type a secret report into a *Confidential*-level memo template, because that would leak secret information into a less-protected container.

**3. Discretionary Security Property (ds-Property)** (1 mark)

$$
a \in M[s,o] \quad \text{for every } (s, o, a) \in b
$$

Even if ss- and *-properties allow an access, the **access matrix** $M$ must explicitly authorize it. *Real-world analogy*: The file *owner* (not the security officer) decides who can access their personal folder.

> The combination of mandatory (ss, *) and discretionary (ds) controls is what makes BLP a **mandatory + discretionary hybrid** model. (1 mark for the synthesis)

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Part (b) — Worked Access Verdicts (7 marks, 1 mark each)

For each request, we apply the relevant property:

**Request 1**: $s_1$ (TS) reads $o_3$ (C). Check ss-Property: $\text{TS} \geq \text{C}$? ✅ **GRANTED**.

**Request 2**: $s_2$ (S) reads $o_1$ (TS). Check ss-Property: $\text{S} \geq \text{TS}$? ❌ **DENIED** (NRU violation).

**Request 3**: $s_3$ (C) writes to $o_2$ (S). Check *-Property: $\text{S} \geq \text{C}$? ✅ Also ss-Property: $\text{C} \geq \text{S}$? ✅ (same level, write is permitted) → Wait, for write both must hold. ss-Property requires $\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)$? $\text{C} \geq \text{S}$? ❌ **DENIED**. *(The student must show both checks.)*

**Request 4**: $s_1$ (TS) appends to $o_4$ (U). Check *-Property: $\text{U} \geq \text{TS}$? ❌ **DENIED** (NWD violation — append is a form of write).

**Request 5**: $s_2$ (S) appends to $o_3$ (C). Check *-Property: $\text{C} \geq \text{S}$? ❌ **DENIED** (NWD violation).

**Request 6**: $s_4$ (U) reads $o_2$ (S). Check ss-Property: $\text{U} \geq \text{S}$? ❌ **DENIED** (NRU violation).

**Request 7**: $s_1$ (TS) writes to $o_2$ (S). Check ss-Property: $\text{TS} \geq \text{S}$? ✅ Check *-Property: $\text{S} \geq \text{TS}$? ❌ **DENIED** (NWD violation — even TS cannot write down to S).

**Verdict Summary Table** (to be drawn in the exam):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. GRANTED, 2. DENIED (NRU), 3. DENIED (ss fails), 4. DENIED (NWD)} \\
&\text{5. DENIED (NWD), 6. DENIED (NRU), 7. DENIED (NWD)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total: 7 marks (1 per request)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B)**: For *write* access, **both** ss-Property *and* *-Property must be checked. A common mistake is to apply only one. Also, do *not* confuse `append` with `write` — they have the same property check, but the consequence differs (append does not allow reading the object's content).

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — *Tranquility + Basic Security Theorem*

**(a)** Define the **Tranquility Principle** in the BLP model. Differentiate between **Strong** and **Weak Tranquility** with examples. Discuss why the choice between them matters for a practical multi-level security system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State the **Basic Security Theorem** of the BLP model. Provide a proof sketch using mathematical induction and explain its significance in real-world Common Criteria evaluations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Tranquility Principle (7 marks)

> The Tranquility Principle addresses whether subject clearances $\mathcal{F}(s)$ and object classifications $\mathcal{H}(o)$ can change during system operation. (1 mark)

**Strong Tranquility (3 marks)**:

Under **Strong Tranquility**, the clearance and classification functions are **invariant** for the lifetime of the system. Formally:

$$
\forall t \in \mathbb{N}, \forall s \in S, \forall o \in O: f_t(s) = f_0(s) \;\land\; h_t(o) = h_0(o)
$$

No subject's clearance can be raised or lowered at runtime, and no object's classification can change. **Example**: In a military deployment, an officer's clearance is set at the time of induction and remains fixed throughout the operation.

**Weak Tranquility (2 marks)**:

Under **Weak Tranquility**, levels *may* change at runtime, but **only in ways that preserve the security properties**. A subject's clearance may be *raised* (not lowered) and an object's classification may be *raised* (not lowered) — but never the reverse. **Example**: A new employee is initially cleared only at Confidential level, and after a vetting process is promoted to Secret level at runtime.

**Why the Choice Matters (1 mark)**:

Strong tranquility is **easier to verify formally** (the system is a static lattice) but **inflexible** for dynamic enterprises. Weak tranquility is **practically necessary** (employees get promoted, files get reclassified) but requires **runtime monitors** to ensure no transition violates the security properties.

#### Part (b) — Basic Security Theorem (7 marks)

**Statement (2 marks)**:

A system $\Sigma(R, D, W, z_0)$ is **secure** (denoted $\Sigma \models \phi$, where $\phi$ is the security policy) if and only if:

1. The initial state $z_0$ is a **secure state** (i.e., $z_0 \models \phi$).
2. For every state transition $(z_i, R_i, z_{i+1}) \in W$, the resulting state $z_{i+1}$ is also a **secure state** (i.e., $z_{i+1} \models \phi$).

**Proof Sketch by Induction (3 marks)**:

- **Base Case** ($i = 0$): Given $z_0$ is secure. ✅
- **Inductive Hypothesis**: Assume $z_k$ is secure for some $k \geq 0$.
- **Inductive Step**: Transition $z_k \to z_{k+1}$ is via $\mathcal{W}$, which by *construction* only produces secure successor states. So $z_{k+1}$ is secure.
- **Conclusion**: By induction, $\forall k \geq 0: z_k$ is secure.

**Significance in Real-World Evaluation (2 marks)**:

The Basic Security Theorem reduces verification of an **infinite** state sequence to verification of just **two finite objects** — the initial state and the transition function. This is the principle behind **Common Criteria EAL7 (Formal Verification)**, where the OS kernel is model-checked (e.g., using **Isabelle/HOL** or **Coq**) for BLP compliance. Real-world systems certified to this level include **Multics** and **SCOMP**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in BLP Questions (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Inverted Inequality**: Writing $\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)$ for ss-Property. *Correct*: $\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)$ (subject ≥ object for read).
> 2. **Forgetting to Check Both Properties for Write**: A `write` access requires *both* ss- and *-property checks; many students only check one.
> 3. **Confusing BLP with Biba**: BLP protects *confidentiality* (no read up, no write down). Biba protects *integrity* (no read down, no write up). Mixing these up is an instant 3–4 mark loss.
> 4. **Ignoring Discretionary Property**: The ds-Property is the *third* property; skipping it costs 1 mark per sub-question.
> 5. **Misstating Tranquility**: Saying "Tranquility means levels can change freely." *Wrong.* Tranquility is the *constraint* on level changes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **BLP = confidentiality model** (1973, Bell & LaPadula, MITRE MTR-2997).
- ✅ **Four access modes**: $r$ (read), $w$ (write), $a$ (append), $e$ (execute).
- ✅ **Three properties**: ss-Property (NRU), *-Property (NWD), ds-Property (discretionary matrix).
- ✅ **ss-Property formula**: $\mathcal{F}(s) \geq \mathcal{H}(o)$ for read access.
- ✅ **\*-Property formula**: $\mathcal{H}(o) \geq \mathcal{F}(s)$ for write/append access.
- ✅ **Write access needs BOTH** ss- and *-Property checks.
- ✅ **Append mode** is write-only; it bypasses ss-Property.
- ✅ **Execute mode** has no property check (modeled as safe).
- ✅ **Security levels** form a **lattice** $(\mathcal{L}, \leq, \wedge, \vee)$.
- ✅ **Strong Tranquility** = levels never change; **Weak Tranquility** = levels change safely.
- ✅ **Basic Security Theorem** = secure initial state + security-preserving transitions ⟹ all reachable states are secure.
- ✅ **BLP does not protect integrity** → Biba Model (1977) is the integrity dual.
- ✅ **Real systems using BLP**: Multics (1974), SCOMP (1983), Trusted Solaris, SELinux MLS, AWS GovCloud.
- ✅ **Common vulnerabilities** of BLP: covert channels (timing, storage), Trojan horses, lack of integrity.
- ✅ **Common Criteria EAL7** uses BLP-style formal verification via theorem provers.
- ✅ **Mental Mnemonic**: "**Read UP is forbidden, Write DOWN is forbidden**" — keep this as your anchor.
- ✅ **Sample KTU Mark Distribution** (Module 4): Part A 6 marks + Part B 14 marks (with internal choice) = 20 marks total per question.

---

*End of Notes — Bell-LaPadula Model, PECST863 Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrity Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integrity Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Integrity

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework of information security, **Integrity** is one of the three pillars of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). Formally, an **Integrity Policy** is a set of rules, constraints, and enforcement mechanisms designed to ensure that data, programs, and system resources are modified only in authorized, well-defined, and verifiable ways.

$$
\text{Integrity} = f(\text{Authorized Modification}, \text{Detection of Unauthorized Change}, \text{Recovery})
$$

An **Integrity Policy** thus specifies:
1. **Subjects (active entities)** — who can perform modifications.
2. **Objects (passive entities)** — what is being protected.
3. **Integrity Levels (I-Labels)** — classification of trustworthiness.
4. **Allowed Operations** — which transitions of state are legal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> Integrity is concerned with **trustworthiness of data and origin** rather than its secrecy. The Biba Model and the Clark-Wilson Model are the two most frequently examined integrity policy frameworks.

## 1.2 Goals of an Integrity Policy (Clark-Wilson Formulation)

According to the foundational work on database integrity, an integrity policy must pursue three concurrent goals:

| # | Goal | Meaning |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **Prevention** | Block unauthorized modification before it occurs. |
| 2 | **Detection** | Identify unauthorized modification after it occurs. |
| 3 | **Recovery** | Restore data to a known valid state. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Vault"

Imagine a bank vault containing **gold biscuits**.
- Each biscuit has a **purity grade** (analogy: integrity level).
- Only **authorized assayers** (analogy: trusted subjects) can grade or alter biscuits.
- Every movement is recorded in a **tamper-proof ledger** (analogy: IVP — Integrity Verification Procedure).
- A junior clerk **cannot downgrade** a high-purity biscuit (analogy: *no write down* rule in Biba).

Just as the bank manager cares more about whether the **gold is authentic and unaltered** than about hiding its existence, integrity policies care more about **data correctness and provenance** than secrecy.

## 1.4 Why Integrity Policies Are Distinct from Confidentiality Policies

| Property | Confidentiality Policy | Integrity Policy |
|----------|------------------------|------------------|
| Primary Concern | Hiding information | Preserving correctness |
| Information Flow | High → Low (Biba *) | High → Low restricted |
| Typical Model | Bell–LaPadula | Biba / Clark–Wilson |
| Insider Threat | Lower priority | Central concern |
| Concurrency Issues | Not central | Central (e.g., Chinese Wall) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When KTU questions ask to "distinguish Biba from Bell–LaPadula", the examiner looks for: *Biba is about preventing modification by untrusted subjects, whereas Bell–LaPadula prevents disclosure to unauthorized readers.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biba Integrity Model (Static / Mandatory)

The **Biba Model**, proposed by Kenneth Biba in 1977, is the integrity analogue of the Bell–LaPadula model. It uses **integrity levels (I-Labels)** arranged in a **lattice** and enforces rules on information flow to protect data from being contaminated by lower-integrity data.

### 2.1.1 Three Variations of the Biba Model

#### (a) Strict Biba Integrity Policy (Biba * for Integrity)

$$
\forall s \in S, \; o \in O : \quad \text{Read} \Rightarrow I(s) \le I(o) \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Write} \Rightarrow I(o) \le I(s)
$$

- **No Read Down (NRD)**: A subject may **only read** objects at or **below** its own integrity level.
- **No Write Up (NWU)**: A subject may **only write** to objects at or **above** its own integrity level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A highly-trusted process is prevented from being "contaminated" by low-integrity data, and is forbidden from modifying data of higher integrity than itself.

#### (b) Low-Water-Mark Integrity Policy

After reading an object of lower integrity, the subject's integrity is dynamically **lowered** to that of the object:

$$
I'(s) = \min\bigl(I(s),\, I(o)\bigr)
$$

This allows more flexible read access but tracks contamination dynamically.

#### (c) Ring Policy

A subject may read any object regardless of integrity level, but the subject's own level is **not** modified. This is the least restrictive Biba variant.

### 2.1.2 Comparison of Biba Variants

| Variant | Read Rule | Write Rule | Subject Integrity Change | Restrictiveness |
|---------|-----------|------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Strict Biba** | $I(s) \le I(o)$ | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | None | Highest |
| **Low-Water-Mark** | Always allowed | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | Lowered to $\min(I(s),I(o))$ | Medium |
| **Ring** | Always allowed | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | None | Lowest |

## 2.2 The Clark–Wilson Integrity Model (Commercial / Transaction-Based)

Designed by David Clark and David Wilson in 1987 specifically for **commercial / banking applications**, this model focuses on **well-formed transactions** rather than lattice-based labels.

### 2.2.1 Core Components (The "Nine Elements")

| Symbol | Term | Role |
|--------|------|------|
| **CDI** | Constrained Data Item | Data whose integrity must be preserved |
| **UDI** | Unconstrained Data Item | Untrusted input data |
| **IVP** | Integrity Verification Procedure | Verifies that CDIs are in a valid state |
| **TP** | Transformation Procedure | Well-formed transaction that moves CDIs between valid states |

### 2.2.2 The Nine Certification / Enforcement Rules

**Certification Rules (C1–C5)** — defined by the *security officer*:

| Rule | Statement |
|------|-----------|
| **C1** | All IVPs must be applied at the moment a CDI is changed. |
| **C2** | CDIs may be modified **only** by TPs. |
| **C3** | Each CDI must be linked to a specific TP that can modify it. |
| **C4** | Subjects must be *authenticated* to invoke TPs. |
| **C5** | TPs must accept only UDI inputs, never untrusted CDIs directly. |

**Enforcement Rules (E1–E4)** — enforced by the *system*:

| Rule | Statement |
|------|-----------|
| **E1** | System maintains and protects the list of (subject, TP, CDI) triples. |
| **E2** | Subjects may invoke TPs **only** on authorized CDIs. |
| **E3** | System must authenticate each subject attempting to invoke a TP. |
| **E4** | Only the security officer may change authorization lists. |

### 2.2.3 Well-Formed Transaction Principle

A *well-formed transaction* is the cornerstone of Clark–Wilson:

$$
\text{State}_{\text{valid}}(CDI_t) \;\xrightarrow{\;\;TP\;\;}\; \text{State}_{\text{valid}}(CDI_{t+1})
$$

The system moves from one **consistent state** to another through the controlled application of TPs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Meaning |
|---------|---------------------|---------|
| Integrity Level Lattice | $(L, \le)$ | Partial order on integrity labels |
| Biba Strict Read | $I(s) \le I(o)$ | No-Read-Down rule |
| Biba Strict Write | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | No-Write-Up rule |
| Low-Water-Mark Adjustment | $I'(s) = \min(I(s), I(o))$ | Dynamic subject degradation |
| Well-Formed Transition | $TP : \text{Valid}_{t} \to \text{Valid}_{t+1}$ | Clark–Wilson core |
| Separation of Duty | $(U_1, U_2, \dots, U_n) \mapsto \text{TP}$ | Multiple users required |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Integrity Policies

- **Banking systems** — preventing fraudulent transaction logs.
- **Supply-chain management** — ensuring shipment records cannot be silently altered.
- **Healthcare EHR systems** — maintaining tamper-proof patient records.
- **Air Traffic Control** — preventing unauthorized modification of flight plans.
- **Software Update Infrastructure** — protecting signed binaries from downgrade attacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Exam Point:** A frequently asked KTU question: *“Why is the Biba Model considered the dual of the Bell–LaPadula Model?”* Answer: Biba swaps the *no-read-down* and *no-write-up* directions because integrity (untrusted modifying trusted) is the inverse problem of confidentiality (trusted reading secret).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of Biba Strict Policy Rules from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let $S$ denote the set of subjects, $O$ the set of objects, and $I : S \cup O \to L$ the integrity-level mapping function, where $L$ is a finite lattice of integrity labels. We require $L$ to be a **partially ordered set** (poset) under the dominance relation $\le$.

The objective: **prevent an untrusted subject from contaminating a trusted object**.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal:} \quad & \text{If } I(s) < I(o), \text{ then subject } s \text{ must NOT modify object } o. \\
\text{Logical Form:} \quad & \forall s, o : \bigl( I(s) < I(o) \bigr) \Rightarrow \text{deny}(\text{write}, s, o). \\
\text{Equivalently:} \quad & \text{permit}(\text{write}, s, o) \iff I(o) \le I(s). \quad \text{[No Write Up]} \\[4pt]
\text{By dual reasoning:} \quad & \text{permit}(\text{read}, s, o) \iff I(s) \le I(o). \quad \text{[No Read Down]} \\[4pt]
\text{Combined Strict Biba Policy:} \quad & \text{permit}(s,o) \iff I(s) \le I(o) \;\wedge\; I(o) \le I(s). \\
\text{Special Case (No-Read-Down alone):} \quad & \text{permit}(\text{read}, s, o) \iff I(s) \le I(o). \\
\text{Special Case (No-Write-Up alone):} \quad & \text{permit}(\text{write}, s, o) \iff I(o) \le I(s).
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose we have three integrity levels arranged as a linear lattice:

$$
L = \{ \text{Low} < \text{Medium} < \text{High} \}
$$

Subjects and objects with their integrity levels:

| Entity | Type | $I(\cdot)$ |
|--------|------|------------|
| $s_1$ (Junior Officer) | Subject | Medium |
| $s_2$ (Senior Auditor) | Subject | High |
| $o_1$ (Public Web Page) | Object | Low |
| $o_2$ (Internal Report) | Object | Medium |
| $o_3$ (Classified Ledger) | Object | High |

**Step 1 — Apply No-Read-Down ($I(s) \le I(o)$):**

| Action | Evaluation | Verdict |
|--------|------------|---------|
| $s_1$ reads $o_1$ | $I(s_1)=\text{M} \le I(o_1)=\text{L}$? **No** | Denied |
| $s_1$ reads $o_2$ | $\text{M} \le \text{M}$? **Yes** | Permitted |
| $s_1$ reads $o_3$ | $\text{M} \le \text{H}$? **Yes** | Permitted |
| $s_2$ reads $o_1$ | $\text{H} \le \text{L}$? **No** | Denied |

**Step 2 — Apply No-Write-Up ($I(o) \le I(s)$):**

| Action | Evaluation | Verdict |
|--------|------------|---------|
| $s_1$ writes $o_1$ | $\text{L} \le \text{M}$? **Yes** | Permitted |
| $s_1$ writes $o_3$ | $\text{H} \le \text{M}$? **No** | Denied |
| $s_2$ writes $o_3$ | $\text{H} \le \text{H}$? **Yes** | Permitted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even though $s_2$ is highly trusted, the *Low-Water-Mark* policy would **drop** $s_2$'s level to Low if it ever read $o_1$. This is why strict Biba simply **prohibits** such reads.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Low-Water-Mark Integrity Adjustment

### 3.2.1 Setup

We relax the read rule to allow $s$ to read $o$ even when $I(s) > I(o)$. However, the resulting state must reflect that the subject is now "contaminated" by lower-integrity information.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial integrity of } s &: I(s) = i_s \\
\text{Integrity of object read } &: I(o) = i_o \quad \text{where } i_o < i_s \\
\text{Contamination principle:} \quad & s \text{ now holds information of level } i_o. \\
\text{New integrity level:} \quad & I'(s) = \min(i_s, i_o) = i_o.
\end{aligned}
$$

Generalizing to multiple reads:

$$
I'(s) \;=\; \min\!\bigl( I(s),\; I(o_1),\; I(o_2),\;\dots,\; I(o_n) \bigr).
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Clark–Wilson Transaction Verification

Consider a banking database with the following CDIs:
- $CDI_1$ = Account balance
- $CDI_2$ = Transaction log
- $TP_1$ = Debit operation
- $TP_2$ = Credit operation
- $IVP_1$ = Daily reconciliation procedure

**Step 1 — Apply Certification Rule C2 (CDIs modified only by TPs):**

$$
\text{Modify}(CDI_1) \Rightarrow \text{via } TP_1 \text{ or } TP_2.
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Certification Rule C3 (binding CDIs to TPs):**

$$
\text{Binding}: \;(CDI_1) \mapsto \{TP_1, TP_2\}.
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Enforcement Rule E2 (subjects invoke TPs on authorized CDIs):**

$$
\text{User } u \text{ may invoke } TP_1 \text{ on } CDI_1 \iff (u, TP_1, CDI_1) \in \text{AllowedTriples}.
$$

**Step 4 — Verify well-formedness via IVP:**

$$
\text{IVP}_1 : \text{Balance}(CDI_1, t+1) \;=\; \text{Balance}(CDI_1, t) \;+\; \text{Sum}(\text{Credits}) \;-\; \text{Sum}(\text{Debits}).
$$

If the equality holds, the system has transitioned between two **valid states**.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Model | Trust Basis | Granularity | Key Mechanism | Primary Domain |
|-------|-------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| Biba Strict | Lattice labels | Object-level | Information flow control | Military |
| Biba Low-Watermark | Lattice labels | Subject-level | Dynamic degradation | Mixed |
| Clark–Wilson | Application semantics | Field-level | Well-formed transactions | Commercial |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Biba Integrity Lattice Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[User Subject S1 - Integrity Medium] --> nodeB[Read Request]
    nodeB --> nodeC{No Read Down Check}
    nodeC -->|I S1 less than equal I Obj| nodeD[Permit Read]
    nodeC -->|I S1 greater than I Obj| nodeE[Deny Read - Contamination Risk]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Write Request]
    nodeF --> nodeG{No Write Up Check}
    nodeG -->|I Obj less than equal I S1| nodeH[Permit Write]
    nodeG -->|I Obj greater than I S1| nodeI[Deny Write - Trusted Object Protected]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[State Transition Logged]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The subject $s_1$ with integrity level *Medium* first passes the *No Read Down* gate, then the *No Write Up* gate, before any state transition is logged.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Clark–Wilson Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Untrusted Input UDI] --> nodeB[Transformation Procedure TP]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is Input Valid}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Constrained Data Item CDI]
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE[Reject Input]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Integrity Verification Procedure IVP]
    nodeF -->|CDI in Valid State| nodeG[Commit to Log]
    nodeF -->|CDI in Invalid State| nodeH[Rollback via Recovery TP]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Next Valid State]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeA -.->|Enforcement Rule E1| nodeJ[Access Triple List]
    nodeB -.->|Enforcement Rule E2| nodeJ
    nodeD -.->|Enforcement Rule E3| nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Authentication Module]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The dashed lines on the right show the **Enforcement Rules (E1–E4)** that gate the data path via the *Access Triple List* and the *Authentication Module*. The solid lines depict the *Certification Rules (C1–C5)* that govern the data path itself.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparative Hierarchy of Integrity Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Integrity Policy Family] --> nodeB[Mandatory Integrity - Lattice Based]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Commercial Integrity - Transaction Based]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Strict Biba]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Low Watermark Biba]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Ring Biba]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Clark Wilson Model]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Seperation of Duty Extension]
    nodeG --> nodeI[CDI]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[UDI]
    nodeG --> nodeK[TP]
    nodeG --> nodeL[IVP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this diagram in exams:** A clean two-branch tree clearly shows that integrity models split into **lattice-based** (Biba family) and **transaction-based** (Clark–Wilson family). This is a high-scoring answer for "Classify integrity models" questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]`

> **Q1.** Define the concept of **integrity** in information security. List the **three goals** of an integrity policy as formulated by Clark and Wilson.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Integrity is the property whereby data, programs, and system resources are modified only in authorized, well-defined, and verifiable ways, ensuring their **correctness, consistency, and trustworthiness** throughout their lifecycle.

**Three Goals of Clark–Wilson (2 Marks):**

1. **Prevention** of unauthorized modification through access controls and well-formed transactions.
2. **Detection** of any unauthorized modification that may have occurred, typically through IVPs.
3. **Recovery** of data to a known consistent valid state after integrity violation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "availability" as a third goal. The third goal is **recovery**, not availability. Availability belongs to a different policy family.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Part A Pattern]`

> **Q2.** State the **two fundamental rules** of the **Strict Biba Integrity Model** and explain their significance with one real-world example.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Rule 1 — No Read Down (1 Mark):**
A subject $s$ may read an object $o$ only if $I(s) \le I(o)$. This prevents a high-integrity subject from being contaminated by reading low-integrity (untrusted) data.

**Rule 2 — No Write Up (1 Mark):**
A subject $s$ may write to an object $o$ only if $I(o) \le I(s)$. This prevents a low-integrity subject from corrupting high-integrity data.

**Real-World Example (1 Mark):**
A *system administrator* (high integrity) is forbidden from directly browsing *untrusted external websites* (low integrity) using the privileged terminal, because doing so could introduce contaminated data into a trusted log file.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently interchange "No Read Down" and "No Write Up". Mnemonic: **R**ule applies to **R**ead → ensures the reader is *at least as clean* as the data; **W**rite rule ensures the data is *at most as clean* as the writer.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):** Students must answer **one** of the two provided questions, each worth 14 marks and split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> **Q (A).** Discuss in detail the **Biba Integrity Model** and its **three variants**. With suitable examples, illustrate how the **Strict Biba** variant preserves integrity. **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Overview of Biba and its Three Variants

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Biba Model, proposed in 1977, is the **integrity analogue** of the Bell–LaPadula confidentiality model. It protects data from unauthorized modification by enforcing rules on the *information flow* between subjects and objects labeled with **integrity levels**.

**Step 2 — Lattice Foundation (1 Mark):**
Integrity levels form a lattice $(L, \le)$, where $i_1 \le i_2$ implies $i_1$ is *less trusted* than $i_2$.

**Step 3 — Three Variants (5 Marks):**

| Variant | Read Rule | Write Rule | Subject Adjustment | Use Case |
|---------|-----------|------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Strict Biba** | $I(s) \le I(o)$ | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | None | Military / High-assurance |
| **Low-Water-Mark** | Always | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | $I'(s) = \min(I(s), I(o))$ | Adaptive systems |
| **Ring** | Always | $I(o) \le I(s)$ | None | Practical OS / UNIX-like |

The **Strict Biba** is mathematically provably secure but too restrictive; the **Ring** policy is practical but weaker; the **Low-Water-Mark** is a compromise.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Strict Biba with Worked Example

**Step 1 — Rules Restated (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{Read: } I(s) \le I(o) \quad\quad \text{Write: } I(o) \le I(s).
$$

**Step 2 — Example Lattice (1 Mark):**
Levels: Untrusted (U) $<$ User (USR) $<$ System (SYS) $<$ Security Officer (SO).

**Step 3 — Subjects and Objects (2 Marks):**

| Entity | Label |
|--------|-------|
| $s_1$ (Guest) | USR |
| $s_2$ (Admin) | SYS |
| $o_1$ (Public data) | U |
| $o_2$ (User file) | USR |
| $o_3$ (System file) | SYS |
| $o_4$ (Audit log) | SO |

**Step 4 — Access Decisions (3 Marks):**

- $s_1$ reads $o_1$ → $\text{USR} \le \text{U}$? **No** → **Denied** (No-Read-Down enforced).
- $s_1$ writes $o_3$ → $\text{SYS} \le \text{USR}$? **No** → **Denied** (No-Write-Up enforced).
- $s_2$ reads $o_3$ → $\text{SYS} \le \text{SYS}$? **Yes** → **Permitted**.
- $s_2$ writes $o_4$ → $\text{SO} \le \text{SYS}$? **No** → **Denied** (audit log protected).

**Step 5 — Concluding Statement (1 Mark):**
The Strict Biba Model guarantees that *no low-integrity subject can ever corrupt a high-integrity object* and *no high-integrity subject can be passively contaminated* by untrusted data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning:** Students lose marks by failing to provide a **worked example** in part (b). KTU valuation strictly requires application-level demonstration, not just rule restatement. Always present at least one full decision-table like the one above.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]`

> **Q (B).** Explain the **Clark–Wilson Integrity Model** in detail. Discuss the **role of well-formed transactions, certification rules, and enforcement rules** with reference to a real-world **banking application**. **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Clark–Wilson Model Architecture

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 Mark):**
Clark and Wilson observed that **commercial applications** require integrity guarantees that lattice-based models cannot provide — specifically, **application-level consistency** of data items.

**Step 2 — Core Components (2 Marks):**
- **CDI** — Constrained Data Item (integrity-protected data).
- **UDI** — Unconstrained Data Item (untrusted external input).
- **TP** — Transformation Procedure (well-formed transaction).
- **IVP** — Integrity Verification Procedure (state validator).

**Step 3 — Certification Rules (C1–C5) (2 Marks):**
These rules are defined by the **security officer** at the application design stage. They specify *which* TPs may modify *which* CDIs and *how* IVPs confirm consistent states.

**Step 4 — Enforcement Rules (E1–E4) (2 Marks):**
These rules are enforced **automatically by the system**. They maintain access lists, authenticate subjects, and prevent bypass of certification constraints.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Banking Application Scenario

**Step 1 — Identify CDIs (1 Mark):**

| CDI | Meaning |
|-----|---------|
| $CDI_{\text{balance}}$ | Account balance field |
| $CDI_{\text{log}}$ | Transaction log |
| $CDI_{\text{limit}}$ | Daily transaction limit |

**Step 2 — Define TPs (1 Mark):**
- $TP_{\text{debit}}(CDI_{\text{balance}}, CDI_{\text{log}}, UDI_{\text{amount}})$
- $TP_{\text{credit}}(CDI_{\text{balance}}, CDI_{\text{log}}, UDI_{\text{amount}})$

**Step 3 — Define IVPs (1 Mark):**
- $IVP_1$ — Daily reconciliation: $\sum \text{debits} - \sum \text{credits} = 0$.
- $IVP_2$ — Balance $\ge 0$ for non-overdraft accounts.

**Step 4 — Apply Certification and Enforcement Rules (3 Marks):**

- **C2**: Balance may be modified *only* by $TP_{\text{debit}}$ or $TP_{\text{credit}}$.
- **C3**: Binding — $(CDI_{\text{balance}} \mapsto \{TP_{\text{debit}}, TP_{\text{credit}}\})$.
- **C4**: Teller $T$ must be authenticated before invoking any TP.
- **E1**: System maintains the authorized triple list: $(T, TP_{\text{debit}}, CDI_{\text{balance}})$.
- **E3**: Authentication via password + OTP before TP execution.

**Step 5 — Demonstrate Well-Formedness (1 Mark):**
A withdrawal of ₹5,000 from Account A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_t &: \text{Balance}(A) = 50{,}000,\; \text{Log} = \{ \text{prev records} \}. \\
TP_{\text{debit}} &: \text{Balance}(A) \leftarrow 50{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 45{,}000. \\
\text{Log} &\leftarrow \text{Log} \cup \{ (\text{debit}, 5{,}000, t) \}. \\
\text{State}_{t+1} &: \text{Balance}(A) = 45{,}000,\; \text{Log} = \{ \text{prev records} \} \cup \{ (\text{debit}, 5{,}000, t) \}. \\
\text{IVP}_1 &: \text{Check: log integrity and balance non-negativity.}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the IVP passes, the new state is **valid and consistent**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students omit the **separation of duty** aspect. For full marks, mention that in a real bank, no single individual should be able to both *initiate* and *approve* a transaction — this is enforced via the *Access Triple List* (E1) and dual-control TPs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Integrity** protects *correctness and trustworthiness* of data, distinct from confidentiality.
- [x] The **CIA Triad** is **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability — Integrity is the second pillar.
- [x] The **Biba Model** uses a **lattice of integrity levels** and prohibits *No Read Down* and *No Write Up* in its strict form.
- [x] The **Low-Water-Mark** variant dynamically degrades a subject's integrity after it reads a lower-level object.
- [x] The **Ring** variant is the least restrictive — no read restriction, no subject degradation.
- [x] The **Clark–Wilson Model** uses **CDI, UDI, TP, IVP** to enforce well-formed transactions.
- [x] **Nine rules** of Clark–Wilson: **C1–C5** (certification, by security officer) and **E1–E4** (enforcement, by system).
- [x] Biba is the **dual** of Bell–LaPadula — read/write directions are inverted because integrity is the *inverse* problem of confidentiality.
- [x] **Well-formed transaction** is the cornerstone of Clark–Wilson: a valid state must transition only to another valid state via a TP.
- [x] **Separation of duty** in Clark–Wilson prevents a single user from both initiating and verifying a transaction.
- [x] For a **14-mark answer**, always include (i) a labelled diagram or table, (ii) at least one numerical/worked example, and (iii) a concluding synthesis statement.
- [x] The **Biba Model** is best for **military/government**; **Clark–Wilson** is best for **commercial/financial** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Reminder:** When asked to "compare" two models, examiners expect a **tabular comparison with at least four parameters** (e.g., trust basis, granularity, primary mechanism, application domain). Always close your answer with a one-line synthesis statement such as: *"Thus, while Biba enforces information flow control, Clark–Wilson enforces application-level well-formedness."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biba Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biba Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Biba Model — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Biba Model** is a formal **state-transition integrity model** for computer security, proposed by **Kenneth J. Biba in 1977**. It is a lattice-based access control model that addresses the integrity of information rather than its confidentiality, ensuring that data is not improperly modified by unauthorized or less-trusted subjects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Biba Model is widely regarded as the **integrity counterpart** of the **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model**, which instead protects **confidentiality**. While BLP focuses on *“No Read Up, No Write Down”*, the Biba Model inverts these rules to enforce *“No Read Down, No Write Up”*.

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Document Hierarchy"

Imagine a corporate office where documents are classified by **trust level** (like integrity levels):

- **CEO** (High Integrity — Authoritative documents)
- **Manager** (Medium Integrity — Reviewed documents)
- **Intern** (Low Integrity — Drafts/Working notes)

**Rules of the Biba-style office:**

1. An **Intern cannot read** the CEO's final report (No Read Down → reading untrusted drafts could pollute the CEO's understanding).
2. An **Intern cannot write/modify** the CEO's final report (No Write Up → low-trust users cannot inject unverified content into high-trust assets).
3. Only the **CEO can invoke/instruct** the Manager (Invocation Property → only higher-integrity subjects can request services from others to prevent bad data flow).

In short, **Biba protects the system from bad data flowing upward** into trusted zones, just like a hygiene check on incoming food in a cleanroom.

---

## 🧩 Core Building Blocks

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Subject (S)** | $S$ | Active entity (user, process) that requests access |
| **Object (O)** | $O$ | Passive entity (file, record) that contains information |
| **Integrity Level** | $I(s)$, $I(o)$ | A numerical or categorical value denoting trustworthiness |
| **Lattice** | $(IL, \leq, \oplus, \otimes)$ | The mathematical ordering structure of integrity levels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrity Levels (IL)** typically include categories like **{Untrusted, Slightly-Trusted, Trusted, Critical, System-High}** mapped to numeric scales such as **{0, 1, 2, 3, 4}**. A subject/object's *integrity* is bounded by its level and a set of categories (the "compartments").

---

## 🎯 Why the Biba Model Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

- Biba is **mandatory** in the *Topics in Security* syllabus (PECST863 — Module 4).
- It illustrates **non-discretionary** (mandatory) integrity enforcement.
- Forms the foundation for **Clark-Wilson**, **Lipner’s Integrity Matrix**, and **role-based integrity models** in modern systems.
- Critically used in **banking systems, military command chains, and OS kernel protections**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The Biba Model enforces integrity through a **lattice of integrity levels** and a strict set of access axioms. The complete model is formally defined over a **lattice** $(\mathcal{L}, \leq, \oplus, \otimes)$ where:

- $\mathcal{L}$ = set of integrity labels
- $\leq$ = partial-order relation ("less than or equal in integrity")
- $\oplus$ = **least upper bound** (join) — used for combining integrity levels
- $\otimes$ = **greatest lower bound** (meet) — used for intersecting integrity levels

---

## 📐 Mathematical Formulation of the Biba Lattice

A subject $s$ and object $o$ are each assigned an integrity level from a finite lattice $\mathcal{L}$.

The integrity level of a subject at any time $t$ is denoted:

$$I_s(t) \in \mathcal{L}$$

Similarly, for any object:

$$I_o(t) \in \mathcal{L}$$

Subjects may also have a **current integrity level** $I_s^{cur}$ that can be dynamically reduced after a read operation (to prevent contamination).

---

## 🛡️ The Three Mandatory Access Axioms of Biba

### **Axiom 1 — Simple Integrity Condition (No Read Down / “NRD”)**

> A subject $s$ may **read** an object $o$ **only if** the integrity level of $o$ is **greater than or equal to** the integrity level of $s$.

Formally:

$$\text{Read}(s, o) \iff I(o) \geq I(s)$$

- **Why?** A high-integrity subject should not be “contaminated” by reading low-integrity (untrusted) data, which could later influence high-integrity decisions.

### **Axiom 2 — Star Integrity Axiom (No Write Up / “NWU”)** *(often marked with ★)*

> A subject $s$ may **modify (write)** an object $o$ **only if** the integrity level of $s$ is **greater than or equal to** the integrity level of $o$.

Formally:

$$\text{Write}(s, o) \iff I(s) \geq I(o)$$

- **Why?** A high-integrity object must not be polluted by data from a less-trusted subject.

### **Axiom 3 — Invocation Property (Trusted Invocation)**

> A subject $s_1$ may **invoke (call/request service from)** another subject $s_2$ **only if** $I(s_2) \geq I(s_1)$.

Formally:

$$\text{Invoke}(s_1, s_2) \iff I(s_2) \geq I(s_1)$$

- **Why?** Prevents a high-integrity subject from being influenced by a low-integrity subject via system calls or service requests.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Rule** | **Symbolic Form** | **Plain English** | **Direction** |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Simple Integrity (NRD) | $\text{Read}(s, o) \iff I(o) \geq I(s)$ | No Read Down | Subject reads only from $\geq$ its level |
| Star Integrity (NWU) | $\text{Write}(s, o) \iff I(s) \geq I(o)$ | No Write Up | Subject writes only to $\leq$ its level |
| Invocation Property | $\text{Invoke}(s_1, s_2) \iff I(s_2) \geq I(s_1)$ | Trusted Invocation Only | Subject may call only those at $\geq$ its level |
| Lattice Join | $I(a) \oplus I(b)$ | Least Upper Bound (LUB) of two levels | Used when combining integrity labels |
| Lattice Meet | $I(a) \otimes I(b)$ | Greatest Lower Bound (GLB) of two levels | Used when intersecting categories |
| Current Integrity | $I_s^{cur}(t+1) = I_s^{cur}(t) \otimes I_o(t)$ | Downgrade after read | Subjects absorb the integrity of objects they read |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** The “No Read Down / No Write Up” mnemonic is the **single most asked** concept. Memorize its **complementarity to BLP**.

---

## 🌐 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Biba Model

| **Domain** | **Application of Biba** |
|------------|--------------------------|
| **Banking & Finance** | Enforces that accounting clerks (low IL) cannot modify audit/balance sheets (high IL) directly. |
| **Operating Systems** | Used in capability-based systems like **L4 microkernels** to protect kernel integrity from user-mode processes. |
| **Military C2 Systems** | Ensures low-ranking operators cannot tamper with strategic command data. |
| **Healthcare EMRs** | Patient data modifications must be performed only by users with the right integrity (e.g., certified physicians). |
| **Industrial SCADA** | Sensors and actuators classified by integrity level to prevent malicious sensor injection. |
| **Blockchain Validation** | Validator nodes enforce Biba-like rules to prevent low-trust nodes from submitting altered blocks. |

---

## 🔁 Comparison with Bell-LaPadula (BLP) — High-Yield for KTU

| **Property** | **BLP (Confidentiality)** | **Biba (Integrity)** |
|--------------|----------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Primary Concern** | Secrecy / Confidentiality | Trust / Integrity |
| **No Read Up (NRU)** | ✅ Enforced | ❌ (inverse: No Read Down) |
| **No Write Down (NWD)** | ✅ Enforced | ❌ (inverse: No Write Up) |
| **Star Property** | ★-property: No write down | ★-property: No write up |
| **Tranquility** | Strong/Weak | Strong/Weak |
| **Discretionary Access** | ds-property | Optional |
| **Year Proposed** | 1973 (Bell & LaPadula) | 1977 (Biba) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Biba Model is the integrity dual of the BLP Model.** Where BLP uses the relation “$\leq$” for security levels, Biba uses “$\geq$” for integrity levels — this duality is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

This section presents the **exhaustive logical derivations** of the Biba access axioms, the lattice-based reasoning, and worked analytical examples.

---

## **Derivation 1 — Deriving the Simple Integrity Condition (No Read Down)**

### **Setup:**

Let the integrity lattice be $(\mathcal{L}, \leq)$. We define:

- $S$ = the set of subjects
- $O$ = the set of objects
- Each subject $s$ has integrity level $I(s) \in \mathcal{L}$
- Each object $o$ has integrity level $I(o) \in \mathcal{L}$

### **Goal:**

Determine the condition under which a subject $s$ may **read** an object $o$ without compromising integrity.

### **Step 1: Define the integrity of data**

The integrity of an object is a property of its **provenance** — i.e., the trust placed in the chain of custody of that data. If $I(o) = \text{high}$, the data is verified, audited, and trusted.

### **Step 2: Define the integrity of a subject**

The integrity of a subject represents the **trustworthiness of its decisions**. A subject with $I(s) = \text{high}$ is verified and authorized to make high-impact decisions.

### **Step 3: Examine the effect of a read operation**

When $s$ reads $o$, the information from $o$ flows into $s$'s decision-making context. Therefore, the *effective* integrity of $s$ after the read becomes:

$$
I_s^{\text{post}} = I(s) \otimes I(o)
$$

where $\otimes$ is the **greatest lower bound** (GLB) in the lattice.

### **Step 4: Prevent integrity downgrade**

To prevent $s$ from being *contaminated* by a low-integrity $o$, we require:

$$
I_s^{\text{post}} = I(s)
$$

That is, the subject's integrity must not decrease.

### **Step 5: Apply lattice properties**

For the GLB to equal $I(s)$, we require:

$$
I(s) \otimes I(o) = I(s)
$$

By the absorption identity of a lattice, this holds **if and only if**:

$$
I(s) \leq I(o)
$$

i.e., the object’s integrity is **at least** the subject’s.

### **Step 6: Final condition**

$$
\boxed{\text{Read}(s, o) \iff I(o) \geq I(s)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A subject may read only objects whose integrity is **greater than or equal to** its own. This is the **No Read Down** rule.

---

## **Derivation 2 — Deriving the Star Integrity Axiom (No Write Up)**

### **Step 1: Effect of a write operation**

When $s$ writes to $o$, the integrity of $o$ post-write is bounded below by the integrity of $s$ (since untrusted data is being introduced):

$$
I_o^{\text{post}} = I(s) \otimes I(o)
$$

### **Step 2: Prevent integrity corruption of $o$**

To ensure that $o$ is not contaminated, we require:

$$
I_o^{\text{post}} = I(o)
$$

i.e., the object’s integrity must not be lowered.

### **Step 3: Apply lattice property**

$$
I(s) \otimes I(o) = I(o) \iff I(o) \leq I(s)
$$

### **Step 4: Final condition**

$$
\boxed{\text{Write}(s, o) \iff I(s) \geq I(o)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A subject may write to an object only if the subject's integrity is **at least** the object's. This is the **No Write Up** rule.

---

## **Derivation 3 — Combining Biba with a Concrete Numerical Example**

### **Worked Example:**

Consider the lattice:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \{ \text{Public} (0), \text{Internal} (1), \text{Confidential} (2), \text{Critical} (3) \}
$$

with partial order $0 \leq 1 \leq 2 \leq 3$.

Let:
- $s_1$ = Clerk with $I(s_1) = 1$ (Internal)
- $s_2$ = Auditor with $I(s_2) = 2$ (Confidential)
- $o_1$ = Customer Database with $I(o_1) = 2$ (Confidential)
- $o_2$ = Working Drafts with $I(o_2) = 1$ (Internal)

#### **Question:** Which of the following operations are permitted under Biba?

| **Operation** | **Condition** | **Result** | **Justification** |
|---------------|---------------|------------|--------------------|
| $s_1$ reads $o_1$ | $I(o_1) \geq I(s_1) \Rightarrow 2 \geq 1$ | ✅ Permitted | No Read Down satisfied |
| $s_1$ reads $o_2$ | $1 \geq 1$ | ✅ Permitted | Equal level — allowed |
| $s_1$ writes to $o_1$ | $I(s_1) \geq I(o_1) \Rightarrow 1 \geq 2$ | ❌ Denied | No Write Up violated |
| $s_1$ writes to $o_2$ | $1 \geq 1$ | ✅ Permitted | Equal level — allowed |
| $s_2$ reads $o_1$ | $2 \geq 2$ | ✅ Permitted | Equal level — allowed |
| $s_2$ reads $o_2$ | $1 \geq 2$ | ❌ Denied | No Read Down violated |
| $s_2$ writes to $o_1$ | $2 \geq 2$ | ✅ Permitted | Equal level — allowed |
| $s_2$ writes to $o_2$ | $2 \geq 1$ | ✅ Permitted | Subject higher than object |

#### **Key Learning:**
- The Clerk (low IL) can **read** trusted data but **cannot modify** confidential data.
- The Auditor (high IL) can **modify** confidential data but **cannot read** drafts (which may be unverified) — to prevent contamination of audit decisions.

---

## **Derivation 4 — Formal Proof that Biba is a Lattice-Based Model**

### **Claim:** The set of all integrity levels $\mathcal{L}$ of a Biba-protected system forms a **bounded lattice**.

### **Proof Outline:**

1. **Partial Order:** $\leq$ is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive — given by the system security policy.

2. **Least Upper Bound:** For any two elements $a, b \in \mathcal{L}$, define $a \oplus b$ = the lowest element $\geq$ both. This exists by the system’s **integrity hierarchy**.

3. **Greatest Lower Bound:** Similarly, $a \otimes b$ = the highest element $\leq$ both exists.

4. **Bounds:** The lattice has a **System-Low** $\bot$ (e.g., *Untrusted*) and a **System-High** $\top$ (e.g., *Critical*).

5. Hence, $(\mathcal{L}, \leq, \oplus, \otimes)$ is a **bounded lattice**. ∎

> **Importance:** This lattice property is what enables Biba to enforce integrity through **mathematical certainty** rather than ad-hoc rules — a key KTU evaluation point.

---

## **Analytical Matrix: Biba Variants**

| **Variant** | **Rules Enforced** | **Strength vs. Flexibility** | **Use Case** |
|-------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| **Strict Biba** | All three axioms (NRD + NWU + Invocation) | 🔒 Maximum integrity, very restrictive | Military, Kernel protection |
| **Biba with Low-Watermark** | NRD + Invocation (no NWU) | ⚖️ Allows writes up, but tracks contamination | Trusted OS designs |
| **Ring Biba (Biba with Subjects at Higher Levels)** | Modified NWU + NRD | 🛡️ Stronger write protection | Banking systems |
| **Clark-Wilson (extension of Biba concepts)** | Well-formed transactions, separation of duties | 🧩 Practical for commercial DBs | Enterprise systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** — all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords or markdown formatting are used inside node labels.

---

## **Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Biba Model**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Entry Point] --> B{Subject s requests Access}
    B --> C{Determine Operation Type}
    C -->|Read| D[Check Simple Integrity Condition]
    C -->|Write| E[Check Star Integrity Axiom]
    C -->|Invoke| F[Check Invocation Property]
    D --> G{I of o greater than or equal to I of s}
    E --> H{I of s greater than or equal to I of o}
    F --> I{I of s2 greater than or equal to I of s1}
    G -->|TRUE| J[Grant READ Access]
    G -->|FALSE| K[DENY Read Down]
    H -->|TRUE| L[Grant WRITE Access]
    H -->|FALSE| M[DENY Write Up]
    I -->|TRUE| N[Grant INVOKE Access]
    I -->|FALSE| O[DENY Invocation]
    J --> P[Log Access to Audit Trail]
    L --> P
    N --> P
    K --> Q[Violation Reported]
    M --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> R[Continue Session]
    Q --> S[Trigger Integrity Alarm]
```

---

## **Diagram 2: Biba Integrity Lattice — Vertical Hierarchy View**

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Level 0 - Untrusted] --> L1[Level 1 - Slightly Trusted]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 - Trusted]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Highly Trusted]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Critical System High]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - Top Secret Integrity]
    L0 -.-> L5
    classDef low fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    class L0,L1 low
    class L2,L3 mid
    class L4,L5 high
```

---

## **Diagram 3: Biba vs. Bell-LaPadula — Comparative Access Flow**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLP[Bell LaPadula Confidentiality]
        B1[Subject S] -->|No Read Up| B2{Security Level Check}
        B2 -->|Allowed| B3[Read at greater than or equal level]
        B2 -->|Denied| B4[Block NRU]
        B1 -->|No Write Down| B5{Star Property}
        B5 -->|Allowed| B6[Write at less than or equal level]
    end

    subgraph Biba[Biba Integrity]
        I1[Subject S] -->|No Read Down| I2{Integrity Level Check}
        I2 -->|Allowed| I3[Read at greater than or equal level]
        I2 -->|Denied| I4[Block NRD]
        I1 -->|No Write Up| I5{Star Integrity Axiom}
        I5 -->|Allowed| I6[Write at less than or equal level]
    end
```

---

## **Diagram 4: Information Flow Direction in Biba**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Integrity Subject] -->|Trusted Write Allowed| B[Equal Integrity Object]
    C[Low Integrity Object] -->|Read Permitted to Lower Subject| D[Low Integrity Subject]
    E[High Integrity Subject] -.->|NO Write Up Blocked| F[High Integrity Object]
    G[High Integrity Subject] -.->|NO Read Down Blocked| H[Low Integrity Object]
    classDef allowed fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    classDef denied fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D allowed
    class E,F,G,H denied
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A (3 marks), Part B (14 marks, with internal choice).

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. Define the Biba Integrity Model. List its three main access rules.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Biba Model**, proposed by **Kenneth J. Biba in 1977**, is a formal lattice-based access control model designed to **preserve the integrity** of information in a computer system. It is the integrity counterpart of the Bell-LaPadula Model.

The three primary access rules are:

1. **Simple Integrity Condition (No Read Down):** A subject may read an object only if the object’s integrity level is $\geq$ the subject’s integrity level.
2. **★-Integrity Axiom (No Write Up):** A subject may write to an object only if the subject’s integrity level is $\geq$ the object’s integrity level.
3. **Invocation Property:** A subject $s_1$ may invoke another subject $s_2$ only if $I(s_2) \geq I(s_1)$.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between the Bell-LaPadula Model and the Biba Model in terms of their primary security goals.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Bell-LaPadula (BLP)** | **Biba** |
|------------|---------------------------|----------|
| **Primary Goal** | Confidentiality (preventing unauthorized disclosure) | Integrity (preventing unauthorized modification) |
| **No Read Rule** | No Read Up (NRU) | No Read Down (NRD) |
| **No Write Rule** | No Write Down (NWD) | No Write Up (NWU) |
| **Year** | 1973 | 1977 |

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain in detail the Biba Model with its three access axioms. State the formal conditions using a lattice structure. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The **Biba Model** is a formal state-transition integrity model based on the lattice

$$(\mathcal{L}, \leq, \oplus, \otimes)$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the set of integrity levels, $\leq$ is a partial order, and $\oplus, \otimes$ are join and meet operators.

**1. Simple Integrity Condition — No Read Down (3 marks):**

A subject $s$ may read an object $o$ only if:

$$I(o) \geq I(s)$$

**Reason:** Reading low-integrity data would *contaminate* the subject’s decision base, lowering its effective integrity.

**2. ★-Integrity Axiom — No Write Up (2 marks):**

A subject $s$ may write to an object $o$ only if:

$$I(s) \geq I(o)$$

**Reason:** Prevents a low-trust subject from polluting a high-trust object.

**3. Invocation Property (2 marks):**

A subject $s_1$ may invoke $s_2$ only if:

$$I(s_2) \geq I(s_1)$$

**Reason:** Stops indirect contamination via service requests.

---

#### **(b) Compare and contrast the Biba Model with the Bell-LaPadula Model. Why is Biba considered the integrity dual of BLP? (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **BLP** | **Biba** |
|-------------|---------|----------|
| **Security Goal** | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| **Access Control Basis** | Security levels | Integrity levels |
| **Read Rule** | No Read Up | No Read Down |
| **Write Rule** | No Write Down | No Write Up |
| **Tranquility** | Strong/Weak | Strong/Weak |
| **Information Flow** | Sensitive → less sensitive | Trusted → less trusted |
| **Year / Author** | 1973, Bell & LaPadula | 1977, Biba |

**Why is Biba the Integrity Dual of BLP? (3 marks):**

1. BLP restricts *information flow downward* to preserve confidentiality.
2. Biba restricts *information flow upward* to preserve integrity.
3. The rules are **logically inverted**:
   - BLP: $\text{Read}(s,o) \iff I(s) \geq I(o)$
   - Biba: $\text{Read}(s,o) \iff I(o) \geq I(s)$
4. The dual nature is reflected in the lattice: BLP uses *security labels*; Biba uses *integrity labels* with the opposite ordering of trust.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) With a suitable example, illustrate how the Biba Model prevents integrity violations. Show the lattice and subject/object assignments. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Lattice:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \{ \text{Public} (0), \text{Clerk} (1), \text{Manager} (2), \text{Director} (3) \}
$$

**Subjects and Objects:**

| Entity | Type | Integrity Level |
|--------|------|------------------|
| $s_1$: Intern | Subject | 0 (Public) |
| $s_2$: Clerk | Subject | 1 (Clerk) |
| $s_3$: Manager | Subject | 2 (Manager) |
| $o_1$: Draft Notes | Object | 0 (Public) |
| $o_2$: Daily Log | Object | 1 (Clerk) |
| $o_3$: Monthly Report | Object | 2 (Manager) |
| $o_4$: Annual Budget | Object | 3 (Director) |

**Sample Access Checks:**

- **Clerk ($s_2$, level 1) reads Monthly Report ($o_3$, level 2):** $I(o_3) \geq I(s_2) \Rightarrow 2 \geq 1$ ✅ Allowed.
- **Intern ($s_1$, level 0) writes to Annual Budget ($o_4$, level 3):** $I(s_1) \geq I(o_4) \Rightarrow 0 \geq 3$ ❌ **Denied (No Write Up)**.
- **Manager ($s_3$, level 2) reads Draft Notes ($o_1$, level 0):** $I(o_1) \geq I(s_3) \Rightarrow 0 \geq 2$ ❌ **Denied (No Read Down)**.
- **Manager invokes Clerk:** $I(s_2) \geq I(s_3) \Rightarrow 1 \geq 2$ ❌ **Denied (Invocation violated)**.

**Conclusion:** The Biba rules prevent both upward modification and downward contamination, ensuring end-to-end integrity.

---

#### **(b) Discuss the limitations of the Biba Model and how Clark-Wilson or Lipner’s Integrity Matrix addresses them. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Limitations of Biba (4 marks):**

1. **Overly restrictive:** The No Read Down rule prevents useful access to public reference data.
2. **No concept of roles or separation of duties.**
3. **Lacks well-formed transaction support** — the model is not concerned with *how* writes are performed.
4. **Static integrity levels** — no concept of dynamic trust degradation or recovery.

**How Clark-Wilson Addresses It (2 marks):**

- Enforces **well-formed transactions** (valid state transitions).
- Supports **separation of duties** between users and certifiers.
- Uses **constrained data items (CDIs)** and **unconstrained data items (UDIs)**.

**How Lipner’s Integrity Matrix Addresses It (1 mark):**

- Combines Biba’s lattice with **Bell-LaPadula** to handle both **integrity and confidentiality** simultaneously — used in commercial OS designs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse “No Read Down” with BLP’s “No Read Up.”** Students commonly mix up the directions — this is the **#1 reason for losing 2–3 marks** in Biba questions.
> - **Always state the lattice explicitly** when writing the Biba rules — examiners award 2 marks just for the lattice notation.
> - When asked “Compare with BLP,” mention **both** the rule inversion *and* the security goal duality.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute Prep):**

- 🧠 **Biba = Integrity Model** (not confidentiality).
- 👨‍💻 **Author:** Kenneth J. Biba, **1977**.
- 🏛️ **Mathematical Basis:** Bounded lattice $(\mathcal{L}, \leq, \oplus, \otimes)$.
- 📏 **Three Axioms:**
  1. Simple Integrity: $\text{Read}(s,o) \iff I(o) \geq I(s)$ → **No Read Down**
  2. Star Integrity: $\text{Write}(s,o) \iff I(s) \geq I(o)$ → **No Write Up**
  3. Invocation: $\text{Invoke}(s_1, s_2) \iff I(s_2) \geq I(s_1)$
- 🔄 **Biba is the Integrity Dual of BLP** (rules are inverted).
- 🧪 **Variants:** Strict Biba, Low-Watermark Biba, Ring Biba.
- 🏦 **Real-world:** Banking, OS kernels, military C2, healthcare EMRs.
- ❌ **Limitations:** Too restrictive, no roles, no transaction support → solved by **Clark-Wilson** and **Lipner’s Matrix**.
- ✍️ **Mnemonic:** **“Biba prevents BAD data from going UP”** — *Biba* blocks *B*ad *A*ccess *D*irection *U*pward.
- 🎯 **Most-Asked KTU Question Pattern:** “Compare Biba and BLP” (14 marks) — always present a **comparison table**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clark-Wilson Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clark-Wilson Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clark-Wilson Integrity Model (1987):** A formal security model developed by David D. Clark and David R. Wilson that addresses **data integrity** (rather than confidentiality) in commercial and industrial computing environments. It is based on the principle of *well-formed transactions* and enforces integrity through constrained data manipulation.

The Clark-Wilson model was published in 1987 as a direct response to the perceived inadequacy of the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model for real-world commercial systems. While BLP focuses on **confidentiality** (preventing unauthorized disclosure), Clark-Wilson shifts the security paradigm to **integrity** (preventing unauthorized modification), which is the primary concern in banking, payroll, accounting, and inventory systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault System

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Modern Bank Branch:**
> Imagine a bank where customers (users) cannot directly walk into the vault (CDI storage) and rearrange currency notes. Instead, every transaction must be carried out by an authorized bank teller (Transformation Procedure) following strict banking protocols. A chief auditor (IVP) periodically inspects the vault to ensure everything is in proper order. No single employee can both *initiate* and *approve* a high-value transaction (separation of duties). This is exactly how Clark-Wilson models real-world commercial data integrity.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

The model defines the following foundational entities (all are **integrity-bearing components** as per KTU syllabus PECST863 Module 4):

- **Constrained Data Item (CDI):** A data item whose integrity is to be preserved. Modifications to CDIs are strictly controlled.
- **Unconstrained Data Item (UDI):** A data item that is *not* protected by the model — it can be freely modified but must undergo validation before becoming a CDI.
- **Transformation Procedure (TP):** A well-defined, certified procedure that manipulates CDIs. It transforms the system from one valid integrity state to another.
- **Integrity Verification Procedure (IVP):** A procedure that verifies whether the system CDIs are in a valid integrity state.
- **Access Triple (Subject, TP, CDI):** The fundamental access control binding — specifies which subject can execute which TP on which CDI.
- **Separation of Duties:** A principle requiring that no single user can both modify and verify data, preventing fraud.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST863 Module 4):** Students must understand *why* Clark-Wilson replaced the pure-confidentiality paradigm of BLP for commercial applications and be able to identify the role of each component in a real-world access control scenario.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Clark-Wilson: Certification vs Enforcement

The model achieves its integrity guarantees through **two distinct classes of rules**:

#### 2.1.1 Certification Rules (C1 – C5) — *Correctness of the System*

These are **policy-level assertions** that describe what the system *should* do to preserve integrity. They are verified by the system's security officer or auditor.

- **C1:** When an IVP is executed, all CDIs must be verified to be in a valid state.
- **C2:** All TPs must transform CDIs from a valid state to another valid state. (The *well-formed transaction* property.)
- **C3:** The access triple **(Subject, TP, CDI)** relation must enforce the *separation of duties* principle.
- **C4:** All TPs must write to a *log* (audit trail) sufficient to reconstruct the operation.
- **C5:** Any TP that handles a UDI must either *validate* it (converting it to a CDI) or *reject* it — never silently mix untrusted input with trusted CDIs.

#### 2.1.2 Enforcement Rules (E1 – E4) — *System Mechanism*

These are **mandatory implementation requirements** enforced by the trusted computing base (TCB).

- **E1:** The system must maintain and enforce the certified access triple relations.
- **E2:** The system must associate each user with a valid set of access triples.
- **E3:** The system must *authenticate* the identity of every user requesting access.
- **E4:** Only the *security officer* (a privileged role) may change the set of access triples — no ordinary user can escalate privileges.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a high-yield reference for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments. All 9 rules are mandatory recall items for Module 4 of PECST863.

| Rule ID | Type | Function | KTU Exam Tip |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **C1** | Certification | IVP validates CDIs on execution | Often asked as 3-mark short answer |
| **C2** | Certification | TP must preserve valid state transitions | Core *well-formed transaction* principle |
| **C3** | Certification | Enforces separation of duties via triples | Connects to C4 audit trail |
| **C4** | Certification | TP must generate reconstructable audit log | Essential for forensic integrity |
| **C5** | Certification | UDIs validated before becoming CDIs | Prevents *contamination* of trusted data |
| **E1** | Enforcement | System must enforce triple relations | Mandatory system-level control |
| **E2** | Enforcement | User-to-triple binding maintained | Bridges authentication to authorization |
| **E3** | Enforcement | User identity must be authenticated | Standard login/credential mechanism |
| **E4** | Enforcement | Only security officer can modify triples | Privilege escalation prevention |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Clark-Wilson is the foundational integrity model behind:

- **Database Management Systems (DBMS):** Enforcing ACID transactions and trigger-based validation.
- **Banking & Financial Software:** Core banking platforms use its principles for transaction logging and dual-control approvals.
- **E-commerce & ERP Systems:** SAP and Oracle ERP modules implement TP/CDI semantics for inventory and ledger integrity.
- **Healthcare Records Systems:** Ensures only validated prescriptions can modify patient record CDIs.
- **Aerospace & SCADA Systems:** Prevents unauthorized command injection by enforcing well-formed command procedures.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Well-Formed Transactions

The core innovation of Clark-Wilson is the rejection of arbitrary data modification. In a BLP-style read/write model, a user with write privilege could write *any* value, possibly corrupting system semantics. Clark-Wilson fixes this by saying:

> **A user may only modify data through a pre-certified procedure that mathematically guarantees a valid state transition.**

This shifts the security burden from *controlling who can write* to *controlling what transformation is applied* — a far more practical approach for commercial systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Case Study — University Payroll System

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A university uses a payroll database. Faculty salaries are stored in CDIs. Payroll officers (subjects) can run the *salary-calculation* procedure (TP) but cannot directly edit salary values. An external auditor runs IVPs monthly.

**Step 1: Identify CDIs and UDIs**

The model's first analytical step is to classify every data item:

- **CDIs:** $\text{Salary\_Record}$, $\text{Bank\_Account\_Mapping}$, $\text{Employee\_Master}$
- **UDIs:** $\text{External\_Tax\_Data\_Feed}$, $\text{Submitted\_Bank\_Form\_PDF}$

> **Logic:** External feeds are *unconstrained* until validated (Rule C5).

**Step 2: Define Transformation Procedures (TPs)**

Each TP is a deterministic, well-defined operation. For the payroll system:

- $\text{TP}_{\text{calc}}$: Calculates monthly salary from base + allowances − deductions.
- $\text{TP}_{\text{credit}}$: Credits net salary to mapped bank account.
- $\text{TP}_{\text{validate}}$: Validates an external feed UDI and promotes it to a CDI.

**Step 3: Define Integrity Verification Procedures (IVPs)**

- $\text{IVP}_{\text{balance}}$: Verifies that *total debits = total credits* across all accounts.
- $\text{IVP}_{\text{integrity}}$: Confirms all employee records have valid bank account mappings.

**Step 4: Construct Access Triples (Rule C3, E1, E2)**

$$
\text{AccessTriples} = \left\{ (\text{PayrollOfficer},\ \text{TP}_{\text{calc}},\ \text{Salary\_Record}),\ (\text{Auditor},\ \text{IVP}_{\text{balance}},\ \text{AllCDIs}),\ (\text{DataEntry},\ \text{TP}_{\text{validate}},\ \text{UDIs}) \right\}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Separation of Duties (Rule C3)**

No single user can both *calculate* AND *credit* salaries. Hence:

$$
\text{Permissions}(\text{PayrollOfficer}) \cap \text{Permissions}(\text{AccountsClerk}) = \emptyset
$$

> **Logic:** If a single user could calculate *and* credit, they could fabricate a ghost employee and route the salary to themselves.

**Step 6: Validate State Transitions (Rule C2)**

The state transition is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{State}_{\text{valid}} \xrightarrow{\text{TP}} \text{State}_{\text{valid}}
$$

For example, before $\text{TP}_{\text{calc}}$, the gross pay field must be non-negative; after $\text{TP}_{\text{calc}}$, the net pay must equal gross minus tax. The TP guarantees this invariant.

**Step 7: Audit Logging (Rule C4)**

Each TP execution appends a record:

$$
\text{LogEntry} = \langle \text{UserID},\ \text{TP},\ \text{TimeStamp},\ \text{CDI}_{\text{old}},\ \text{CDI}_{\text{new}} \rangle
$$

This log is itself a CDI protected from modification.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Clark-Wilson vs Bell-LaPadula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** This matrix is a frequent 14-mark question in PECST863 Module 4 assessments.

| Property | Bell-LaPadula (BLP) | Clark-Wilson (CW) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Primary Focus** | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| **Application Domain** | Military / classified data | Commercial / industrial systems |
| **Access Basis** | Security labels (Lattice) | Access triples (Subject, TP, CDI) |
| **Modification Control** | Not primary concern | Strict — only via TPs |
| **Trust Model** | Trusted subjects may violate \* property | Strict separation of duties |
| **Audit Requirement** | Optional | Mandatory (C4) |
| **External Data Handling** | Not addressed | Explicit UDI → CDI validation (C5) |
| **Mathematical Foundation** | Lattice theory | Transactional state-machine model |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Triple Cardinality Analysis
