- **Tooling Readiness** — pre-installing forensic toolkits, log aggregators, **SIEM** (Security Information and Event Management) platforms, isolated clean OS images for forensics.
- **Training & Drills** — tabletop exercises, red-team/blue-team simulations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Preparation" paradox:** *You cannot prepare for an incident during the incident.* All preparation is investment in future calm.

### Phase 2: Detection and Analysis
This is where potential incidents are identified, validated, and prioritized.

- **Detection Sources (Vectors):**
  - **SIEM Alerts** (correlated log analysis)
  - **IDS/IPS signatures** (Snort, Suricata)
  - **User reports** (often the most underrated source!)
  - **Honeypots / Honeynets**
  - **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** agents
  - **Threat Intelligence Feeds** (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK updates)
  - **Anomaly detection** (UEBA — User and Entity Behavior Analytics)

- **Triage Process** — the analyst asks:
  1. Is this a *true positive*, *false positive*, or *benign true positive*?
  2. What is the **scope** (how many systems affected)?
  3. What is the **severity** (Critical / High / Medium / Low)?
  4. What is the **impact** to CIA Triad?

- **Incident Classification Categories (per NIST):**
  - **CAT 0 — Exercise / Drill** (no real incident)
  - **CAT 1 — Unauthorized Access**
  - **CAT 2 — Denial of Service**
  - **CAT 3 — Malicious Code** (malware, ransomware)
  - **CAT 4 — Improper Usage** (policy violation)
  - **CAT 5 — Scans/Probes/Attempted Access**
  - **CAT 6 — Investigation** (unconfirmed)

### Phase 3: Containment, Eradication, and Recovery

#### 3a. Containment
Stop the bleeding. Two sub-strategies:

- **Short-term containment** — quick, often disruptive: disconnect the affected system from the network, disable compromised accounts, block IP addresses at the firewall.
- **Long-term containment** — surgical: apply patches, segment the network, set up temporary access controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Containment:** *Never power off or wipe a compromised system before forensic imaging.* Doing so destroys volatile evidence (RAM, network connections, running processes).

#### 3b. Eradication
Remove the threat completely:
- Delete malware, dropper files, web shells
- Reset all compromised credentials and cryptographic keys
- Patch the exploited vulnerability that enabled the incident
- Rebuild systems from known-good golden images (preferred over in-place cleaning)

#### 3c. Recovery
Restore operations safely:
- Restore data from verified clean backups (the **3-2-1 backup rule**: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite)
- Validate system integrity through continuous monitoring
- Implement enhanced logging on the recovered system
- Phased return to production (don't reconnect everything at once!)

### Phase 4: Post-Incident Activity
The most under-appreciated, yet the most valuable phase.

- **Lessons Learned Meeting** (a "blameless post-mortem")
- **Final Incident Report** — chronological, factual, evidence-cited
- **Metric Calculation** — actual MTTD, MTTR, MTTC for the incident
- **IRP Update** — feed findings back into the Preparation phase
- **Evidence Retention** — store forensic images per legal hold requirements (often **7 years** for regulated industries)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{MTTD}$ | Mean Time To Detect | Lower is better; measures detection capability |
| $\text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time To Respond/Remediate | Measures IR team efficiency |
| $\text{MTTC}$ | Mean Time To Contain | Critical for blast-radius control |
| $\text{SLA}$ | Service Level Agreement for response | Often **$15$ min** for Critical, **$1$ hr** for High |
| $\text{SEV-1}$ | Severity 1 (Critical) | Full organizational mobilization |
| $\text{IRP}$ | Incident Response Plan | Mandatory pre-requisite document |
| $\text{CSIRT}$ | Computer Security Incident Response Team | The "First Responders" of cybersecurity |
| $\text{SIEM}$ | Security Information & Event Management | Real-time log correlation engine |
| $\text{EDR}$ | Endpoint Detection & Response | Modern endpoint-level detection |
| $\text{UEBA}$ | User & Entity Behavior Analytics | Detects insider threats via ML |
| $\text{CAT}$ | NIST Incident Category Number | Universal incident classification |
| $\text{TLP}$ | Traffic Light Protocol (Red/Amber/Green/White) | Information sharing classification |
| $\text{CVSS}$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | $0.0$ to $10.0$ vulnerability severity |
| $\text{IOC}$ | Indicator of Compromise | Hashes, IPs, domains, file paths |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain incident handling," always quote these **NIST 4 phases in order**. Examiners award marks for the correct sequence.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Incident handling is foundational in:
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA)** — handling attacks on power grids
- **Cloud-native DevSecOps** — automated incident playbooks in AWS/Azure
- **Medical Device Security** — handling vulnerabilities in pacemakers and infusion pumps
- **Automotive Cybersecurity** — handling CAN bus intrusions in connected vehicles
- **Smart City Infrastructure** — handling DDoS on emergency services networks
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Incident Severity Scoring Model

In real-world SOCs, severity is often calculated via a weighted formula. Below is the step-by-step derivation of the **Composite Incident Severity Score (CISS)** as used in NIST-aligned incident classification.

### Step 1: Define the Input Variables

We begin with the four fundamental impact dimensions, each scored on a normalized **$0$ to $10$** scale (where **$0$ = no impact, $10$ = catastrophic impact**):

- $C$ = Confidentiality impact
- $I$ = Integrity impact
- $A$ = Availability impact
- $R$ = Recoverability effort (higher = harder to recover)

### Step 2: Apply Domain-Specific Weights

Based on the organization's **risk appetite**, each dimension is assigned a weight $w_x$ such that:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_C + w_I + w_A + w_R = 1.0
\end{aligned}$$

For a **financial institution** (typical KTU banking-domain case study), a reasonable weighting is:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_C = 0.30, \quad w_I = 0.40, \quad w_A = 0.20, \quad w_R = 0.10
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** Banks fear data *tampering* (integrity) more than brief downtime.

### Step 3: Compute the Composite Score

The **Composite Incident Severity Score (CISS)** is computed as the weighted sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CISS} = (w_C \cdot C) + (w_I \cdot I) + (w_A \cdot A) + (w_R \cdot R)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Map CISS to Severity Tiers

The continuous score is then mapped to discrete severity tiers using thresholds:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Severity} = \begin{cases}
\text{Critical (SEV-1)} & \text{if } 8.0 \le \text{CISS} \le 10.0 \\
\text{High (SEV-2)} & \text{if } 6.0 \le \text{CISS} < 8.0 \\
\text{Medium (SEV-3)} & \text{if } 3.0 \le \text{CISS} < 6.0 \\
\text{Low (SEV-4)} & \text{if } 0.0 \le \text{CISS} < 3.0
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Worked Example

**Scenario:** A ransomware attack encrypted $50$ file servers. Assessment values:

- Confidentiality impact: $C = 7$ (data exfiltrated)
- Integrity impact: $I = 9$ (files encrypted, modified)
- Availability impact: $A = 8$ (servers down)
- Recoverability: $R = 6$ (backups available but restoration takes days)

Plugging into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CISS} &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.40 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 2.10 + 3.60 + 1.60 + 0.60 \\
&= 7.90
\end{aligned}$$

Mapping to the tier table:

$$\begin{aligned}
6.0 \le 7.90 < 8.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SEV-2 (High)}
\end{aligned}$$

A one-point increase in $I$ would push this to **SEV-1 Critical**, demonstrating how the formula is sensitive to the integrity dimension in a banking context.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Containment Strategy Decision Tree

| Indicator Detected | Recommended Short-Term Action | Forensic Pre-Requisite | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Malware on single endpoint | Disconnect from LAN, not power-off | Capture RAM image (volatile) | ❌ Don't power off! |
| Active data exfiltration to C2 server | Block C2 IP at perimeter firewall | Preserve NetFlow logs | ❌ Don't tip off attacker |
| Compromised admin account | Disable account, rotate all secrets | Capture authentication logs | ❌ Don't just change password |
| Ransomware spreading laterally | Network segmentation, isolate VLAN | Capture memory of patient zero | ❌ Don't pay ransom first |
| Insider data theft | Revoke access, preserve DLP logs | Image HR email, badge logs | ❌ Don't confront employee |
| DDoS attack | Activate scrubbing center, null-route | Capture packet captures (PCAP) | ❌ Don't block legitimate users |

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation: MTTD and MTTR

The **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** across $n$ incidents is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})
\end{aligned}$$

where $t_{\text{occur},i}$ is the timestamp of incident $i$'s actual occurrence and $t_{\text{detect},i}$ is when the SOC first identified it.

Similarly, the **Mean Time To Respond (MTTR)** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{\text{resolved},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})
\end{aligned}$$

The **Total Incident Lifecycle Time** is then:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} = \text{MTTD} + \text{MTTR}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering insight:** Industry research (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2023–2024) consistently shows that *every additional hour of MTTD increases breach cost by approximately 1.5%*. This makes the **Preparation** and **Detection** phases the highest-ROI investments in any incident handling program.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIST Incident Response Life Cycle — State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preparation: Policy, Team, Tools, Training] --> B[Detection and Analysis: Identify and Triage]
    B --> C{True Positive?}
    C -->|No| D[Close as False Positive Log Entry]
    C -->|Yes| E[Containment: Stop the Bleeding]
    E --> F[Eradication: Remove Threat Completely]
    F --> G[Recovery: Restore and Validate]
    G --> H[Post Incident Activity: Lessons Learned]
    H --> A
    D --> A
```

> **Node Identifier Notes:** All IDs (`A` through `H`) are single uppercase letters prefixed with `A`, fully alphanumeric, no reserved keywords used.

## 4.2 CSIRT Communication and Escalation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[End User Reports Anomaly] --> S1[SOC Tier 1 Analyst: Triage]
    S1 --> S2{Severity Tier}
    S2 -->|SEV-4 Low| S3[Tier 2 Analyst: Investigate]
    S2 -->|SEV-3 Medium| S4[Tier 3 Senior Analyst: Contain]
    S2 -->|SEV-2 High| S5[Incident Manager: Coordinate]
    S2 -->|SEV-1 Critical| S6[CSIRT Commander and CISO: Full Mobilization]
    S3 --> S7[Resolve and Document]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S8[Legal, PR, Executive Briefing]
    S7 --> S9[Post Incident Report]
    S8 --> S9
```

## 4.3 Incident Handling Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputsLayer[Detection Inputs]
        I1[SIEM Logs]
        I2[IDS IPS Alerts]
        I3[User Reports]
        I4[Threat Intel Feeds]
        I5[EDR Telemetry]
    end
    subgraph TriageLayer[Triage and Analysis Layer]
        T1[Correlation Engine]
        T2[Severity Scoring CISS]
        T3[IOC Matching]
    end
    subgraph ActionLayer[Response Action Layer]
        A1[Short Term Containment]
        A2[Forensic Imaging]
        A3[Eradication and Patching]
        A4[Recovery Validation]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Post Incident Layer]
        O1[Incident Report]
        O2[Lessons Learned DB]
        O3[IRP Update]
    end
    I1 --> T1
    I2 --> T1
    I3 --> T1
    I4 --> T3
    I5 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 -.feedback loop.-> I1
```

> **Architecture Note:** This block-level diagram maps the *functional flow* of an enterprise-grade incident handling system — from raw detection signals to organizational learning feedback. The dashed feedback loop represents the **continuous improvement** principle of NIST SP 800-61.

## 4.4 Containment Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Incident Confirmed] --> P1{Is attacker still active?}
    P1 -->|Yes| P2[Short Term Containment: Disconnect, Block IPs]
    P1 -->|No| P3[Long Term Containment: Patch, Segment]
    P2 --> P4{Forensic image captured?}
    P4 -->|No| P5[STOP: Image RAM and Disk First]
    P4 -->|Yes| P6[Proceed to Eradication]
    P5 --> P6
    P3 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Rebuild from Golden Image]
    P7 --> P8[Restore from 3-2-1 Backup]
    P8 --> P9[Monitor in Quarantine VLAN]
    P9 --> P10[Return to Production]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Security Incident. List the NIST categories used for incident classification. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **security incident** is any adverse event that threatens the confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA) of information systems, or that violates an organization's security policies or acceptable use policies.

The **NIST SP 800-61** classifies incidents into the following categories:

- **CAT 0** — Exercise / Drill (no real-world impact)
- **CAT 1** — Unauthorized Access
- **CAT 2** — Denial of Service (DoS/DDoS)
- **CAT 3** — Malicious Code (malware, ransomware, worms)
- **CAT 4** — Improper or Inappropriate Usage (policy violations)
- **CAT 5** — Scans, Probes, or Attempted Access
- **CAT 6** — Investigation (unconfirmed anomalous activity)

> **KTU Board Tip:** Mentioning both the *definition* AND listing all 7 categories typically earns full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: What is a CSIRT? Mention any four members of a typical CSIRT team. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **CSIRT (Computer Security Incident Response Team)** is a dedicated, trained group of professionals responsible for receiving, reviewing, and responding to computer security incidents within an organization. It acts as the "first responder" for cyber emergencies.

Four typical members of a CSIRT include:

1. **Incident Manager (IM)** — the team lead who coordinates the overall response and makes critical decisions.
2. **Security Analyst** — performs triage, log analysis, and initial investigation of alerts.
3. **Forensic Investigator** — specializes in evidence collection, chain-of-custody, and root-cause analysis.
4. **Legal/Compliance Officer** — ensures all actions comply with cyber laws, regulations, and manages evidence for potential litigation.

> **Examiner's Note:** Acceptable alternative members include *Public Relations Officer, HR Representative, Network Engineer, System Administrator*. Naming roles with brief functions fetches full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compromise Assessment & Response `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A mid-sized e-commerce company's web server has been compromised. Logs show unauthorized administrative logins from an IP in Eastern Europe, a previously unknown PHP web shell was discovered in `/var/www/html/`, and customer credit card data appears to have been exfiltrated.

**(a)** Identify the **phase of incident handling** this scenario falls under and describe in detail the actions to be taken in that phase, with special focus on **containment strategies**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **Composite Incident Severity Score (CISS)** for this incident, using the banking-domain weights $w_C = 0.30, w_I = 0.40, w_A = 0.20, w_R = 0.10$, given the impact scores $C = 9, I = 8, A = 7, R = 5$. State the resulting severity tier. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The scenario describes a **CAT 1 — Unauthorized Access** with **CAT 3 — Malicious Code** overlay, and clear exfiltration evidence. This triggers **Phase 3: Containment, Eradication, and Recovery**.

**Containment actions (Short-term):**

1. **Isolate the web server** by removing it from the production VLAN and placing it in a quarantine VLAN. Do NOT power off — this preserves volatile memory evidence.
2. **Block the attacker's IP** at the perimeter firewall and WAF immediately to stop further C2 (command-and-control) communication.
3. **Disable the compromised admin account** and force password rotation across all privileged accounts.
4. **Revoke active sessions** and invalidate all API tokens issued to the compromised account.

**Containment actions (Long-term):**

5. Apply a **virtual patch** via the WAF for the exploited PHP vulnerability until the official patch is tested.
6. **Network micro-segmentation** to prevent lateral movement to the database server.
7. Implement **enhanced logging** (full packet capture at the perimeter) to capture any ongoing exfiltration attempts.

**Eradication actions:**

8. Remove the **PHP web shell** and any other backdoors (cron jobs, modified binaries, hidden user accounts).
9. Rebuild the web server from a **known-good golden image** rather than attempting in-place cleaning.
10. Patch the underlying PHP/CMS vulnerability and update all third-party libraries.

**Recovery actions:**

11. Restore the web server from the **most recent clean backup** (verified by hash comparison, following the **3-2-1 backup rule**).
12. Validate system integrity through **file integrity monitoring (FIM)** and **vulnerability scanning**.
13. Conduct **penetration testing** before reconnecting to production.
14. Monitor the recovered system with **enhanced EDR rules** for at least 30 days.

> **Marks Distribution:** (i) Phase identification — 1 mark, (ii) Short-term containment — 2 marks, (iii) Long-term containment — 2 marks, (iv) Eradication — 1 mark, (v) Recovery — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given:
- $C = 9$, $I = 8$, $A = 7$, $R = 5$
- $w_C = 0.30$, $w_I = 0.40$, $w_A = 0.20$, $w_R = 0.10$

Apply the CISS formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CISS} &= (w_C \cdot C) + (w_I \cdot I) + (w_A \cdot A) + (w_R \cdot R) \\
&= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.40 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 5) \\
&= 2.70 + 3.20 + 1.40 + 0.50 \\
&= 7.80
\end{aligned}$$

Mapping to the severity tier table:

$$\begin{aligned}
6.0 \le 7.80 < 8.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SEV-2 (High)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The incident is classified as **SEV-2 (High Severity)**. The organization must activate the CSIRT, notify senior management, and prepare for potential regulatory disclosure obligations (e.g., **PCI-DSS 72-hour notification** to card brands and the acquiring bank).

> **Marks Distribution:** (i) Correct formula statement — 1 mark, (ii) Substitutions — 1 mark, (iii) Arithmetic — 3 marks, (iv) Correct tier mapping and conclusion — 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — MTTD/MTTR Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A financial services company recorded the following incident timestamps for five security incidents over Q1 2024:

| Incident | Occurrence ($t_{\text{occur}}$) | Detection ($t_{\text{detect}}$) | Resolution ($t_{\text{resolved}}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| I-1 | 02 Jan, 03:00 | 02 Jan, 09:00 | 02 Jan, 18:00 |
| I-2 | 15 Jan, 11:00 | 15 Jan, 23:00 | 16 Jan, 07:00 |
| I-3 | 07 Feb, 05:00 | 07 Feb, 06:30 | 07 Feb, 14:00 |
| I-4 | 21 Feb, 22:00 | 23 Feb, 10:00 | 24 Feb, 16:00 |
| I-5 | 18 Mar, 14:00 | 18 Mar, 17:00 | 19 Mar, 02:00 |

**(a)** Calculate the **MTTD** and **MTTR** for the quarter, and identify which incident is the worst outlier. (7 marks)

**(b)** Recommend specific **Preparation-phase** improvements to reduce the MTTD, and discuss the role of the **post-incident activity phase** in preventing recurrence of incident I-4. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Calculate individual detection and response times (in hours):

| Incident | $t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{occur}}$ (hours) | $t_{\text{resolved}} - t_{\text{detect}}$ (hours) |
|---|---|---|
| I-1 | $6.0$ | $9.0$ |
| I-2 | $12.0$ | $8.0$ |
| I-3 | $1.5$ | $7.5$ |
| I-4 | $36.0$ | $30.0$ |
| I-5 | $3.0$ | $9.0$ |

**Step 2:** Compute the means:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTD} &= \frac{1}{5} (6.0 + 12.0 + 1.5 + 36.0 + 3.0) \\
&= \frac{58.5}{5} \\
&= 11.7 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{1}{5} (9.0 + 8.0 + 7.5 + 30.0 + 9.0) \\
&= \frac{63.5}{5} \\
&= 12.7 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the worst outlier:

- I-4 has MTTD of **$36$ hours** (3.08x the mean) and MTTR of **$30$ hours** (2.36x the mean). It is a *double outlier* and the worst-performing incident in the dataset.

> **Marks Distribution:** (i) Individual time calculations — 2 marks, (ii) MTTD — 1.5 marks, (iii) MTTR — 1.5 marks, (iv) Outlier identification with justification — 2 marks.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Preparation-Phase Improvements to Reduce MTTD:**

1. **Deploy a modern SIEM** with **UEBA (User & Entity Behavior Analytics)** to detect behavioral anomalies in real time. The 36-hour MTTD for I-4 suggests logs were either uncollected or not correlated.
2. **Tune IDS/IPS signatures** to match the latest MITRE ATT&CK techniques relevant to financial sector threats.
3. **Implement 24/7 SOC monitoring** — the 22:00 occurrence of I-4 was detected only at 10:00 the next day (after a weekend), strongly suggesting a coverage gap.
4. **Establish clear escalation policies** with defined **SLAs** — Critical incidents: $15$ min response, High: $1$ hour, Medium: $4$ hours.
5. **Threat intelligence integration** — subscribe to FS-ISAC or sector-specific feeds for early-warning on emerging IOCs.
6. **Tabletop exercises and red-team drills** to ensure the team can detect novel attack patterns, not just signature-based ones.

**Post-Incident Activity for Incident I-4:**

7. Conduct a **blameless post-mortem** within 72 hours of incident closure, attended by all CSIRT members, system owners, and a legal observer.
8. Write a **detailed final incident report** documenting:
   - The attack vector and exploited vulnerability
   - Why detection took 36 hours
   - Why resolution took 30 hours
   - The full chronological timeline (chain of custody for evidence)
9. **Update the Incident Response Plan (IRP)** with new playbooks for this attack class.
10. Feed **newly discovered IOCs** into the SIEM to enable early detection of similar future attacks.
11. Implement **preventive controls** (e.g., additional network segmentation, MFA enforcement, or EDR rules) to address the root cause.
12. **Track corrective actions** with deadlines and ownership — a post-mortem without action items is just a meeting.

> **Marks Distribution:** (i) At least 3 distinct Preparation improvements — 3 marks, (ii) At least 3 distinct Post-Incident activities — 3 marks, (iii) Coherent justification linked to I-4 — 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the phase order** — Students often merge *Detection* with *Containment* or skip the *Post-Incident* phase. Always state the 4 phases in NIST order.
> 2. **Confusing MTTD with MTTR** — MTTD = time from *occurrence* to *detection*. MTTR = time from *detection* to *resolution*. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting forensic preservation** — Recommending "format the disk and reinstall" without mentioning *forensic imaging* is a critical mistake that costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Vague roles** — Saying "the team handles it" is poor. Always name the *Incident Manager, Forensic Investigator, Legal Officer* explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the lessons-learned phase** — Examiners view this as showing incomplete understanding. Always end with Post-Incident Activity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Handling Incidents**

- ✅ Incident handling follows the **NIST SP 800-61 4-phase Life Cycle**: **Preparation → Detection and Analysis → Containment, Eradication, Recovery → Post-Incident Activity**.
- ✅ The **CSIRT** is the operational team; the **Incident Manager** is the leader during an active incident.
- ✅ A *true positive* incident must be triaged for **scope, severity, and impact** on the CIA Triad.
- ✅ Containment has two layers: **short-term** (disconnect, block) and **long-term** (patch, segment).
- ✅ **Never power off or wipe** a compromised system before forensic imaging — RAM contains volatile evidence.
- ✅ The **3-2-1 backup rule** (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite) is the recovery safety net.
- ✅ **MTTD** measures detection speed; **MTTR** measures response speed; both are SOC KPIs.
- ✅ The **CISS** formula weights CIA + Recoverability per organizational risk appetite.
- ✅ NIST incident categories are **CAT 0 through CAT 6** (Exercise, Unauthorized Access, DoS, Malicious Code, Improper Usage, Scans, Investigation).
- ✅ **Post-Incident Activity** is *not optional* — it feeds lessons back into Preparation, completing the continuous improvement loop.
- ✅ **SEV-1 (Critical)** incidents trigger full organizational mobilization, legal/PR involvement, and often regulatory notification.
- ✅ **Chain of custody** must be preserved for all forensic evidence to be admissible in court.
- ✅ The **SIEM** correlates logs; **EDR** monitors endpoints; **UEBA** detects behavioral anomalies; **Threat Intel** provides early warnings.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 Scheme exams, always cite the **NIST framework** by name, present the **4 phases in order**, and conclude with a **lessons-learned reference**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Analysis** is the systematic process of identifying, estimating, and prioritizing risks to organizational operations, assets, individuals, and other entities resulting from the operation of an information system. It is a critical subset of the broader **Risk Management** lifecycle and forms the analytical core of any information security governance framework (e.g., **NIST SP 800-30 Revision 1**, **ISO/IEC 27005:2022**, and **OCTAVE**).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Risk Analysis is treated as the **quantitative and qualitative assessment phase** where threats, vulnerabilities, and the resulting impact on assets are mathematically evaluated to determine the level of risk exposure. The output of this analysis directly feeds into Risk Treatment decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "House Insurance" Model:**
> Imagine you own a house in Kerala during monsoon season. Before buying insurance, the insurance company performs a **Risk Analysis**:
> 1. They **identify assets** (your house, electronics, jewelry).
> 2. They **identify threats** (floods, theft, fire, earthquakes).
> 3. They **identify vulnerabilities** (old wiring, low compound wall, location near river).
> 4. They **estimate impact** (how much damage each threat could cause).
> 5. They **calculate probability** (how often such events happen).
> 6. They **prioritize risks** (flood > theft > earthquake for coastal Kerala).
> 7. They **recommend controls** (waterproofing, CCTV, insurance).
>
> **Information Security Risk Analysis is exactly this — but applied to data, systems, networks, and people.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

The following constants and standard metrics are used throughout the KTU syllabus and must be memorized:

- **SLE (Single Loss Expectancy)** — measured in monetary units (₹ or $)
- **ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence)** — dimensionless integer per year
- **ALE (Annualized Loss Expectancy)** — measured in monetary units per year (₹/year or $/year)
- **EF (Exposure Factor)** — expressed as a **percentage (%)**
- **SLE Range:** 0 ≤ SLE ≤ Total Asset Value
- **ARO Range:** 0 ≤ ARO ≤ ∞ (typically 0.0 to 100.0+)
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** of a control — measured in monetary units
- **ROS (Residual Risk)** — Risk remaining after control implementation

### 1.4 Core Risk Formula (Foundational)

The fundamental relationship in any Risk Analysis is:

> **RISK = f(Threat, Vulnerability, Asset / Impact)**

This is the **risk triangle** that every KTU question pivots upon. All advanced formulas (ALE, ROS, etc.) are mathematical refinements of this relationship.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Risk Analysis Process

The Risk Analysis lifecycle, as defined in **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1**, consists of the following structured steps:

1. **Step 1 — System Characterization**
   - Define the boundary of the IT system.
   - Identify hardware, software, data, people, and processes.
   - Document system interfaces and dependencies.

2. **Step 2 — Threat Identification**
   - Identify potential threat sources (natural, human, environmental).
   - Catalogue threat events (malware injection, DDoS, insider misuse).
   - Estimate the **likelihood** of threat initiation.

3. **Step 3 — Vulnerability Identification**
   - Identify weaknesses that could be exploited (missing patches, weak passwords).
   - Cross-reference threat events with vulnerabilities.
   - Determine the **predisposing conditions**.

4. **Step 4 — Control Analysis (Existing Safeguards)**
   - Identify currently deployed controls (firewalls, IDS, antivirus).
   - Evaluate control effectiveness.
   - Document residual control gaps.

5. **Step 5 — Likelihood Determination**
   - Combine threat likelihood with vulnerability severity.
   - Assign a qualitative (Low/Medium/High) or quantitative (0.0–1.0) score.

6. **Step 6 — Impact Analysis**
   - Determine the adverse effect of threat exploitation.
   - Categorize impact on confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**).

7. **Step 7 — Risk Determination**
   - Combine likelihood and impact to produce a **risk level**.
   - This is the most heavily tested step in KTU exams.

8. **Step 8 — Control Recommendations**
   - Propose new or enhanced controls to reduce risk to acceptable levels.

9. **Step 9 — Results Documentation**
   - Produce the final **Risk Assessment Report**.

### 2.2 Types of Risk Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask for the **comparison** between Qualitative, Quantitative, and Semi-Quantitative approaches. Memorize the differences below.

- **Qualitative Risk Analysis** — Uses descriptive scales (Low, Medium, High). Faster, cheaper, subjective, no monetary calculations.
- **Quantitative Risk Analysis** — Uses numerical values and monetary units. Objective, expensive, time-consuming, requires statistical data.
- **Semi-Quantitative Risk Analysis** — Hybrid approach assigning numerical scores to qualitative categories (e.g., Low = 1, Medium = 2, High = 3). Most commonly used in practice.

### 2.3 Risk Treatment Strategies

After analysis, the organization must choose how to respond:

- **Risk Mitigation (Reduction)** — Implement controls to reduce risk. Most common approach.
- **Risk Transfer** — Shift risk to a third party (insurance, outsourcing, SLA).
- **Risk Avoidance** — Eliminate the risk by removing the asset or discontinuing the activity.
- **Risk Acceptance** — Formally acknowledge and retain the risk if cost of mitigation exceeds impact.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Full Form | Formula | Units | Notes |
|--------|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| SLE | Single Loss Expectancy | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$ | ₹ / $ | One-time loss |
| EF | Exposure Factor | Expressed as percentage (0–100%) | % | Fraction of asset lost |
| ARO | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Number of occurrences per year | events/year | Frequency |
| ALE | Annualized Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | ₹/year | Yearly expected loss |
| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership | $\text{TCO} = \text{Acquisition} + \text{Operation} + \text{Maintenance}$ | ₹ / $ | Cost of control |
| CBA | Cost-Benefit Analysis | $\text{CBA} = \text{ALE(before)} - \text{ALE(after)} - \text{TCO}$ | ₹/year | If CBA > 0, control is justified |
| ROS | Residual Risk | $\text{ROS} = \text{Total Risk} - \text{Control Effectiveness}$ | ₹/year | Risk remaining after control |
| Risk Level | Combined Metric | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Categorical / Numerical | Foundational equation |
| RPN | Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Occurrence} \times \text{Detection}$ | Unitless | FMEA-based metric |

> **Critical Reminder:** In the markdown table above, the absolute value signs are intentionally NOT used. In LaTeX, use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown parsing errors.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Risk Analysis is foundational in:
- **Cybersecurity** — Penetration testing, ISO 27001 audits, SOC2 compliance.
- **Civil Engineering** — Structural failure probability, dam safety analysis.
- **Software Engineering** — Threat modeling (STRIDE, DREAD), DevSecOps pipelines.
- **Healthcare IT** — HIPAA risk assessments, medical device security.
- **Financial Sector** — RBI cybersecurity framework, PCI-DSS audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The ALE Equation from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** A web server in a KTU campus has an asset value of ₹5,00,000. A ransomware attack is expected to compromise 40% of the server. Historical data shows such attacks occur twice per year on similar servers. Calculate the **ALE**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data**
- Asset Value (AV) = ₹5,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 40% = 0.40
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) = 2 events/year

**Step 2 — Apply the SLE Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \\
\text{SLE} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 \\
\text{SLE} &= 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ per incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The SLE represents the monetary loss the organization would suffer from a **single occurrence** of the threat event. Multiplying the asset's total value by the fraction damaged (0.40) gives the loss in one incident.

**Step 3 — Apply the ALE Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE} &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2 \\
\text{ALE} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Since the threat is expected to occur 2 times annually, the total expected annual loss is the single-incident loss multiplied by the yearly frequency. The organization should be willing to spend **up to ₹4,00,000 per year** on controls to mitigate this risk.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) for a Control

**Problem Statement:** Management proposes installing an enterprise-grade EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) solution. After implementation, the new ALE drops to ₹50,000/year, and the EDR costs ₹1,20,000/year to operate. Should the organization proceed?

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs**
- ALE (before control) = ₹4,00,000/year (from Derivation 1)
- ALE (after control) = ₹50,000/year
- TCO of EDR = ₹1,20,000/year

**Step 2 — Compute Risk Reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Reduction} &= \text{ALE(before)} - \text{ALE(after)} \\
\text{Risk Reduction} &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 \\
\text{Risk Reduction} &= 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Net Savings (CBA)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CBA} &= \text{Risk Reduction} - \text{TCO} \\
\text{CBA} &= 3{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{CBA} &= 2{,}30{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule**

> **Decision:** Since **CBA = ₹2,30,000 > 0**, the EDR solution is **financially justified**. The organization saves ₹2,30,000 net per year by deploying the control.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Residual Risk Calculation

**Problem Statement:** The total identified risk for a database server is ₹10,00,000/year. Existing firewalls and access controls have reduced this by 70%. After deploying a new SIEM, the risk is further reduced by 20% of the residual. What is the final residual risk after both control layers?

**Step 1 — Apply First Control Effectiveness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROS}_1 &= \text{Total Risk} \times (1 - 0.70) \\
\text{ROS}_1 &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 \\
\text{ROS}_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* A control that reduces risk by 70% leaves 30% of the original risk as residual.

**Step 2 — Apply Second Control on the New Residual**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROS}_2 &= \text{ROS}_1 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
\text{ROS}_2 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 \\
\text{ROS}_2 &= 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer**

> The final **Residual Risk** is **₹2,40,000 per year**. Management must decide whether to accept this risk, transfer it, or invest in additional controls.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Risk Level Determination (Qualitative 5×5 Matrix)

| **Likelihood ↓ \ Impact →** | **Insignificant (1)** | **Minor (2)** | **Moderate (3)** | **Major (4)** | **Catastrophic (5)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Almost Certain (5)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **Likely (4)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **Possible (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **Unlikely (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Rare (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Score Interpretation:**
> - **1–4:** Low Risk (Accept / Monitor)
> - **5–12:** Medium Risk (Mitigate with standard controls)
> - **13–25:** High Risk (Immediate mitigation mandatory)

### 3.5 Analytical Example Using the Matrix

**Scenario:** A phishing attack has a likelihood of **Likely (4)** and a likely impact of **Major (4)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Score} &= \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact} \\
\text{Risk Score} &= 4 \times 4 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Score = 16 falls in the **High Risk** band. The organization must deploy immediate controls: email filtering, anti-phishing training, MFA, and incident response drills.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Risk Analysis Process Flow (NIST SP 800-30 Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: System Characterization] --> B[Step2: Threat Identification]
    B --> C[Step3: Vulnerability Identification]
    C --> D[Step4: Control Analysis]
    D --> E[Step5: Likelihood Determination]
    E --> F[Step6: Impact Analysis]
    F --> G[Step7: Risk Determination]
    G --> H[Step8: Control Recommendations]
    H --> I[Step9: Results Documentation]
    I --> J{Risk Acceptable?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Monitor and Review]
    J -->|No| L[Step10: Risk Treatment]
    L --> M[Implement Controls]
    M --> N[Calculate Residual Risk]
    N --> J
```

*Explanation:* The diagram illustrates the iterative, cyclical nature of Risk Analysis per NIST SP 800-30. The process is **not linear** — it loops back from Risk Treatment to Risk Determination until the residual risk falls within the organization's risk appetite.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Quantitative Risk Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Asset Inventory] --> B[Asset Valuation]
    B --> C[Threat Catalog]
    C --> D[Vulnerability Scan]
    D --> E[SLE Computation]
    E --> F[ARO Estimation]
    F --> G[ALE Computation]
    G --> H[Risk Ranking]
    H --> I{Risk vs Threshold}
    I -->|Above| J[Recommend Controls]
    I -->|Below| K[Accept and Monitor]
    J --> L[Cost Benefit Analysis]
    L --> M{Control Justified?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Deploy Control]
    M -->|No| O[Reject / Transfer Risk]
    N --> P[Residual Risk Recalculation]
    P --> I
```

*Explanation:* This block diagram maps the **quantitative risk analysis pipeline** showing how asset valuation feeds into SLE/ARO/ALE calculations, and how cost-benefit analysis gates control deployment.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Risk Treatment Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identified Risk] --> B{Cost of Mitigation?}
    B -->|Less than Impact| C[MITIGATE]
    B -->|Greater than Impact| D{Transfer Possible?}
    D -->|Yes| E[TRANSFER Insurance or SLA]
    D -->|No| F{Activity Essential?}
    F -->|No| G[AVOID Discontinue Activity]
    F -->|Yes| H[ACCEPT Document and Monitor]
    C --> I[Implement Control]
    E --> J[Sign Contract]
    G --> K[Remove Asset or Process]
    H --> L[Sign-Off by Management]
```

*Explanation:* A decision tree showing the four canonical risk treatment strategies and the logical conditions under which each is selected — a high-frequency KTU diagrammatic question.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Components of the Risk Equation (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[THREAT] --> R[RISK]
    V[VULNERABILITY] --> R
    I[IMPACT or ASSET VALUE] --> R
    R --> S[SCORE]
    S --> T[RANKING]
    T --> U[PRIORITY FOR TREATMENT]
    C[CONTROLS] -.Reduce.-> R
```

*Explanation:* The foundational risk triangle is presented as a conceptual block diagram, where Threat, Vulnerability, and Impact combine multiplicatively to produce a risk score, which is ranked and prioritized for treatment. Controls act as a negative modifier on Risk.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Risk Analysis. Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative Risk Analysis.*

**Model Answer:**

**Risk Analysis** is the systematic process of identifying, estimating, and prioritizing risks to information system assets by evaluating threats, vulnerabilities, and potential impacts. It is a core component of the Risk Management lifecycle (NIST SP 800-30 / ISO 27005).

| Parameter | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| Data Type | Descriptive (Low/Med/High) | Numerical (₹, probabilities) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Time | Fast | Slow |
| Subjectivity | High | Low |
| Output | Risk levels (categorical) | ALE in monetary units |
| Best For | Initial screening, small orgs | Large orgs with historical data |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)? How is it computed? Mention its significance.*

**Model Answer:**

**ALE (Annualized Loss Expectancy)** is the expected monetary loss to an asset due to a specific threat over a one-year period.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

Where:
- $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}$
- $\text{ARO}$ = estimated number of occurrences per year

**Significance:**
1. Provides a **monetary baseline** for risk exposure.
2. Used in **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** to justify security controls.
3. Enables **risk ranking and prioritization** in financial terms.
4. Supports management **decision-making** on risk treatment budgets.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **NIST SP 800-30 Risk Assessment Methodology** in detail. List and describe each step of the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company has identified the following risks for its cloud database:
- Total Asset Value = ₹20,00,000
- Threat A: DDoS attack, EF = 30%, ARO = 4/year
- Threat B: SQL Injection, EF = 60%, ARO = 1/year
- Threat C: Insider Data Theft, EF = 80%, ARO = 0.5/year

**Compute** the SLE and ALE for each threat, **rank** the threats by ALE, and **recommend** the most critical threat to mitigate first. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**The NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1 Risk Assessment Methodology consists of 9 sequential steps:**

1. **System Characterization** — Define system boundaries, identify hardware, software, data, people, and interfaces. Output: System Description Document.

2. **Threat Identification** — Identify threat sources (natural, human accidental, human intentional) and threat events. Estimate likelihood of threat initiation.

3. **Vulnerability Identification** — Catalogue system weaknesses (missing patches, misconfigurations, weak authentication) and assess their severity.

4. **Control Analysis** — Evaluate existing security controls (preventive, detective, corrective) and their effectiveness.

5. **Likelihood Determination** — Combine threat likelihood and vulnerability severity into an overall likelihood rating (Low/Moderate/High).

6. **Impact Analysis** — Determine the adverse effect on **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** of the asset.

7. **Risk Determination** — Combine likelihood and impact to assign a risk level. Use a **Risk Matrix** (typically 3×3 or 5×5).

8. **Control Recommendations** — Propose new or enhanced controls (administrative, technical, physical) to reduce risk.

9. **Results Documentation** — Compile the **Risk Assessment Report** with findings, recommendations, and management sign-off.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for diagram + 6 marks for step-wise explanation with weighting of ~0.75 per step)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Asset Value (AV) = ₹20,00,000

**Step 1 — Compute SLE for each threat**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_A &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{SLE}_B &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{SLE}_C &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 16{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE for each threat**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_A &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 4 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} \\
\text{ALE}_B &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} \\
\text{ALE}_C &= 16{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rank the Threats by ALE (Highest First)**

| Rank | Threat | SLE (₹) | ARO (/year) | ALE (₹/year) |
|------|--------|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | Threat A (DDoS) | 6,00,000 | 4 | **24,00,000** |
| 2 | Threat B (SQL Injection) | 12,00,000 | 1 | **12,00,000** |
| 3 | Threat C (Insider Theft) | 16,00,000 | 0.5 | **8,00,000** |

**Step 4 — Recommendation**

> **Threat A (DDoS Attack) should be mitigated FIRST** because it has the highest ALE (₹24,00,000/year), driven by its high frequency (ARO = 4). The organization should deploy a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)**, **CDN with DDoS absorption**, and **rate-limiting controls**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for SLE, 2 marks for ALE, 1.5 marks for table/ranking, 1.5 marks for justified recommendation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **four Risk Treatment Strategies** with suitable real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce website processes ₹50,00,000 in annual transactions. The IT team has identified a credit-card fraud risk with an EF of 25% and an ARO of 3/year. Calculate SLE, ALE, and ROS after deploying a fraud-detection control that reduces risk by 60% at an annual TCO of ₹1,50,000. Perform a Cost-Benefit Analysis and recommend whether to deploy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

The four canonical **Risk Treatment Strategies** (per ISO 27005) are:

1. **Risk Mitigation (Reduction)** — Apply controls to reduce likelihood or impact.
   - *Example:* Deploying MFA reduces the risk of credential theft by 80%.

2. **Risk Transfer** — Shift financial impact to a third party.
   - *Example:* Purchasing cyber insurance or outsourcing payroll to a vendor with SLA.

3. **Risk Avoidance** — Eliminate the risk entirely by removing the source.
   - *Example:* Discontinuing an insecure legacy service used by only 0.1% of customers.

4. **Risk Acceptance** — Formally acknowledge and retain the risk.
   - *Example:* Accepting the risk of a 1-hour website downtime during planned maintenance.

*(7 marks: 4 strategies × 1.5 marks each = 6 marks + 1 mark for introduction/ISO reference)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Asset Value (AV) = ₹50,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 25% = 0.25
- ARO = 3 events/year
- Control Effectiveness = 60% (0.60)
- TCO of Control = ₹1,50,000/year

**Step 1 — Compute SLE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF} \\
\text{SLE} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 \\
\text{SLE} &= 12{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹ per incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE (Before Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 12{,}50{,}000 \times 3 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 37{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Residual Risk (After Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROS} &= \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} \times (1 - 0.60) \\
\text{ROS} &= 37{,}50{,}000 \times 0.40 \\
\text{ROS} &= 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CBA} &= (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ROS}) - \text{TCO} \\
\text{CBA} &= (37{,}50{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000) - 1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{CBA} &= 22{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{CBA} &= 21{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation**

> Since **CBA = ₹21,00,000/year > 0**, the fraud-detection control is **strongly recommended** for deployment. The organization achieves a net benefit of ₹21,00,000 per year after recovering the control cost.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks for SLE, 1.5 marks for ALE, 1.5 marks for ROS, 1.5 marks for CBA, 1 mark for recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert EF from % to decimal** (e.g., writing 25 instead of 0.25) — costs 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up SLE and ALE** — SLE is per-incident; ALE is per-year. Examiners deduct heavily for this.
> 3. **Ignoring TCO in CBA** — students often compute (ALE_before − ALE_after) only and forget to subtract the control cost.
> 4. **Confusing Residual Risk with Initial Risk** — Residual Risk is calculated **after** controls, not before.
> 5. **Forgetting units in final answer** — always write **₹/year** for ALE and **₹/incident** for SLE.
> 6. **Qualitative questions demand definitions** — in 3-mark Part A, do not skip the formal definition; jump straight to comparison and lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk Analysis (Module 4)**

- **Foundational Equation:** Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact (Asset Value)
- **SLE Formula:** $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$
- **ALE Formula:** $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$
- **CBA Formula:** $\text{CBA} = \text{ALE(before)} - \text{ALE(after)} - \text{TCO}$
- **ROS Formula:** $\text{ROS} = \text{Total Risk} \times (1 - \text{Control Effectiveness})$
- **Decision Rule for CBA:** Deploy control **if and only if** CBA > 0.
- **Decision Rule for Risk Treatment:** Choose **Mitigation** unless cost > impact, then **Transfer/Avoid/Accept**.
- **NIST SP 800-30 has 9 steps** — remember order: Characterization → Threat → Vulnerability → Control → Likelihood → Impact → Determination → Recommendation → Documentation.
- **Risk Matrix Scoring:** Multiply Likelihood × Impact; classify 1–4 Low, 5–12 Medium, 13–25 High (5×5 matrix).
- **Three Analysis Types:** Qualitative (subjective, cheap), Quantitative (objective, monetary), Semi-Quantitative (hybrid scoring).
- **Four Treatment Strategies:** Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid, Accept — memorize with one example each.
- **Standards to Reference in Answers:** NIST SP 800-30, ISO/IEC 27005:2022, OCTAVE Allegro.
- **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA Triad)** are the three impact dimensions — always mentioned in impact analysis answers.
- **Risk Analysis is iterative** — it loops back after treatment; it is not a one-time process.
- **Units Matter:** Always specify ₹/year for ALE, % for EF, events/year for ARO.
- **Common Exam Traps:** Forgetting to convert %, confusing SLE/ALE, omitting TCO in CBA, ignoring units in final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dealing with Disaster
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST863 - TOPICS IN SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dealing with Disaster

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disaster (in Information Security Context):** A sudden, unplanned, catastrophic event that causes severe disruption to an organization's critical information systems, infrastructure, or business operations, resulting in the loss of data, services, or operational capacity for an unacceptable period of time.

According to the **NIST SP 800-34 Rev. 1** framework (a globally accepted standard referenced in KTU syllabus), a disaster is defined as any event that disrupts an organization's essential business functions and requires the implementation of **continuity and recovery** procedures to restore normal operations.

### 1.2 Classification of Disasters (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

| Classification | Definition | Security Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Disasters** | Environmental/Geological events beyond human control | Physical destruction of data centers, power loss |
| **Man-Made Disasters** | Intentional or accidental human-induced events | Cyber-attacks, sabotage, terrorism, arson |
| **Technical/Infrastructure Disasters** | Hardware, software, or network failures | Server crashes, OS corruption, network outages |
| **Environmental Disasters** | Utility failures and HVAC breakdowns | Power outages, cooling failures, water leaks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus:** Examiners frequently ask students to **classify disasters and provide two examples for each category**. Memorizing the four-tier classification above is a guaranteed **3-mark question** in Part A.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Emergency Plan" Intuition

Think of disaster management like a **hospital's emergency preparedness protocol**:

- **Just as a hospital** prepares for earthquakes, mass casualties, or pandemic outbreaks **before** they happen, an IT organization must build its **Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP)** *before* the breach or outage occurs.
- **Triage System**: When patients flood in, doctors don't start from scratch — they have color-coded triage tags (**Red/Critical, Yellow/Urgent, Green/Stable**). Similarly, in IT disaster recovery, we use **prioritization tiers** to decide which systems come back online first.
- **Backup Power Generators**: A hospital never depends on a single power source — it has **generators, UPS, and battery backups**. An IT organization does the same with **redundant servers, offsite backups, and cloud mirrors**.
- **Drills & Simulations**: Hospitals conduct **monthly mock drills**. IT organizations must conduct **Disaster Recovery Testing** quarterly to ensure their plans work when actually needed.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"A disaster without a plan is just chaos with a deadline."* — Always remember that **preparation** distinguishes a recoverable incident from a company-ending catastrophe.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology Block (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **eight (8) foundational terms** MUST be memorized verbatim for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination):

1. **BIA** — Business Impact Analysis
2. **BCP** — Business Continuity Plan
3. **DRP** — Disaster Recovery Plan
4. **RTO** — Recovery Time Objective
5. **RPO** — Recovery Point Objective
6. **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair/Recover
7. **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures
8. **SLA** — Service Level Agreement (often contains RTO/RPO clauses)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Disaster Recovery Lifecycle

The **NIST Disaster Recovery Planning (DRP) Lifecycle** is the gold-standard framework adopted by KTU's 2024 scheme syllabus. Below is the exhaustive five-phase breakdown:

### **Phase 1 — Project Initiation & Scope Definition**
- Identify stakeholders, executive sponsors, and regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., **RBI, SEBI, HIPAA, GDPR**).
- Define the boundaries of the plan: which business units, systems, and geographic sites are covered.
- Acquire formal **top-management sign-off** (mandatory — without it, the plan is non-binding).

### **Phase 2 — Vulnerability Assessment & Risk Assessment**

This phase answers three critical questions:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})$$

Where:
- **Threat** = Any potential danger (natural, human, technical).
- **Vulnerability** = Weakness that a threat can exploit.
- **Impact** = Business consequence if exploited.

### **Phase 3 — Business Impact Analysis (BIA)**

The **BIA** is the *heart* of disaster recovery planning. It identifies:

- **Critical Business Functions** (e.g., payment processing, customer login).
- **Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD)** — Total time a system can be down before business survival is threatened.
- **Resource Requirements** — People, technology, facilities, data.
- **Dependencies** — Upstream/downstream system chains.

### **Phase 4 — Strategy & Plan Development**
Choose recovery strategies based on cost-benefit:

| Strategy | Cost | Recovery Speed | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Site** | Low (₹) | Slow (Days-Weeks) | Non-critical workloads |
| **Warm Site** | Medium (₹₹) | Moderate (Hours-Days) | Mid-priority systems |
| **Hot Site** | High (₹₹₹) | Fast (Minutes-Hours) | Mission-critical systems |
| **Cloud DR (DRaaS)** | Variable (Pay-per-use) | Variable | Modern enterprises |

### **Phase 5 — Testing, Training & Maintenance**
- **Tabletop exercises** (paper walkthroughs)
- **Simulation drills** (controlled mock disasters)
- **Parallel testing** (live data, isolated environment)
- **Full interruption testing** (rare, high-risk)

---

## 2.2 The Master Equation: RTO vs RPO — The KTU Favourite

These two metrics are **THE most frequently tested formulas** in KTU ESE for this module. Every student must internalize them.

### **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)**

$$\text{RTO} = T_{\text{disaster occurs}} - T_{\text{system fully restored}}$$

> **Definition:** Maximum acceptable time gap between **system failure** and **full restoration of service**.

### **Recovery Point Objective (RPO)**

$$\text{RPO} = T_{\text{last successful backup}} - T_{\text{disaster occurs}}$$

> **Definition:** Maximum acceptable **data loss** measured in time (i.e., how far back you must roll back to your last backup).

### **Critical Relationship: The RTO-RPO Trade-off**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower RPO = More Frequent Backups = Higher Cost.**
> **Lower RTO = Faster Recovery = Higher Cost.**
> KTU students must explain this **inverse cost relationship** between RTO/RPO and infrastructure spending.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Day Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is your one-stop revision sheet.** Print this mentally before every ESE attempt.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Score** | $R = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact) | Numeric (0–10 scale) | Often paired with 5×5 or 3×3 matrices |
| 2 | **RTO** | $RTO = T_{restore} - T_{fail}$ | Minutes / Hours / Days | Always state assumptions clearly |
| 3 | **RPO** | $RPO = T_{disaster} - T_{last\_backup}$ | Time units | Drives backup frequency |
| 4 | **MTD (Maximum Tolerable Downtime)** | $MTD \geq RTO + RPO$ | Time units | If MTD < RTO → plan is infeasible |
| 5 | **MTTR** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Hours | Higher MTTR → weaker DR process |
| 6 | **MTBF** | $MTBF = \frac{T_{total\_operation}}{N_{failures}}$ | Hours | Higher MTBF → more reliable system |
| 7 | **Availability** | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Percentage (0–100%) | Five-9's = 99.999% uptime |
| 8 | **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Currency (₹/Year) | Used in quantitative risk analysis |
| 9 | **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor$ | Currency (₹) | Exposure Factor is a percentage |
| 10 | **Cost-Benefit Ratio** | $CBA = \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}}{Cost\_of\_Control}$ | Ratio | If CBA > 1, control is justified |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** In your answer sheet, never write **|x|** with vertical pipes — examiners have been instructed to **deduct 0.5 marks** for ambiguous mathematical notation. Use the LaTeX **\vert** or **\mid** style explicitly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These DR concepts are not academic — they power real industries:

- **Banking Sector (RBI Mandate):** Indian banks must maintain **RPO ≤ 15 minutes** for core banking systems as per RBI's Cyber Security Framework (2016, updated 2023).
- **Stock Exchanges (NSE/BSE):** Trading systems require **RTO ≤ 5 minutes** to avoid massive financial losses.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Patient data must have **RPO ≤ 1 hour** for life-critical systems.
- **Cloud DR (AWS/Azure):** Modern cloud-native DR uses **geo-redundant storage** to achieve RPO near zero.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From Disaster to Recovery — A Complete Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **eight-step logical progression** represents the canonical KTU-evaluated disaster handling framework. Each step contains its reasoning row, formula justification, and examiner commentary.

---

### **Step 1: Disaster Identification & Classification**

$$\text{Disaster}_{category} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Natural} & \text{if environmental cause} \\
\text{Man-Made} & \text{if human-induced} \\
\text{Technical} & \text{if hardware/software failure} \\
\text{Environmental} & \text{if utility/HVAC failure}
\end{cases}$$

> **Reasoning Row:** Before any recovery can begin, the disaster must be correctly classified. This classification determines which **playbook** is invoked from the DR runbook library.

---

### **Step 2: Initial Damage Assessment (IDA)**

$$D_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to system component *i* (e.g., database server = 0.4, web server = 0.2).
- $s_i$ = Severity score (0–10) for the damage to component *i*.
- $n$ = Total number of affected components.

> **Reasoning Row:** The damage score $D_{score}$ ranges from 0 (no damage) to 10 (catastrophic). A score > 7 triggers **immediate full DR activation**; a score 4–7 triggers **partial DR**; a score < 4 is handled by **standard incident response**.

---

### **Step 3: BIA-Based Priority Ranking**

$$\text{Priority}_{system\_j} = \frac{(I_j \times U_j)}{T_j^{recovery}}$$

Where:
- $I_j$ = Business Impact (₹ loss per hour if down).
- $U_j$ = Number of users/customers affected.
- $T_j^{recovery}$ = Estimated time to restore system *j*.

> **Reasoning Row:** Systems with **higher impact, more users, and faster recovery** are restored first. This is the **classic triage logic** borrowed from emergency medicine.

---

### **Step 4: Selection of Recovery Site**

Let $C_{site}$ = total cost of operating a recovery site over one year.

$$C_{cold} < C_{warm} < C_{hot}$$

And the recovery times follow the reverse order:

$$RTO_{cold} \gg RTO_{warm} \gg RTO_{hot}$$

> **Reasoning Row:** There is an **inverse relationship** between cost and recovery speed. Selection follows the constraint:

$$RTO_{selected} \leq MTD_{business\_function}$$

If this inequality is violated, the chosen strategy is **infeasible** and a higher-tier site must be selected.

---

### **Step 5: Backup Verification**

The **3-2-1 Backup Rule** is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Backup}_{coverage} = B_1 \cup B_2 \cup B_3$$

Subject to:
- **3** total copies of data (production + 2 backups).
- **2** different storage media types (e.g., disk + tape).
- **1** copy stored offsite (geographically distant).

> **Reasoning Row:** The 3-2-1 rule ensures that the failure of any single storage medium, or even a localized disaster (fire, flood), cannot destroy all copies. This is a **necessary (but not sufficient)** condition for robust DR.

---

### **Step 6: RTO & RPO Configuration**

Given:

$$T_{disaster} = 14{:}30 \text{ hrs}$$

$$T_{last\_backup} = 14{:}00 \text{ hrs}$$

$$T_{system\_restored} = 15{:}45 \text{ hrs}$$

Compute:

$$\text{RPO} = T_{disaster} - T_{last\_backup} = 14{:}30 - 14{:}00 = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\text{RTO} = T_{restored} - T_{disaster} = 15{:}45 - 14{:}30 = 75 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Reasoning Row:** The system lost **30 minutes worth of data** (RPO) and was down for **75 minutes total** (RTO). If business requirements demanded RPO ≤ 15 min, this backup strategy is **inadequate** and must be tightened.

---

### **Step 7: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) Calculation**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset\_Value} \times \text{Exposure\_Factor (EF)}$$

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- Server value = ₹10,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 0.6 (60% destruction expected)
- ARO = 0.25 (once every 4 years)

$$\text{SLE} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

> **Reasoning Row:** The organization stands to lose ₹1.5 lakh per year on average. Any control costing less than ₹1.5 lakh/year that mitigates this risk yields a **positive ROI** (Return on Investment).

---

### **Step 8: Cost-Benefit Verification for Control**

$$\text{CBA} = \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}}{C_{control}}$$

If CBA > 1 → **Control is economically justified**.
If CBA < 1 → **Control is not worth implementing**.

> **Reasoning Row:** This final step closes the loop — disaster planning is not just technical; it must be **financially defensible** to the board. KTU examiners love asking students to justify controls using CBA.

---

## 3.2 Summary Matrix: Disaster Types vs Recovery Strategies

| Disaster Type | Detection Speed | Recovery Strategy | Typical RTO | Typical RPO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Earthquake** | Immediate | Hot site + Cloud failover | < 1 hour | < 15 min |
| **Cyber-Attack (Ransomware)** | Hours-Days | Isolated restore from clean backup | 4–24 hours | 1–4 hours |
| **Power Outage** | Immediate | UPS → Generator → Cloud failover | < 30 min | 0 (real-time mirror) |
| **Hardware Failure** | Minutes | Redundant components + Hot spare | < 1 hour | < 5 min |
| **Insider Threat** | Days-Weeks | Forensics + Clean rebuild | 1–7 days | Variable |
| **Pandemic** | Days | Work-from-home infrastructure | < 24 hours | 0 (cloud-based) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 — Definition Type**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: Define Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP). List any four types of disasters that an organization must prepare for.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP)** is a documented, structured set of procedures and policies that outline how an organization will respond to and recover from catastrophic events that disrupt its information systems and business operations. It forms the technical-operational subset of the broader Business Continuity Plan (BCP).

The four categories of disasters are:

1. **Natural Disasters** — earthquakes, floods, hurricanes, lightning strikes.
2. **Man-Made Disasters** — terrorist attacks, arson, sabotage, insider threats.
3. **Technical Disasters** — hardware failure, software corruption, network outages.
4. **Environmental Disasters** — power failures, HVAC breakdowns, water leaks.

---

### **Question A2 — Conceptual Short Answer**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Differentiate between RTO and RPO with a suitable numerical example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | RTO (Recovery Time Objective) | RPO (Recovery Point Objective) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum time to restore service | Maximum acceptable data loss |
| **Measures** | Downtime duration | Data loss duration |
| **Drives** | Recovery infrastructure cost | Backup frequency cost |

**Numerical Example:** Suppose a database crashes at **10:00 AM**. The last successful backup was at **09:30 AM**. The system is fully restored at **11:00 AM**.

$$\text{RPO} = 10{:}00 - 09{:}30 = 30 \text{ minutes of data loss}$$

$$\text{RTO} = 11{:}00 - 10{:}00 = 60 \text{ minutes of downtime}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Choice Format)

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

> **Q: (a)** Explain the five phases of the Disaster Recovery Planning (DRP) lifecycle in detail. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** A company has a server worth ₹20,00,000 with an exposure factor of 0.4. The threat is expected to occur once every 5 years. Calculate the SLE and ALE. If a control costs ₹50,000 per year and reduces the ARO to once in 20 years, evaluate whether the control is justified using the Cost-Benefit Analysis. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **NIST SP 800-34 Disaster Recovery Planning Lifecycle** consists of the following five phases:

**Phase 1 — Project Initiation and Scope Definition (1 Mark)**
- Secure executive sponsorship and top-management commitment.
- Define scope: which business units, systems, and sites are covered.
- Establish the disaster recovery team with clear roles and responsibilities.

**Phase 2 — Vulnerability Assessment and Risk Assessment (1.5 Marks)**
- Identify all credible threats (natural, man-made, technical, environmental).
- Identify vulnerabilities in hardware, software, personnel, and procedures.
- Calculate risk scores using the formula $R = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact).
- Rank risks in a risk register.

**Phase 3 — Business Impact Analysis (BIA) (1.5 Marks)**
- Identify critical business functions and their dependencies.
- Determine the **Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD)** for each function.
- Establish **RTO** and **RPO** targets for each system.
- Quantify financial, operational, and reputational impacts.

**Phase 4 — Strategy and Plan Development (1.5 Marks)**
- Select recovery site type (Cold / Warm / Hot / Cloud DRaaS).
- Design backup architecture (3-2-1 rule compliance).
- Develop communication plans, escalation procedures, and recovery runbooks.
- Document the complete DRP and obtain formal sign-off.

**Phase 5 — Testing, Training, and Maintenance (1.5 Marks)**
- Conduct **tabletop exercises**, **simulation drills**, and **parallel tests**.
- Train all staff on their DR roles and responsibilities.
- Review and update the plan **annually** or after any major change.
- Conduct post-incident reviews and integrate lessons learned.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Asset Value (AV) = ₹20,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 0.4
- Original ARO = 1/5 = 0.2 occurrences/year
- Control Cost = ₹50,000/year
- New ARO after control = 1/20 = 0.05 occurrences/year

**Step 1: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 8{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Calculate ALE Before Control**

$$\text{ALE}_{before} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{before} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 = 1{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 3: Calculate ALE After Control**

$$\text{ALE}_{after} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{after} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 40{,}000$$

**Step 4: Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = \text{ALE}_{before} - \text{ALE}_{after} = 1{,}60{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$\text{CBA} = \frac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Control Cost}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{50{,}000} = 2.4$$

**Conclusion:** Since **CBA = 2.4 > 1**, the control is **economically justified**. The organization saves ₹2.40 in expected losses for every ₹1.00 invested in the control.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

> **Q: (a)** Discuss the various types of recovery sites (Cold, Warm, Hot) with their merits, demerits, and suitable use cases. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** Describe the 3-2-1 backup rule and explain why it is considered a best practice in disaster recovery. Illustrate with a real-world scenario where its absence led to a catastrophic data loss. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Site Type | Setup Time | Cost (₹) | Hardware | Data Currency | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Site** | 2–7 days | Low | Empty space + power | None (must be loaded) | Non-critical, archival systems |
| **Warm Site** | 4–24 hours | Medium | Partial hardware + power | Stale (hours/days old) | Mid-priority systems |
| **Hot Site** | Minutes–1 hour | High | Full hardware + active data | Real-time replicated | Mission-critical systems |

**Cold Site (1.5 Marks):**
- *Merits:* Lowest cost; suitable for budget-constrained organizations.
- *Demerits:* Slowest recovery (days to weeks); requires manual hardware procurement and software installation; **not suitable for time-sensitive operations**.

**Warm Site (2 Marks):**
- *Merits:* Balanced cost-vs-speed; pre-installed hardware and OS; recovery within hours.
- *Demerits:* Data may be stale; requires periodic synchronization; moderate capital expenditure.

**Hot Site (2 Marks):**
- *Merits:* Fastest recovery (near-zero RTO); real-time data replication; minimal business disruption.
- *Demerits:* Highest cost (often **5–10x** warm site cost); continuous synchronization overhead; requires sophisticated infrastructure.

**Modern Alternative — Cloud DRaaS (1.5 Marks):**
- Pay-per-use model; elastic scaling; geo-redundancy built-in.
- Examples: **AWS CloudEndure, Azure Site Recovery, Google Cloud DR Service.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**The 3-2-1 Backup Rule (3.5 Marks):**

The 3-2-1 rule, popularized by photographer Peter Krogh and adopted by NIST, mandates:

- **3** — Maintain at least **three (3) copies** of all critical data (production + 2 backups).
- **2** — Store backups on **two (2) different types of media** (e.g., disk + tape, or disk + cloud).
- **1** — Keep **one (1) copy offsite** (geographically distant from primary site).

**Why It Is a Best Practice (2 Marks):**

- **Defense-in-Depth:** Even if two storage media fail simultaneously, data survives.
- **Geographic Resilience:** An offsite copy survives localized disasters (fire, flood, earthquake).
- **Ransomware Protection:** Air-gapped offline backups defeat encryption-based attacks.
- **Compliance:** Meets regulatory requirements (RBI, SEBI, GDPR, HIPAA).

**Real-World Scenario — The Missing 3-2-1 Rule (1.5 Marks):**

In **2017, Maersk (the global shipping giant)** suffered a NotPetya ransomware attack that destroyed **45,000 PCs and 4,000 servers** across 150 countries. Maersk's domain controllers were completely wiped, and crucially, **their backups were also infected** because they were connected to the same network. The only surviving data was a single, accidentally-isolated backup in a domain controller in **Ghana** that had been disconnected due to a local power outage.

This single, offsite, network-isolated copy saved Maersk from total collapse and cost them approximately **$300 million** in remediation. Had the 3-2-1 rule been rigorously applied, the recovery would have been significantly faster and cheaper.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 PLACES WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RTO and RPO:** Students frequently swap these definitions. **Memory trick:** RPO is about **POint-in-time** (data), RTO is about **Time-to-recover** (service).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions in numerical problems:** When asked to calculate RTO/RPO, students must **explicitly state** the values of $T_{disaster}$, $T_{last\_backup}$, and $T_{restored}$ from the problem statement. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Listing disaster types without examples:** Examiners want **specific examples** (e.g., "earthquake" not just "natural disaster"). Lose 1 mark per missing example.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the "offsite" requirement in 3-2-1:** Students often write "three copies on two media" but forget the **offsite** aspect. This is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **CBA Misinterpretation:** Students calculate CBA but forget to **interpret the result** ("Since CBA > 1, control is justified"). A naked number without interpretation loses 2 marks.

---

- [x] **RPO Formula:** $RPO = T_{disaster} - T_{last\_backup}$ — measures **data loss**.
- [x] **MTD Constraint:** $MTD \geq RTO$ — if violated, plan is infeasible.
- [x] **Three Recovery Sites:** Cold (slow, cheap), Warm (balanced), Hot (fast, expensive).
- [x] **3-2-1 Backup Rule:** 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite.
- [x] **SLE Formula:** $SLE = Asset\_Value \times EF$.
- [x] **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$.
- [x] **CBA Rule:** If $CBA > 1$, control is **justified**.
- [x] **Real-World Reference:** Know Maersk NotPetya (2017) case study for 3-2-1 rule illustration.
- [x] **Modern Trend:** Cloud DRaaS (AWS, Azure, GCP) is the contemporary alternative to physical hot sites.
- [x] **Indian Regulatory Context:** RBI mandates RPO ≤ 15 min for core banking; SEBI mandates RTO ≤ 5 min for trading systems.
